document: Relation extraction (RE) can be categorized by granularity into sentence-level and document-level tasks (Doddington et al., 2004; Xu et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Early work centers on sentence-level RE, including rule-based patterns and feature-driven classifiers (Pantel and Pennacchiotti, 2006; Mintz et al., 2009). Neural architectures subsequently became dominant for RE, leveraging distributed representations and end-to-end training (Xu et al., 2015; Song et al., 2019). Document-level relation extraction (DocRE) has emerged to capture interactions across mentions distributed over multiple sentences (Yao et al., 2019). Sequence-based DocRE models encode documents as token or sentence sequences, frequently adopting pre-trained Transformers for representation learning (Devlin et al., 2019). Zhou et al. (2021) report that attention weights can indicate cross-sentence cues and introduce pair-specific thresholds for decision making. Zhang et al. (2021) formulate DocRE as semantic segmentation and predict a relation matrix over entities to capture local and global structure while exploring calibration of confidence predictions. Pre-trained sequence encoders have also been adapted to aggregate token-level evidence across long contexts (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020), and dataset curation practices emphasize document completeness (Wadden et al., 2019). Quirk and Poon (2017) propose an entity-centric graph, and Christopoulou et al. (2019) add typed edges. Zeng et al. (2020) develop dual graphs for path reasoning, and Xu et al. (2021a) fuse commonsense. Different from prior studies, we propose SIEF, a general framework that guides diverse DocRE architectures with sentence importance scoring and a focusing loss to prioritize evidence sentences, improving robustness and overall performance.

document:
Detection of adversarial examples is a well-explored field in the image domain. Earlier works have tackled detection in various ways such as input transformation (Xu et al., 2018), statistical analysis (Grosse et al., 2017), and training a separate binary classifier (Metzen et al., 2017). Carlini and Wagner (2017) showed that an adversary with partial knowledge of the detector can often nullify it. In parallel, early work in novelty detection suggested that generative models can flag anomalous samples (Bishop, 1994). Building on this idea, recent approaches use internal neural features with likelihood-based scoring to detect out-of-distribution inputs.

In the NLP domain, comparatively fewer efforts target word-level adversarial examples and their detection, though character-level noise has been documented to degrade models (Pruthi et al., 2019). Zhou et al. (2019) proposed DISP, a detector trained on adversarial samples in a joint detect-and-defend framework. FGWS (Mozes et al., 2021) reported improved detection by leveraging the prevalence of rare words in attacked texts. FGWS experiments focus on two proposed attack methods and highlight the benefits of frequency-aware features. Le et al. (2021) addressed UniTrigger attacks (Wallace et al., 2019), which prepend or append a fixed phrase to all sentences, but the method requires significant adjustment for other attack families. Jia et al. (2019) studied adversarial spelling errors and character-level defenses for text classifiers. Ebrahimi et al. (2018) presented HotFlip to flip characters by following gradient-based saliency. Alzantot et al. (2018) introduced a population-based attack with similarity constraints on candidate replacements. Ren et al. (2019) proposed PWWS to prioritize word substitutions for crafting adversarial examples in NLP. Complementing these strands, our work studies corpus-driven cues that remain stable across attacks and models, enabling a unified detector without attack-specific tuning.

spans: Kobayashi (2018) estimates distributions over substitutes with bidirectional context to generate label-preserving replacements using a language model. Masked language modeling with BERT has been used to propose candidates for token edits, EDA methods rely on simple lexical heuristics for insertions and swaps, and back-translation produces paraphrases across languages that diversify surface forms (Devlin et al., 2019; Wei and Zou, 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016) without aligning them explicitly to task constraints.

Back-translation with iterative round trips expands examples for classification and QA, consistency training promotes agreement under perturbations, and UDA combines unlabeled data with augmentation-driven regularization to reduce overfitting on small corpora (Sennrich et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2018). Manifold mixup interpolates hidden states to encourage linear behavior, Paraphrase-T5 sampling yields diverse rewrites, and Cutoff masks spans in embeddings to simulate deletions while training robust predictors for sequence labeling and NLI tasks across diverse domains with gains on sentiment, entailment, and slot filling benchmarks (Zhang et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020).
document: Data augmentation for NLP is commonly grouped into input-level editing and representation-level perturbation, where the latter is often realized via adversarial or stochastic noise in embeddings (Miyato et al., 2016). At the input level, token replacement produces paraphrases by swapping words with semantically related alternatives, frequently using WordNet for synonyms and simple lexical rules (Miller, 1995; Wei and Zou, 2019). Kobayashi (2018) estimates distributions over substitutes with bidirectional context to generate label-preserving replacements using a language model. Masked language modeling with BERT has been used to propose candidates for token edits, EDA methods rely on simple lexical heuristics for insertions and swaps, and back-translation produces paraphrases across languages that diversify surface forms (Devlin et al., 2019; Wei and Zou, 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016) without aligning them explicitly to task constraints. Representation-level approaches replace one-hot inputs with soft distributions or adversarial perturbations, and smoothing with MLM can leverage bidirectional context while supporting efficient batching across an entire sentence (Gao et al., 2019). Back-translation with iterative round trips expands examples for classification and QA, consistency training promotes agreement under perturbations, and UDA combines unlabeled data with augmentation-driven regularization to reduce overfitting on small corpora (Sennrich et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2018). Manifold mixup interpolates hidden states to encourage linear behavior, Paraphrase-T5 sampling yields diverse rewrites, and Cutoff masks spans in embeddings to simulate deletions while training robust predictors for sequence labeling and NLI tasks across diverse domains with gains on sentiment, entailment, and slot filling benchmarks (Zhang et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020). Building on these insights, we introduce an interpolation-constrained MLM smoothing scheme that produces controllable soft substitutions at scale and demonstrate improved robustness for classification with strong parallelism across full sequences.

document: Few-shot slot tagging seeks to recognize and label semantic spans for novel intents with minimal annotated data, a setting increasingly relevant for rapid domain expansion in task-oriented dialogue. Prior work in classic sequence labeling frames slot tagging as token-level classification with contextual encoders and structured decoders such as BiLSTM-CRF and BERT-CRF (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016; Devlin et al., 2019). These models rely on abundant labeled examples per slot type, motivating research into transfer learning and meta-learning to reduce annotation cost while preserving label consistency (Bapna et al., 2017; Fritzler et al., 2019). Recent advances explore label-aware representations where slot descriptions or examples guide the tagger to generalize beyond seen schemas (Shah et al., 2019; Nam et al., 2022). In parallel, prompt-based learning reuses pretrained language model priors by casting tasks into cloze or generation formats that better align with pretraining objectives. Schick and Schütze (2021) showed that cloze-style prompts can elicit label semantics from masked language models. Gao et al. (2021) examined template choice and in-context exemplars for few-shot classification with calibrated probabilities. Liu et al. (2021) introduced continuous prompt embeddings that are optimized end-to-end across tasks using a small validation set for rapid adaptation in real deployments. Despite these advances, applying prompts to sequence labeling requires mapping subtoken predictions to spans, reconciling BIO constraints with free-form completions, and carefully choosing templates and verbalizers for open-set regimes. We evaluate approaches on ATIS, SNIPS, and MultiWOZ-style slot tagging splits with protocols emphasizing domain transfer and label sparsity. Hou et al. (2020) leveraged label descriptions to transfer slots across domains. Ding et al. (2021) applied prompt-conditioned decoders to sequence tagging, and Lee et al. (2022) reported gains on ATIS and SNIPS with task-specific verbalizers and templates. Our analysis highlights persistent challenges around label name ambiguity, cross-domain lexical drift, and calibration under extreme class imbalance. Building on these insights, we present a modular framework that integrates label descriptions, prompt templates, and constrained decoding while preserving efficiency under few-shot budgets. In this part, we first present a formal definition of the few shot slot tagging task in Section 2.1, followed by an introduction of the conventional sequence labeling approaches in Section 2.2 and Sequence Labeling with Prompts in Section 2.3.

spans: Prompt-based control methods steer generation using attribute classifiers or energy models (Dathathri et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Influence functions estimate the effect of training points on predictions and have been used to interpret NLP models (Koh and Liang, 2017; Han and Tsvetkov, 2021). document: There has been a great deal of recent interest in providing explanations of black-box machine learning models, emphasizing instance-level interpretability and model limitations (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2016; Ribeiro et al., 2018; Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola, 2018; Wallace et al., 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2020). In contrast, our goal is to explain how the input can be changed to achieve a desired outcome, a form of counterfactual explanation that prescribes actionable edits to attain a target label (Wachter et al., 2017; Ustun et al., 2019). While natural language rationales have been used to describe visual counterfactuals for image classifiers, they have not been tailored to counterfactual predictions for text-based tasks (Hendricks et al., 2018). Prior interactive learning for semantic parsing aims to correct the system via feedback rather than reveal minimal changes that would flip the model decision, making our approach complementary (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017). In NLP, counterfactually augmented datasets indicate that minimal edits can both probe model robustness and improve generalization by breaking spurious correlations (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). Beyond data augmentation, algorithmic recourse studies formalize cost-aware, feasible transformations under domain constraints, an idea increasingly adapted to discrete text spaces (Wachter et al., 2017; Ustun et al., 2019; Karimi et al., 2020). For textual counterfactuals, constraints on fluency and semantics are commonly enforced with pretrained language models, paraphrase filters, and minimality objectives to maintain meaning while altering the decision boundary (Madaan et al., 2021; Morris et al., 2020; Ribeiro et al., 2020). Prompt-based control methods steer generation using attribute classifiers or energy models (Dathathri et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Influence functions estimate the effect of training points on predictions and have been used to interpret NLP models (Koh and Liang, 2017; Han and Tsvetkov, 2021). Editing approaches prioritize fluency constraints and minimal changes only. We formalize textual recourse as an optimization problem over edit operations constrained by task-specific feasibility sets derived from ontologies and lexical resources. Our method proposes a search procedure that trades off cost, fluency, and label confidence, yielding actionable suggestions that can be realized by end users. Unlike adversarial example generation, which often tolerates imperceptible perturbations, we require edits to be comprehensible and to preserve the author's intent except for the attribute under change. We evaluate on sentiment classification and toxicity detection, measuring success rate, average cost, semantic similarity, and human-judged plausibility to assess practical usefulness. Baseline comparisons include gradient-based token replacements, paraphrase-based edits, and nearest-neighbor retrieval from counterfactual corpora, and results show consistent gains in actionable validity and user preference, highlighting the value of explicit feasibility constraints and interpretable cost models for counterfactual guidance.

document: A large body of recent work (Søgaard and Bingel, 2017; Vu et al., 2020; Bettgenhäuser et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2020; Poth et al., 2021) exists that examines when these transfer learning methods are more effective than simply fine-tuning on the target task. Oftentimes, these explanations involve recognizing catastrophic forgetting (Phang et al., 2018; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020b; Wang et al., 2018a) although recent work has called for them to be reexamined (Chang and Lu, 2021). This paper is orthogonal to those, as we examine when you should choose MTL or STILTs, rather than when they are more effective than the standard fine-tuning case. In fact, these strategies could be combined to predict transfer and then use the best method. As our task is different, theoretical explanations for how these methods work in relation to each other will need to be explored in future work. Potential theories suggested by our results are discussed in Appendix C, and are left to guide those efforts. Prior surveys have contrasted multi-task scheduling, auxiliary task selection, and curriculum design in neural NLP (Ruder, 2017; Bingel and Søgaard, 2017). Within that literature, performance is sensitive to task relatedness, representation sharing, and optimization dynamics (Caruana, 1997; Hashimoto et al., 2017). In parallel, intermediate-task training has been shown to stabilize fine-tuning and improve low-resource generalization (Phang et al., 2019; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020a). Analysis of when intermediate tasks help often invokes notions of domain similarity, label entropy, and hypothesis class bias (Aghajanyan et al., 2021; Vu et al., 2020). Recent benchmarks report gains on GLUE and SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). T5 pretraining objectives unify supervision under a text-to-text interface (Raffel et al., 2020). Dataset cartography characterizes example difficulty and training dynamics (Swayamdipta et al., 2020). Calibration for transfer has been studied (Guo et al., 2017). Despite these advances, there is little guidance on choosing between concurrent multi-task optimization and sequential intermediate training for a new target. Our study addresses this gap by quantifying task affinity and interference signals that can be computed from small pilot runs. We operationalize affinity using gradient cosine similarity, mutual information between labels and shared features, and performance deltas under parameter freezing. For interference, we examine negative transfer events across epochs, forgetting curves after curriculum switches, and validation loss hysteresis. We evaluate on diverse classification and sequence labeling tasks spanning sentiment, natural language inference, paraphrase, and named entity recognition. Models are standardized to control for capacity, tokenizer, and optimization schedule so that strategy effects can be isolated. The resulting decision rules indicate regimes where MTL is preferred, such as balanced, related tasks with complementary inductive biases. They also indicate regimes where STILTs is preferred, notably when auxiliary supervision is rich but loosely aligned and catastrophic interference is likely under joint training.

document: Machine translation seeks to map a source sentence to a fluent and adequate target sentence. Given a source sequence, the model estimates the conditional distribution over target sequences. Neural sequence-to-sequence models operationalize this objective with encoder–decoder architectures. Attention mechanisms and Transformers have substantially improved accuracy over recurrent baselines (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017). Monolingual summarization compresses a document into a shorter text that preserves salient information. Abstractive systems generate novel phrasings while extractive systems select spans from the input (Rush et al., 2015; Nallapati et al., 2016). Evaluation typically relies on n-gram overlap and factuality judgments, motivating models that plan content before generation. Cross-lingual summarization requires producing a summary in a target language for a source document written in a different language. The task couples translation and summarization under length constraints and with asymmetric discourse structures. Pipeline approaches translate first and then summarize, which amplifies errors and drifts away from the source intent. Joint models share multilingual encoders and decoders to reduce mismatch and encourage semantic alignment (Liu et al., 2020). Multilingual pretraining provides cross-lingual representations that benefit low-resource settings (Conneau et al., 2020). Latent variable models introduce stochastic structure that can capture alternative content plans and control information density. Smith et al. (2017) introduced a multilingual pointer-generator for summarization across related languages. Liu and Lapata (2019) explored transformer-based summarizers with pretraining objectives such as masked language modeling. Ladhak et al. (2020) presented WikiLingua as a benchmark for cross-lingual summarization. Zhu et al. (2020) proposed a dual attention mechanism for machine translation with document context. Narayan et al. (2018) studied controllable summarization with length constraints and styles. We study a conditional latent-variable framework for cross-lingual summarization that integrates planning into generation. Our hypothesis is that a compact, language-agnostic representation of summary-worthy content improves faithfulness and coverage. Concretely, we employ a conditional variational auto-encoder with a prior that conditions on the source article and target language tag. The recognition network approximates the posterior using the gold summary during training to shape the latent space through KL regularization (Kingma and Welling, 2014). At inference, the model samples or selects latent codes from the prior to balance diversity and adequacy. We compare against pivoting, multilingual fine-tuning, and constrained decoding baselines under a unified training budget. Experiments on WikiLingua and newly curated news corpora show consistent ROUGE and factuality gains with modest computational overhead.

document: Vision-and-Language Navigation introduced the first VLN benchmark that combines real imagery and natural language route instructions (Chang et al., 2017). To address this task, a SERL model was proposed to learn reward functions from expert demonstrations. Combining imitation learning and reinforcement learning has proven beneficial for VLN (Wang et al., 2019). Because the dataset is relatively small, augmentation approaches enhance robustness (Fried et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2021a). Auxiliary losses incorporate semantic signals to guide training and improve grounding (Majumdar et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020). Pretraining methods learn generic cross-modal representations and were extended with recurrent modules to improve sequential action prediction (Hao et al., 2020; Hong et al., 2021). Nonetheless, the limited variety of pretraining environments constrains generalization to unseen buildings. Most related to this work, VLN-BERT transfers knowledge from abundant but out-of-domain image–text data to improve path–instruction matching (Majumdar et al., 2020). We further construct an in-domain corpus by sampling trajectories and composing templated instructions, and we introduce a prompt-based pretraining strategy for VLN. Vision-and-language pretraining has advanced rapidly by extending BERT-style objectives to image–text pairs while exploring one-stream and two-stream architectures for inter-modal grounding (Devlin et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Alberti et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020a; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020b). UNITER (Chen et al., 2020) aligns regions and words with a unified transformer. LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019) learns separate encoders for vision and language with cross-modality layers. OSCAR (Li et al., 2020b) uses object tags as anchor points to improve grounding. VisualBERT (Li et al., 2019) unifies vision-language pretraining with a single-stream architecture. Pixel-BERT (Huang et al., 2020) removes object detectors and learns directly from image patches end-to-end.

(2019) examined correlations between these aspect ratings and automatic scores across VIST and related narrative datasets. BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) and ROUGE (Lin, 2004) were contrasted with BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019), BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020), UNION (Guan and Huang, 2020), and reference-free scoring using pretrained checkpoints (Sinha et al., 2020).
document: Visual storytelling was introduced by Huang et al. (2016) as the task of generating a coherent story from five images. The Sequential Images Narrative Dataset (SIND) contains photo sequences paired with multiple human-written short stories to serve as references. For each image sequence, 2 to 5 reference stories are provided, encouraging models to capture event progression rather than isolated captions. Subsequent work emphasizes modeling temporal and causal structure, and some researchers integrate knowledge graphs to diversify entities and plots (Hsu et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021a). Reinforcement learning has also been explored to reward narratives that reduce factual and topical errors (Hu et al., 2020a). Despite these advances, evaluation remains a bottleneck because surface-similarity metrics often fail to reflect narrative quality. Hu et al. (2020b) and Wang et al. (2020b) ask crowd workers to rate stories on predefined aspects such as coherence, relevance, and expressiveness. Hsu et al. (2019) examined correlations between these aspect ratings and automatic scores across VIST and related narrative datasets. BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) and ROUGE (Lin, 2004) were contrasted with BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019), BLEURT (Sellam et al., 2020), UNION (Guan and Huang, 2020), and reference-free scoring using pretrained checkpoints (Sinha et al., 2020). Yet reference-based metrics can penalize legitimate plot choices that diverge from the single reference phrasing, weakening alignment with human preferences. We therefore evaluate both reference-based and reference-free metrics on VIST story pairs and analyze which signals best predict human rankings.

document: Human values are of concern to most if not to all social sciences (Rokeach, 1973) and have also been integrated into computational frameworks of argumentation (Bench-Capon, 2003). In NLP, values have been analyzed for personality profiling (Maheshwari et al., 2017), but not yet for argument mining, as considered here. Argument mining seeks to detect claims, premises, and their relations in text, supporting downstream tasks like persuasion analysis and policy synthesis (Stab and Gurevych, 2017; Lippi and Torroni, 2016). Recent work has begun to align argumentative components with normative dimensions such as fairness, autonomy, and harm to enable value-aware reasoning (Kiesel et al., 2019; Wachsmuth et al., 2017). Yet systematic mappings from surface rhetoric to explicit value taxonomies remain underexplored in large corpora. Existing value theories in the social sciences provide structured inventories that could ground such mappings, including Schwartz’s basic values and related extensions (Schwartz, 1992; Schwartz, 2012). Computational models can operationalize these inventories via lexicons, embeddings, and prompt-based classifiers trained on lightly supervised signals (Hessel et al., 2020; Demszky et al., 2020). Some studies in computational ethics annotate moral foundations to capture normative cues in text, offering proxy labels for value dimensions (Hoover et al., 2020; Garten et al., 2016). Cross-domain transfer remains challenging because value expressions are context-sensitive and rhetorically strategic in debates and policy discussions (Habernal and Gurevych, 2017). Large language models have been prompted for controllable generation using pattern-based instructions and retrieval-augmented exemplars (Gao et al., 2021). Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated in-context learning with GPT-3 across diverse tasks, highlighting emergent capabilities under few-shot conditioning. Liu et al. (2021) provided a comprehensive survey of prompt design, soft tuning, and calibration methods for aligning pretrained models with task goals. Sanh et al. (2022) released a multitask mixture for instruction tuning that standardizes templates and formats. Despite advances in generative control, argument mining requires explicit linkage between argumentative roles and value claims grounded in domain discourse. Prior argument-quality models rate persuasiveness or clarity but rarely annotate normative rationales that motivate stances (Habernal and Gurevych, 2016; Potash et al., 2019). Value detection differs from sentiment because it targets abstract commitments rather than affective polarity, demanding richer semantic features and contextual cues. Recent datasets include debates, petitions, and position papers, yet they either lack value labels or rely on coarse moral categories that conflate distinct priorities (Ajjour et al., 2017; Strzalkowski et al., 2019). Weakly supervised methods can bootstrap seed lexicons into domain-specific cues, but noise accumulation hinders precise mapping to taxonomies (Arora et al., 2020). Contrastive learning offers a way to align argumentative spans with prototype value representations by maximizing agreement between paraphrases and canonical definitions (Gao et al., 2021). Multi-task setups that jointly predict stance, claim type, and value signals may improve data efficiency and calibrate uncertainty in out-of-domain contexts (Sanh et al., 2022; He et al., 2019). Our framework operationalizes a compact value schema, integrates cross-encoder and bi-encoder architectures for retrieval and scoring, and incorporates rationale extraction to support transparency. We evaluate on heterogeneous sources and report both classification metrics and calibration diagnostics to measure the reliability of value assignments in argument mining.

document: Data augmentation has emerged as a practical remedy for data scarcity in semantic parsing and related structured prediction tasks. Early methods emphasized surface manipulations, often relying on context-free templates or lexical substitutions. Jia and Liang (2016) proposed recombination with synchronous context-free grammars to generate adversarial yet grammatical examples. Andreas (2019) introduced rule-based token exchanges that respect local context while varying lexical realizations. These techniques operate on the form rather than deep meaning, limiting their ability to rearrange compositional structure. Beyond rule systems, Akyürek et al. (2020) trained a prototype-based generative model to synthesize recombined utterances and meaning representations. In parallel, substructure substitution has been effective for tasks outside semantic parsing, including paraphrase and slot-filling (Shi et al., 2021). Syntactic manipulations such as tree cropping and rotation have benefited POS tagging in low-resource settings (Şahin and Steedman, 2019) and improved dependency parsing robustness (Vania et al., 2019). Tree swapping strategies have also been tested for dependency parsing with promising results (Dehouck and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020). Neural program induction has been surveyed in the context of compositional generalization (Lake and Baroni, 2018). Data augmentation by back-translation improved question answering and natural language inference (Edunov et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018). Prompt-based tuning enabled flexible task adaptation with large language models using cloze-style objectives (Liu et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Brown et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation reduced redundancy in encoders substantially. To the best of our knowledge, fine-grained substitution of semantic subtrees tied to meaning functions, rather than syntax alone, has not been systematically explored for semantic parsing. document: Large-scale pre-training models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) have drawn much attention in the NLP and speech communities, due to their strong capability of generalization and efficient usage of large-scale data (Devlin et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021c; Baevski et al., 2020; Kharitonov et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021a). However, the research mentioned above largely gears toward single-modal learning, hence these models can only be used in either text or speech modeling. Although some speech-language pre-training work (Chung et al., 2021b; Kim et al., 2021) attempts to improve spoken language understanding tasks, these methods only focus on an encoder with task-specific layers for different tasks and do not pre-train a decoder for generation tasks such as speech synthesis or text generation. This separation between modality-specific encoders and absent or shallow decoders limits the transfer of generative knowledge across tasks. Unified sequence-to-sequence formulations that span both speech and text remain comparatively underexplored in pre-training. Bridging this gap requires architectures and objectives that can accommodate heterogeneous inputs while normalizing representations into a common latent space. The proposed SpeechT5 method is most related to T5 (Raffel et al., 2019). The core idea of the T5 model, a unified framework for a variety of text-based language problems, is to treat every text processing problem as a text-to-text problem. SpeechT5 is also related to Speech Chain (Tjandra et al., 2020), which leverages the ASR model and TTS model to build a closed-loop machine speech chain to train models on the concatenation of both labeled and unlabeled data, and SpeechNet (Chen et al., 2021b), which designs a universal modularized model to perform multiple speech processing tasks with multi-task learning. The differences from the above models are that (1) SpeechT5 is a shared cross-modal encoder-decoder framework, whose input and output are speech or text through multiple pre- and post-nets; (2) SpeechT5 attempts to pre-train and improve the universal model with large-scale unlabeled text and speech data. Concretely, SpeechT5 employs modality-specific front-ends to map raw waveforms and tokenized sentences into a joint embedding space processed by a common Transformer backbone. A suite of self-supervised objectives, including masked reconstruction and cross-modal denoising, encourages the model to learn alignments that benefit downstream tasks. Prior studies explored aligning audio and text with contrastive objectives (Radford et al., 2021). Multitask frameworks added adaptor layers for speech translation and intent classification (Tsai et al., 2019; Ravanelli et al., 2021). Large-scale instruction tuning has been investigated for chatbots (Ouyang et al., 2022; Thoppilan et al., 2022). Another related work is SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021), a benchmark to examine the capability of pre-trained models such as HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021). SUPERB focuses on investigating a simple framework to learn tasks with a frozen and shared pre-trained encoder and lightweight prediction modules fine-tuned for each task. In contrast, the goal of SpeechT5 is to learn spoken language processing tasks by fine-tuning a unified-modal encoder-decoder model that is pre-trained on unlabeled speech and text corpora. Unlike benchmarks that constrain adaptation to linear or shallow heads, our approach explicitly trains a decoder that can generate sequences in either modality. This design enables end-to-end fine-tuning for ASR, TTS, speech translation, and text summarization within a single parameterization. Prior cross-modal frameworks such as CLIP-style contrastive learning focus on paired image–text signals and do not directly address speech generation (Radford et al., 2021). Multilingual encoder-decoder pre-training has advanced speech translation, yet typically omits pure TTS or pure text generation in the same model (Li et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021). Our formulation unifies conditional generation, recognition, and representation learning under one backbone with lightweight modality adapters only at the I/O layers. We evaluate zero-shot transfer to unseen speakers and domains by freezing the backbone and tuning modest adapters to probe generalization. We further ablate the contribution of each self-supervised objective and show that cross-modal denoising yields the largest improvements on low-resource settings. Together, these observations position SpeechT5 as a practical step toward general-purpose speech–language modeling that preserves simplicity while covering a broad spectrum of tasks.

document: In this section, we first go through some of the most related works for BERT compression in the literature and then review the few works that have used Kronecker decomposition for compression of CNNs and RNNs. Model compression for transformer encoders has explored pruning, quantization, distillation, and structured factorization to meet latency and memory budgets in production. Pruning approaches remove redundant weights or attention heads to shrink inference cost without retraining from scratch. Head- and neuron-level pruning demonstrate that many components are dispensable for specific tasks (Michel et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019). Quantization reduces arithmetic precision while preserving accuracy under post-training or quantization-aware regimes (Jacob et al., 2018; Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020). Distillation transfers knowledge from a large teacher to a compact student with task-aligned objectives (Hinton et al., 2015; Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020). Low-rank and tensorized parameterizations substitute dense projections with compact structures that exploit separability (Wang et al., 2020; Noach and Goldberg, 2020). Within this space, Kronecker-structured decompositions provide a principled way to approximate large matrices using products of small factors and have been explored in vision and speech networks (Novikov et al., 2015; Tai et al., 2016; Tjandra et al., 2017). Layer dropping in Transformers reduces depth while keeping accuracy on GLUE tasks (Fan et al., 2019). Movement pruning applies magnitude-aware masks to fine-tuned models and yields sparse yet competitive checkpoints (Sanh et al., 2020). Dynamic quantization reports CPU serving for BERT-base, and structured matrices were explored for linear layers (Zafrir et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018). These strategies largely focus on parameter removal or precision reduction, while structural reparameterization via Kronecker products remains comparatively underexplored for transformers. Kronecker factorization approximates dense matrices with structured products, reducing parameters while preserving algebraic properties (Zhang et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2018). For CNNs, Kronecker-factored convolutional kernels report solid accuracy–efficiency tradeoffs on ImageNet and CIFAR benchmarks (Tai et al., 2016; Wen et al., 2017). For RNNs, decomposing recurrent weight matrices by Kronecker products stabilizes training and compresses gates with minimal degradation (Tjandra et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2018). Extending these designs to self-attention and feed-forward layers encourages separable token–channel modeling and reduces the memory footprint of projection blocks. Parameter sharing across layers cuts memory while retaining quality on translation tasks (Dehghani et al., 2019). Block-circulant matrices accelerate multiplications in fully connected sublayers and show hardware-friendly sparsity patterns (Cheng et al., 2015). Low-rank adapters reduce fine-tuning cost for shifts with compact updates (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022). We position our Kronecker-based approach within this landscape by targeting attention and feed-forward layers with Kronecker factors and reporting favorable accuracy–latency profiles on standard NLU benchmarks.

document: Recently, many attempts have been made to accelerate large neural networks. Knowledge distillation is a prominent method for training compact networks to achieve comparable performance to a deep network. Hinton et al. (2015) first introduced the idea of knowledge distillation to exploit the "dark knowledge" (i.e., soft label distribution) from a large teacher model as additional supervision for training a smaller student model. Since its introduction, several works (Romero et al., 2015; Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2017; Tung & Mori, 2019; Park et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019) have investigated methods that align different latent representations between the student and teacher models for better knowledge transfer. In the context of knowledge distillation, MetaDistil shares some common ideas with the line of work that utilizes a sequence of intermediate teacher models to make the teacher network better adapt to the capacity of the student model throughout the training process, including teacher assistant knowledge distillation (TAKD) (Mirzadeh et al., 2020) and route constraint optimization (RCO). The intermediate teachers are heuristically selected independently of the training process and the evolution of the teacher network is discrete. In contrast, MetaDistil employs meta learning to make the teacher model adapt to the current state of the student model and provide a continuously evolving meta-teacher that can better teach the student. Park et al. (2021) update the teacher jointly with the student using task losses and capacity regularization with curriculum schedules and soft-target annealing across epochs. Shi et al. (2021) introduce co-training constraints for teacher awareness, Kim et al. (2020) study self-distillation without external teachers, and Gou et al. (2021) survey distillation objectives across architectures. However, the teacher models in their methods are still not optimized for knowledge transfer. Different from all aforementioned methods, MetaDistil employs meta learning to explicitly optimize the teacher model for better knowledge transfer ability, and leads to improved performance of the resulting student model.

document: Adversarial GLUE (Wang et al., 2021) employed expert-annotated templates for constructing adversarial data; however, it does not include NER as a task. A few previous works have explored the generation of adversarial examples specifically for the NER task. For instance, Gui et al. (2021) performed augmentations by concatenating sentences, swapping, inserting, and deleting characters inside entities, swapping with out-of-vocabulary entities, and cross category swapping. Zeng et al. (2020) took a random entity swap approach but selected entities of the same label to preserve linguistic correctness. In our work, we purposely alter entities and add targeted surrounding context with the goal of changing the label to surface NER hard cases. Ma and Hovy (2016) introduced a BiLSTM CNN CRF architecture for sequence labeling with character level features. Lample et al. (2016) explored neural CRF models with subword representations for multilingual NER. Devlin et al. (2019) presented BERT as a contextual encoder that boosts a wide range of token level tasks. Peters et al. (2018) proposed deep contextualized word representations that capture syntactic and semantic information. Akbik et al. (2018) introduced Flair embeddings derived from character level language models for robust sequence tagging. Clark et al. (2020) described ELECTRA pretraining that discriminatively learns token replacements and improves efficiency across tagging benchmarks. In terms of techniques to improve generalization, we take a closer look at mixup, proposed by Zhang et al. (2018). Mixup involves linear interpolation of examples and labels to create a vicinal distribution around the original data space, which improves the classifier's generalization when trained on these interpolated samples. Prior studies demonstrated that mixup reduces overconfidence under distribution shift and enhances calibration compared to models trained without mixup (Thulasidasan et al., 2020; Verma et al., 2019). The method also yields a smoother decision surface than empirical risk minimization, leading to gains in robustness against adversarial perturbations (Liang et al., 2018). We adapt mixup to token classification by defining interpolation over hidden representations aligned to spans and by adjusting loss weighting across mixed labels. This design reduces spurious correlations induced by our adversarial augmentations while preserving boundary-sensitive cues needed for NER. We integrate mixup with a standard transformer encoder and train jointly on original and augmented batches using identical optimization settings. Our training recipe retains the base model architecture and hyperparameters to isolate the contribution of augmentation and mixup. Experiments on diverse NER benchmarks evaluate the effect of our adversarial examples and the added regularization, showing consistent improvements in out-of-domain settings.

document: We study text-to-SQL semantic parsing over tables, where the goal is to translate questions and schemas into executable programs. This problem relates to instruction following, code generation, and knowledge graph QA (Chen and Mooney, 2011; Artzi and Zettlemoyer, 2013; Oda et al., 2015; Iyer et al., 2018; Berant et al., 2013; Yih et al., 2015). While we focus on English questions, similar formulations have been explored in multilingual settings (Jie and Lu, 2014; Sherborne et al., 2020). Formally, we map a question q and table T to a program π that yields the answer when executed on T under full supervision of gold programs during training. Generalization typically assumes i.i.d. splits, but real deployments face new compositions and new domains (Gu et al., 2021). Compositional generalization has been probed with synthetic data and specialized splits (Keysers et al., 2020; Lake and Baroni, 2018; Finegan-Dollak et al., 2018; Shaw et al., 2021). We address domain generalization for text-to-SQL, a setting underexplored compared to classification, translation, and QA (Daumé III and Marcu, 2006; Daumé III and Jagarlamudi, 2011; Talmor and Berant, 2019). Data synthesis for SQL parsing is explored (Yin et al., 2021). Meta-learning addresses few-shot adaptation on table scenarios (Wang et al., 2021). Pretraining aids schema linking and planning substantially (Herzig et al., 2020). We specifically target column operations that require mapping phrases to expressions over multiple or structured columns. Such operations are poorly covered in existing benchmarks, motivating a careful look at evaluation resources. WIKISQL emphasizes template-limited queries over Wikipedia tables (Zhong et al., 2017), whereas SPIDER stresses cross-schema generalization but often echoes column names in questions (Yu et al., 2018). Repartitioning and new test suites reduce these artifacts yet still omit many multi-column and background-knowledge cases (Deng et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2021; Suhr et al., 2020). We therefore construct splits on SQUALL, which augments WIKITABLEQUESTIONS with SQL and refined column types (Shi et al., 2020; Pasupat and Liang, 2015). For models, we study a sequence-to-sequence parser and a bottom-up parser built on BERT, representative of recent state-of-the-art on these datasets (Shi et al., 2020; Rubin and Berant, 2021; Devlin et al., 2019).

document: Recent multilingual translation research suggests that adapting subnetworks for each language or language pair can outperform fully shared models. Li et al. (2020) train a single multilingual encoder-decoder and then select task-specific layers for each pair. Lin et al. (2021) prune entries in weight matrices of a shared model to specialize it per language pair. Gong et al. (2021) target attention heads and feed-forward blocks to derive compact, language-adapted variants. Xie et al. (2021) identify general and language-specific neurons and tune them only with the corresponding language data. These trends motivate our non-shared pruning setting for multilingual pre-training rather than translation alone. Structured pruning for monolingual pre-trained language models has matured into a practical toolbox. Michel et al. (2019) propose gradient-based importance scores to remove attention heads while maintaining accuracy. Prasanna et al. (2020) and Liang et al. (2021) extend head importance to other components, especially intermediate units in feed-forward networks. Wang et al. (2020c) decompose weights and impose L0-style regularization to control low-rank structure during fine-tuning. Sajjad et al. (2020) study layer dropping and show that truncating upper layers can be surprisingly effective on downstream tasks. Peer et al. (2021) explore sensitivity-aware criteria that align pruning decisions with end-task gradients rather than proxy scores. Smith et al. (2019) measured cross-lingual transfer using diagnostic classifiers on XNLI and PAWS-X. Johnson and Lee (2020) introduced language-specific adapters for multilingual Transformers trained on Wikipedia corpora. Conneau et al. (2020) scaled masked language modeling to 100 languages with a larger shared vocabulary. Artetxe et al. (2021) examined zero-shot dependency parsing under limited supervision across typologically diverse families. Zhang and Zhou (2022) provided a multilingual evaluation suite that probes morphology, word order, and agreement phenomena. Against this background, we ask whether non-shared pruning can capture language-specific structure without incurring separate model instances. Our hypothesis is that gating vectors at the head, neuron, and embedding rank levels suffice to express controllable specialization. We adopt a Transformer encoder backbone trained with masked language modeling, following Vaswani et al. (2017) and Devlin et al. (2019). The encoder comprises N stacked layers, each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward sublayer. Residual connections and layer normalization wrap each sublayer to stabilize depth and training dynamics. Within attention, we learn binary or continuous gates over heads to determine which computations remain active after pruning. For feed-forward networks, we introduce a vector of unit gates that modulate intermediate activations and induce structured sparsity. To reduce the embedding matrix, we adopt a low-rank factorization with a gated basis that directly controls effective rank. All gates are trained jointly with the language modeling objective and sparsity-inducing penalties to balance accuracy and size. During fine-tuning, we freeze pruned structures and adapt only task parameters, enabling efficient specialization for new languages. We evaluate on cross-lingual understanding and transfer benchmarks, as well as supervised translation for a subset of pairs. Our baselines include unpruned shared models, per-language adapters, and fully separate per-language models of matched compute. The results show that non-shared pruning provides strong trade-offs between capacity, latency, and quality while preserving transfer.

document: Existing methods for taxonomy expansion can be grouped into alignment-driven techniques across multiple taxonomies (Ruiz-Casado and colleagues, 2005; Toral and Monachini, 2008; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2009; Yamada and collaborators, 2011) and machine learning approaches that score subgraphs for insertion. The learning approaches are commonly separated into synonymy-based Merge operations and hypernymy-based Attach operations. Synonymy-based expansion discovers equivalent senses by representing lexical items with distributional features and training classifiers to predict synonymy relations (Nakashole and colleagues, 2012; Wang and coauthors, 2019; Fei and collaborators, 2019). Additional heuristics such as spelling normalization, abbreviation handling, and gloss similarity have been shown to strengthen merge precision in curated resources. Hypernymy-oriented methods estimate the best parent among candidate nodes for a new term by combining lexical, path, and structural signals. Recent advances include neural ranking with path encoders and graph-aware objectives, as explored by Shen and colleagues (2018), Shen and coauthors (2020), Yu and collaborators (2020b), and Zhang and colleagues (2021). Alignment-based strategies propagate structure by mapping nodes across independently curated taxonomies and then transferring edges (Ruiz-Casado and colleagues, 2005; Toral and Monachini, 2008; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2009). While effective in some settings, attach-only pipelines often mis-handle near-synonyms by placing them as children instead of merging senses. Conversely, merge-only pipelines can collapse fine-grained distinctions that downstream reasoning and search rely on. Expanding WordNet highlights this tension because editors jointly add new concepts and fold duplicates. We address this setting by modeling both attach and merge actions within a single probabilistic framework. Shwartz and colleagues (2016) introduced path-based features with neural composition for hypernymy detection. Roller and collaborators (2018) explored large-scale is-a induction with count-based and embedding models across noisy web corpora. Jiang and coauthors (2017) presented taxonomic relation learning using entailment graphs and global consistency objectives. Camacho-Collados and colleagues (2018) leveraged distributional inclusion and lexical contrast signals for taxonomy enrichment. Bhagavatula and coauthors (2015) induced hierarchies from multi-relational graphs data. The framework uses a latent action variable to choose between operations while optimizing a shared ranking loss over candidate edges. Feature extractors aggregate evidence from lexical definitions, contextual usage examples, and local graph neighborhoods to support calibrated decisions. Learning signals are derived from historical diffs of WordNet releases and from high-quality distant supervision constructed via cross-resource anchors. Benchmarks include general-domain noun hierarchies and specialized scientific taxonomies with varying branching factors. Across these datasets, the unified model improves attachment accuracy and synonym recall without degrading ancestor consistency or path length metrics. We open-source code, data splits, and evaluation scripts to enable reproducibility and standardized comparisons.

document: Compression is a widely used strategy to speed up large language models, encompassing quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, and model distribution across devices (Gupta and Agrawal, 2020; Bai et al., 2020; Hinton et al., 2015; Banitalebi-Dehkordi et al., 2021). Among these techniques, distillation-based creation of compact models has been particularly popular in recent years. Sanh et al. (2019) introduced DistilBERT as a smaller BERT trained with distillation for general purposes. MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020) employed inverted bottlenecks and progressive knowledge transfer to compress while maintaining accuracy. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020) used a two-stage distillation process for pre-training and task-specific tuning, and SqueezeBERT (Iandola et al., 2020) leveraged grouped convolutions. ELM (Jiao et al., 2021) mapped layers to guide BERT distillation, and GhostBERT employed softmax-normalized convolutions as ghost modules. Despite their effectiveness, aggressive compression can harm accuracy and forces practitioners to maintain separate fixed-size models for different computational budgets. As an alternative, dynamic inference includes early-exit and length- or depth-adaptive transformers that allocate compute based on input difficulty (Shen et al., 2017; Kim and Cho, 2021; Hou et al., 2020). Early-exit methods such as ReasoNet and DeeBERT, and adaptive-width/depth methods such as the Length-Adaptive Transformer and DynaBERT, typically require architectural changes and retraining and are often limited to encoder-only classification, whereas our approach operates out of the box on encoder–decoder models and sequence-to-sequence tasks (Shen et al., 2017; Xin et al., 2020; Kim and Cho, 2021; Hou et al., 2020).

In particular, previous work (Kaushik et al., 2020) relied on large-scale crowd-sourcing to generate useful augmented data. More recently, Wang and Culotta (2021) investigated the efficacy of automatically generated counterfactuals for sentiment analysis. Similar to our work, these methods also consider the most salient features that a model uses when generating augmented data, which is in line with our rationale definition. Yet they use sentiment lexicon matching for identifying rationales, which is task-specific and not necessarily fully relevant. By contrast, we employ human annotators to identify rationales, which can be task-agnostic and robust. In addition, our method generates semi-factuals instead of counterfactuals used in previous work. Human-in-the-loop machine learning (Wu et al., 2021a) has received increasing research attention. Active learning (Settles, 2009; Margatina et al., 2021), the most common example of human-in-the-loop learning, asks annotators only to provide high-level labels for informative examples. There is also work exploring more explainable AI systems by exploiting feature-based information. Brown et al. (2020) presented large-scale in-context learning with GPT-3, showing that prompts alone can steer model behavior across tasks without gradient updates. Gao et al. (2021) explored discrete prompt construction for few-shot classification using retrieval-augmented templates. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt-based tuning methods and reported gains on diverse benchmarks, and Wei et al. Very recently, Yao et al. Prior work largely targets increasing the explainability of AI systems for high-stakes domains such as health and finance, instead of improving robustness or generalisation. Many approaches also assume access to a large amount of labelled data. In contrast, we focus on few-shot learning scenarios that are more compelling. Our approach integrates annotator-provided rationales with targeted semi-factual perturbations to amplify scarce supervision. We evaluate across sentiment, topic, and natural language inference tasks to test cross-domain transfer under tight budget settings. The results indicate consistent gains in robustness to lexical shortcuts and spurious correlations while preserving accuracy on in-domain data.

(2018) release a formality dataset with rule-based and neural baselines and discuss evaluation issues. Mir et al. document: When collecting nonparallel style transfer corpora, style labels often already exist in the data (e.g., positive and negative reviews (Li et al., 2018a)) or its source serves as a label (e.g., Twitter, academic texts, legal documents, etc.). Thus, data collection is reduced to fetching the texts from their sources, and the corpus size depends only on the available amount of text. Conversely, parallel corpora are usually more difficult to obtain. Some parallel style transfer datasets are drawn from naturally available parallel sources: the Bible dataset (Carlson et al., 2018) features multiple translations of the Bible from different epochs, and the biased-to-neutral Wikipedia corpus (Pryzant et al., 2020) leverages edit histories. Shen et al. (2017) present a cross-alignment autoencoder for style transfer without paired data and report improvements on sentiment-controlled generation. Li et al. (2018b) use controlled deletion, retrieval, and generation to modify attribute markers in text. Lample et al. (2019) explore unsupervised machine translation techniques for style transfer using denoising and back-translation. Xu et al. (2018) release a formality dataset with rule-based and neural baselines and discuss evaluation issues. Mir et al. (2019) examine content preservation metrics for style transfer and propose reference-free measures. Prabhumoye et al. (2018) employ back-translation through a pivot language to disentangle content and style representations. Besides these special cases, there exists a large style transfer dataset that was created from scratch. This is the GYAFC dataset (Rao and Tetreault, 2018) of informal sentences and their formal versions written by crowd workers and reviewed by experts. Since toxic-neutral pairs also do not occur in the wild, we follow this data collection setup with a notable difference—we replace expert validation of crowdsourced sentences with crowd validation and additionally optimize the cost. We sample prompts from diverse domains to reduce topical confounds and balance source attributes across topics. Crowd workers are instructed to preserve propositional content while rewriting to target style using lightweight rubrics. To mitigate label noise, we require triple agreement among independent raters and filter with adversarial checks. Our protocol yields parallel pairs with controlled lexical overlap, enabling reliable measurement of content preservation. We release predefined train, development, and test splits stratified by domain, sentence length, and attribute intensity. For evaluation, we report reference-based adequacy, style accuracy with a calibrated classifier, and human judgments on fluency. All data collection and annotation procedures were approved by an IRB and accompanied by fair-pay policies.

document: Fine-tuning of Language Models. Better fine-tuning of language models can be achieved by proper initialization (Dodge et al., 2020), regularization, or prompts (Schick and Schütze, 2021). Other tricks include bias correction in optimizers and re-initialization of top Transformer layers (Zhang et al., 2021). Instead of updating all parameters, some work explores learning only a few vectors (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Guo et al., 2021) or a few adapter modules (Houlsby et al., 2019). These parameter-efficient methods reduce memory while preserving downstream quality across diverse tasks. Hallucination Methods. Feature hallucination was introduced for visual recognition (Hariharan and Girshick, 2017) and later realized via meta-learning (Wang et al., 2018), variational inference (Luo et al., 2021; Lazarou et al., 2022), and adversarial learning (Tjio et al., 2022). Label hallucination assigns soft pseudo-labels to extend few-shot datasets (Jian and Torresani, 2022). In NLP, generative augmentation has moved from discrete edits to representation-space synthesis to preserve semantic control. Few-shot Language Models. Few-shot learning has improved machine translation (Arthaud et al., 2021), abstractive summarization (Fabbri et al., 2021), question answering (Hua et al., 2020; Ram et al., 2021), and entity recognition (de Lichy et al., 2021; Tong et al., 2021; Ding et al., 2021). Brown et al. (2020) introduced in-context few-shot prompting with large language models, and Gao et al. (2021) explored automatic prompt selection using mined templates for classification. He et al. (2022) studied calibration of verbalizer-based predictions in prompt tuning, while Min et al. (2022) empirically argued that label semantics rather than instructions often drive apparent in-context learning effects. Approaches include meta-learning (Li and Zhang, 2021; Bansal et al., 2020; Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Wei et al., 2021; Wei and Zou, 2019; Karimi et al., 2021b), and prompt-based tuning (Gao et al., 2021; Tam et al., 2021). Our method targets the data augmentation axis by learning to generate training features. Our method is a generative data augmentation technique in the embedding space. Different from Wei et al. (2021), which uses EDA to augment examples in the discrete input space, we hallucinate auxiliary examples in the embedding space. Our approach retains label compatibility by conditioning on task-specific embeddings and class prototypes. Our method shares similarity to FDA (Kumar et al., 2019), which is also a generative data augmentation method but operates in the feature space. Unlike FDA, which focuses on two intent classification tasks, our method applies to a wide range of NLP tasks, as shown by experiments on fifteen diverse benchmarks. We further integrate parameter-efficient fine-tuning to minimize the cost of adapting the generator. The generator is trained with contrastive reconstruction to avoid mode collapse in the learned features. We provide ablations comparing prompt tuning, adapters, and full fine-tuning under identical budgets. Our results demonstrate gains on classification, natural language inference, and question answering with limited labels. We also analyze sensitivity to the number of hallucinated samples per class and the diversity of prototypes. Finally, we examine robustness to distribution shifts and show improved calibration without additional labels.

document: There have been numerous attempts at compressing Transformer models that can be grouped into three broad directions. The first direction targets parameter redundancy through structure pruning that drops attention heads or feed-forward blocks while preserving topology (McCarley, 2019; Michel et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b; Fan et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021a). Knowledge distillation and quantization further reduce memory and compute by transferring predictions to smaller students and shrinking precision (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020b; Bhandare et al., 2019; Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2020). The second direction mitigates the quadratic attention cost using sparse patterns such as local and global windows (Child et al., 2019; Kitaev et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2019; Zaheer et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020). Kernelized or low-rank approximations convert attention into a linear-time estimator, with Performers offering unbiased random feature maps (Wang et al., 2020a; Choromanski et al., 2020). Our focus is the third direction, which reduces redundancy from carrying full token sequences across all layers in tasks driven by sequence-level objectives (Dai et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021b; Pietruszka et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021; Kim and Cho, 2020). Zhang et al. (2020) compress tokens with hierarchical pooling on long documents during inference. Dai et al. (2020) slide a fixed window and average representations to shrink the sequence. Wu et al. (2021b) compute centroid attention over a subset of tokens in intermediate layers. Ye et al. (2021) train a policy to drop low-utility tokens. These studies motivate methods that progressively shorten sequences while retaining class-discriminative evidence near the top of the network. We adopt a lightweight fine-tuning scheme that learns per-layer token selectors without any specialized pretraining. In contrast to reinforcement learning approaches that require training an additional policy over the full backbone, our selectors are optimized end-to-end with standard cross-entropy (Ye et al., 2021). We do not compare against works that provide only non-standard datasets or unreleased code, and we emphasize transparent, reproducible evaluation on common text benchmarks. Vision Transformers that pool spatial tokens are tailored to images and are not directly comparable to linguistic token selection (Pan et al., 2021; Heo et al., 2021). Overall, we present a simple attention-guided pruning mechanism that removes tokens across intermediate layers while preserving accuracy on sequence-level classification.

document: Neural dialogue models can acquire a range of conversational skills from large corpora. Prior work has shown progress on personality expression, empathy, and knowledge grounding in response generation. For example, Mou et al. (2016), Xing et al. (2017), and Zhao et al. (2017) established encoder–decoder baselines for open-domain chit-chat. Models that capture persona attributes include Qian et al. (2018) and Zhang et al. (2018b). Emotion and empathy have been addressed by Zhou et al. (2018) and Rashkin et al. (2019). Continual learning for dialogue seeks to accumulate skills across tasks while preventing catastrophic forgetting. Inductive transfer from pretrained language models is commonly operationalized via feature-based reuse, fine-tuning, or adapters (Ruder, 2019). We adopt the adapter-based approach to leverage shared backbone knowledge with a small number of task-specific parameters. Unlike conventional adapters that are optimized per task and then frozen, our adapters partition knowledge into general and task-specific components to mitigate interference. Knowledge in the proposed adapters is also used to bootstrap the acquisition of subsequent dialogue skills via transfer. Houlsby et al. (2019) introduced adapter layers for efficient parameterization of transfer in transformers, Poth et al. (2021) studied modular updates for multilingual tasks, and Pfeiffer et al. (2021) reported benefits of adapter composition across benchmarks in standard evaluation settings and datasets. Madotto et al. (2020) applied adapters to conversational agents without explicit transfer objectives, Smith et al. (2019) investigated replay strategies for sequential learning in classification, and Lee and Kim (2020) proposed dynamic routing in task-conditioned architectures for NLP. We extend this line by enabling forward transfer while strictly controlling cross-task contamination through selective routing. Our formulation treats each task as a skill primitive with explicit generalization channels shared across tasks. We evaluate continual acquisition on a suite of persona, empathy, knowledge, and stance dialogue datasets. Baselines include standard fine-tuning, replay with exemplar buffers, and parameter-isolation strategies such as adapters and LoRA. We report metrics for both within-task quality and cross-task retention to quantify stability–plasticity trade-offs. Ablations isolate the contribution of general versus task-specific adapter subspaces and study transfer depth across task orderings.

Since Rashkin et al. (2019) released EmpatheticDialogues, many approaches have been proposed to generate empathetic responses. Lin et al. (2019) propose a mixture of emotional experts. Majumder et al. (2020) propose emotion grouping, emotion mimicry, and stochastic sampling. Li et al. (2020) extract emotional words through a lexicon and propose an adversarial generative model. Shen et al. (2021) apply dual-learning with unpaired data for bidirectional empathy. Gao et al. (2021) integrate emotion cause into the response generation process through a gated mechanism. Sabour et al. (2021) and Li et al. (2022) use implicit commonsense for context modelling. Kim et al. (2021) train a model to extract words that cause the speaker's emotion and attach the RSA Framework (Frank and Goodman, 2012) to any generative model to generate responses, focusing on emotion-cause words.

Recently, many studies have shown remarkable improvements through recognizing transitions of features between utterances in open-domain multi-turn dialogues. Qiu et al. (2020) perceive transitions of emotion states for context modelling. Zou et al. (2021) propose a module to manage keyword transitions. Zhan et al. (2021) model external knowledge transitions to select knowledge used for generation. In multi-turn empathetic dialogues, we consider emotions, keywords, and utterance-level meaning (Gu et al., 2021) as important features of each utterance and propose a novel approach of recognizing feature transitions between utterances. To place our work in context, we review complementary lines on discourse modeling and controllable generation that inform feature tracking across turns. Barlas et al. (2021) present a discourse-aware encoder that predicts dialog acts to structure multi-turn context with hierarchical attention and constrained decoding. Xu and Hu (2022) introduce a planning-based decoder that selects content plans before generation in open-domain chat using explicit content sketches and controllable constraints. Liu et al. (2020) study topic flow constraints for dialog coherence with latent variables and report gains on DailyDialog under limited supervision settings. Building on these insights, our model jointly infers feature transitions and decodes responses with adaptive planning to enhance empathy and relevance.

document: Examples of the cross-modal learning paradigm are central to scalable multimodal retrieval. Many existing methods employ modality-independent encoders that map each modality to a shared embedding space. This approach attains strong performance on video-text, video-audio, and image-audio retrieval benchmarks despite differing pretraining corpora and losses. Bain et al. (2021) trained contrastive dual encoders for video-text alignment with data. Monfort et al. (2021) developed audiovisual representations from weakly labeled videos to support retrieval across sound and vision. Harwath et al. (2020) integrated vector-quantized units into speech-image models, and Miech et al. (2018) used attention for cross-modal matching in narrated videos. Despite differing datasets and architectures, these efforts rely on modality-independent encoders enabling precomputation of per-modality embeddings. Consequently, retrieval reduces to dot products between embeddings and yields fixed inference time at large scale. Alternative frameworks introduce cross-modal encoders or attention within the encoder stack, which raises retrieval complexity from O(m+n) to O(mn) and hinders single-modality analysis. Our framework retains light-weight retrieval while permitting targeted cross-modal interaction via a learned codebook and a Cross-Modal Code Matching objective. Beyond image-audio correspondences and VQ-based discretization reported by prior work, our approach uncovers modality-invariant units and generalizes to video-audio and video-text data.

Event argument typing with weak supervision has also been explored (Liu et al., 2018). Cross-lingual AMR alignment was proposed to harmonize semantic graphs (Damonte and Cohen, 2018). document:
Research efforts on monolingual ED are extensive and varied. Hand-crafted, feature-based, language-specific methods were the basis of early ED approaches (Ahn, 2006; Ji and Grishman, 2008; Patwardhan and Riloff, 2009; Liao and Grishman, 2010a,b; Hong et al., 2011; McClosky et al., 2011; Li et al., 2013; Miwa et al., 2014; Yang and Mitchell, 2016). More recent efforts have primarily made use of deep learning techniques such as convolutional neural networks (Nguyen and Grishman, 2015; Chen et al., 2015; Nguyen et al., 2016b), recurrent neural networks (Nguyen et al., 2016a; Sha et al., 2018; Nguyen and Nguyen, 2019), graph convolutional networks (Nguyen and Grishman, 2018a; Yan et al., 2019), adversarial networks (Hong et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019b), and pre-trained language models (Wadden et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019a; Yang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020).

Works on cross-lingual ED are not as prevalent and generally make use of cross-lingual resources employed to address the differences between languages such as bilingual dictionaries or parallel corpora (Muis et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019) and, more recently, pre-trained multilingual language models (M'hamdi et al., 2019; Hambardzumyan et al., 2020; Majewska et al., 2021). Unlike these previous efforts, our method leverages unlabeled data to further refine the language-invariant qualities of the language models. Adversarial Language Adaptation, inspired by models in domain adaptation research (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015; Naik and Rose, 2020), has been successfully applied at generating language-invariant models (Joty et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018). Our method improves upon these approaches by optimizing the adversarial training process through selecting the most informative examples from the unlabeled data. We implement a selector that scores unlabeled instances by uncertainty and agreement, encouraging updates that generalize across source and target languages. Prior work on instance selection for domain adaptation has explored active learning signals (Hsu and Lin, 2019) and curriculum strategies for robust training (Zhou et al., 2020). Several studies focus on multilingual representations for ED transfer, including zero-shot setups (Huang et al., 2020). Event argument typing with weak supervision has also been explored (Liu et al., 2018). Cross-lingual AMR alignment was proposed to harmonize semantic graphs (Damonte and Cohen, 2018). Denoising objectives improved multilingual encoders for sequence tagging (Artetxe et al., 2020).

In the context of ED, multilingual pre-training has also been complemented by alignment objectives that tie predicate-argument structures across languages (Subburathinam et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). We differ from this line by not requiring external alignment supervision and by keeping the architecture unchanged at inference time. We release code and model checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility and further research on cross-lingual ED.

document: Saliency modeling focuses on how visual attention is allocated across scenes and text in a task-dependent manner. Early computational models of visual attention used bottom-up mechanisms to approximate pre-attentive selection from visual input (Koch and Ullman, 1985). The central idea of a global saliency map was later introduced to integrate feature competition into a unified priority representation (Niebur and Koch, 1996). A longstanding hypothesis, often attributed to Yarbus, states that viewer goals can be decoded from fixation patterns, with mixed empirical support across tasks (Yarbus, 1967; Greene et al., 2012; Henderson et al., 2013; Borji and Itti, 2014). Deep convolutional and readout architectures have substantially improved saliency prediction by leveraging pre-trained feature hierarchies (Kümmerer et al., 2016). These advances have extended to vision–language applications including image captioning (Sugano and Bulling, 2016), visual question answering (Das et al., 2016), and text-guided object detection (Vasudevan et al., 2018). Predicting gaze during reading has likewise been studied using probabilistic generative accounts of fixation timing and skipping (Feng, 2006; Hara et al., 2012; Matthies and Søgaard, 2013; Hahn and Keller, 2016). Complementary token transition and scanpath models capture local dependencies in saccade sequences and regressions (Nilsson and Nivre, 2009; Haji-Abolhassani and Clark, 2014; Coutrot et al., 2017). More recently, deep linguistic encoders have been exploited as feature extractors for text saliency, raising questions about their cognitive plausibility and alignment with human reading strategies (Sood et al., 2020a). Eye-tracking signals have also been used to augment NLP systems through attention supervision and auxiliary losses that regularize model focus. Studies report gains for part-of-speech tagging (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015a,b; Barrett et al., 2018), sentence compression (Klerke et al., 2016), sentiment analysis (Mishra et al., 2016, 2017), and reading comprehension (Malmaud et al., 2020). In these settings, generic free-viewing gaze corpora are often repurposed without tailoring to the target task, leaving open how task demands modulate reading. From natural to task-specific reading, recent work combines eye-tracking with neuroimaging to probe the neural correlates of comprehension and attention (Wenzel et al., 2017). fMRI and EEG studies contrast natural reading with pseudo-reading paradigms to isolate lexical, syntactic, and semantic effects (Hillen et al., 2013; Choi et al., 2014). Behavioral analyses concurrently examine which textual regions attract fixations under different objectives, such as information search or summarization (Loboda et al., 2011; Wenzel et al., 2017). Despite progress, little is known about how task-specific supervision reshapes fixation allocation on core NLP tasks like relation extraction and sentiment classification. Building on this gap, we synthesize prior findings in vision and language to motivate models that condition gaze prediction on explicit task cues. We further assess whether task-aware gaze signals can serve as plug-in supervision for downstream encoders without sacrificing generalization across domains. To contextualize our approach, we review methodological advances in saliency learning objectives and cross-modal alignment. Liu et al. (2018) introduced a multi-level attention mechanism for hierarchical document modeling. Peters et al. (2018) presented deep contextualized word representations for language understanding. Ba et al. (2016) described robust layer normalization for stabilizing deep network training. We then contrast optimization criteria that calibrate probabilistic saliency with rank-based metrics to reconcile calibration and discriminative ordering. Finally, we situate task-aware reading within multimodal alignment, where gaze, text, and vision signals are jointly modeled to capture shared latent structure. Together, these contributions clarify when and how explicit task signals improve both gaze modeling and downstream language performance.
