spans: Smith et al. (2020) studied metric-based meta-learning for image classification. Chen et al. (2021) explored prompt calibration in language models for few-shot tasks. Zhao and Lin (2022) proposed retrieval-augmented classification with nearest-neighbor prototypes. Results were reported on benchmarks.
document: Few-shot Learning is often tackled by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021;Bansal et al., 2020;Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Arthaud et al., 2021;Wei et al., 2021;Kumar et al., 2019), and prompt-based fine-tuning (Gao et al., 2021;Tam et al., 2021;Schick and Schütze, 2021). Basu et al. (2021) applies contrastive learning in their few-shot semi-supervised intent classification, by using pairwise objectives to separate classes while leveraging unlabeled utterances. Meta-learning approaches typically learn an initialization or a metric that can be rapidly adapted to a new task from a handful of labeled examples (Finn et al., 2017;Vinyals et al., 2016). Optimization-based methods such as MAML optimize for fast parameter updates, while metric-based methods emphasize comparison in an embedding space. Data augmentation techniques, including paraphrasing and mixup, expand the effective training set to better cover the label space and reduce overfitting under scarce supervision (Xie et al., 2020;Sennrich et al., 2016). Prompt-based methods reframe classification as masked or generative completion, allowing pretrained language models to leverage their stored knowledge for label induction with minimal task-specific parameters (Liu et al., 2023). Recent work also investigates calibration, verbalizer learning, and template search to stabilize few-shot predictions and reduce prompt sensitivity (Zhao et al., 2021;Hambardzumyan et al., 2021;Jiang et al., 2020). Semi-supervised variants combine few labeled seeds with large unlabeled corpora through consistency training, pseudo-labeling, or contrastive objectives to further improve robustness (Miyato et al., 2017;Sohn et al., 2020). Smith et al. (2020) studied metric-based meta-learning for image classification. Chen et al. (2021) explored prompt calibration in language models for few-shot tasks. Zhao and Lin (2022) proposed retrieval-augmented classification with nearest-neighbor prototypes. Results were reported on benchmarks. Despite these advances, domain shift and label imbalance can degrade performance when target distributions deviate from source tasks. To mitigate shift, retrieval-augmented and adapter-based methods condition on external memory or lightweight modules without updating the full model (Khandelwal et al., 2020;Houlsby et al., 2019). Another line of work focuses on task-aware verbalizer induction that learns label words directly from data, thereby aligning model priors with task semantics (Meng et al., 2022;Zhou et al., 2023). For intent classification, template design and schema linking influence how well prompts capture label meaning, especially when intents are fine-grained and lexically overlapping (Chen et al., 2022). Contrastive learning has been integrated with prompts to structure representation spaces around label prototypes and to dampen spurious correlations from surface cues (Gunel et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2022). Cross-lingual and cross-domain few-shot settings add challenges related to tokenization, label transfer, and cultural variation in expressions of intent (Pires et al., 2019;Keung et al., 2020). Evaluation protocols vary widely in sample budgets, episode construction, and hyperparameter tuning ranges, making direct comparison difficult and underscoring the need for standardized, transparent reporting (Musgrave et al., 2020;Dodge et al., 2019). In this work, we revisit semi-supervised intent classification under limited labels by combining prompt-based supervision with curriculum-guided contrastive pretraining on unlabeled utterances. Our method learns label semantics through a small set of anchor demonstrations while aligning unlabeled examples via instance- and prototype-level objectives. We show consistent gains over strong baselines across three intent datasets and provide ablations that isolate the contributions of curriculum scheduling, prompt design, and contrastive temperature.

spans: XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) proposes a Translation Language Model over parallel sentences, reporting gains for cross-lingual classification. document: Multilingual pre-training has enabled strong cross-lingual transfer without parallel data, with models like mBERT and XLM-R demonstrating competitive performance across tasks. mBERT extends BERT by training a single masked language model on Wikipedia in over 100 languages, encouraging a language-invariant feature space. XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020a) scales this recipe to 2.5T Common Crawl sentences across 100+ languages (Wenzek et al., 2020), highlighting the benefits of massive corpora. Across diverse benchmarks, XLM-R consistently improves zero-shot transfer, suggesting that corpus size and coverage are key drivers of multilingual robustness. XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) proposes a Translation Language Model over parallel sentences, reporting gains for cross-lingual classification. Unicoder (Huang et al., 2019) applies multitask learning to corpora, ALM (Yang et al., 2020) models code-switching, and ERNIE-M (Ouyang et al., 2021) adds back-translation, INFOXLM (Chi et al., 2021) uses contrastive signals, VECO improves alignment. In this study, we deliberately restrict attention to multilingual masked language modeling without parallel signals and defer a systematic comparison with parallel-data methods to future work. To probe where cross-lingual generalization arises in such models, we revisit evidence from Pires et al. (2019) and Wu and Dredze (2019), contrast it with anchor-word-free tests by Conneau et al. (2020b) and K et al. (2020), and analyze structural factors in syntax and co-occurrence.

document: Text-to-speech systems have rapidly advanced with neural architectures that generate natural speech at low latency. Non-autoregressive TTS aims to remove sequential decoding dependencies to improve throughput while preserving quality. By predicting durations and other prosodic attributes in parallel, these models avoid exposure bias and reduce inference cost. Practical deployment favors such designs because they scale with input length and hardware parallelism. Early non-autoregressive approaches such as FastSpeech and ParaNet relied on knowledge distillation from autoregressive teachers to stabilize training. Subsequent work eliminated the teacher requirement by supervising duration, pitch, and energy directly from alignments and acoustic measurements. FastPitch introduced pitch-conditioned generation to improve control and audio fidelity. AlignTTS reported competitive quality with monotonic alignment search for robust duration modeling. FastSpeech 2 unified duration, pitch, and energy prediction with simple regression losses and became a strong baseline in the field. Beyond acoustic supervision, pre-trained language models have been explored to inject semantic context that guides prosody choices. Word- or phoneme-level BERT embeddings can provide information about syntax and focus that correlates with natural phrasing. Shen et al. (2018) integrated contextual vectors into an autoregressive decoder to improve prosody in long sentences. Fang et al. (2019) examined sentence-level embeddings for phrase break prediction and reported gains on internal benchmarks. Hsu et al. (2019) studied unsupervised unit discovery for speech and discussed its connection to linguistic structure. Kim et al. (2020) proposed a semantic prosody predictor using BERT features for expressive reading. Li et al. (2021) evaluated cross-lingual prosody transfer with multilingual encoders and noted stability issues on out-of-domain text. Zhang et al. (2022) introduced prompt-tuning for prosodic control in TTS and presented human preference results. In our framework, we condition the encoder on contextualized token embeddings while keeping the duration, pitch, and energy predictors unchanged. This design allows the model to leverage syntactic cues without complicating the acoustic stack or introducing dependence on external teachers. Variational autoencoders are commonly used to capture residual prosodic variability that is not explained by text alone. The latent variables summarize speaker intent and discourse-level factors, and they can be sampled or predicted from context at inference time. We adopt a conditional VAE with a posterior collapse mitigation schedule and a simple KL annealing scheme to preserve informative latents. The decoder consumes both deterministic predictors and the sampled latent vector, yielding controllable expressiveness without losing stability. We evaluate on multi-speaker corpora and show improvements in MOS, pitch RMSE, and duration MAE over competitive non-autoregressive baselines. Our analysis also demonstrates better robustness to out-of-domain punctuation and longer contexts, highlighting benefits of combining language-aware conditioning with stochastic prosody.

spans: Phuong and Lampert (2019) examined empirical knowledge transfer in classification networks and characterized student-teacher training dynamics with softened targets. Xu et al. (2020) progressively replaced teacher blocks with student counterparts using a probabilistic schedule on convolutional architectures and reported accuracy on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100; Wang et al. (2020) distilled self-attention relations to build MiniLM for language understanding; and Yang et al. (2019) explored layer dropping and representation alignment for compressing BERT.
document: We propose sparse progressive distillation to mitigate overfitting that arises when pruning under the pretrain-and-finetune paradigm. We demonstrate its effectiveness via controlled ablations and validate on six GLUE datasets spanning single-sentence and sentence-pair classification. Our method consistently outperforms competitive baselines while reaching high sparsity without large accuracy loss. In the pruning literature, magnitude-based pruning and structured variants remain standard starting points (Han et al., 2015), and the lottery ticket hypothesis highlights the existence of trainable sparse subnets (Frankle and Carbin, 2019). Comparisons between small-dense and large-sparse regimes further suggest superior expressivity for the latter when parameter counts match (Zhu and Gupta, 2018). Yet after pretraining, naïve sparsification often amplifies overfitting during finetuning, motivating distillation-guided sparsity control in our framework. Knowledge distillation transfers behavior from a high-capacity teacher to a compact student and stabilizes training for compressed models (Hinton et al., 2015; Mirzadeh et al., 2020). Distilled Transformers for language understanding demonstrate that student encoders can retain general linguistic competence from unlabeled corpora and task mixtures while exposing limits at extreme sparsity (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). Phuong and Lampert (2019) examined empirical knowledge transfer in classification networks and characterized student-teacher training dynamics with softened targets. Xu et al. (2020) progressively replaced teacher blocks with student counterparts using a probabilistic schedule on convolutional architectures and reported accuracy on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100; Wang et al. (2020) distilled self-attention relations to build MiniLM for language understanding; and Yang et al. (2019) explored layer dropping and representation alignment for compressing BERT. For Transformer-based compression, we adopt a progressive grafting schedule that preserves intermediate activations early in training and tightens sparsity targets as the student stabilizes, enabling high compression with limited degradation.

document:
Prechelt (1998) experimented on 14 different validation-based stop criteria. Prechelt (1998) focused on an issue that the validation error during training may represent many local minima prior to a global optimum.

Existing non-validation stop-criteria are generally based on statistical inference. Duvenaud et al. (2016) interpreted stochastic gradient descent in terms of variational inference and proposed an estimation method for the marginal likelihood of the posterior, which was applied as an early stopping criterion. This method requires considerable computation for the Hessian, which is not practical in large models. Mahsereci et al. (2017) proposed a gradient-related stopping method referred to as evidence-based stopping. The evidence-based criterion relies on fast-to-compute local statistics of the computed gradients. The criterion represents whether the gradients of the training samples lie within the expected range. Intrinsic dimensionality, the minimum number of parameters required to represent a dataset, has been used to analyze the training or redundancy of neural networks (Amsaleg et al., 2015). Local intrinsic dimensionality estimates the subspace dimensions of local regions. Recent work considered stopping rules derived from predictive uncertainty. Smith and Jones (2019) proposed a halting test based on posterior checks over mini-batch losses. Tan et al. (2020) studied early exits in multi-exit networks using confidence margins. Garcia et al. (2021) examined curvature signals from Fisher spectra as indicators of training sufficiency. Kim and Park (2022) reported batch-size dependent thresholds that modulate when to stop in practice. Lee and Chung (2021) found that local intrinsic dimensionality works well as a stopping criterion in several few-shot image classification datasets. In addition, local intrinsic dimensionality can be applied to unlabeled samples. Another method involves pre-estimating the number of training epochs by training the model multiple times, such as cross validation (Choi and Lee, 2021). The model can then stop at the pre-estimated stop epoch when training all labeled samples.

These methods have not been commonly studied for NLP tasks and do not consider the performance metrics observed during training. Furthermore, comprehensive comparisons among non-validation stopping methods have not been reported. In this study, we compare our method with evidence-based stopping, local intrinsic dimensionality, pre-estimated stop epochs, and validation-based stopping on five text classification datasets. We also study the sensitivity of each method to dataset size and label availability. Our approach operationalizes a budget-aware utility score that trades off training cost with validation-free gains. Algorithm 1 specifies a preliminary stage for the proposed budget-aware utility stopping procedure. The stage initializes a model, splits labeled data into training and validation at a ratio r, trains the model, and records the checkpoint that achieves the best validation score. This checkpoint is subsequently reused to calibrate thresholds for stopping without further validation queries. For completeness, we reimplemented the baselines with shared optimization settings and logging. The variational early stopping method of Duvenaud et al. (2016) was not compared because it incurs considerable computational cost owing to Hessian-related terms.

document: In this section we first discuss standard approaches to hate countering and studies on CN effectiveness on social media platforms, then the existing CN data collection and generation strategies. Hate countering has emerged as a complement to automatic hate speech detection, which has been advanced by large annotated datasets and robust classifiers. Several corpora target abusive language and hate speech across platforms (Mathew et al., 2021; Cao et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2018; Waseem, 2016; Burnap and Williams, 2016). Surveys synthesize methods and pitfalls while foregrounding ethical concerns and annotation practices (Poletto et al., 2020; Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017; Nunes and Fortuna, 2018; Kiritchenko et al., 2020). Bias remains a central limitation, as models mirror spurious demographic and lexical cues present in training data (Binns et al., 2017; Davidson et al., 2019; Sap et al., 2019; Tsvetkov, 2020). Public debate also questions the downstream use of detection for content moderation and its implications for speech (Munger, 2017; Myers West, 2018). Within this context, counterspeech (CN) is studied as a non-removal intervention that responds to hateful content with alternative narratives and prosocial framing. Benesch (2014) examines counterspeech principles in policy contexts, and Gagliardone et al. (2015) present case studies on responses to digital hate across African media ecosystems. Schieb and Preuss (2016) report moderation patterns on German news platforms, Silverman et al. (2016) outline tactics used by activist networks to engage extremists, and Mathew et al. (2019) measure engagement gains from replies on Twitter alongside Allison and Bussey (2016) bystander outcomes. Recent computational work has shifted from manual CN by NGOs to automated generation that tailors tone and content to the target message and audience. Studies propose retrieval-augmented drafting and controlled decoding to avoid escalation while preserving stance, and several datasets curate CN responses aligned with specific hate typologies. Qian et al. (2019) develop a pipeline for argument-based responses using stance detection. Chung et al. (2021) introduce politeness-conditioned generation for toxicity mitigation, Gupta et al. (2022) fine-tune dialogue models with safety constraints, and Mathew et al. (2018) compile annotated counterspeech on YouTube comments across multiple languages and platform-specific moderation settings and community norms. Beyond generation, researchers outline collection protocols for pairing hate speech with CN through expert writing, crowd annotation, and semi-automatic harvesting from platform replies. We build on these practices by releasing a balanced, multi-platform corpus and a controllable generation baseline with transparent evaluation focused on feasibility and harm minimization.

spans: Classic benchmarks target proportional analogies such as king:queen::man:woman and are widely adopted now for evaluating word vector representations (Turney et al., 2003; Mikolov et al., 2013b; Gladkova et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018a). Pre-trained transformers are examined on analogy sets, and studies of sense definitions in commonsense resources and morphological inflection present complementary findings without shared protocols across tasks and datasets worldwide today (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Ma and Shih, 2018; Cotterell et al., 2016).

Probing approaches evaluate whether models encode analogical structure using diagnostic classifiers, and retrieval-augmented methods report improvements on relation completion by injecting external knowledge bases (Hewitt and Liang, 2019; Tenney et al., 2019; Guu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Cognitive theories of analogy are mentioned with instruction-tuned language models and few-shot prompting, and resources mixing encyclopedic and commonsense facts appear in benchmarks without stating how they assess reasoning in practice (Gentner, 1983; Wei et al., 2022).

document: Many recent studies leverage external knowledge to improve emotion recognition in conversation (ERC). KET (Zhong et al., 2019) integrates ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) and the NRC_VAD lexicon (Mohammad, 2018) to inject commonsense affect into context representations. COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019), trained on ATOMIC, has been used to infer if-then relations, and ToDKAT combines COMET with topic discovery via VHRED (Serban et al., 2017) to enrich dialogue understanding (Zhu et al., 2021). COSMIC (Ghosal et al., 2020) and a psychologically informed framework (Li et al., 2021) further model speaker states and implicit causes across turns to boost ERC. Datcu and Rothkrantz (2014) fuse speech and visual cues to recognize emotions in constrained settings. Alm et al. (2005) investigate text-based emotion classification on newswire and narrative corpora with lexical and syntactic features. Ekman (1992) formalizes six basic emotions from facial expressions, and multimodal corpora such as MELD and IEMOCAP offer aligned text, audio, and vision for benchmarking. While ICON and MELD analyses suggest text features carry the strongest signal (Hazarika et al., 2018a), multimodality still benefits robustness in noisy conversational settings. Early ERC datasets primarily captured dyadic exchanges, with many methods following suit (Hazarika et al., 2018b; Zadeh et al., 2017; Jiao et al., 2020). The advent of multi-party resources like MELD and EmoryNLP has highlighted stronger speaker dependency and contextual entanglement, motivating discourse-aware models and knowledge grounding (Ghosal et al., 2020). Building on these insights, we develop a text-centric ERC system that integrates external commonsense to better infer implicit emotions in multi-speaker dialogues.

document: Injecting Structured Knowledge into LMs. Conceptually, our work is most related to recent work on knowledge injection into PLMs. Know-BERT (Peters et al., 2019) connects entities in text and KGs via an entity linker and then recontextualizes BERT representations conditioned on the KG embeddings. KG-BERT (Yao et al., 2019) trains BERT directly on knowledge triples by linearizing their entities and relations into a sequence and predicting plausibility of the sequence. Wang et al. (2021a) improve KG-BERT by splitting a subject-relation-object knowledge triple into a subject-relation pair representation and an object entity representation, then modeling their similarities with a dual or Siamese architecture. Multilingual LMs pretrained via MLM, such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), cover over one hundred languages and are the starting point of Prix-LM. Smith et al. (2020) examine prompt-based fine-tuning for task adaptation in English encoders. Johnson and Lee (2019) propose data augmentation heuristics to diversify inputs for sentence classification. Zhou et al. (2021) investigate curriculum schedules for multilingual training with progressively harder batches, reporting gains on tagging and paraphrase benchmarks. Kim et al. (2022) assess robustness under constraints. With the notable exception of Calixto et al. (2021) who rely on the prediction of Wikipedia hyperlinks as an auxiliary task to improve XLM-R's multilingual representation space for cross-lingual transfer, there has not been any work on augmenting multilingual PLMs with structured knowledge. Previous work has indicated that off-the-shelf mBERT and XLM-R struggle on knowledge-intensive multilingual tasks such as entity linking and KG completion, especially for low-resource languages (Liu et al., 2021b). Our approach addresses these gaps by connecting knowledge across languages so that information can be transferred and collectively enriched. Before PLMs, rule-based systems and multi-staged information extraction pipelines were typically used for automatic KB construction (Auer et al., 2007; Fabian et al., 2007; Hoffart et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2014). Such methods require substantial human effort for rule or feature creation (Carlson et al., 2010; Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014) or rely on semi-structured corpora with easy-to-consume formats (Lehmann et al., 2015). Petroni et al. (2019) showed that modern PLMs could also be used as KBs by answering cloze-style prompts that elicit stored facts. Jiang et al. (2020) and Kassner et al. (2021) extended the idea to multilingual settings, highlighting both potential and limitations. Work in monolingual settings closest to ours is COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019), and Prix-LM can be seen as an extension to multilingual and cross-lingual setups. The key property of our model is enabling knowledge population by transferring complementary structured knowledge across languages. In another line of work, multilingual KG embeddings (Chen et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2020a; Sun et al., 2021) were developed to support cross-KG alignment and link prediction. Such methods learn a unified embedding space that allows link prediction in a target KG based on aligned prior knowledge in other KGs (Chen et al., 2020). These approaches assume a closed world and cannot leverage open-world knowledge from natural language texts, whereas Prix-LM integrates signals from multilingual KGs and multilingual corpora.

spans: Switch Transformers scale conditional computation with sparse expert routing for modeling (Fedus et al., 2021). Lottery Ticket Hypothesis reports that dense networks contain matching subnetworks that can be trained in isolation (Frankle and Carbin, 2019). Temperature scaling and label smoothing have been used to calibrate confidence in classifiers (Guo et al., 2017; Szegedy et al., 2016).
document: Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved great success in recent years. Despite their power, inference is time-consuming, which hinders deployment in low-latency scenarios. To accelerate PLM inference, two main streams have emerged. One compresses cumbersome PLMs through knowledge distillation, pruning, quantization, and module replacement (Sanh et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Michel et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020a; Gordon et al., 2020). The other selectively activates parts of the model conditioned on the input, including Universal Transformer, FastBERT, DeeBERT, PABEE, LeeBERT, CascadeBERT, and ElasticBERT (Dehghani et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020a; Xin et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020; Zhu, 2021; Li et al., 2021a; Liu et al., 2021a). Early-exit mechanisms reduce average depth by learning confidence-aware halting policies with internal classifiers attached to intermediate layers. While effective, these policies introduce extra parameters and supervision, and can be brittle under distribution shift (Schwartz et al., 2020; Elbayad et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021; Liao et al., 2021). Mixture-of-experts designs pursue conditional computation by sparsely routing tokens to expert submodules, yielding high throughput at the cost of routing overhead and training complexity (Shazeer et al., 2017; Lepikhin et al., 2021). Our work follows the selective activation paradigm but removes the need for learned internal classifiers by using hash-based early exiting. We study whether lightweight, supervision-free halting can preserve accuracy while substantially decreasing latency across diverse backbones. Switch Transformers scale conditional computation with sparse expert routing for modeling (Fedus et al., 2021). Lottery Ticket Hypothesis reports that dense networks contain matching subnetworks that can be trained in isolation (Frankle and Carbin, 2019). Temperature scaling and label smoothing have been used to calibrate confidence in classifiers (Guo et al., 2017; Szegedy et al., 2016). These lines of work underscore the opportunity to trade computation for accuracy under controllable policies. In parallel, retrieval-augmented models demonstrate that adaptive context selection can cut redundant processing without retraining all parameters (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). For sequence classification, adaptive depth also interacts with calibration, as miscalibrated confidence can trigger premature exits (Desai and Durrett, 2020). Prior studies mitigate this with margin-based criteria and ensemble-style patience mechanisms (Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021a). We extend this direction by replacing learned predictors with deterministic hashes that map token states to exit decisions. The hashing view avoids extra classifiers and supervision while preserving per-example adaptivity. We evaluate HASHEE on GLUE, SQuAD, and document classification benchmarks with BERT- and RoBERTa-based backbones. Experiments compare accuracy–latency trade-offs against FastBERT, DeeBERT, PABEE, CascadeBERT, and ElasticBERT under identical hardware and batching settings. We further analyze robustness under covariate shift using corrupted inputs and out-of-domain splits, and the results show that hash-based early exiting maintains competitive accuracy and significantly reduces average compute without modifying pre-trained weights.

document: In this section, we discuss various attempts on N-NER corpora. As shown in Table , existing N-NER corpora are mostly high-resource languages, i.e., English and German, while Vietnamese is the only Asian language that has an N-NER dataset. In terms of the number of classes, it is also worth noting that three out of six corpora have fewer than ten classes and only NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) has more than 100 classes. The details of these corpora are given as follows. ACE-2004 (Doddington et al., 2004) and ACE-2005 (Walker et al., 2006) are early examples of N-NER datasets. The ACE-2005 (Walker et al., 2006) dataset comprises 30,966 mentions from 12,548 sentences with seven coarse-grained entity types. In addition to N-NER annotations, ACE-2005 also contains labels for other tasks such as recognition of relation and event extraction. GENIA introduces an N-NER dataset for bioinformatics. This project provides a high-quality corpus annotated for biological entity names. The dataset is composed of 2,000 abstracts and 92,681 mentions from 9,533 sentences, with 32 entity types. Ju et al. (2018) proposed a layered BiLSTM-CRF that processes nested structures. Wang and Lu (2018) introduced a hypergraph-based approach for overlap handling in entity spans. Sohrab and Miwa (2018) enumerated all span candidates to recover nested mentions. Luan et al. (2019) modeled entities and relations jointly with dynamic span graphs. Fisher and Vlachos (2019) explored nested named entity recognition with mention separators and reranking and global features. NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) is a recent large fine-grained N-NER dataset composed of 114 classes. Unlike previous N-NER corpora, the NNE dataset annotates entities with more details. For example, "6 September 2019", a date named entity mention, in the NNE dataset, each element in this mention is annotated with finer detail, "6" is annotated with day tag, "September" with month tag, and "2019" with year tag. NoSta-D (Benikova et al., 2014) is a German corpus for nested entities. DAN+ (Plank et al., 2020) presents the first N-NER dataset for Danish. This work investigates the possibility of transfer learning between languages for the N-NER task. DAN+ is a multi-domain dataset; the authors also study the challenges of domain shift in their dataset. The dataset contains 6,425 mentions, 130,095 tokens, and four classes from 6,867 sentences, obtained from multiple domains such as news and social media (Reddit, Twitter, and Arto). NoSta-D, VLSP-2018, and DAN+ have a modest corpus size and a small number of entity types compared to the NNE dataset. This shows that there is still a resource gap for non-English corpora. For Thai, there are only coarse-grained flat NER datasets which are publicly available.

document: In this section, we introduce background knowledge about contrastive learning and our phrase encoder LUKE (Yamada et al., 2020). Contrastive learning frames representation learning as pulling semantically related samples together while pushing unrelated samples apart. A common objective is the InfoNCE loss, which estimates a lower bound on mutual information between paired views (van den Oord et al., 2018). In computer vision, SimCLR demonstrates strong performance by maximizing agreement under diverse augmentations (Chen et al., 2020). In NLP, unsupervised sentence embeddings benefit from contrastive objectives built from in-batch negatives and hard positive mining (Gao et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021). Supervised contrastive learning further incorporates label structure to shape class-aware neighborhoods in representation space (Khosla et al., 2020). Hard negative mining and temperature scaling are known to affect both stability and downstream retrieval performance (Robinson et al., 2021). Beyond sentence-level representations, phrase-level contrast seeks to align subspans across paraphrases and contexts to improve compositionality (Wu et al., 2020). Our work builds on this perspective by encoding phrases with entity-aware transformers that link text spans to knowledge bases. LUKE extends BERT with a dedicated entity embedding space and entity–word attention, enabling fine-grained modeling of mentions grounded in Wikipedia (Yamada et al., 2020). Prior studies on entity-centric pretraining show that masking and linking named entities improves factual recall and reasoning in downstream tasks (Sun et al., 2019; Peters et al., 2019). ERNIE incorporates knowledge masking and phrase-level objectives to strengthen the integration of lexical and entity semantics (Sun et al., 2019). Karpukhin et al. (2020) present dense passage retrieval for open QA with in batch negatives. Lee et al. (2019) explore latent alignment for question answering with distant supervision. Johnson and Zhang (2017) investigate character-level CNNs for text classification with little preprocessing. Mikolov et al. (2013) introduce word2vec for efficient distributional representations used across many tasks. Pennington et al. (2014) release GloVe word vectors for semantic similarity tasks. We adapt these entity-aware ideas to contrastive setups by constructing positive pairs from co-referent mentions and anchored hyperlinks in large corpora. To operationalize phrase-level contrast, we generate views through span dropout, mention boundary jitter, and entity substitution using canonical aliases. For negative sampling, we combine lexical confusables with frequency-controlled entity distractors to avoid trivial separations. The encoder is trained with a temperature-tuned NT-Xent loss and a late interaction scorer to support retrieval over phrase tables. Evaluation covers semantic search, slot filling, and open-domain QA, accompanied by ablations isolating the effects of entity linking and contrastive objectives.

spans: Maruf et al. (2019) introduced memory-based context encoders that capture document relations across sentence boundaries for improved discourse coherence in neural translation. Kim et al. (2019) explored cache-augmented models to reuse lexical choices across a document and stabilize terminology. Tu et al. (2018) examined context gating to regulate how much document information should influence each decoding step. Voita et al. (2020) proposed training objectives that bias models toward document consistency without changing decoder capacity. document: Integrating document-level information meaningfully into NMT is a challenging task and has inspired research on context-aware architectures, including simple concatenation-based models (Tiedemann and Scherrer, 2017; Junczys-Dowmunt, 2019; Lopes et al., 2020), multi-source models (Jean et al., 2017; Bawden et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018), hierarchical models (Miculicich et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2020), multi-pass models (Voita et al., 2019; Mansimov et al., 2020), and dynamic context models (Kang et al., 2020). It has also motivated the field to revisit evaluation protocols (Freitag et al., 2021). Despite measured gains, most methods assume abundant document resources and overlook data scarcity. In this study, we adopt a simple concatenation model as our experimental protocol and leave broader architectural exploration to future work. Given the rapidly changing landscape of contextual MT evaluation, we report multiple metrics, including human judgments, while acknowledging disagreements about the right way to evaluate MT systems (Kocmi et al., 2021). Multilingual modeling often clusters sentences of similar meaning from different languages within a shared semantic space (Kudugunta et al., 2019). Such shared representation is hypothesized to enable zero-shot transfer, improving many cross-lingual tasks (Eriguchi et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020; Chi et al., 2021; Ruder et al., 2021), especially with large-scale pretrained multilingual Transformers (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau and Lample, 2019; Xue et al., 2021). In translation, multilingual SenNMT achieves zero-shot translation, transferring sentence-level generation capabilities to unseen pairs (Firat et al., 2016; Johnson et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019a), even in massively multilingual settings (Aharoni et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019b). Our study extends multilingual SenNMT to multilingual DocNMT to enable document-level transfer from languages with document data to languages with only sentence-level data. To our knowledge, we are the first to demonstrate document-level zero-shot transfer across languages for multilingual machine translation. Document-level translation quality often depends on modeling discourse phenomena such as coreference, lexical cohesion, deixis, and consistency of terminology. Maruf et al. (2019) introduced memory-based context encoders that capture document relations across sentence boundaries for improved discourse coherence in neural translation. Kim et al. (2019) explored cache-augmented models to reuse lexical choices across a document and stabilize terminology. Tu et al. (2018) examined context gating to regulate how much document information should influence each decoding step. Voita et al. (2018) presented contrastive test suites targeting pronoun translation and lexical cohesion. Li et al. (2020) proposed training objectives that bias models toward document consistency without changing decoder capacity. Liu and Zhang (2021) investigated discourse-aware pretraining on synthetic document pairs datasets. Building on these insights, we study whether multilingual supervision at the document level can be distilled into languages that lack document-aligned corpora. We construct mixed-granularity training batches that interleave document and sentence examples while preserving language balance. To mitigate negative transfer, we add a lightweight domain tagger and a context-length curriculum that progressively increases the number of preceding sentences. For evaluation, we combine reference-based metrics with targeted discourse tests and blinded human assessments at the document level. Our results suggest that context-aware multilingual pretraining yields gains on pronouns, lexical cohesion, and consistency, even for zero-shot document translation.

document:
Knowledge Probing Benchmarks for PLMs.

Pre-trained language models have been evaluated for factual recall using cloze-style probes and curated relation sets. LAMA introduced this paradigm by assembling single-token triples from Wikidata and ConceptNet to test masked prediction. Its design emphasized coverage across relation types while keeping prompts minimal and fixed. Subsequent analyses showed that surface cues can leak head-entity information and inflate scores. To mitigate this, LAMA-UHN removed overlapping strings between head and tail mentions to form a harder subset. Multilingual extensions, including X-FACTR and mLAMA, broadened the setting to many languages and permitted multi-token answers. These resources also prompted research on decoding strategies that piece together longer targets. Our initial experiments indicate that such strategies underperform on MedLAMA due to domain-specific terminology. BioLAMA emerged concurrently to evaluate biomedical knowledge using a comparable probing recipe. We report summary statistics across LAMA, BioLAMA, and MedLAMA in the appendix to contextualize scale and answer lengths. Prompt formulation plays a central role in probing outcomes across models and datasets. Jiang et al. (2020b) mined paraphrased prompts and ensembled predictions for more stable probe accuracy. Shin et al. (2020b) optimized continuous prompt embeddings to steer masked language models. Qin and Eisner (2021) proposed gradient-based soft patterns, and Fichtel et al. (2021) studied data requirements for prompt tuning across tasks. Liu et al. (2021) compared templates. We purposely avoid approaches that require task-labeled data because they train an auxiliary model atop the PLM. Prior work further notes that prompt tuning may demand substantial supervision and risks overfitting to dataset artifacts. In our setting, we instead adapt models using a small pool of raw sentences to elicit knowledge without exposing benchmark biases. Biomedical knowledge probing also examines whether PLMs can complete missing links when trained as knowledge base completion models. Nadkarni et al. instantiated this view by fine-tuning biomedical PLMs on link prediction and evaluating in-domain relations. BioLAMA foregrounded OptiPrompt as a continuous prompt learner that depends on ground-truth answers from the task. We expand upon these threads with a novel probing technique and human validation of predicted facts. Our experiments assess sensitivity to prompt phrasing, decoding choice, and adaptation data across multiple PLMs. We analyze error modes including entity aliasing, boundary truncation, and spurious lexical memorization. Case studies illustrate when domain adaptation helps recall specialized biomedical relations that generic PLMs miss. Cross-lingual comparisons highlight gaps in multilingual probing when medical terminology diverges from general corpora. Finally, we discuss implications for benchmark design and the limits of cloze-style probes as faithful knowledge tests. We release code and scripts to reproduce all datasets, prompts, and evaluation settings. Ethical considerations address potential misuse of extracted biomedical facts and dataset licensing constraints.

document: Abstractive opinion summarization aims to generate fluent summaries that reflect salient opinions in user reviews. Owing to limited reference summaries, unsupervised approaches have dominated (Chu and Liu, 2019; Bražinskas et al., 2020b; Amplayo et al., 2021; Elsahar et al., 2021; Im et al., 2021; Wang and Wan, 2021; Isonuma et al., 2021). More recently, few-shot fine-tuning of pretrained Transformers with small review–summary pairs has achieved strong gains over unsupervised baselines (Bražinskas et al., 2020a; Oved and Levy, 2021). Yet most methods target general summarization rather than the comparative setting, where the goal is to surface contrasts and commonalities across item groups. Lerman and McDonald (2009) defined contrastive summarization and gave an early extractive method. Wang et al. (2013) selected discriminative sentences from multiple related document sets in practice. Bista et al. (2019) chose representative documents, Ren and de Rijke (2015) described comparative topic models, He et al. (2016) and Ibeke et al. (2017) examined thematic structure across corpora. We develop the first abstractive model and benchmark for generating both contrastive and common summaries, showing that generative training better captures cross-group salience than extractive pipelines.

document: Language models typically use a softmax layer to predict the probability of the next token given its left context. Formally, the model parameterizes P_S(x | c_t) as a linear projection of the hidden state h_{c_t} onto the output embedding vector w_x followed by normalization. This yields a logit for every vocabulary item x whose exponentiated value is normalized across the vocabulary V. Prior work has noted that this parameterization induces a low-rank constraint on the family of distributions a model can represent. Yang et al. (2018) argued that the log probability matrix over contexts and words is effectively bounded by the dimensionality of the hidden state, often called the softmax bottleneck. Intuitively, if the hidden state has dimension D, then the number of linearly independent log distributions cannot exceed D. This restriction can limit expressivity when modeling heterogeneous contexts and long-tail events. Press and Wolf (2017) proposed tying input and output embeddings in neural language models. Grave et al. (2017) examined subword regularization and large-vocabulary softmax approximations. Ma et al. (2018) introduced a spherical softmax variant for improved gradient behavior. Gu et al. (2018) explored vocabulary selection for efficient decoding in sequence models. Yang et al. (2019) studied adaptive input layers with learned clustering for rare words robustly. In training, the ideal output vector for a word should lie near hidden states of contexts that co-occur with that word and away from irrelevant contexts. This contrastive view links the softmax objective to Word2Vec style embedding learning while accounting for contextualization in modern encoders (Mikolov et al., 2013; Kong et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Empirically, linear analogies such as king minus man plus woman approximate queen in embedding spaces trained on co-occurrence statistics (Ethayarajh et al., 2019). Comparable relations emerge in the output embeddings of autoregressive transformers when optimization aligns logits with these geometric offsets (Wang et al., 2019). Brown et al. (2020) introduced GPT-3 with few-shot prompting for a wide array of tasks. Kaplan et al. (2020) presented empirical scaling laws for loss across data, parameters, and compute, and Rae et al. (2021) described retrieval-augmented models with external memory and long-context training strategies. Tay et al. (2022) surveyed efficiency. Our study situates these geometric insights within practical modeling choices, evaluating when altered output parameterizations improve generalization without incurring prohibitive computational cost.

Ji and Eisenstein (2014) study document-level discourse using latent variables for global consistency. Wang et al. (2018) introduce multi-task learning with sentiment signals. document: A key finding in previous studies in discourse analysis is that most sentences have an independent well-formed sub-tree in the full document-level discourse tree (Joty et al., 2012). Researchers have taken advantage of this finding to build parsers that utilize different granularity levels of the document to achieve state-of-the-art results (Kobayashi et al., 2020). This design has been empirically verified to be a generally advantageous framework, improving not only works using traditional feature engineering (Joty et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2017), but also deep learning models (Jia et al., 2018b; Kobayashi et al., 2020). We, therefore, introduce this design to our dependency parsing framework. Specifically, sub-trees for each sentence in a discourse are first built separately, then assembled to form a complete discourse tree. Our model differs from prior works in that we derive contextualized representations of EDUs by fine-tuning BERT separately for intra- and inter-sentential levels. This separation allows the encoder to dynamically capture informational cues needed for discourse analysis at different granularities. We also design stacked sequence labeling models for discourse relation identification so that a hierarchical decoder can explicitly capture both intra-sentential and inter-sentential writing patterns. Prior research on implicit relations emphasizes the role of context in disambiguation when explicit connectives are absent. Feng and Hirst (2014) rely on a linear-chain CRF with hand-crafted features to model sequential characteristics in constituent-based discourse parsing. Their approach greedily builds structure bottom up, assigning locally optimal labels to nodes as the tree grows. As a result, only limited information from the top of a partially constructed tree is available when predicting relations. Moreover, nodes at inference time may represent heterogeneous discourse units, such as EDUs, sentences, or paragraphs, which complicates local decisions. In contrast, we train sequence labeling models at both intra- and inter-sentential levels after constructing a complete discourse tree, enabling global reasoning over the whole context. Li et al. (2014) propose a neural architecture for RST parsing with joint structure and relation prediction. Ji and Eisenstein (2014) study document-level discourse using latent variables for global consistency. Wang et al. (2018) introduce multi-task learning with sentiment signals. Liu and Lapata (2018) explore discourse-aware summarization using graph-based encoders. Biran and McKeown (2015) analyze. These lines of research collectively underscore the need for models that reconcile global structural constraints with rich local representations. Our framework addresses this need by decoupling relation labeling across two stacked taggers aligned with EDU- and sentence-level scopes. We pretrain the encoders on masked language modeling and continue fine-tuning on discourse objectives derived from silver annotations. During training, we alternate batches produced by intra- and inter-sentential samplers so that optimization trajectories remain specialized yet mutually informative. At inference time, we first parse sentence-level sub-trees, then assemble cross-sentence arcs, and finally decode relation labels with beam-aware constraints. Experiments on RST-DT and GUM demonstrate consistent gains over strong baselines in both structure F1 and relation accuracy, with ablations confirming the benefits of hierarchical fine-tuning and stacked labeling.

document: Text Knowledge enhanced methods. Recently, huge efforts on integrating knowledge into PTLMs have been made. One typical form of knowledge is a knowledge graph. There have been efforts of using knowledge graph to inject entity and relation representations, which are pre-computed from external source, into PTLMs (Zhang et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2019;He et al., 2020). Some other works try to retrieve or generate the sub-graph from the graph to solve the problem (Lin et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020). Another existing form of knowledge is extra large-scale corpus. Works that use such corpus present knowledge-related pre-training objectives such as concept order recovering (Zhou et al., 2021), entity category prediction (Yu et al., 2020) and source of knowledge prediction (Wang et al., 2021). They are mostly focused on injecting world knowledge presented in text, rather than physical and visual commonsense knowledge that can be found in images. Recent multimodal studies aim to bridge this gap by aligning textual encoders with visual or embodied signals. Approaches leverage visual question answering datasets and image-grounded captions to provide procedural and physical affordance cues to PTLMs. Methods such as visual commonsense pretraining, masked region modeling, and retrieval-augmented captioning have reported gains on grounded reasoning benchmarks. Antol et al. (2015) introduced VQA for assessing joint vision–language understanding. Gao et al. (2021) proposed prefix tuning for generation, and Sun et al. (2020) examined knowledge graphs for dialogue systems in settings. Our work builds on this line by coupling structured and unstructured sources while explicitly supervising cross-modal grounding of actions and object states. We evaluate models on tasks requiring physical plausibility, counterfactual scene manipulation, and stepwise tool use with minimal prompt engineering.

document: Cross-lingual/Multilingual Machine Reading Comprehension research has shown promising transfer with multilingual PLMs; Hsu et al. (2019) evaluate mBERT on MRC and report that zero-shot transfer is feasible even between distant languages like English and Chinese. Building on this, a range of multilingual MRC methods leverage PLMs, and Cui et al. (2019) combine mBERT with back-translation to improve cross-lingual answer extraction. Different from these studies, we examine how syntactic divergences between source and target languages hinder zero-shot transfer in multilingual PLMs, aiming to decouple semantics from syntax and transfer only semantic content. Xue et al. (2021) present the XTREME benchmark spanning cross-lingual tasks and report results across typologically diverse languages. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) introduce multilingual sentence embeddings trained on parallel corpora, and Conneau et al. (2020) release XLM-R based on masked language modeling. Li et al. (2020) study translation language modeling with pretraining for question answering, and Karthikeyan et al. (2020) analyze overlap effects on cross-lingual generalization. This motivation connects to disentangled representation learning in NLP, where models separate sentence-level semantics and syntax (Hu et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019), and is closely related to the vMF-Gaussian VAE of Chen et al. (2019) that uses dual latent variables and paraphrase-based objectives. We adapt that framework to multilingual data with bilingual pairs, adding cross-lingual reconstruction and semantic discrimination terms and augmenting with POS- and tree-informed losses to reinforce syntactic factors during training.

document: Multiple efforts have looked into defining, measuring, and mitigating biases in NLP models (Sun et al., 2019; Mehrabi et al., 2019a; Sheng et al., 2021). Foundational work on formal notions of fairness provides terminology and desiderata for downstream metrics (Dwork et al., 2012). In static representations, gender and racial stereotypes have been revealed in word embeddings and quantified with intrinsic scores (Bolukbasi et al., 2016; Caliskan et al., 2017; Manzini et al., 2019). Contextualized encoders exhibit related phenomena that require adapted probes and tests (Kurita et al., 2019; Nadeem et al., 2021). Several resources propose datasets and metrics to capture social biases and stereotypes in model outputs (Nangia et al., 2020; Webster et al., 2018; Rudinger et al., 2018). Kiritchenko and Mohammad (2018) examine offensiveness ratings across demographic groups. Zhang et al. (2018) present adversarial learning to reduce sensitive information in representations. Zhao et al. (2018) analyze occupation terms and gender associations in coreference models. Garg et al. (2018) track social bias trajectories using large historical corpora longitudinally. Many bias metrics are computed automatically using proxy classifiers, such as toxicity, sentiment, and regard models trained on separate corpora (Dixon et al., 2018; Sheng et al., 2019). These automatic scores are often combined with group-based rates like true positive rate differences to approximate fairness gaps (De-Arteaga et al., 2019b; Hardt et al., 2016). Yet the correlation between automatic metrics and human judgments can be inconsistent and dataset dependent (Blodgett et al., 2020; Sap et al., 2019). Ethayarajh (2020) proposes Bernstein bounds to quantify uncertainty in bias estimates and to guide sample-efficient evaluation. Recent critiques argue that benchmark framing and annotation protocols deeply influence measured disparities and their interpretability (Blodgett et al., 2021). Existing approaches also vary in whether they target model internals, data curation, or decoding-time controls, which complicates comparisons across systems (Sun et al., 2019). Mitigation techniques include counterfactual data augmentation and label flipping to balance demographic cues in training sets (Zhao et al., 2018; Maudslay et al., 2019). Another family employs adversarial objectives that discourage encoding protected attributes while preserving task information (Zhang et al., 2018; Elazar and Goldberg, 2018). Post-hoc calibration and constrained decoding offer complementary controls that can reduce disparately harmful outputs at generation time (Schick et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). Despite progress, most metrics are tied to particular tasks, domains, or label availability, which limits portability to open-ended generation and retrieval settings. Group-based parity measures further require demographic labels that may be missing, noisy, or ethically restricted in production environments. Speicher et al. (2018) propose a unified view of group and individual fairness via inequality indices, showing connections to well-studied economic measures. Extending such indices to unstructured text is challenging because subtle lexical cues may signal identity without explicit mentions. For instance, references to sports, occupations, or relational roles can alter perceived gender or ethnicity in ways that are context sensitive and domain specific. In this work we introduce accumulated prediction sensitivity, a metric that aggregates local perturbation responses into a corpus-level disparity score. The method estimates directional influence functions by minimally editing inputs along identity-bearing features and integrating response magnitudes. Because it relies on prediction gradients rather than ground-truth labels, it can be computed online for streaming or interactive systems. We evaluate its alignment with human ratings across multiple datasets and reporting protocols to assess convergent validity. We also analyze relationships to statistical fairness notions, including risk parity and equalized odds, leaving preference-based frameworks for future work (Zafar et al., 2017; Hardt et al., 2016). Together, these contributions offer a practical and extensible approach to measuring social bias across tasks while clarifying connections to the broader fairness literature.

document:
Several approaches to zero-shot cross-lingual transfer exist and can broadly be divided into: a) Data-based Transfer, which focuses on training data transformation, and b) Model-based Transfer that centres around modifying models' training routine.

For Data-based Transfer, translating utterances for the intent classification task is relatively straightforward, so previous works focused on projecting and aligning entity labels between translated utterances (Dou and Neubig, 2021). This technique is followed by standard supervised training with those pseudo-labels and is commonly known as a translate-train method. One of the earliest works still used for this purpose is fastalign (Dyer et al., 2013). It is an unsupervised word aligner trained on a parallel corpus to map each word, and thus its entity label, in the source utterance to the word(s) in the target utterance. Projecting the entity labels can also be done with word-by-word translation and source label copying (Yi and Cheng, 2021). A teacher model then weakly labels the target data, which is used to train the final student model. Sometimes, this type of label projection is complemented with an additional entity alignment step (Li et al., 2021a). Better performance can be achieved by using machine translation with entity matching and distributional statistics (Jain et al., 2019), though this can be a costly process for each language. A category of word substitution methods such as code-switching (Qin et al., 2020; Kuwanto et al., 2021) or dictionary-enhanced pretraining has also been shown to improve cross-lingual transfer.

For Model-based Transfer, prior to the adoption of multilingual transformers (Lample and Conneau, 2019), task-oriented XNLU methods employed a BiLSTM encoder combined with multilingual embeddings (Schuster et al., 2019). Newer approaches usually involve a pretrained XLM and the addition of some new training component with the inference routine remaining mostly unchanged. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) built language agnostic sentence embeddings using an encoder on parallel corpora, aligning representations for languages with one model and evaluating cross lingual retrieval. Pires et al. (2019) observed emergent cross lingual capabilities in multilingual BERT across languages without explicit alignment, reporting transfer on NER and POS tagging across scripts. Conneau et al. (2020) introduced XLMR, a model pretrained on CommonCrawl that delivers zero shot performance on benchmarks without task specific fine tuning. Xu et al. (2020) learn to jointly align and predict entity labels by fusing source and target language embeddings with attention and using the resulting cross-lingual representation for entity prediction. Qi and Du (2020) include an adversarial language detector in training whose loss encourages the model to generate language-agnostic sentence representations for improved zero-shot transfer. Pan et al. (2020) and Chi et al. (2020) add a contrastive loss to pretraining that treats translated sentences as positives and unrelated sentences as negatives, helping the XLM produce similar embeddings across languages. These methods require large annotated datasets and expensive model pretraining (Chi et al., 2020), whereas our approach uses only English task data and its translations for each language.

The most related prior works are Arivazhagan et al. (2019) for machine translation and a task-oriented XNLU variant called XeroAlign, both of which use translated training data to align representations and enable zero-shot transfer. XeroAlign generates a sentence embedding for each language using the CLS token of the XLM and minimizes their distance with a Mean Squared Error loss that is backpropagated alongside the main task loss, implicitly biasing the representation toward intent classification. We reproduce XeroAlign for analysis and comparisons and add a lightweight post-processing step that improves the reported scores.

document: This section reports a survey on closely related popular QA datasets that 1) focus on narratives and/or 2) are designed for educational purposes (dataset features in Table 1). We outline dataset goals, sources, question formats, reasoning skills, and typical evaluation protocols to situate our contributions. NarrativeQA (Kociský et al., 2018) targets story understanding via questions about books and movie scripts with answers grounded in long summaries. MCTest (Richardson et al., 2013) provides short children’s stories with multiple-choice questions designed to test basic narrative comprehension. DREAM (Sun et al., 2019) focuses on multi-turn dialogues from language exams to assess reasoning over conversational narratives. Rajpurkar et al. (2016) introduced SQuAD with crowdsourced passages and span extraction questions targeting local evidence. Dasigi et al. (2019) built Quoref to focus on coreference resolution in reading comprehension. Huang et al. (2019) presented COSMOS QA dataset. RACE (Lai et al., 2017) collects English examination passages and multi-choice questions authored by teachers for middle and high school students. ARC (Clark et al., 2018) compiles science exam questions and distinguishes easy from challenge sets to probe knowledge and reasoning. OpenBookQA (Mihaylov et al., 2018) augments a small core of science facts with additional retrieval to answer questions requiring novel combinations. SciQ (Welbl et al., 2017) was constructed by generating and filtering science questions from textbook passages to approximate classroom assessments. Textbook Question Answering (Kembhavi et al., 2017) integrates curricula, diagrams, and explanatory chains to measure multimodal science understanding. QASC (Khot et al., 2020) emphasizes compositional reasoning by linking two facts to support a single correct answer. QuALITY (Pang et al., 2021) introduces long articles with carefully validated multiple-choice questions to mitigate annotation artifacts and guessing. Across these corpora, annotation quality, passage length, and distractor design vary substantially, affecting the balance between retrieval and reasoning. Khashabi et al. (2018) introduced MultiRC with multi-sentence evidence and multiple correct answers. Clark et al. (2019) released BoolQ for yes/no questions drawn from real queries. Yang et al. (2018) proposed HotpotQA requiring multi-hop reasoning over supporting paragraphs. Educational QA datasets also differ in prerequisite knowledge, with grade-level alignment enabling curriculum-aware evaluation. To compare across settings, we group question types into extraction, multiple-choice, and free-form generation, and we note distinct supervision signals. For narrative-focused sets, long-context modeling and coreference tracking often dominate error profiles in published baselines. For exam-oriented sets, external knowledge, weak supervision for explanation, and distractor robustness emerge as recurring challenges. We adopt reporting practices that normalize metrics by question family and include calibration-aware analyses to reduce confounds from annotation artifacts. This survey highlights gaps at the intersection of long-context narratives and formal educational assessment that motivate our dataset design. Concretely, we prioritize passages drawn from age-appropriate stories and textbooks, questions authored by educators, and evaluations that dissect retrieval, reasoning, and explanation faithfulness.

spans: DistilBERT compresses BERT, retaining most of its performance and efficiency through knowledge distillation (Sanh et al., 2019). XLM introduces cross-lingual pretraining to align multilingual representations for transfer across languages and domains (Conneau and Lample, 2019). T5 frames all NLP tasks in a unified, flexible text-to-text format with a large-scale pretraining corpus and objective (Raffel et al., 2020).
document:
2.1 Pre-trained language model (PLM)

Most modern language models are based on the transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture. The PLMs generally use only the encoder block of the transformer, which consists of two sublayers, a self-attention layer, and a feed-forward layer. BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) is the most widely used PLM, which consists of several layers of transformer encoders. The BERT was pre-trained for 4 days with a large amount of text data, which consists of 3.3 billion words, using masked language modeling and the next sentence prediction task in an unsupervised manner. This pre-trained parameter can be easily used in various downstream tasks by fine-tuning the model using the labeled dataset. Since the success of BERT, a variety of similar PLMs have emerged. RoBERTa refines masked language modeling by improving training dynamics and data scale (Liu et al., 2019), while XLNet integrates permutation language modeling to capture bidirectional contexts without masking (Yang et al., 2019). DistilBERT compresses BERT, retaining most of its performance and efficiency through knowledge distillation (Sanh et al., 2019). XLM introduces cross-lingual pretraining to align multilingual representations for transfer across languages and domains (Conneau and Lample, 2019). T5 frames all NLP tasks in a unified, flexible text-to-text format with a large-scale pretraining corpus and objective (Raffel et al., 2020). ELECTRA further reduces pretraining cost by replacing the generator–discriminator interface with a token-level discrimination task, yielding strong gains under limited compute (Clark et al., 2020). DeBERTa disentangles content and relative position embeddings to sharpen attention and improves downstream accuracy across GLUE and SQuAD (He et al., 2021). Domain-adaptive pretraining and intermediate-task training have also been shown to be effective for specialty corpora such as biomedicine and law (Gururangan et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2020). In practice, these models are typically fine-tuned with task-specific heads, regularization, and parameter-efficient adapters to balance accuracy and cost (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022). Our work builds on these insights by selecting a strong encoder-only PLM and leveraging parameter-efficient fine-tuning for resource-constrained settings.

document: Explainable classification differs from post-hoc analysis approaches for interpretability (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Sundararajan et al., 2017), as prototype classification networks (Li et al., 2018; Hase et al., 2019) are white-box models grounded in case-based reasoning (Kolodner, 1992) rather than extractive rationales (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019; Jain et al., 2020; Glockner et al., 2020). These models are the neural variant of classical prototype classifiers (Bien and Tibshirani, 2011; Kim et al., 2014), predicting by matching inputs to representative training instances. Contemporary work highlights the value of global explanations via examples (Rajagopal et al., 2021), yet their similar training instances are not directly integrated into the decision, whereas we leverage learned prototypes that participate in the prediction. Our approach builds on the architecture of Li et al. (2018), which we describe in Section 3.1. Subsequent research showed that prototypes can capture salient parts of inputs, including partial-image regions in vision (Hase et al., 2019) and retrieved spans in text generation (Guu et al., 2018). Hase and Bansal (2020) further evaluated prototype-style and feature-activation explanations, noting that feature activations approximate post-hoc saliency and do not address missing evidence. Recent studies explore evaluation beyond accuracy. Doshi-Velez and Kim (2017) outlined formalism for assessing interpretability, Jacovi and Goldberg (2020) examined faithfulness tests for attention mechanisms, and Nguyen et al. (2018) investigated interactive explanation settings with crowd workers. Liao et al. (2020) presented design guidelines for XAI users, and Bansal et al. (2021) reported failure modes of human–AI collaboration in decision support tasks. User trust remains uncertain across settings. We evaluate our white-box model for explainable propaganda detection in a human+AI setting that measures simulatability, task accuracy, and calibration.

document: There has been the argument revolving deep learning models that they are black-box and hard to offer explainable and interpretable predictions, which has led to a considerable amount of research work regarding their interpretability (Linardatos et al., 2021). The pre-trained BERT language model has been extensively analyzed since its launch, in particular, feature-based probes have been proposed to show how a particular layer, head, or neuron of BERT works on a downstream NLP task. Usually with a small set of additional parameters, a probe is trained in a supervised manner using feature representations from the pre-trained BERT, e.g. contextualized embeddings, to solve a particular task (Wu et al., 2020). Attention and structural probes have been invented to investigate different aspects of BERT and linguistics (Lin et al., 2019;Jawahar et al., 2019;Clark et al., 2019;Hewitt and Manning, 2019;Manning et al., 2020;Tenney et al., 2019a,b;Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). Latent ontology of contextual embeddings has also been investigated via cluster analysis (Michael et al., 2020). For works that emphasize probing contextualized representations for lexico-semantic relations, previous studies have investigated BERT for lexical relation classification via a neural network probe on type-level embeddings (Vulić et al., 2020). Beyond generic interpretability, a large body of work studies lexical entailment and hypernymy detection as core signals of taxonomic knowledge in distributional semantics. Pattern-based methods following Hearst patterns and supervised distributional approaches have been contrasted with modern contextualized models that encode hierarchical relations implicitly (Levy et al., 2015; Shwartz et al., 2016; Roller et al., 2018). Hearst (1992) introduced lexico-syntactic patterns for identifying hypernymy in text using fixed surface templates extracted from broad-coverage corpora. Mikolov et al. (2013) examined distributional embeddings that represent words in spaces and reported analogical structure emerging from vector arithmetic on datasets. Pennington et al. (2014) proposed GloVe with co-occurrence objectives, and Socher et al. (2013) built recursive network for entailment on parse trees with annotations. Recent work probes whether transformers encode monotonicity and transitivity in entailment, drawing connections between structural attention and logical inference (Goodwin et al., 2020; Talmor et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2021). In hypernymy, contextualized encoders combined with sense linking have been used to disambiguate polysemous nouns and to align mentions to synsets before relation classification (Bouraoui et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Resources such as WordNet and sense-annotated corpora support evaluating whether models recover hierarchical consistency across sense-specific IS-A edges. In this work, we differ from previous works by a goal to explicitly investigate how much BERT understands the IS-A relations and more importantly, transitivity. That is, we aim to find if and how often BERT makes logically consistent predictions for transitive relations. Moreover, we are devoted to investigating sense-aware IS-A relations, where IS-A relations are extracted from explicit word senses with associated contexts, so contextualized embeddings can be clearly mapped to senses. We contribute a probing framework with controlled sense contexts, a transitivity consistency metric, and experiments on established benchmarks with detailed ablations.

document: Pretrained language models for open-domain dialogue have been primarily built by pretraining on large-scale social comment chains such as Reddit to model conversational behavior (Zhang et al., 2020; Adiwardana et al., 2020). These models are then finetuned on diverse target dialogue datasets to improve engagingness and humanness, with automatic filtering by predefined criteria used to exclude toxic or biased content. This strategy yields scalable corpora yet can leave residual safety issues that manifest during open-ended generation (Gehman et al., 2020). Subsequent work explored post-training objectives such as supervised fine-tuning on curated conversations to shape style, persona, and factuality (Roller et al., 2021; Thoppilan et al., 2022). Retrieval-augmented dialogue systems ground responses in external documents to improve faithfulness and reduce hallucinations (Lewis et al., 2020; Shuster et al., 2021). Safety-specific datasets provide adversarial prompts and human preference annotations to guide safer responses in these models (Dinan et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021). Reinforcement learning from human feedback has been adapted to conversational agents to better align outputs with user preferences and safety norms (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022). We position our work within this line by examining pretraining data selection and alignment signals jointly for open-domain chat. Smith et al. (2020) introduced a calibration metric for conversational risk that estimates unsafe response likelihood under topical drift. Lee and Park (2021) presented prompt-based steering for neural chat models using lightweight control codes trained from crowd feedback. Brown et al. (2020) showed that large pretrained language models can follow few-shot instructions to produce extended dialogue. Gao et al. (2021) examined dense retrieval for multi-turn grounding with dual encoders in conversation. Despite these advances, many systems still exhibit exposure bias and topic fragmentation across long contexts in live deployments. Data-centric approaches therefore focus on constructing mixture-of-domains pretraining corpora with explicit safety, recency, and quality slices to balance coverage and reliability (Gururangan et al., 2020; Wenzek et al., 2020). On the objective side, contrastive learning and unlikelihood training penalize toxic continuations while preserving fluency and diversity (Welleck et al., 2020; He and Glass, 2020). Another complementary direction is tool use, where the dialogue model delegates factual queries to retrieval or calculators and generates only the social glue around results (Shuster et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2023). Instruction-tuning on multi-turn datasets has been shown to substantially improve helpfulness and compliance in chat settings (Wei et al., 2022; Chiang et al., 2023). Evaluating such systems remains challenging due to the open-ended nature of user goals and the long-tail of unsafe or sensitive topics. Automatic metrics based on reference-free judges and toxicity classifiers offer scalability but can misestimate pragmatic appropriateness in nuanced contexts (Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020; Gehman et al., 2020). Human evaluations with paired comparisons and rubric-based scoring provide higher fidelity but are costly and difficult to standardize across labs. Our study addresses this gap by integrating data governance, alignment, and grounding into a single pretrain–posttrain recipe for open-domain dialogue. We release code and evaluation templates to encourage robust, safety-aware benchmarking in future work.
