document:
Quality Estimation Current SOTA models in sentence-level QE, which is typically framed as a regression task, mainly use multilingual representations from pre-trained transformers (Devlin et al., 2018), notably XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2019). The input to a sentence-level QE model is a concatenation of the source and translated sentences, separated by the [SEP] token. The sequence is encoded by the pre-trained Transformer model, and the [CLS] token is passed through a multilayer perceptron (MLP) layer to obtain a sentence-level score. During fine-tuning both the parameters of the pre-trained model and the parameters corresponding to the MLP layer are updated.

Word-level QE is typically addressed as a binary classification task, where the QE model needs to predict a binary label indicating whether a word is correct or wrong for each word in the MT output (Lee, 2020). As illustrated in Figure (left), some supervised approaches use both sentence-level and word-level objectives in a multi-task setting, which results in superior performance (Kim et al., 2017;Lee, 2020). Methods that do not require word-level training data either need access to the MT model (Rikters and Fishel, 2017;Fomicheva et al., 2020b), or still treat the problem as a supervised task but use synthetically generated data for supervision (Tuan et al., 2021).

Rationale Extraction for NLP SOTA NLP models based on deep neural networks achieve high performance in a variety of tasks, often at the cost of interpretability (Lipton, 2016). Ribeiro et al. (2016) present perturbation-based explanations for classifiers, Yu et al. (2019) study compact rationales for constrained selection, and Chen et al. (2019) describe prototype-driven reasoning with representative cases. Sundararajan et al. (2017) propose integrated gradients for sensitivity analysis, Jain and Wallace (2019) examine whether attention weights are explanations, Wiegreffe and Pinter (2019) discuss faithfulness criteria for attention, and Chefer et al. (2021) adapt relevance propagation to transformers in pretrained language models and large encoders alike.

Typically, explainability methods operate by selecting a portion of the input that justifies model prediction for a single data point. This can be done either by modifying the model architecture, or by trying to explain the predictions of a given model. The first type of approaches (a.k.a. rationalization by construction) involves imposing restrictions on the generated rationales to satisfy certain constraints, e.g. compactness (Yu et al., 2019;Chalkidis et al., 2021). Note that such restrictions often result in lower performance and indeed are not guaranteed to explain the behaviour of an unconstrained model (Jain et al., 2020). The second type of approaches (the so called post hoc) usually rely on feature attribution methods, which assign an importance value to each input feature of a network (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Schulz et al., 2020). These methods do not allow for introducing useful biases during training, but focus on faithfully explaining model behaviour.

Feature attribution has a long tradition in image recognition tasks (Simonyan et al., 2013) and only recently have been applied to some NLP tasks, most commonly text classification (DeYoung et al., 2019). QE is fundamentally different from text classification where clues are typically separate words or phrases (Zaidan et al., 2007) which often can be considered independently of the rest of the text. This independence assumption does not hold for the task of evaluating translation quality where a word cannot be identified as a clue (e.g. translation error) without considering the surrounding context. Furthermore, SOTA NLP models based on contextualized representations for input words make rationale extraction especially challenging, as the representation for a given word can encode not only the word identity but also its interactions with other words in the text. Recent work has revealed various interesting properties that characterize the information flow through hidden layers in deep transformer models (Voita et al., 2019;De Cao et al., 2020;Yun et al., 2021). We provide additional insights on this topic in Section 5.2.

document: Synthetic translations have primarily been studied as a data augmentation strategy for neural machine translation. Early approaches relied on forward translation of monolingual sources to expand the training corpus (Zhang and Zong, 2016). The most influential line of work generated target-side monolingual text via back-translation to create synthetic sources paired with real targets (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Marie et al., 2020). Beyond simple augmentation, researchers use synthetic data to reshape the distribution of training examples. Nguyen et al. (2020) diversify both sides of the corpus with multiple models and merge them with the original bitext to train a stronger system. Jiao et al. (2020) identify low-contributing sentence pairs and refresh them using new synthetic translations to rejuvenate the dataset. Sequence-level knowledge distillation reframes training as learning from teacher outputs rather than human references (Kim and Rush, 2016). Distillation is particularly important for non-autoregressive architectures that struggle to learn from heterogeneous references (Gu et al., 2017). Subsequent analyses argue that distilled or back-translated samples are simpler and therefore easier to fit, accelerating optimization (Zhou et al., 2019a; Xu et al., 2021). Other pipelines mix synthetic and authentic bitext according to quality estimates or domain similarity to reduce distributional mismatch. Edunov et al. (2018) analyze noise and sampling strategies for back-translation across domains. Hoang et al. (2018) study iterative back-translation with tags that mark synthetic segments in the training corpus. He et al. (2016) present dual learning with monolingual corpora and reinforcement signals to train translation systems. Caswell et al. (2019) propose tagged back-translation for domain control. Xie et al. (2020) examine noisy student training for translation. Tagging synthetic segments has also been used to let the model condition on data provenance and avoid overfitting to artifacts. Another active thread studies when to regenerate or filter synthetic pairs based on uncertainty and quality estimation signals. Marie et al. (2020) compare sampling, beam search, and noisy decoding for creating better sources from target monolingual corpora. In low-resource and domain adaptation settings, synthetic data can bootstrap coverage while gradually shifting toward task-relevant distributions. Recent work integrates back-translation with multilingual pretraining to better exploit cross-lingual transfer in mid-resource regimes. To mitigate risks of distribution drift, researchers propose curriculum schedules that increase the share of authentic bitext as training proceeds. Our study consolidates these findings by evaluating mixtures of synthetic and authentic data across data scales, domains, and architectures.

document: Conversational question answering is a core task in dialog research. In recent years, several datasets have emerged, such as QuAC (Choi et al., 2018), CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019), and DoQA (Campos et al., 2020). Different from single-turn QA datasets like SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), CQA requires the model to interpret questions within multi-turn conversational history. These benchmarks challenged models to manage under-specified queries, context carryover, and unanswerability. Progress on CQA has been steady, with transformer encoders and task-specific training objectives driving gains. Ohsugi et al. (2019) separated dialog act modeling from span prediction to better exploit BERT representations. Qu et al. (2019) proposed history selection to filter irrelevant context before evidence aggregation. Chen et al. (2020) used graph neural networks over utterance nodes to capture long-range dependencies. Kim et al. (2021) investigated pretraining signals targeted at conversational grounding and topic transitions. Alongside text-only CQA, some benchmarks require combining textual evidence with background knowledge and structured sources. Saeidi et al. (2018) formulated rule-aware QA requiring integration of knowledge base triples. Saha et al. (2018) and Guo et al. (2018) developed CQA over heterogeneous KBs with schema alignment challenges. Other work has explored CQA in different modalities, such as visual dialog for images and navigation (Das et al., 2017). Despite advances, evaluation practices have lagged behind realistic usage settings. Most leaderboard evaluations feed gold conversational histories to the model during testing. This practice masks exposure bias, since model predictions at one turn influence inputs at subsequent turns in deployment. Mandya et al. (2020) and Siblini et al. (2021) argued that predicted-history evaluation better reflects real-world performance. They reported notable degradations when gold answers were replaced with model predictions in the history. These findings motivated techniques for error-aware training, history correction, and rewrite-based disambiguation. We extend this direction with large-scale human evaluation and model-based question rewriting. Shuster et al. (2021) explored retrieval augmentation for dialogue with multi-turn grounding. Rastogi et al. (2020) introduced a multi-domain task-oriented benchmark emphasizing context carryover and schema diversity. Reddy et al. (2019) analyzed extractive and abstractive answers in CoQA under answer verification protocols with human studies on error cascades online. Together, these considerations highlight the importance of measuring robustness under realistic conversational feedback. We formalize predicted-history evaluation by defining rollout policies and calibration-aware scoring. Our study quantifies how question rewrites can reduce ambiguity propagated from imperfect histories. We design a rewriting module that conditions on evidence, dialog state, and uncertainty signals from the reader. Human raters judge rewritten questions for clarity, answerability, and faithfulness to prior turns. The results reveal consistent gains on QuAC, CoQA, and DoQA when training with predicted histories and targeted rewrites. We release evaluation protocols, annotation templates, and model checkpoints to support reproducible CQA research.

(2020) studied calibration of generative models, Camburu et al. (2018) released e-SNLI with explanations, and Williams et al. (2018) assembled MultiNLI to probe genre diversity. Bowman et al. document: With the great success of natural language inference, many recent works explore the interpretability of neural networks by providing evidence to support their predictions (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2019; Kumar and Talukdar, 2020). These efforts typically fall into feature-based, token-level, and sentence-level explanations, covering attention, saliency maps, gradients, and human-readable rationales (Xu et al., 2015; Samek et al., 2017; Li et al., 2016; Linzen et al., 2016; Camburu et al., 2018; Talmor et al., 2018). While token-level highlights are convenient, they can be ambiguous, whereas sentence-level rationales often better align with human judgments in NLI and fact verification (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2019; DeYoung et al., 2020). Beyond cataloging explanation types, recent research studies the bidirectional influence between explanation quality and task performance through learning-to-explain, selective rationalization, and self-explaining architectures (Lei et al., 2016; Jain et al., 2020; Pruthi et al., 2020). Clark et al. (2020) introduced evidence aggregation for open-domain QA, Rajani et al. (2019) elicited commonsense rationales via annotation, Narang et al. (2020) studied calibration of generative models, Camburu et al. (2018) released e-SNLI with explanations, and Williams et al. (2018) assembled MultiNLI to probe genre diversity. Bowman et al. (2015) presented SNLI for entailment, Petroni et al. (2019) examined factual recall in pretraining, Zhang et al. (2020) proposed ATOMIC for reasoning, Lewis et al. (2020) explored retrieval-augmented generation, Geva et al. (2021) analyzed facts in transformers, and Zhong et al. (2020) and Min et al. (2020) investigated question decomposition. In this study, we propose a mutual promotion mechanism that jointly improves inference accuracy and sentence-level explanations by coupling a rationale selector with an entailment predictor. The approach differs from pipeline explanation systems by enabling gradients from the task loss to inform rationale extraction and using rationale sufficiency to regularize the predictor. We situate our method alongside weakly supervised rationale models and decision-focused learning, highlighting complementary training signals (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019; Ross et al., 2021). Empirically, we evaluate on SNLI, MultiNLI, FEVER, and e-SNLI with automatic and human-based metrics for faithfulness and plausibility. Results show consistent gains over strong baselines and reveal that tighter coupling reduces shortcut reliance while preserving evidence sufficiency.

spans: Prior work on secure aggregation formalizes server-side protection of client updates in federated settings with cryptographic guarantees (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Bell et al., 2020). Large-scale audits of public data crawling document provenance, licensing, and consent status for web text used in pretraining corpora (Kreutzer et al., 2022; Dodge et al., 2021; Luccioni and Viviano, 2021). Reinforcement learning from human feedback introduces alignment objectives that reshape model behavior at inference time and can reduce the likelihood of emitting unsafe content (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022). PII detection heuristics complement rule-based scrubbing in multilingual pipelines worldwide (Ahmed, 2021).
document:
2.1 Security and Privacy Concern of Pre-trained Models

Pre-trained models like BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) rely on massive text corpora to achieve competitive generalization across tasks. Such reliance raises security and privacy risks because training data may contain personally identifiable information, health details, or behavioral traces extracted from web-scale sources (Christoph et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2007). Empirical studies show that large language models can memorize rare strings and regurgitate them under adversarial prompting, highlighting concrete leakage pathways (Carlini et al., 2021; Kandpal et al., 2022). Threat models typically include membership inference, property inference, model inversion, and data reconstruction, which vary in adversarial capability and access assumptions (Shokri et al., 2017; Fredrikson et al., 2015; Melis et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Mitigations emphasize training-time defenses such as differential privacy, architectural constraints, and federated aggregation, along with post-hoc auditing to quantify residual risk (Dwork et al., 2006; McMahan et al., 2017; Kairouz et al., 2021). TextHide demonstrates learning with mixed representations for text classification, while DP fine-tuning adds calibrated noise during gradient updates, yet both approaches show utility-privacy trade-offs and task coverage limitations (Huang et al., 2020; Kerrigan et al., 2020). Sequence labeling, question answering, and generation amplify privacy-utility tensions because token-level supervision and long contexts increase exposure surfaces relative to sentence-level classification. Measurement advances include exposure metrics, canary insertion, and red-teaming protocols that systematically probe memorization under controlled prompts (Carlini et al., 2019; Mehrotra et al., 2022; Perez et al., 2022). Recent benchmarks combine synthetic identifiers with realistic documents to standardize evaluation across attacker knowledge regimes and model scales. Prior work on secure aggregation formalizes server-side protection of client updates in federated settings with cryptographic guarantees (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Bell et al., 2020). Large-scale audits of public data crawling document provenance, licensing, and consent status for web text used in pretraining corpora (Kreutzer et al., 2022; Dodge et al., 2021; Luccioni and Viviano, 2021). Reinforcement learning from human feedback introduces alignment objectives that reshape model behavior at inference time and can reduce the likelihood of emitting unsafe content (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022). PII detection heuristics complement rule-based scrubbing in multilingual pipelines worldwide (Ahmed, 2021). Despite advances, privacy guarantees can degrade under distribution shift when prompts trigger rare memorized sequences. Practitioners must calibrate epsilon budgets, clip norms, sampling rates, and training schedules to preserve accuracy while bounding leakage in DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016; Bu et al., 2020). In federated deployments, client drift, heterogeneous participation, and stragglers complicate privacy accounting and widen attack surfaces through side channels (Kairouz et al., 2021; Truex et al., 2019). Secure model release further demands policy controls, provenance tracking, and redaction of fine-tuning sets used to adapt general models to domains with heightened sensitivity. Watermarking and provenance signatures support forensic attribution when memorized artifacts surface in generated outputs during incident response (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023; Kuditipudi et al., 2023). Robust evaluation combines black-box and white-box adversaries, contrasting success rates under top-k sampling, temperature variation, and constrained decoding strategies. Human review remains necessary for borderline cases, since automated precision on PII detectors varies across languages and code-switched content. Emerging methods integrate privacy constraints into pretraining objectives to shape token-level likelihoods away from sensitive strings without catastrophic interference. Hybrid pipelines couple synthetic canaries with real-world de-identification cases to validate detection thresholds and calibrate triage policies. Our work synthesizes these strands by benchmarking privacy defenses on token-level and sequence-level tasks under matched utility budgets. We provide ablations that isolate the impact of clipping, noise multipliers, and regularization on exposure metrics and task accuracy. The results inform deployment guidance for risk-aware fine-tuning, secure evaluation, and governance aligned with organizational tolerance for leakage.

spans: Hu et al. (2020) released the XTREME benchmark covering diverse tasks and writing systems. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) introduced LASER, a multilingual sentence encoder trained on parallel data and evaluated on cross-lingual classification. Chi et al. (2021) proposed InfoXLM with mutual information maximization objectives for cross-lingual pretraining and reported gains on XNLI and MLQA. Wu and Dredze (2019) examined vocabulary sharing in multilingual settings.

Liu et al. (2020) studied multilingual adversarial training for robust transfer. Ruder et al. (2021) surveyed challenges in cross-lingual transfer and compiled best practices across tasks and benchmarks. Lauscher et al. (2020) discussed sociolinguistic biases in multilingual corpora.
document: Contextual representations such as ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) catalyzed a shift from static word embeddings to deep, task-adaptive features. Beyond monolingual settings, multilingual BERT, XLM, and XLM-RoBERTa exhibit strong cross-lingual generalization relative to aligned static embeddings (Pires et al., 2019; Wu and Dredze, 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Their success has motivated a research agenda around universal representations that can support zero-shot transfer across typologically diverse languages. At the same time, pooling training signal across dozens or hundreds of languages raises capacity allocation and interference concerns for a single encoder. Conneau et al. (2020) documented the so-called curse of multilinguality, where adding more languages helps up to a point before degrading average accuracy on downstream tasks. Hu et al. (2020) released the XTREME benchmark covering diverse tasks and writing systems. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) introduced LASER, a multilingual sentence encoder trained on parallel data and evaluated on cross-lingual classification. Chi et al. (2021) proposed InfoXLM with mutual information maximization objectives for cross-lingual pretraining and reported gains on XNLI and MLQA. Wu and Dredze (2019) examined vocabulary sharing in multilingual settings. While these results are encouraging, the distribution of training data and the partition of languages seen during pretraining may shape transfer in underexplored ways. We focus on decomposing transfer according to exposure, measuring how models behave on held-out languages that never appear in pretraining corpora. Prior studies often conflate seen and unseen typologies by evaluating only on widely resourced languages or by mixing development sets across families. This gap obscures the limits of universal representations and complicates capacity planning for deployment in low-resource scenarios. Johnson et al. (2017) demonstrated zero-shot translation in multilingual NMT, highlighting emergent interlingua behavior under shared subword vocabularies. Aharoni et al. (2019) scaled multilingual NMT to 100 languages and described trade-offs in capacity sharing and balancing. Wang et al. (2020) argued that data temperature and sampling strategies critically influence cross-lingual generalization in both translation and understanding. Liu et al. (2020) studied multilingual adversarial training for robust transfer. Ruder et al. (2021) surveyed challenges in cross-lingual transfer and compiled best practices across tasks and benchmarks. Lauscher et al. (2020) discussed sociolinguistic biases in multilingual corpora. Together, these strands suggest that evaluation protocols must disentangle transfer to unseen languages from gains attributable to shared subword statistics. We address this by constructing pretraining schedules that systematically exclude families while holding model size, optimizer, and data volume constant. Our experiments span classification and sequence labeling tasks drawn from XTREME and focus on balanced coverage of scripts and morphologies. We report results in terms of seen-to-seen, seen-to-unseen, and unseen-to-unseen generalization, providing a fine-grained view of capacity allocation. The analysis quantifies when additional languages dilute representations and when targeted diversity yields additive benefits across families. This framing complements Conneau et al. (2020) by testing whether the curse of multilinguality manifests for genuinely unseen languages and by isolating the role of exposure.

Our goal is to enable end-to-end training of goaldirected dialogue agents. In these settings, an agent aims to complete a particular task with its utterances (Smith and Hipp, 1994). Goal-directed agents have been explored in contexts such as personal assistants (McTear, 2002;Budzianowski et al., 2018;Williams et al., 2014), recommendation systems (Liu et al., 2010;Kang et al., 2019), education (Yuan et al., 2008), and negotiation (He et al., 2018;Lewis et al., 2017). While there are multiple approaches to constructing dialogue agents, in this work we frame the problem of generating dialogue as a sequential decision making problem within a (partially observed) Markov Decision Process (MDP) (Singh et al., 1999;Young et al., 2013). Prior works that utilize such an MDP formulation typically aim to train a dialogue management system (Singh et al., 2002), in which the agent reasons about higher-level abstractions of the state of the conversation, and language generation is performed using a downstream procedure. Dialogue management systems have been trained using techniques such as online reinforcement learning via policy gradients (Gašić et al., 2011;He et al., 2018), off-policy reinforcement learning (Pietquin et al., 2011;Yu et al., 2016) or actor-critic methods (Su et al., 2017). Our method differs from dialogue management systems in that CALM is an end-to-end system optimized for successful task completion, and performs both high-level decision making and language generation.

Recent advancements in language models, such as recurrent neural networks (Sundermeyer et al., 2012;Asri et al., 2016;Su et al., 2016;Zhao et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhang et al., 2020) and attention-based architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2018;Brown et al., 2020), have spurred increasing interest in such endto-end dialogue systems (Hosseini-Asl et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Adiwardana et al., 2020). Modelbased approaches, in which a learned agent is substituted for a human, allow learning to be done entirely within simulation without human intervention (Li et al., 2016;He et al., 2018;Kang et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018). Serban et al. (2017) survey neural conversational models across tasks. Bordes and Weston (2016) introduced memory-augmented dialogue for QA, and Roller et al. (2021) explored retrieval-augmented chit-chat with large-scale pretraining and human evaluations. In contrast to these approaches, CALM augments the traditional language modeling objective with taskspecific rewards in order to finetune a model that is more aware of task goals, which significantly improves performance over a naïve language model without the need for simulating human responses in an interactive training loop. Jaques et al. (2019) recently proposed a model-free, offline approach to undirected dialogue, or dialogue without a specific task goal. Our method differs in that we aim to solve goal-oriented dialogue which allows us to optimize task-specific objectives, and that we take a model-based RL approach which enables us to leverage fine-tuned language models. End-to-end optimization for dialog has also been studied through sequence-level training objectives that combine supervision with reinforcement learning signals (Ranzato et al., 2016;Paulus et al., 2018). For task-oriented settings, supervised semantic parsers translate utterances into formal actions or API calls, which can be composed with learned policies (Wen et al., 2016;Zhong et al., 2018). Model-based RL introduces a learned environment model to plan or improve data efficiency, which can reduce the cost of collecting interactive dialogues (Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018;Janner et al., 2019). Within dialogue, agenda-based and user simulators have long been used to approximate human interlocutors, though modeling errors can lead to reward hacking and overfitting (Schatzmann et al., 2007;Asri et al., 2016). CALM adopts a text-conditioned dynamics model to roll out candidate continuations, permitting credit assignment through differentiable or gradient-estimated objectives while staying faithful to the pretrained distribution. To balance fluency and goal progress, we combine a language modeling loss with sparse extrinsic rewards that reflect slot completion and task success, aligning with best practices in reward shaping (Ng et al., 1999). We evaluate on multi-domain goal-oriented dialogue benchmarks and simulate users with both rule-based and learned agents to assess robustness and sample efficiency. Compared with strong pretrained baselines trained purely with next-token likelihood, our approach yields higher task completion rates and more concise exchanges at comparable perplexity.

Automatic evaluation of open-domain dialogue systems has a multifaceted nature with many finegrained quality aspects (Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Turn-level aspects show the quality of the system's utterance given a dialogue context from different perspectives including appropriateness, relevance, engaging, and etc (Lowe et al., 2017;Tao et al., 2018;Ghazarian et al., 2020). Whereas, conversation-level facets such as coherence, diversity, informative take into account the whole conversation and dialog flow (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2021;Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Dialogue coherence evaluation is pertinent to the discourse coherence as a dialogue is counted as a multi-party discourse. Similar to discourse coherence, many original coherence evaluation metrics derived from Centering Model for monitoring the local focus of utterances and the entities distribution over utterances (Grosz and Sidner, 1986;Miltsakaki and Kukich, 2004;Lapata and Barzilay, 2005). Work on entity-based coherence has expanded through varied designs. Barzilay and Lapata (2008) modeled entity transitions in news summarization, and Elsner and Charniak (2011) explored entity grids for conversational transcripts. Li and Jurafsky (2014) introduced discourse relation features for ranking responses, while See and Manning (2019) examined repetition and specificity in neural dialogue outputs. Gupta and Smith (2020) analyzed topic drift using turn-level lexical chains in dialogs. A group of studies assess the coherence of dialogues with respect to entities and dialogue acts (Cervone and Riccardi, 2020;Mesgar et al., 2020). Another inspected approach for dialogue coherence evaluation is to represent dialogue in a structured graph format where contextually dependent neighbor utterances or concepts are connected nodes in the graph (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020). Graph convolutional networks are used to complete this task.

High quality training dataset is identified as one of the momentous and indelible components in automatic coherence evaluation. Some previous works construct such datasets by collecting human judgments (Higashinaka et al., 2014;Cervone and Riccardi, 2020). While many recent works rely on a more timely and costly affordable approach by automatically generating negative samples. The utterances of the coherent conversations from humanhuman interactions are manipulated by shuffling their order, inserting or replacing irrelevant utterances (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). Research on adversarial data curation addresses related questions in evaluation. Kiela and Williams (2021) introduced Dynabench with interactive human adversaries to elicit hard examples across tasks. Nie and Bansal (2020) curated Adversarial NLI by collecting premise–hypothesis pairs that fool strong baselines, and Potts and Pavlick (2021) extended evaluation suites with targeted perturbations. Dataset shifts were emphasized. In this work, we show that such changes can not truly represent machine generated incoherent conversations. We propose a diagnostic benchmark that contrasts real model failures with synthetic perturbations to close this gap.

document: We review prior work on visual semantic AI, on the geometry and semantic properties of representations formed by language models, and on semantic intrinsic evaluation tasks. Visual semantic AI has increasingly unified perception and language through multimodal pretraining and cross-modal alignment. Early approaches learned joint embeddings of images and captions using contrastive objectives over paired data. CLIP demonstrated that large-scale internet supervision yields transferable visual-language features suitable for zeroshot recognition. Subsequent methods refined alignment with region-level grounding, fine-grained matching, and instruction tuning for vision-language models. These advances motivate our treatment of semantic structure as a cross-modal property shaped by data and objectives. Parallel to multimodal progress, a rich literature studies the internal geometry of language model representations. Work on linear probes and causal interventions argues that syntactic and semantic variables occupy approximately linear subspaces. Other research characterizes anisotropy, clustering by topic, and layer-wise disentanglement across training scales. Mikolov and colleagues (2013) introduced vector offsets that captured analogies in spaces trained on large corpora. Hewitt and Manning (2019) recovered syntactic tree distances from inner products, estimating a structural probe aligning layers with parse geometry. Ethayarajh (2019) quantified anisotropy and highlighted sentence-level variability, and Conneau and colleagues (2018) compared multilingual embeddings across transfer tasks. Together, these studies suggest that geometry mediates how tasks leverage information across layers. Beyond analysis, representation geometry informs the design of semantic intrinsic evaluation. Tasks such as word similarity, semantic textual similarity, and definitional probing aim to measure meaning alignment without task-specific finetuning. Recent benchmarks curate balanced, multilingual, and domain-specific test suites to reduce confounds and emphasize genuine semantic competence. In multimodal settings, retrieval, caption paraphrase, and cross-modal entailment datasets provide intrinsic signals complementary to downstream tasks. Methodologically, contrastive learning, isotropy regularization, and subspace projection have been proposed to improve semantic faithfulness of learned embeddings. Work on calibration and confidence estimation further addresses whether similarity scores correlate with human judgments. Our study integrates these threads by defining geometry-aware criteria for evaluating semantic robustness across modalities and languages. We focus on interpretable metrics that expose layer-wise trade-offs and cross-modal mismatches. This perspective clarifies how representation structure interacts with intrinsic evaluation and guides principled improvements to visual semantic AI.

document: In this section, we introduce adaptive kNN-MT as a retrieval-augmented approach to neural machine translation within the broader landscape of non-parametric memory for sequence models. The original kNN-MT augments a pretrained NMT system by querying a target-side datastore to adjust token probabilities at decoding time (Khandelwal et al., 2021). Adaptive kNN-MT extends kNN-MT with a Meta-k Network that fuses neighbor information across multiple k values to reduce sensitivity to any single neighborhood size (Zheng et al., 2021a). Related retrieval-augmented methods in language modeling and question answering highlight the benefits of external memories and efficient nearest neighbor search at scale (Lewis et al., 2020; Borgeaud et al., 2022). Grave et al. (2017) proposed a memory-augmented language model with a continuous cache. Kaiser et al. (2017) introduced the Neural Cache for rare word modeling. Karpukhin et al. (2020) developed dense passage retrieval for open-domain QA. He et al. (2021) studied efficient nearest neighbor search for large-scale datastores. Fan et al. (2021) built retrieval-enhanced generation for dialogue with mixture-of-documents training. Li et al. (2022) explored calibration. We focus on the background and formulation of adaptive kNN-MT to clarify how learned k selection is integrated with retrieval-based probability interpolation over a target-side datastore. The datastore comprises key–value pairs extracted by a general-domain NMT model that writes a contextual representation for each target position as the key and the realized token as the value. During decoding, the current context query retrieves the K nearest keys, yielding neighbor values and distances that characterize alternative neighborhoods indexed by candidate k values. For each candidate k, the method forms a kNN distribution over retrieved values via distance-based kernels and then interpolates that distribution with the base model. The Meta-k Network is a small feed-forward module that takes summary features such as distinct value counts and normalized distances to produce mixture weights over the candidate k set. Kim et al. (2022) proposed adaptive softmax routing for retrieval mixtures. Wang and Tu (2020) investigated domain adaptation for NMT with instance weighting and bilingual dictionaries. Chen et al. (2023) analyzed token-level smoothing. The final prediction marginalizes over k by weighting each kNN distribution according to the Meta-k outputs and combining it with the base probabilities to yield the ensemble. A held-out validation set is used to fit Meta-k parameters and select the candidate set before reporting test results, which provides the background for the experiments that follow.

document: Early work on text simplification relied on handcrafted rules to split or shorten long sentences (Chandrasekar and Srinivas, 1997; Carroll et al., 1998; Vickrey and Koller, 2008), then treated simplification as a monolingual phrase-based MT task enriched with syntactic features (Zhu et al., 2010; Coster and Kauchak, 2011; Wubben et al., 2012), before the field shifted toward neural approaches. In supervised generative settings, Seq2Seq models learn simplification operations from aligned pairs (Sutskever et al., 2014; Nisioi et al., 2017), with advances spanning policy learning for tailored rewards (Zhang and Lapata, 2017) and transformer architectures that integrate lexical resources or additional training objectives (Vaswani et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018; Kriz et al., 2019; Martin et al., 2020a; Maddela et al., 2021). Nisioi et al. (2017) trained attention-based Seq2Seq models on aligned corpora to induce lexical and syntactic simplifications, reporting gains on English Wikipedia benchmarks, and Martin et al. (2020b) fine-tuned BART on paraphrased pairs to encourage fluent rewrites, and Surya et al. (2019) framed simplification as unsupervised style transfer without parallel sentences. Scarton and Specia (2018) targeted grade levels with predictors for readability, Nishihara et al. (2019) conditioned generation on labels to steer difficulty, Zhao et al. (2018) added external simplification rules to transformers, Kriz et al. (2019) promoted output diversity, Maddela et al. (2021) combined syntactic rules with neural decoding. Complementing generative lines, revision-based approaches learn or search over edit operations to improve interpretability, spanning supervised pipelines with explicit keep/replace/delete actions (Alva-Manchego et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2019; Omelianchuk et al., 2021; Agrawal et al., 2021) and unsupervised search formulations (Narayan and Gardent, 2016; Kumar et al., 2020), which our method extends by integrating a paraphrasing generator within the edit-and-search loop.

document: The pretraining-finetuning paradigm (Howard and Ruder, 2018) shows tremendous success in fewshot NLU tasks. Various methods have been developed such as [CLS] classification (Devlin et al., 2018), prompting-based methods with discrete prompts (Schick and Schütze, 2021b;Gao et al., 2020) or continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021b;Shin et al., 2020;Li and Liang, 2021;Lester et al., 2021), and methods that calibrate the output distribution (Yang et al., 2021;Zhao et al., 2021). The fact that few-shot learning is sensitive to many factors and thus is extremely unstable (Liu et al., 2021a;Lu et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2020;Dodge et al., 2020) increases the difficulty of fewshot evaluation. Several works address evaluation protocols to mitigate the effects of instability: Gao et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2021b) adopt a held-out set to select models. Perez et al. (2021) proposed K-fold cross-validation and minimum description length evaluation strategies. Our work differs from these works on few-shot evaluation in several aspects: (1) we propose three metrics to evaluate data split strategies; (2) while most prior work proposed evaluation protocols without justification, we conduct comprehensive experiments to support our key design choice; (3) we formulate a general evaluation framework; (4) our re-evaluation under the proposed framework leads to several key findings. Bragg et al. (2021) and Ye et al. (2021) proposed few-shot NLP benchmarks FLEX and Cross-Fit respectively. Both FLEX and CrossFit study principles of designing tasks, datasets, and metrics, while we focus on addressing inherent problems of the evaluation procedure. Prompt selection and verbalizer design have been shown to induce large variance across runs. Calibrated likelihood estimation attempts to reduce label bias in masked-prompt classification. Reproducibility requires standardized splits, fixed seeds, and transparent early stopping criteria. Radford et al. (2019) present language models that acquire knowledge from large corpora. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrate in-context learning with minimal gradient updates. Liu et al. (2021) survey prompt-based learning for natural language understanding and generation. Wei et al. (2022) study chain-of-thought prompting for reasoning tasks and math. Despite broad interest, the community lacks a unified view of which choices matter most for reliability. We define the evaluation unit at the task level and separate design choices into dataset, training, and selection phases. On datasets, we examine the impact of class balance, domain overlap, and leakage between development and test. On training, we investigate optimization budgets, hyperparameter search spaces, and the role of unlabeled data via augmentation. On selection, we study early-stopping criteria, validation aggregation across templates, and thresholding for rejection options. We complement protocol analysis with robustness tests under distribution shift and label permutation. Our framework yields interpretable metrics that diagnose when improvements stem from data curation rather than modeling advances. The experiments cover sentence classification, natural language inference, and paraphrase detection across eleven English datasets. We report mean and dispersion over seeds, prompts, and splits using hierarchical bootstrapping. We release code and scripts to enable exact replication of all tables and figures. The results suggest that data partitioning explains a substantial fraction of headline gains reported by prior work.

document: Building on the vanilla encoder–decoder architecture (Shang et al., 2015; Vinyals and Le, 2015), numerous extensions target structural modeling of dialogue contexts (Serban et al., 2016, 2017; Zhang et al., 2019a), diversity (Li et al., 2015; Xing et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017; Tao et al., 2018), attribute control (Xu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; See et al., 2019), and persona biasing (Li et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Grounding dialogue generation in external knowledge has emerged as a key strategy to narrow the gap between current systems and human conversation, drawing on knowledge graphs (Zhou et al., 2018b; Moon et al., 2019; Tuan et al., 2019), unstructured documents (Dinan et al., 2019; Lian et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020a; Kim et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020), and visual context (Mostafazadeh et al., 2017; Shuster et al., 2018; Huber et al., 2018). We focus on document-grounded dialogue generation, where the system must produce responses that faithfully convey content from retrieved passages. Instead of only selecting relevant snippets and relying on pre-trained language models to verbalize them, we investigate how to explicitly model knowledge expression within the generation process. A central challenge is aligning discourse planning with document structure so that responses integrate salient evidence while remaining concise and coherent. Prior work on content planning highlights the benefits of intermediate representations that capture segment-level decisions before surface realization (Moryossef et al., 2019; Puduppully et al., 2019). This perspective connects to sequence modeling via segmentation, which has gained traction across several language generation tasks. In text segmentation, Wang et al. (2017) propose a probabilistic segmental model and introduce the Sleep-Wake Network (SWAN) to decide when to generate and when to continue within a segment. For machine translation, Chan et al. (2016) model target-side phrase structures with SWAN-style boundaries to alleviate exposure bias at the phrasal level. In data-to-text generation, Wiseman et al. (2018) develop a neural template-like model with an HSMM decoder that supports tractable learning via dynamic programming. To relax the weak Markov assumption over segments, Shen et al. (2020) explicitly segment targets into aligned fragments and jointly learn segmentation and alignment with a DP-based objective. Zhou et al. (2020) examine knowledge-grounded response generation with topic-aware fusion networks. Komeili et al. (2022) present a retrieval-augmented conversational agent trained on Wizard of the Internet. Shuster et al. (2021) study faithful generation with controllable knowledge sources in open-domain dialogue. Dinan et al. (2018) introduce Wizard of Wikipedia to evaluate knowledge selection benchmarks. Despite significant progress in retrieval and grounding, models often conflate selection with expression, producing fluent yet unsupported statements when verbalization is not constrained by document structure. Recent approaches mitigate hallucination by conditioning on evidence spans and calibrating likelihoods via contrastive learning or attribution objectives (Filippova, 2020; Rashkin et al., 2021; Shuster et al., 2021). Our approach complements these methods by viewing evidence realization as a segmentation problem with explicit boundaries tied to discourse acts. Concretely, we introduce segment-level planners that predict which document fragments to realize and how to order them before decoding. The planners operate over compact content units derived from sentence-level salience and entity coherence signals. A segment-conditioned decoder then generates surface text with constraints that preserve lexical anchors from the selected fragments. This design encourages content fidelity while allowing flexible phrasing to fit conversational context. We evaluate on multi-turn document-grounded datasets with both automatic and human metrics focused on factual consistency, specificity, and fluency. Analyses reveal improved evidence coverage and fewer unsupported claims compared to strong retrieval-augmented baselines. Together, these results underscore the value of segment-aware knowledge expression for document-grounded dialogue generation.

document: Toxicity detection systems are typically trained on datasets annotated at the post level (Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017; Pavlopoulos et al., 2017c; Zampieri et al., 2019; Wulczyn et al., 2017; Borkan et al., 2019). While effective for moderation, such models overlook the intra-post locality of harm. We study toxic span detection, which identifies the minimally sufficient substrings responsible for toxicity. Unlike assigning a single label to an entire comment, our approach targets explicit rationales amenable to auditing and intervention. This framing relates to attribution and explanation methods (Li et al., 2016; Ribeiro et al., 2016) that make model decisions interpretable to humans. In particular, gradient- and perturbation-based techniques offer guidance but are not trained with span-level supervision. Dataset practices for toxicity have progressed from community moderation logs to expert-coded schemes with richer schemas. Resources such as Wikipedia Detox and Civil Comments include large-scale labels and identity attributes used to study bias (Wulczyn et al., 2017; Borkan et al., 2019). Yet few corpora provide character- or token-level spans, limiting rigorous evaluation of fine-grained detectors. Davidson et al. (2017) curated a hate speech dataset and reported challenges with annotator agreement. Wiegand et al. (2018) examined abusive language resources and highlighted taxonomy mismatches. Fortuna and Nunes (2018) surveyed classification approaches and feature sets for hate speech detection. Dixon et al. (2018) studied unintended bias in toxicity classifiers using identity terms. Sap et al. (2019) introduced context-sensitive annotation for offensiveness in social media. Despite this breadth, the literature rarely consolidates span-level definitions across platforms. We formalize toxic spans as contiguous segments that suffice to trigger toxicity judgments under minimal context. Our experimental design links span supervision to model calibration and user-facing transparency. We compare sequence tagging and span proposal architectures, evaluating precision–recall trade-offs under partial-credit metrics. To encourage reproducibility, we provide a new benchmark with adjudicated spans, detailed guidelines, and crowd-worker quality controls. We also release strong baselines combining pretrained encoders with conditional random fields and pointer networks. Ethical considerations include annotator well-being, privacy preservation, and safeguards against misuse in targeted harassment. All data and code will be released under a CC0 license to facilitate open research and community auditing.

spans: Prior studies present varied perspectives on controllability. Zhang et al. (2018) introduced seq2seq reranking conditioned on POS tags for paraphrase generation. Hosking and Lapata (2021) explored tree transformations for syntactic control in summarization corpora. Wang et al. (2019) designed lexical constraints for neural decoding in machine translation. Li et al. (2020a) proposed exemplar-guided generation with a copy module. Shen et al. (2020) built a dataset to evaluate structural fidelity across paraphrases.
document: In this section, we summarize existing works on syntactically and lexically CPG. Syntactically CPG generates a paraphrase constrained by a prespecified sentence of a certain syntax structure namely exemplar. However, the exemplar is only available during inference, resulting in a key challenge: obtaining manual exemplars for existing paraphrasing training datasets is prohibitively expensive. To address this, some of the previous works construct syntactical features from target sentences during training, such as POS tagging, Constituent Tree, and masked templates as illustrated in Table 1. For instance, SCPN (Iyyer et al., 2018) makes the first attempt to introduce Linearised Constituent Tree (LCT) of the target sentence into paraphrasing, where LCT is predicted based on predefined parse templates. Similarly, GuiG (Li et al., 2020) proposes two models to expand a partial template LCT and generate paraphrasing, respectively. Prior studies present varied perspectives on controllability. Zhang et al. (2018) introduced seq2seq reranking conditioned on POS tags for paraphrase generation. Hosking and Lapata (2021) explored tree transformations for syntactic control in summarization corpora. Wang et al. (2019) designed lexical constraints for neural decoding in machine translation. Li et al. (2020a) proposed exemplar-guided generation with a copy module. Shen et al. (2020) built a dataset to evaluate structural fidelity across paraphrases. Different from using LCT, SGCP (Kumar et al., 2020) introduces a graph encoder to encode the Constituent Tree of exemplar as the condition. Besides, masked template replaces several words of the exemplar with a special token to form a template as the condition. For example, BCPG (Liu et al., 2020b) follows BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) to randomly mask exemplar words, and ParafraGPT (Bui, 2019) makes the first attempt to integrate keywords with a copy mechanism. Despite their progress, existing works only focus on a special condition under either lexically or syntactically CPG. In comparison, GCPG jointly includes lexically and syntactically CPG, flexibly combining conditions in a unified circumstance. Lexically CPG targets controlling keywords, style, or topical constraints during paraphrasing. A common technique is constrained beam search, where required tokens are forced into the output while preserving fluency. Recent work also explores soft lexical control via attribute-conditioned latent variables and prompt-based control. These approaches, though effective, often ignore alignment between lexical and syntactic conditions, leading to suboptimal fidelity to both. To bridge this gap, we consider a unified view that treats both types of constraints as structured signals over the target space. This view enables training objectives that encourage consistency between predicted structure and required lexical realizations. We evaluate prior systems and our framework on standard paraphrase datasets such as ParaNMT, QuoraQP, and WikiAnswers. Following common practice, we report BLEU, ROUGE, and semantic similarity scores, and we add syntactic divergence and control success rates.

spans: Work on domain adaptation with continual pre-training updates PLMs (Gururangan et al., 2020), adapters add small modules for tasks (He et al., 2021), and sparse experts scale capacity (Gururangan et al., 2021). document: Lifelong learning aims at incrementally acquiring new knowledge for pretrained language models while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Prior approaches include memory-based replay (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Rolnick et al., 2019; de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020; Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017), consolidation-based regularization (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Aljundi et al., 2018), and dynamic architectures that grow with tasks (Rusu et al., 2016; Yoon et al., 2018). Work on domain adaptation with continual pre-training updates PLMs (Gururangan et al., 2020), adapters add small modules for tasks (He et al., 2021), and sparse experts scale capacity (Gururangan et al., 2021). Temporal knowledge updates target shifting facts via retrieval or tuning (Dhingra et al., 2021; Jang et al., 2021), and replay memories and gradient storage address task retention (Sun et al., 2020; Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017). Yet existing studies rarely address lifelong pre-training with streaming, multi-source data and stringent efficiency constraints, which defines our setting and evaluation. A concurrent study benchmarks standard continual learning algorithms for PLM adaptation but does not analyze training efficiency. In parallel, efficient pre-training research optimizes objectives (Clark et al., 2020), architectures (Zhang and He, 2020), optimization and systems (You et al., 2020), and large-scale parallelism (Shoeybi et al., 2019; Shazeer et al., 2018). Back-distillation and progressive parameter recycling accelerate scaling (Qin et al., 2021; Gong et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021) but predominantly assume a single static corpus, limiting direct applicability to lifelong pre-training.

document:
Semantic Parsing via Paraphrasing began with Berant and Liang (2014), who proposed finishing semantic parsing through an intermediate paraphrasing step. Subsequent systems generated paraphrase candidates from canonical-utterance grammars and filtered partial derivations against the target formal language using learned scores (Wang et al., 2015; Marzoev et al., 2020). These approaches did not exploit pretrained sequence models to synthesize intermediate paraphrases or guide search.

Compositional generalization is central because formal meaning representations are inherently structured. One line of work injects inductive bias via modular architectures, symbolic–neural hybrids, or latent intermediate variables to encourage systematicity (Dong and Lapata, 2018; Zheng and Lapata, 2020; Lake, 2019). A second line augments data through controlled recombination and curriculum schedules before training the parser (Andreas, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020; Akyürek et al., 2020). Large pretrained encoders and decoders have also improved compositional parsing when adapted to task data (Oren et al., 2020; Furrer et al., 2020). Prior work rarely studied decomposition with prompt filling or zero-shot inference for generalization.

Natural language prompts are widely used for few-shot and zero-shot learning with autoregressive language models. Petroni et al. (2019) introduced cloze-style prompts for probing factual knowledge in pretrained language models. Brown et al. (2020) presented in-context learning with few-shot exemplars using a language model, and Gao et al. (2020) reported prompt-based tuning can outperform fine-tuning on classification benchmarks. Liu et al. (2021a), Li and Liang (2021), and Schucher et al. (2021) explored prompt formats and parameter-efficient tuning under different supervision regimes. Prompting also reduces the need to select demonstrations for each input and stabilizes latency. Note that Mishra et al. (2021) explored prompt decomposition during tuning-free prompting across several NLP tasks.

In computer vision, Wortsman et al. (2021) found that fine-tuning boosts in-distribution accuracy yet degrades out-of-distribution robustness, whereas zero-shot models exhibit stronger OOD generalization. This observation motivated ensembling and model editing to manipulate pretrained zero-shot behavior while preserving robustness (Wortsman et al., 2021; Mitchell et al., 2021). We attempted weight ensembling in our generation pipeline but observed no gains because distributions over the full vocabulary and a constrained decoding space interact poorly. As a result, weights from a zero-shot model exert little influence when decoding is restricted to a task-specific lexical inventory.

spans: Schick and Schütze (2021) introduced pattern-verbalizer pairs for few-shot classification using masked language models. Gao et al. (2021) investigated context-dependent prompt retrieval to enhance accuracy on multiple text categorization benchmarks. Wei et al. document: Textual entailment, also known as natural language inference, asks whether a premise entails, contradicts, or is neutral with respect to a hypothesis (Dagan et al., 2006). State-of-the-art systems leverage large pre-trained language models fine-tuned on annotated resources such as SNLI, MNLI, FEVER, and ANLI (Bowman et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2018; Nie et al., 2020). Despite high accuracy, data scarcity and distribution shifts limit generalization in low-resource settings. Prompt-based and pivot-task learning have been proposed to mitigate these issues by eliciting knowledge from pre-trained models (Le Scao and Rush, 2021; Min et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021a). Schick and Schütze (2021) introduced pattern-verbalizer pairs for few-shot classification using masked language models. Gao et al. (2021) investigated context-dependent prompt retrieval to enhance accuracy on multiple text categorization benchmarks. Wei et al. (2022) presented instruction tuning across heterogeneous tasks with large-scale mixtures and reported substantial improvements. Sanh et al. (2021) demonstrated zero-shot generalization from multitask prompt training with compact models on publicly available natural language benchmarks and datasets. In entailment-centric pivoting, QA datasets have been recast as NLI to reuse annotations efficiently (Dagan et al., 2006; Demszky et al., 2018). Other semantic tasks such as NER, relation extraction, and semantic role labeling have been reformulated into entailment-style templates (White et al., 2017; Poliak et al., 2018a; Levy et al., 2017). Multi-task learning unifies diverse NLP problems under a common text-to-text interface to share inductive biases across datasets (Subramanian et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020; Aribandi et al., 2021). Recent studies show benefits in both supervised and zero-shot regimes when prompts coordinate training signals across tasks (Sanh et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021a). The effectiveness of language modeling as a pivot appears sensitive to model scale, with mixed evidence for smaller architectures (Wei et al., 2021a; Mishra et al., 2021). Question Answering has also been proposed as a pivot, yet results remain modest relative to direct NLI training (McCann et al., 2018). We study multi-source learning for NLI that aggregates datasets differing in label spaces and surface forms while preserving entailment semantics. Event Argument Extraction can benefit from entailment-driven interfaces by casting role filling as recognizing whether a candidate satisfies an event-specific hypothesis (Du and Cardie, 2020a). Prior work explored end-to-end extraction, QA-style prompting, and constrained generation for roles with predefined schemas (Wadden et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020; Du and Cardie, 2020b; Li et al., 2021a; Lyu et al., 2021; Du et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021b). Our approach leverages NLI formulations to unify supervision across resources while enabling evaluation at both sentence and document levels.

document: There have been sustained attempts to leverage phonetic representations to improve or extend automatic speech recognition (ASR) models. Many systems jointly model text and audio by aligning sequences of phonemes with sequences of characters. Sundararaman et al. (2021), for example, propose a joint transformer that encodes phoneme and character streams simultaneously. The goal is to learn representations that are more robust to transcription errors while keeping a text-conditioned decoding interface. The architecture still requires ASR transcripts as inputs and produces both text and phoneme outputs. By contrast, our approach accepts text, audio, or their combination as inputs to a single language model. Baevski et al. (2021) convert unlabeled text into phoneme sequences to supervise speech models without paired data. A generator maps audio segments to predicted phonemes while a discriminator distinguishes these from phonemes transliterated from raw text, yielding strong audio representations but no unified text–audio inference. Outside of ASR, researchers investigate word-level fusion of audio and text for affective computing. Shen et al. (2020) present the WISE framework for aligning modalities in real time using word-synchronous embeddings. Stappen et al. (2021) outline the MuSe challenge with standardized protocols spanning audio, video, text, and physiological signals for robust benchmarks. Vlasenko et al. (2021) report fusion pipelines that combine acoustic features and contextualized text encoders under multi-objective training. Poria et al. (2020) survey multimodal sentiment analysis and catalog datasets, features, and modeling trends. Despite shared use of multimodal inputs, these emotion-centric systems depend on ASR to produce word timings and do not target general-purpose language modeling. Lakhotia et al. (2021) demonstrate "textless" NLP by training language models directly on learned audio units without any text supervision. While this advances self-supervised speech modeling, it lacks the bidirectional flexibility we pursue for models that accept text, audio, or both at inference.

KEPLER combines textual and KG objectives to bind entities and mentions. KG-BERT scores triple plausibility with a BERT encoder over linearized entity and relation sequences. document: We review methods that leverage Knowledge Graphs (KGs) to improve knowledge-intensive NLP tasks. Broadly, approaches either consult a KG at inference or bake KG information into neural parameters. Retrieval-augmented models store or access external facts during prediction. Embedding-based models encode entities and relations during pre-training or fine-tuning. FILM extends BERT with a differentiable fact memory to support factual queries at run time. REALM retrieves passages from a large corpus to ground masked language modeling and open-domain QA. RAG combines retrievers and generators to condition sequence outputs on retrieved evidence from text collections. KILT standardizes evaluation for knowledge-intensive tasks by aligning datasets with evidence sources. ERNIE trains a knowledge-enhanced encoder on aligned triples and sentences. KnowBert injects entity representations into BERT via knowledge attention modules. KEPLER combines textual and KG objectives to bind entities and mentions. KG-BERT scores triple plausibility with a BERT encoder over linearized entity and relation sequences. OpenKE provides tools for embedding-based link prediction on KGs. In contrast to retrieval, parameterization seeks to internalize structured signals from KGs into encoders. ERNIE incorporates entity-aware masking and alignment between triples and sentences to improve representation learning. KnowBert injects entity embeddings via knowledge attention layers and recontextualization into BERT. KEPLER jointly trains textual and KG embeddings under a unified objective that ties descriptions to entity embeddings. BERT-MK integrates medical KG context to enhance clinical named entity recognition and relation extraction. COMET learns to generate commonsense in natural language conditioned on knowledge relation prompts. KELM constructs synthetic sentences from triples to expand pre-training corpora for T5-style models. Recent work explores graph-aware adapters to transfer KG information without full model retraining. We compare both families under unified metrics for factuality, calibration, and generalization across domains.

document: Our proposed semantic perturbation functions generalize the existing textual adversarial attacks. For typo-based perturbation function F T, existing work (Li et al., 2018; Ebrahimi et al., 2017) applies character-level perturbation to carefully crafted typo words, thus making the model ignore or misunderstand the original statistical cues. Knowledge-based perturbation function F K uses a knowledge base to constrain the search space. For example, Zang et al. (2020) uses a sememe-based knowledge base from HowNet (Dong et al., 2010) to construct a search space for word substitution. Different from our contextualized semantic perturbation function F C, other work (Jin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2018) uses a non-contextualized word embedding from GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014) or Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) to build synonym candidates. Such methods typically query the cosine similarity or Euclidean distance between the original and candidate word and select the closest ones as replacements. Some antonyms also have high cosine similarity in the Word2Vec space, so additional hand-crafted filtering rules are needed to ensure that the meaning is not changed. More recent approaches leverage contextualized encoders to better capture local semantics during substitution and to reduce label leakage. Jin et al. (2020) propose a substitution-based attack that ranks candidate words by directional similarity and semantic constraints. Alzantot et al. (2018) employ a genetic algorithm to evolve adversarial sequences across iterations. Ren et al. (2019) use probability-weighted word saliency to guide token replacement in sentiment and inference models. Morris et al. (2020) present a comprehensive evaluation harness that standardizes attack recipes and metrics. Pruthi et al. (2019) investigate character-level noise affecting neural text classifiers in practice. Other work (Garg and Ramakrishnan, 2020; Li et al., 2020) also leverages BERT to generate contextualized perturbations by masked language modeling, which is parallel to our setting where we explore embedding clusters to propose candidates. In contrast to enumerating substitutions, we treat perturbation as a constrained search over a semantic manifold defined by contextual similarity and task-specific invariances. Our optimization objective balances minimal surface changes with maximal model loss increase while satisfying lexical, syntactic, and semantic constraints. Unlike heuristic-based greedy or genetic strategies, we employ a continuous relaxation that allows efficient gradient-guided exploration of discrete choices. We further incorporate a calibration term that penalizes distributional shift from corpus-level statistics to preserve naturalness. To operationalize these constraints, we use entailment-based consistency checks and language-model perplexity thresholds to reject degenerate candidates. We also adopt task-aware saliency to allocate a limited perturbation budget to the most influential spans of the input. Extensive experiments on classification and QA benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves stronger attack success with fewer token edits than prior work. Human evaluation indicates that the perturbed sentences remain fluent and semantically faithful to the source intent. We report robustness transfer across model families, suggesting that our semantic constraints capture model-agnostic vulnerabilities. Ablation results confirm that contextual similarity, constraint filtering, and optimization jointly contribute to the overall effectiveness. Finally, we release code, attack recipes, and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparison in future studies.

document: PLMs are at the core of many success stories in natural language processing. Yet the extent to which they grasp syntactic and semantic properties of language remains unsettled. Probing studies systematically test internal representations and report gaps across several phenomena. Notably, models can ignore sentence order (Pham et al., 2020; Gupta et al., 2021; Sinha et al., 2021a), mishandle numbers (Wallace et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020; Nogueira et al., 2021), and miss semantic content (Ravichander et al., 2020; Elazar et al., 2021). A central weakness concerns negation, a critical device for NLU tasks. Ettinger (2020) probes whether completions differ with or without explicit negation and finds limited sensitivity. Kassner and Schütze (2020) create negated LAMA queries and observe similar predictions for original and negated forms. Such outcomes suggest that PLMs do not robustly encode the logical effects of negation. To address this, Hosseini et al. (2021) augment language modeling with an unlikelihood objective grounded in negated sentences. Their pipeline generates negations via dependency-based patterns such as Semgrex, using parses, POS tags, and morphological cues. Training replaces objects with [MASK] and penalizes high likelihoods, while positive counterparts supply factual context. Lake and Baroni (2018) investigate compositional generalization through systematicity tests. Brown et al. (2020) present a language model for few-shot learning, and Talmor et al. (2020) analyze multi-hop question answering with decomposed queries. While effective, such reliance on external analyzers can propagate parsing errors and bias the augmented corpus. Prior work often equates negation to tokens like "no" or "not", yet the broader property concerns opposite-meaning beyond surface markers. We therefore explore perturbations that flip meaning without heavy linguistic toolchains to test whether PLMs satisfy the property. Building on this evaluation, we introduce intermediate-training on meaning-matching (IM2), a lightweight objective for aligning representations with contrastive, oppositely entailed pairs. Our experiments unify diagnostics across templates and show improvements on negation-sensitive benchmarks while exposing persistent failure modes.

spans: Li et al. (2018) proposed deleting style markers and retrieving content to guide generation in a non-parallel setting. Park et al. (2019) described SpecAugment for speech recognition and reported gains on LibriSpeech with simple time and frequency masking. Narayan et al. (2018) introduced extreme summarization with a dataset targeting single-sentence outputs and examined control over length and truncation effects. Zhang and Lapata (2017) explored sentence simplification using reinforcement learning to balance simplicity and adequacy, optimizing SARI while monitoring BLEU scores.
document: Few-shot methods are a recent development in English style transfer, with prior work using variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2020), or prompting large pretrained language models at inference (Reif et al., 2021). Most related is the state-of-the-art TextSETTR model from Riley et al. (2021), who use a neural style encoder to map exemplar sentences to a vector used to guide generation. To train this encoder, they use the idea that adjacent sentences in a document have a similar style. Recently, the Universal Rewriter (Garcia et al., 2021) extended TextSETTR to 101 languages, developing a joint model for translation, few-shot style transfer and stylized translation. This model is the only prior few-shot system we found outside English, and our main baseline. We discuss its shortcomings in Section 3.1, and propose fixes in Section 4. Multilingual style transfer is mostly unexplored in prior work: a 35 paper survey by Briakou et al. (2021b) found only one work in Chinese, Russian, Latvian, Estonian, French. They further introduced XFORMAL, the first formality transfer evaluation dataset in French, Brazilian Portuguese and Italian. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to study style transfer for the languages we consider. More related work from Hindi linguistics and on style transfer control is provided in Appendix B. Li et al. (2018) proposed deleting style markers and retrieving content to guide generation in a non-parallel setting. Park et al. (2019) described SpecAugment for speech recognition and reported gains on LibriSpeech with simple time and frequency masking. Narayan et al. (2018) introduced extreme summarization with a dataset targeting single-sentence outputs and examined control over length and truncation effects. Zhang and Lapata (2017) explored sentence simplification using reinforcement learning to balance simplicity and adequacy, optimizing SARI while monitoring BLEU scores. We focus on formality manipulation and politeness softening in low-resource settings with code-mixed inputs. Our method couples a multilingual encoder with language-specific low-rank adapters and a retrieval memory that surfaces two to three exemplars per style. We train with a contrastive objective over retrieved exemplars and a paraphrase consistency loss to stabilize adaptation across domains. For datasets, we combine XFORMAL with mined parallel style pairs from public comment corpora and in-domain conversational data. Automatic evaluation uses reference-free metrics for style accuracy and content similarity, supplemented with targeted tests for honorifics and address terms. Human evaluations follow standardized pairwise protocols with bilingual raters, measuring preference on fluency, preservation, and appropriateness. Against Universal Rewriter, our system obtains higher style accuracy at comparable preservation, and reduces hallucinations on low-resource languages. These findings underscore the importance of multilingual conditioning and retrieval for few-shot style control, setting a strong baseline for future research.

spans: Clinical concept normalization has been leveraged to align note spans with external vocabularies during coding (Moon et al., 2020; Tutubalina et al., 2018). Character-level encoders capture misspellings common in clinical narratives and improve recall for abbreviations (Zhang et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019). Reinforcement learning has been explored to penalize implausible code sets by modeling sequential assignment decisions (Peng et al., 2019; Kerkez et al., 2021).
document: Automatic ICD coding is an important task in the medical NLP community. Earlier works use machine learning pipelines that rely on sparse features and linear classifiers to assign multiple diagnosis and procedure codes to clinical notes. With the rise of deep neural models, CNN and RNN encoders have become dominant for multi-label prediction in this domain (Mullenbach et al., 2018; Baumel et al., 2018). These models address long documents by using hierarchical pooling or section-aware aggregation to preserve salient information from discharge summaries (Shi et al., 2017; Xie and Xing, 2018). Attention mechanisms have been shown to improve both performance and interpretability by linking text spans to code-specific signals (Mullenbach et al., 2018; Vu et al., 2020). Researchers further integrate label descriptions to enrich representations and mitigate data sparsity for rare codes (Rios and Kavuluru, 2018; Huang et al., 2019). Pretrained language models tailored to clinical text provide strong baselines and consistently outperform non-contextual embeddings on standard benchmarks (Alsentzer et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). Graph-based methods exploit the ICD hierarchy to propagate information across related codes and regularize predictions using parent–child relations (Cao et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2021). Clinical concept normalization has been leveraged to align note spans with external vocabularies during coding (Moon et al., 2020; Tutubalina et al., 2018). Character-level encoders capture misspellings common in clinical narratives and improve recall for abbreviations (Zhang et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2019). Reinforcement learning has been explored to penalize implausible code sets by modeling sequential assignment decisions (Peng et al., 2019; Kerkez et al., 2021). Recent advances also consider label imbalance via cost-sensitive losses and calibrated thresholds to prevent overprediction of frequent codes (Dong et al., 2021; Jain et al., 2022). Beyond accuracy, interpretability remains critical for clinical adoption, motivating methods that produce faithful rationales or concept alignments (DeYoung et al., 2020; Lehman et al., 2022). Incorporating structured EHR elements such as medications and lab values has been explored to complement unstructured text and improve robustness (Weng et al., 2017; Song et al., 2020). Domain adaptation is necessary when models are deployed across institutions with differing note styles and coding practices (Romanov and Shivade, 2018; McDermott et al., 2021). Data-efficient learning strategies such as self-training and contrastive objectives help leverage large unlabeled corpora of clinical narratives (Liu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). Zero-shot and few-shot coding leverage ontology semantics to infer labels unseen during training by matching descriptions and synonyms (Rios and Kavuluru, 2018; Sun et al., 2021). Evaluation commonly reports micro and macro F1, precision at k, and area under the precision–recall curve on MIMIC datasets (Johnson et al., 2016; Johnson et al., 2019). Our work builds on these directions by unifying text encoding, label semantics, and hierarchical constraints within a single training objective. We present experiments across multiple datasets and release resources to support reproducibility and future research in automatic ICD coding.
