document: Pre-trained Language Models (LMs) have been shown to encode knowledge that, with task-specific guidance, can solve natural language understanding tasks (Petroni et al., 2019). Building on this, Le Scao and Rush (2021) quantified a reduction in the need for labeled data by hundreds of instances compared to traditional fine-tuning approaches (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). These findings have led to quality improvements in zero- and few-shot learning. Semantic similarity methods such as Explicit Semantic Analysis model the meaning of label names and compute similarity with the input text (Gabrilovich and Markovitch, 2007; Chang et al., 2008). Recent advances in pre-trained LMs for semantic textual similarity, notably Sentence-BERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), have opened opportunities to improve these methods and motivate this work. Xu et al. (2015) introduce a projection approach for label embeddings in classification with no discussion of similarity metrics. Johnson and Zhang (2017) study very fast text classification with linear models and character n-grams. Kiros et al. (2015) present skip-thought vectors trained to predict surrounding sentences across large corpora. Clark et al. (2019) explore multi-task pre-training for general language understanding using a mixture of supervised objectives. Howard and Ruder (2018) describe universal language model fine-tuning and report gains on benchmarks. Chu et al. (2020) propose an unsupervised label-refinement technique that iteratively adjusts prototype labels. They incorporate a modified k-means procedure to refine cross-attention outputs and Siamese encodings, and we extend their analysis in our experiments. We evaluate LR on more diverse benchmarks and observe that pre-training few-shot learners on their NatCat task underperforms pre-training on NLI datasets. document: Event Detection is a critical subtask of event extraction that seeks to locate event instances in text. It has received sustained attention due to its role in downstream information extraction pipelines. Traditional approaches rely on feature engineering and structured models to capture triggers and arguments (Ahn, 2006; Ji and Grishman, 2008; Liao and Grishman, 2010; Hong et al., 2011; Li et al., 2013). Neural models have become dominant, leveraging distributed representations and end-to-end training (Chen et al., 2015; Nguyen and Grishman, 2015; Feng et al., 2016; Nguyen and Nguyen, 2019). Recent advances incorporate syntactic priors, document-level context, and auxiliary supervision to improve robustness (Liu et al., 2018; Lai et al., 2020; Wadden et al., 2019; Du and Cardie, 2020; Lai et al., 2021; Pouran Ben Veyseh et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021; Tong et al., 2020). Despite these gains, many systems train a single classifier over all event types, leading to skewed performance across categories. Two studies reported particularly weak results on context-dependent narratives and proposed improved context exploration strategies to mitigate this issue (Lu et al., 2019). Nonetheless, these efforts focus on empirical improvements rather than diagnosing the underlying causes of type-specific brittleness. Our approach revisits this gap by characterizing event-type regularities and learning signals that reflect those patterns. We model type-level cues as explanations that can be integrated into training and evaluation. Simonyan et al. (2014) introduced saliency maps for visual models and inspired derivative-based explanations in NLP. Ribeiro et al. (2016) proposed a local surrogate approach for model-agnostic attribution across modalities. Sundararajan et al. (2017) defined axioms for path-integrated gradients and offered a practical estimator for deep networks. Springenberg et al. (2015) presented guided backpropagation, while Ding et al. (2017) explored attention alignment for translation and Chen et al. (2018) studied word importance in classifiers and tagging tasks. Feature attribution provides a principled mechanism to score the contribution of input features to a model prediction. Formally, given an input vector x in R^n and a probabilistic model F, an attribution assigns each coordinate a_i reflecting its influence on F(x). Gradient-based methods use local derivatives, while reference-based methods compare predictions between an input and a baseline to quantify contributions (Simonyan et al., 2014; Springenberg et al., 2015; Ribeiro et al., 2016; Sundararajan et al., 2017). We adopt Integrated Gradients because it is efficient, implementation-agnostic, and stable across text tasks (Sundararajan et al., 2017; Liu and Avci, 2019; Bastings and Filippova, 2020). For a token embedding coordinate x_i, the attribution equals the path integral of the output gradient along the straight line between a baseline and x. The baseline encodes absence of information, which we instantiate as all-zero embeddings for the input sequence. By aggregating token scores to types, we obtain type-level saliency profiles that act as weak supervision for ED. Our experiments demonstrate that these profiles improve performance while offering interpretable diagnostics on error modes.

According to the control element, previous works can be divided into two categories. Shi et al. (2021) propose parse-tree–aware control using pointer networks, Krishna et al. (2020) study back-translation schemes for paraphrase augmentation in classification datasets, and Mallinson et al. (2017) examine paraphrase lattices. (2021) investigate POS-conditioned simplification and observe benefits from control signals, Xu et al. (2019) report gains from syntactic control tokens on summarization, and Sun and Zhou (2012) discuss parse reranking methods for preferences. The first strand of research uses sentential exemplar as syntactic control. Differing from previous approaches, we propose a novel tree-transformer to model parent-child and sibling relations for better capturing the tree structure of the parse tree. To learn the alignment relation between words and nodes, we also propose an attention regularization objective, which makes the decoder accurately select corresponding syntax nodes to guide the generation of words. Additionally, we propose a syntactic template retriever that can help to retrieve compatible syntactic structures for any input sentence.

document: In this section, we highlight the information bottleneck principle. Subsequently, the analysis of possible issues when applying it to OOV entity recognition was provided. Furthermore, we review related techniques in deriving our framework. Information Bottleneck (IB) principle originated in information theory, and provides a theoretical framework for analyzing deep neural networks. It formulates the goal of representation learning as an information trade-off between representation compression and predictive power. Given the input dataset (X,Y), it seeks to learn the internal representation Z of some intermediate layers by: where I represents the mutual information(MI), a measure of the mutual dependence between the two variables. The trade-off between the two MI terms is controlled by a Lagrange multiplier β. A low loss indicates that representation Z does not keep too much information from X while still retaining enough information to predict Y. Section 5 suggests that directly applying IB to NER can not bring obvious improvement, and we argue that IB cannot guarantee well-generalizing representation. Shwartz-Ziv and Tishby (2017) examined phases of fitting and compression in deep networks, and Saxe et al. (2019) studied conditions under which compression may not appear, Belghazi et al. (2018) proposed MINE for estimating mutual information in neural settings. Alemi et al. (2016) introduced a variational information bottleneck, Achille and Soatto (2018) related compressibility to invariance and disentanglement, Poole et al. (2019) discussed mutual information estimators and evaluation issues, and Fischer (2020) reviewed priors for representations. On the one hand, it has been shown that it is challenging to find a trade-off between high compression and high predictive power (Tishby et al., 2000;Wang et al., 2019;Piran et al., 2020). When compressing task-irrelevant nuisances, however, useful information will inevitably be left out. On the other hand, it is unclear for the IB principle which parts of features are well-generalizing and which are not, as we usually train a classifier to solely maximize accuracy. Consequently, neural networks tend to use any accessible signal to do so (Ilyas et al., 2019), which is referred to as a shotcut learning problem (Geirhos et al., 2020). For training sets with limited size, it may be easier for neural networks to memorize entity names rather than to classify them by context and common entity features (Agarwal et al., 2021). In Section 4, we demonstrate how we extend BN to the NER task and address these issues.

document: This paper examines the arms race between model scale and dataset design in natural language processing. Prior work has criticized aggressive model scaling on ethical and environmental grounds (Schwartz et al., 2020; Bender et al., 2021) and questioned whether ever-larger language models learn the right abstractions from text (Bender and Koller, 2020). We focus on the dataset side of this dynamic, investigating how balancing strategies aim to mitigate spurious correlations. Balanced datasets are often released with the promise of clearer measurement, accelerated progress, and sharper comparisons among modeling approaches. Yet recent analyses report that model rankings on small or synthetic reading-comprehension benchmarks often mirror those on SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), challenging the premise that balance alone reshapes evaluation. Studies of annotation artifacts revealed shallow cues that models can exploit in NLI and QA (Gururangan et al., 2018; Poliak et al., 2018; Geva et al., 2019). In response, researchers proposed adversarial filtering and counterexample mining to weaken shortcuts (Zellers et al., 2019; Nie et al., 2020). Data augmentation via counterfactual rewriting was shown to reduce reliance on spurious features while preserving label semantics (Kaushik et al., 2020). Ribeiro et al. (2020) introduced a behavioral checklist for targeted capability testing. Dodge et al. (2019) quantified the sensitivity of benchmarking conclusions to training compute and random seeds. Bowman and Dahl (2021) argued that benchmark saturation obscures scientific understanding, and Linzen (2020) highlighted the need for targeted evaluations of linguistic competence. Hooker (2021) discussed measurement pitfalls in machine learning and dataset design and evaluation. Training-time methods such as group distributionally robust optimization aim to upweight worst-case subpopulations and yield more stable generalization (Sagawa et al., 2020). Complementary benchmarks emphasizing domain and demographic shifts have been proposed to track robustness under distribution shift (Koh et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Yet, even with rebalanced data and robust objectives, models often recover shortcut rules when spurious features correlate with labels in residual ways (Tu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Our study contributes a systematic analysis of when balancing alters relative model orderings and when it leaves the leaderboard effectively unchanged. We examine confounder structure, sample complexity, and hypothesis class bias to explain stability in rankings across datasets with matched labels but different feature correlations. Finally, we relate these findings to the broader debate on benchmark scope, echoing concerns that benchmarks capture narrow skills rather than general capability (Raji et al., 2021), and we outline design principles for datasets that better expose abstraction and reasoning.

Lin et al. (2020) propose joint modeling with global features for triggers and arguments, extending structured prediction with span factors and neural encoders across documents. Lu et al. (2021) introduce Text2Event to linearize event structures for sequence-to-structure generation using constrained decoding and emphasizing template design during training. Deng et al. (2020), Shen et al. (2021), and Cong et al. (2021) investigate meta-learning for few-shot event detection, assessing transfer, episodic training, and initialization choices under limited supervision. We focus on low-resource, end-to-end event extraction and present a unified generation framework that preserves label semantics, encourages cross-task knowledge sharing, and yields competitive performance with limited annotation.

document: Traditional methods treat NER as a sequence labeling task, where each word or character in a sentence is assigned a tag. As a representative approach, Huang et al. (2015) used a BiLSTM encoder to learn contextual representations and a Conditional Random Field decoder to enforce label consistency. The BiLSTM-CRF architecture achieved strong performance on diverse datasets, and many subsequent works adopted this backbone (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016). With the advent of deep contextual pretraining, ELMo and BERT substantially boosted NER by providing richer token features (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019). In Chinese NER, character-based modeling is prevalent due to the absence of explicit word boundaries. To bridge character and word granularity, researchers have explored augmenting models with lexicon matches from large dictionaries. Zhang and Yang (2018) proposed Lattice-LSTM to inject candidate words aligned to each character path during encoding. The lattice mechanism increases modeling flexibility but complicates batching and reduces parallelism on modern hardware. Gui et al. (2019) formulated a lexicon-based graph neural network that views characters and matched words as nodes and performs node classification. Other studies integrate all matched words directly into character representations via gating or attention to alleviate graph construction overhead (Ma et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Xue et al. (2019) examined Conditional Random Field extensions to capture higher-order tag interactions in decoding. Peters et al. (2018) introduced deep contextualized word representations that provide layer-wise features for downstream tagging. Devlin et al. (2019) presented a transformer encoder pre-trained with masked language modeling and next sentence prediction. Akbik et al. (2018) built contextual string embeddings from character n-grams to enhance sequence labeling across languages and datasets. Beyond token labeling, span-based NER reframes the task as detecting and typing contiguous spans rather than assigning BIO tags. Methods in this line typically score start and end boundaries, enumerate candidate spans within a length budget, and classify them with span-level features (Lee et al., 2017; Sohrab and Miwa, 2018). Recent studies further add span pretraining objectives or contrastive span learning to improve generalization with limited annotations (Mengge et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2022). Data augmentation and distant supervision using gazetteers and weak rules reduce annotation costs while maintaining coverage (Shang et al., 2018; Jie et al., 2019). Despite progress, robustness to noisy lexicon matches and ambiguous boundaries remains challenging in open-domain and social media texts. Motivated by these gaps, we study internal regularities of contiguous spans such as boundary symmetry and type-specific token distributions. We propose a lightweight architecture that explicitly models span boundaries and internal consistency without using external lexicons. Experiments on standard Chinese NER benchmarks show competitive accuracy, strong robustness under lexicon noise, and notable efficiency.

document: Balancing short-range statistical constraints with long-range structure was a core issue in language generation long before neural LMs: n-gram models and early neural LMs used backoff and interpolation to combine predictors operating at different context lengths (Chen and Goodman, 1996; Bengio et al., 2003). Neural modeling introduced gated recurrent units capable of propagating information across long spans and reintroduced alignment via attention in sequence generation, culminating in Transformer LMs like GPT (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014; Brown et al., 1993; Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017). Despite larger context windows (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021; Press et al., 2021), models can be undersensitive to distant content and syntax and exhibit recency biases in prompts and dialogue (Khandelwal et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021; Sankar et al., 2019). Inference-time control has therefore focused on preventing degeneration, with tempered sampling, top-k, and top-p truncation acting locally on the distribution to reduce entropy and tail mass (Fan et al., 2018; Holtzman et al., 2019). We extend this line by formalizing coherence boosting as a log-linear mixture of distributions conditioned on full and partial contexts to balance global consistency with local fit. In dialog, mutual-information decoding encourages dependence on the prompt and reduces dull replies, which we reinterpret within our framework as a specific parameterization (Li et al., 2016). In multiple-choice evaluation, normalizing candidate scores by unconditional likelihood mitigates generic-answer bias and aligns with our view of coherence-aware reweighting (Brown et al., 2020). Holtzman et al. (2019) introduce nucleus sampling to prune low-probability tails in open-ended generation on English web corpora. Brown et al. (2020) present few-shot prompting with large Transformers and document strong baselines across tasks. Zhao et al. (2021) analyze prompt recency effects in in-context learning, and Sankar et al. (2019) describe multi-turn drift in neural dialog systems. Beyond selection problems, we show analytically that surface-form competition cannot explain token-level choice when the candidate set is the entire vocabulary and the completion is a single token. We then demonstrate that open-ended generation benefits from coherence boosting even when no paraphrastic variants are present among hypotheses. Our experiments evaluate story continuation, multi-turn dialog, and question answering with long contexts exceeding four thousand tokens. We compare against top-k, top-p, temperature scaling, and repetition penalties under matched compute budgets and identical decoding latencies. Human raters judge outputs from our method to be more globally consistent with earlier content while preserving local fluency and style. Ablations disentangle the effects of mixture weight, context truncation strategy, and calibration of unconditional baselines on both automatic and human metrics. We release code and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducibility and encourage work on principled inference-time control for global coherence.

Methods are commonly grouped into uncertainty-, diversity-, and hybrid-based strategies, and recent analyses examine their behavior with pretrained language models (Gal et al., 2017; Sener and Savarese, 2018; Kirsch et al., 2019; Ash et al., 2020; Margatina et al., 2021a,b; Ein-Dor et al., 2020). SSAL studies design query rules tailored to semi-supervised learners or adopt pseudo-labeling to expand training sets (Gao et al., 2020; Song et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2021; Tomanek and Hahn, 2009; Rottmann et al., 2018; Siméoni et al., 2020). We build on self-training by selecting high-confidence unlabeled instances during fine-tuning and jointly querying informative examples, forming ACTUNE, which consistently improves performance while mitigating error accumulation through momentum-based filtering (Rosenberg et al., 2005; Lee, 2013; Arazo et al., 2020; Rizve et al., 2021).

In contrast, we focus on imbuing the student with the causal structure of the teacher. Interventions on neural networks were originally used as a structural analysis method aimed at illuminating neural representations and their role in network behavior (Pryzant et al., 2021; Vig et al., 2020; Elazar et al., 2020; Giulianelli et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2021a). Geiger et al. (2021b) extend these methods to network optimization. Xiao et al. (2021) propose pruning for transfer efficiency, He et al. (2016) develop deep residual networks for recognition, and Devlin et al. (2019) introduce BERT for bidirectional pretraining across tasks. Vaswani et al. (2017) describe Transformer architecture, Lan et al. (2020) streamline pretraining objectives, and Clark et al. (2020) train ELECTRA to improve efficiency. We contribute to this existing research by adapting intervention-based optimization to the task of language model distillation.

document: Large neural language models pretrained on self-supervised tasks have recently gained traction in data-to-text generation research (Lewis et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2019; Ferreira et al., 2020). Following Chen et al. (2020c), several works adopt pretrained language models for few-shot D2T generation (Chang et al., 2021b; Su et al., 2021a). Kale and Rastogi (2020b) and Ribeiro et al. (2020) showed that linearized representations can outperform graph encoders on graph-to-text datasets, later surpassed again by graph-based models (Ke et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020a). Although these models leverage general-domain pretraining, they are ultimately finetuned on domain-specific data to realize competitive performance. This paper situates our method within this line by focusing on controllable planning and realization with minimal task-specific supervision. Using simple handcrafted templates for individual keys or predicates is an efficient way to inject domain knowledge and regularize text-to-text models (Heidari et al., 2021; Kale and Rastogi, 2020a). Transforming individual triples into short textual snippets has also been explored by Laha et al. (2020) in a pipeline for zero-shot D2T generation. Their approach relies on handwritten rules for surface realization and does not explicitly address global content planning. We retain the benefits of lightweight templates while delegating document-level structure to learned components. This design reduces overfitting to schema idiosyncrasies without sacrificing coverage of domain facts. Content planning, understood as selecting and ordering input facts into sentences, is a long-standing component of the D2T pipeline (Ferreira et al., 2019; Gatt and Krahmer, 2018; Reiter and Dale, 1997). Prior neural systems demonstrate that explicit plans yield large gains in coherence and faithfulness (Moryossef et al., 2019a,b; Puduppully et al., 2019; Trisedya et al., 2020). Moryossef et al. (2019a,b) learn sentence orderings from meaning representations with a modular planner. Puduppully et al. (2019) introduce a content selector that tracks entities across records for sports summaries. Trisedya et al. (2020) propose a graph planner for narrative generation from knowledge bases, and Chang et al. (2021b) examine prompt-based few-shot data-to-text with pretrained models. Su et al. (2021a) evaluate adapter-style tuning on limited data in domain-specific table-to-text tasks too. In contrast to these planning strategies, our planner is trained to order natural language sentences produced from facts rather than symbolic predicates. This choice enables reuse of general-purpose encoders while decoupling schema alignment from discourse structuring. Sentence ordering addresses the problem of arranging a set of natural language sentences to maximize global coherence (Barzilay et al., 2001; Lapata, 2003). Neural approaches include pairwise scoring of sentence relations (Li and Jurafsky, 2017), global models over permutations (Gong et al., 2016; Wang and Wan, 2019), and hybrids that combine both views (Cui et al., 2020). We base our ordering module on a pointer-network formulation over PLM embeddings that has shown strong results (Calizzano et al., 2021; Vinyals et al., 2015). The model predicts an index sequence that yields a coherent paragraph under a learned discourse representation. The compact nature of target descriptions often requires aggregating multiple facts into single sentences while preserving salience. Prior work models latent alignments between text spans and records to support aggregation (Wiseman et al., 2018; Shao et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021). We assume an already ordered sequence of fact mentions and insert explicit delimiters that mark sentence boundaries. This assumption simplifies decoding and focuses learning capacity on planning and compression. We formulate paragraph compression as the final stage of realization that fuses redundant material and resolves references while keeping all facts. Unlike summarization or simplification, the objective is not to omit content but to consolidate phrasing under full-coverage constraints (Jiang et al., 2020). Compared with sentence fusion and sentence compression, our setting operates over multi-sentence inputs and emphasizes grammatical cohesion and entity consistency; this motivates the WIKIFLUENT corpus synthesized via split-and-rephrase (Geva et al., 2019; Barzilay and McKeown, 2005; Filippova and Altun, 2013; Botha et al., 2018).

Template-based approaches introduce discrete or soft prompts to steer pre-trained encoders toward downstream tasks (Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Instruction tuning aligns model behaviors with task descriptions across diverse collections of datasets (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022) and demonstrates improved sample efficiency on classification benchmarks (Min et al., 2022).
document: Multi-Hop Reading Comprehension Recent works for multi-hop RC include memory-based methods which apply attentions to iteratively update query and context representations considering their interactions (Dhingra et al., 2018; Clark and Gardner, 2018; Zhuang and Wang, 2019; Yichen Jiang and Bansal, 2019). To explicitly incorporate entity connections, De Cao et al. (2019), Ding et al. (2019), Qiu et al. (2019), Tang et al. (2020), Song et al. (2018), and Tu et al. (2019) build entity graphs and apply Graph Neural Networks for information propagation. Kundu et al. (2019) formalizes reasoning as a path-finding problem with neural encoding to rank candidate paths. Path modeling is also adopted in using pointer networks. However, these approaches only focus on local information without the ability to generalize, and some of them rely on NER tools. Dhingra et al. (2020) converts texts into a virtual knowledge base for retrieval, but requires an entity database. Another category uses neural module networks (Min et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020) to decompose the question into a series of actions, each parameterized with a neural module, which also fail to explicitly uncover the underlying logic for reasoning. Within deep learning with logic reasoning, neurosymbolic learning aims to integrate deep learning's ability for handling uncertainty and logic programming's ability for reasoning. Deep neural networks have been used to parameterize discrete logic operators and logic atoms (França et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2016; Manhaeve et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018; Li and Srikumar, 2019; Wu et al., 2020) given the logic rules. A more challenging direction is inductive logic programming that automatically learns rules through representation learning and differentiable backpropagation (Evans and Grefenstette, 2018; Dong et al., 2019; Yang and Song, 2020). Neuro-symbolic learning has been applied to knowledge-base completion through logic embeddings (Guo et al., 2016), tensor operations (Cohen, 2016), adversarial learning (Minervini et al., 2017), variational learning (Qu and Tang, 2019), or attentions (Yang and Song, 2020). Differentiable theorem proving has also been proposed with weak unifications and backward chaining (Campero et al., 2018; Minervini et al., 2020). However, unlike multi-hop RC, knowledge-base completion only takes structured inputs without the need to address language ambiguity. The most related work to ours is NLProlog (Weber et al., 2019), a neural theorem prover for multi-hop RC by converting language utterances to distributed embeddings. NLProlog relies on a NER tool to extract entities and its expressiveness is limited by the number of rule templates. Beyond theorem proving, recent work explores iterative retrieval and reasoning over unstructured text to bridge evidence across multiple documents. Large-scale prompting with in-context examples has been studied for few-shot generalization in language models (Brown et al., 2020). Template-based approaches introduce discrete or soft prompts to steer pre-trained encoders toward downstream tasks (Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Instruction tuning aligns model behaviors with task descriptions across diverse collections of datasets (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022; Ouyang et al., 2022) and demonstrates improved sample efficiency on classification benchmarks (Min et al., 2022). In our setting, we focus on models that execute chain-of-thought style decompositions while maintaining faithfulness to the underlying evidence. We target robustness to entity aliasing and coreference phenomena that often break purely symbolic pipelines. Prior analyses reveal that multi-hop datasets contain significant lexical overlap biases, which can mislead shallow heuristics. To mitigate such biases, recent models emphasize calibrated confidence estimation and evidence selection regularization. Another line of work augments readers with external knowledge bases to supply missing bridges not present verbatim in the passages. Joint training of retrieval and reasoning improves end-to-end performance by aligning latent questions with passage salience. Graph-augmented transformers encode inter-sentence relations with global attention patterns guided by discourse cues. Contrastive objectives encourage separation between plausible distractors and truly supporting facts in both encoder and decoder representations. Our approach builds on these insights by unifying symbolic constraints with differentiable selection over latent rule instantiations. This perspective situates our contribution within the broader movement toward reliable, data-efficient multi-hop question answering.

spans: Miech et al. (2019) gather large-scale web video-text pairs to train retrieval models with MIL-NCE loss. Sun et al. (2019) propose VideoBERT to learn joint visual-linguistic representations from instructional videos using masked language modeling. Zellers et al. (2021) introduce MERLOT for multimodal commonsense pretraining across frames and subtitles. Lei et al. (2020) release TVQA as a benchmark with subshot-level questions aligned to dialogue and timestamps, and Li et al. (2020) present HERO for hierarchical video-language pretraining. Pu et al. (2021) develop FiT for transferable video-language learning, and Bain et al. (2021) curate HowTo100M variants for robust pretraining evaluation and analysis.
document: Language-informed video understanding is a complex task that has been extensively addressed in the multimodal (natural language and computer vision) machine learning research, by proposing diverse tasks and benchmarks. By coupling linguistic priors with visual cues, systems can align events, reason over temporal structure, and answer natural language queries about dynamic scenes. Prior art spans video captioning, text-video retrieval, video question answering, and temporal grounding, with increasingly large-scale pretraining bridging modalities and tasks. Miech et al. (2019) gather large-scale web video-text pairs to train retrieval models with MIL-NCE loss. Sun et al. (2019) propose VideoBERT to learn joint visual-linguistic representations from instructional videos using masked language modeling. Zellers et al. (2021) introduce MERLOT for multimodal commonsense pretraining across frames and subtitles. Lei et al. (2020) release TVQA as a benchmark with subshot-level questions aligned to dialogue and timestamps, and Li et al. (2020) present HERO for hierarchical video-language pretraining. Pu et al. (2021) develop FiT for transferable video-language learning, and Bain et al. (2021) curate HowTo100M variants for robust pretraining evaluation and analysis. Despite these advances, there remains a gap in leveraging structured linguistic knowledge, such as semantic roles and discourse relations, to guide cross-frame reasoning and long-context alignment. We address this gap with a language-informed pretraining objective that injects predicate-argument constraints into video-text matching and augments supervision with discourse-aware negatives. Our contributions include a unified formulation for temporally grounded reasoning, a pretraining dataset annotated with shallow semantics, and extensive evaluations across retrieval, QA, and captioning benchmarks.

spans: Zhang et al. (2020) present an edit-based generator that applies span operations guided by detected conflicts in text. Narayan et al. (2018) propose a bottom-up summarizer that prunes source sentences using content selectors for improved faithfulness. Goodrich et al. (2019) study hierarchically controlled rewriting with constraints over entities and dates to steer factual surface forms precisely.
document: Early work on writing assistants largely focuses on grammar error correction; for a survey see Wang et al. (2020). Neural models have expanded the capabilities of writing assistants to solve a wider variety of tasks including autocompletion and following rhetorical directives such as paraphrasing and elaboration (Sun et al., 2021). In this work, we seek to expand these capabilities further to producing grounded updates, which has been previously studied by Kang et al. (2019), however only for post-modifier generation. As our primary focus is on writing grounded updates to Wikipedia articles, our work is closely related to existing works on Wikipedia article generation, which generally uses one of two approaches: data-to-text generation (Lebret et al., 2016; Bao et al., 2018; Parikh et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2020), or multi-document summarization (Banerjee and Mitra, 2016; Liu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2021). In particular, the hyperlink-based approach for associating evidence to articles is directly inspired by these works, and our annotation procedure for removing unsupported text directly draws from Parikh et al. (2020). Determining which facts contradict claims in the existing article is a central topic of work on fact extraction and verification (Thorne et al., 2018). Recently, Schuster et al. (2021) introduced the VITAMIN-C dataset of factual revisions to Wikipedia articles and the task of factually consistent generation. This work differs from FRUIT in that it only focuses on sentences and does not require adding new facts or content selection. Beyond verification, maintaining topical coherence during edits has been explored under controllable rewriting and discourse-aware summarization. Zhang et al. (2020) present an edit-based generator that applies span operations guided by detected conflicts in text. Narayan et al. (2018) propose a bottom-up summarizer that prunes source sentences using content selectors for improved faithfulness. Goodrich et al. (2019) study hierarchically controlled rewriting with constraints over entities and dates to steer factual surface forms precisely. Complementary to these strands, update summarization frameworks target the delta between document states and aim to avoid redundancy (Dang and Owczarzak, 2008; Kedzie et al., 2018). In the Wikipedia domain, recommendation systems propose candidate citations and sources to support or revise claims (Redi et al., 2020; Fetahu et al., 2019). Entity-centric content planning has been shown to improve coverage and reduce hallucinations in encyclopedic generation (Liu et al., 2018; Puduppully et al., 2019). Our approach operationalizes grounded updates as minimal edits supported by verifiable evidence while preserving local discourse structure. We formalize the task as selecting evidence, identifying conflicting or outdated statements, and proposing edits constrained by sentence- and section-level context. To this end, we curate training signals from revision histories and hyperlink neighborhoods that emphasize alignment between claims and sources. We evaluate on Wikipedia pages with time-sensitive facts, combining automatic metrics for factuality and human judgments of edit usefulness. The resulting analysis highlights when edit selection, evidence attribution, and constrained rewriting jointly yield reliable updates.

Hoover et al. (2020) describe corpus construction and labeling schemes for moral foundations. Haidt and Joseph (2004) outline the theoretical basis of intuitive ethics and virtues. Graham et al. (2013) consolidate.
document:
We introduce the Moral Foundation Theory (MFT) (Graham et al., 2013) and the Moral Foundation Twitter Corpus (MFTC) (Hoover et al., 2020) used in our experiments.

The MFT is a well-established theory of moral values developed by social and cultural psychologists. It argues that human morality is composed of a finite set of innate moral foundations, similar to how the five taste receptors (for sweet, sour, salt, bitter, and umami) combine to yield the tastes we experience. The MFT includes five foundations, each composed of a vice–virtue duality, resulting in the 10 moral values shown in Table 1. The MFTC is divided into seven datasets, each corresponding to a topic: All Lives Matter (ALM), Baltimore protests (BLT), Black Lives Matter (BLM), hate speech and offensive language (DAV) (Davidson et al., 2017), 2016 presidential election (ELE), MeToo movement (MT), and Hurricane Sandy (SND). These datasets from complex and diverse socio-political issues allow us to evaluate transferability by treating each dataset as belonging to a domain. Garten et al. (2016) analyze moral narratives with dictionary-based features across news. Johnson and Goldwasser (2018) present structured models for predicting moral frames in tweets. Hoover et al. (2020) describe corpus construction and labeling schemes for moral foundations. Haidt and Joseph (2004) outline the theoretical basis of intuitive ethics and virtues. Graham et al. (2013) consolidate.

The tweets were annotated by multiple annotators with the MFT taxonomy. Hoover et al. (2020) provide additional details on the annotation process. They recognize that the vice and the virtue constituting one moral foundation are expressed differently in natural language. For example, an utterance describing a care concern (e.g., taking care of one's offspring) does not necessarily also contain harm expressions. For this reason, each tweet was annotated with all 10 individual moral values plus an additional nonmoral label, resulting in 11 possible labels per tweet. Due to the subjective nature of moral values, different annotators may label the same tweet differently. For this reason, Hoover et al. (2020) apply a majority vote to select the definitive label(s) of each tweet. Tweets with no majority label are labeled as nonmoral. Table 2 shows three examples of annotated tweets. Table 3 shows the distribution of labels. The MeanIR is a measure of imbalance in a dataset (Charte et al., 2015). MeanIR is the mean of IR l for each label l, where IR l is the ratio of the number of instances having the majority (i.e., nonmoral) label and the number of instances having label l. The degree of imbalance varies largely across datasets, which is realistic since different domains are likely to have different distributions of moral content. We follow prior work in modeling moral language by treating each label as a binary dimension in a multi-label setup. This formulation supports cross-domain evaluation by enabling per-foundation metrics and aggregate comparisons.

Hazarika et al. (2020) described modality-invariant and private subspaces for fusion. Wu et al. (2021) studied curriculum strategies for multimodal encoders. Li et al. document: Multimodal sentiment analysis has attracted sustained attention as applications demand robust understanding of affect across speech, vision, and language. This section synthesizes progress along alignment, end-to-end optimization, and supervision. Cross-modal alignment has emerged as a central driver of effective fusion. Early pipelines emphasized holistic features that captured global dynamics but missed fine-grained cues. To address granularity, researchers aligned acoustic and visual streams to word tokens using forced alignment to obtain word-level features. A representative architecture is GME-LSTM(A), which combines gated multimodal embedding with temporally attentive LSTMs for fusion. Yet the reliance on forced alignment increases preprocessing cost and brittleness to transcription errors. MulT replaces explicit alignment with cross-modal attention that learns correspondences implicitly. Beyond temporal matching, semantic alignment reconciles heterogeneous modality-specific representations. MISA maps modality-specific embeddings into a shared space that disentangles private and shared semantics before fusion. A cross-modal prediction objective further separates shared and private signals for non-text modalities relative to text. These studies collectively indicate that alignment, whether temporal or semantic, strengthens multimodal modeling. A second trend is end-to-end training that jointly optimizes unimodal encoders and multimodal fusion. Traditional two-stage pipelines freeze unimodal features prior to fusion, limiting adaptation to task supervision. Zhang et al. (2019) presented a factorized multimodal transformer for sentiment benchmarks. Tsai et al. (2019) introduced cross-modal attention mechanisms evaluated on MOSI and MOSEI. Hazarika et al. (2020) described modality-invariant and private subspaces for fusion. Wu et al. (2021) studied curriculum strategies for multimodal encoders. Li et al. (2022) examined robustness to missing modalities on synthetic ablations. Sun et al. (2020) analyzed bias in evaluations. Experiments on emotion benchmarks report gains over pipeline baselines when gradients flow through the entire stack. A third direction leverages unimodal sentiment labels to learn stronger encoders for fusion. Yu et al. introduced CH-SIMS with both multimodal and unimodal labels to supervise a multi-task framework. Self-MM infers pseudo unimodal labels from multimodal annotations and trains with joint objectives when gold labels are unavailable. These approaches highlight the value of unimodal supervision for disentangling modality contributions. Despite these advances, robustness to noisy inputs remains underexplored in realistic deployment. Gated Multimodal Embedding filters unreliable acoustic and visual signals during fusion, and MCTN copes with missing nonverbal streams by cross-modal translation. Mittal et al. emphasized noise handling for audio and vision using word-aligned features derived from gold transcripts, while Dumpala et al. treated ASR outputs as textual inputs without analyzing error propagation. We benchmark strong MSA models on real-world ASR transcripts and observe substantial drops driven by polarity-flipping substitutions on sentiment-bearing words. Building on this analysis, we propose a sentiment-word-aware multimodal refinement model that trains and tests exclusively on ASR text without gold transcripts.

spans: Smith et al. (2020) introduced a stance-aware rumor classifier for microblogs using hierarchical attention and weak supervision. Zhou and Zafarani (2020) surveyed fake news detection pipelines across content features and social context. Kiela et al. (2020) released the Hateful Memes benchmark combining images and text for multimodal classification, and Shrestha et al. (2022) studied cross-modal retrieval robustness. Qian et al. document: There exist a number of large-scale Twitter datasets concentrated on topics such as COVID-19 (Banda et al., 2021) or Climate Change (Littman and Wrubel, 2019). However, it remains difficult to collect labeled misinformation. Researchers have collected COVID-19 misconceptions on social media via manual annotation (Hossain et al., 2020) or by linking to fact checking articles (Patwa et al., 2021). Not only are these datasets small (a few thousand samples), but they focus on false claims rather than multimodal inconsistency. In the news domain, Luo et al. (2021) automatically generate out-of-context images. Here, we curate social media posts that are topical and multimodal, and demonstrate an application to misinformation detection of human-generated fakes. Prior work on image repurposing detection aligns visual content with textual claims to flag mismatches between captions and images (Tan et al., 2020). Complementary approaches examine provenance signals such as EXIF metadata, repost trajectories, or reverse image search to contextualize visual claims (Boididou et al., 2018; Nakas et al., 2019), and others query external knowledge bases for multimodal verification (Jaiswal et al., 2017; Müller-Budack et al., 2020). Vision-language models pretrained with contrastive objectives have shown strong zero-shot alignment and are natural candidates for assessing cross-modal consistency (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). Despite these advances, few datasets combine organic social posts with verified labels about whether the attached media is contextually appropriate to the text. Smith et al. (2020) introduced a stance-aware rumor classifier for microblogs using hierarchical attention and weak supervision. Zhou and Zafarani (2020) surveyed fake news detection pipelines across content features and social context. Kiela et al. (2020) released the Hateful Memes benchmark combining images and text for multimodal classification, and Shrestha et al. (2022) studied cross-modal retrieval robustness. Qian et al. (2021) analyzed meme propagation patterns in political online communities. Our work complements these lines by emphasizing naturally occurring pairings of text and imagery where semantic alignment is ambiguous yet verifiable through external context. We focus on posts where the image is genuine but reused to support an unrelated narrative, a common manipulation in fast-moving news cycles. Compared with template-based generation of out-of-context images, our collection captures the linguistic and visual variability introduced by human authors. To model cross-modal inconsistency, we adapt a pretrained contrastive encoder and introduce claim-aware pooling to summarize each post. We further incorporate lightweight metadata such as timestamps and account history to disambiguate repeated content without relying on a fixed knowledge base. For evaluation, we assemble a stratified benchmark with disjoint topics and time splits to prevent lexical memorization. We report results across precision–recall operating points and analyze error modes related to polysemy, sarcasm, and event evolution. The resulting resource and models provide a testbed for studying robustness in multimodal misinformation detection.

spans: Event-specific audits described travel, accommodation, and venue operations in detail, reporting large per-capita footprints and highlighting inventory choices that shift totals (Astudillo and AzariJafari, 2018; Neugebauer et al., 2020). Discipline-level studies aggregated emissions over multi-year periods and across institutions, presenting contrasting system boundaries and sample designs without resolving comparability questions (Waring et al., 2014; Poom et al., 2017). Lab-level accounts reported disproportionate travel shares relative to commuting and building energy, detailing internal audits, staff surveys, and itinerary reconstructions, with inconsistent baselines and aggregation rules that complicate comparisons across sites and years (Mariette et al., 2021). Virtual delivery highlighted fees and energy use, with separate observations on distance-related citation patterns and remote interaction formats (Mubin et al., 2021; Raby and Madden, 2021; Ong et al., 2014; Chalvatzis and Ormosi, 2020).
document: Conferences impose substantial environmental costs, with air travel dominating emissions, prompting calls for rethinking event formats and reporting practices (Ciers et al., 2019; Pierce et al., 2020; Johnson et al., 2020). Event-specific audits described travel, accommodation, and venue operations in detail, reporting large per-capita footprints and highlighting inventory choices that shift totals (Astudillo and AzariJafari, 2018; Neugebauer et al., 2020). Discipline-level studies aggregated emissions over multi-year periods and across institutions, presenting contrasting system boundaries and sample designs without resolving comparability questions (Waring et al., 2014; Poom et al., 2017). Lab-level accounts reported disproportionate travel shares relative to commuting and building energy, detailing internal audits, staff surveys, and itinerary reconstructions, with inconsistent baselines and aggregation rules that complicate comparisons across sites and years (Mariette et al., 2021). Virtual delivery highlighted fees and energy use, with separate observations on distance-related citation patterns and remote interaction formats (Mubin et al., 2021; Raby and Madden, 2021; Ong et al., 2014; Chalvatzis and Ormosi, 2020). In parallel, ML and NLP communities are documenting and reducing training-related emissions through standardized reporting, efficiency benchmarking, transfer learning, and smaller architectures that maintain adequate performance (Schwartz et al., 2019; Henderson et al., 2020; García-Martín et al., 2019; Sanh et al., 2019; Kocmi and Bojar, 2020).

document: Monolingual DS-RE: Early works in DS-RE build probabilistic graphical models for the task (e.g., Hoffmann et al., 2011; Ritter et al., 2013). Most later works follow the multi-instance multilabel learning framework (Surdeanu et al., 2012) in which there are multiple labels associated with a bag and the model is trained with an at-least-one assumption. Most neural models for the task encode each sentence separately, for example using Piecewise CNNs (Zeng et al., 2015). Attention-based aggregation at the bag level became standard in selective attention models to reduce label noise (Lin et al., 2016). To evaluate DS-RE, at test time, the model makes a prediction for an unseen bag. Unfortunately, the most popular DS-RE dataset, NYT-10d, has a noisy test set because it is automatically annotated (Riedel et al., 2010). Recently, Gao et al. (2021) released NYT-10m and Wiki-20m with manually annotated test sets. We use all three datasets in our work. Brown et al. (2020) presented large-scale language models that can perform few-shot learning across diverse NLP tasks. Gao et al. (2021) explored prompt-based retrieval for knowledge-intensive language tasks using unsupervised patterns. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed pretrain–prompt–predict paradigms and documented gains on classification and generation benchmarks. Yao et al. (2020) proposed a graph-based retrieval architecture that integrates entity linking with neural reasoning over heterogeneous sources. Lee et al. (2019) introduced reinforcement learning for document-level information extraction with a reward shaped by downstream QA accuracy. Petroni et al. (2019) showed masked language models encode knowledge that can be probed without supervision. Multilingual DS-RE: A bilingual DS-RE model named MNRE introduced cross-lingual attention in language-specific CNN encoders and was evaluated on English and Mandarin (Lin et al., 2017). Pretrained multilingual encoders such as mBERT and XLM-R improved cross-lingual generalization when combined with DS-RE architectures (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Recently, Bhartiya et al. (2021) released DiS-ReX covering English, Spanish, German, and French with distant labels aligned across languages. We compare mPARE against the state of the art on DiS-ReX, which combines the MNRE architecture with an mBERT encoder. Beyond supervised transfer, zero-shot cross-lingual DS-RE has been studied through aligned entity embeddings and shared relation schemas (Ni et al., 2019). Data selection and curriculum strategies for DS-RE reduce noise by reweighting instances using confidence scores or teacher models (Jia et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2019). Contrastive objectives at the sentence and bag levels further stabilize training under sparse supervision (Peng et al., 2020). See Appendix E for details on models, hyperparameters, and datasets.

Banerjee and Lavie (2005) presented METEOR with stemming and synonym matching to improve adequacy sensitivity. Snover et al. document: In this section, we briefly introduce the three directions of translation evaluation. Research on translation evaluation traditionally spans reference-based automatic metrics, reference-free quality estimation, and human assessment. Reference-based metrics compare system outputs to one or more human references using heuristics or learned models. Classical n-gram overlap measures provided early baselines for system comparison across languages and domains. Embedding-based and learned metrics aim to better correlate with human judgments by exploiting pretrained language models. Human evaluation continues to be the gold standard, with recent protocols improving consistency and diagnostic power. Benchmarking practices and shared tasks have standardized the evaluation landscape in both academic and industrial settings. Reference-based metrics such as BLEU and METEOR remain widely reported despite known limitations in sensitivity to meaning preservation. Advances in neural metrics demonstrate superior sentence-level correlation on diverse datasets and error types. Papineni et al. (2002) introduced BLEU to quantify n-gram precision with a brevity penalty. Banerjee and Lavie (2005) presented METEOR with stemming and synonym matching to improve adequacy sensitivity. Snover et al. (2006) designed TER as an edit distance over operations aligning hypothesis and reference. Post (2018) refined chrF with smoothing for character-based evaluation metrics. More recent neural metrics learn semantic alignment from large-scale human ratings to deliver fine-grained sensitivity. BERTScore computes token alignments in embedding space and has shown improved recall for paraphrases. BLEURT leverages synthetic noise and human ratings to calibrate a pretrained encoder for absolute quality prediction. COMET and its variants integrate multilingual encoders and task-specific regressors to optimize correlation across languages. Multi-reference settings mitigate reference sparsity by offering diverse phrasing and coverage of valid translations. Nevertheless, cost and availability of high-quality references motivate exploration of reference-free approaches. Quality estimation predicts system output quality without references by modeling uncertainty, fluency, and source-target adequacy. Early QE relied on engineered features and shallow learners trained on sentence-level labels. Neural QE systems employ pretrained encoders that jointly condition on the source and hypothesis to infer errors and confidence. Direct assessment protocols collect continuous adequacy ratings from crowd workers and experts to anchor model development. Multidimensional Quality Metrics enables fine-grained annotation of error categories that inform metric diagnostics and training data. Combining human signals with automatic scores supports better system selection and risk-aware deployment in production pipelines. Shared tasks such as WMT have provided corpora, baselines, and rigorous comparisons that drive progress on evaluation. Cross-lingual robustness, domain shift, and out-of-distribution behavior remain central challenges for trustworthy evaluation. Recent studies emphasize calibration, uncertainty quantification, and explainability for metric outputs used in decision making. Our work situates within this landscape by unifying reference-based, reference-free, and human-centered signals for consistent reporting. We aim to clarify trade-offs and propose practical guidelines for selecting metrics under data, cost, and risk constraints.

(2020) unified supervised and unsupervised objectives under a sequence-to-sequence framework, Clark et al. document: Pre-trained model analysis is a rapidly growing area in NLP today, and there are a number of methods for analyzing internal representations of a model, including structured head and FCN pruning (Michel et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019; Prasanna et al., 2020), residual connection and layer normalization analysis (Kovaleva et al., 2021; Kobayashi et al., 2021), and analyzing attention patterns (Clark et al., 2019; Kovaleva et al., 2019). Compared to these methods, probing tasks (Conneau et al., 2018; Tenney et al., 2019) provide a more direct way to evaluate what a model can and cannot accomplish. While it is possible to probe embeddings or hidden representations directly (Tenney et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019a), the adoption of pre-trained language models has made it possible to evaluate such models by framing probing tasks close to the original model objective (Radford et al., 2019; Talmor et al., 2019; Ettinger, 2020; Goldberg, 2019). However, when a research area moves this quickly, it can be hard to keep up with many new models. Gururangan et al. (2020) explored domain- and task-adaptive pretraining for transferring to downstream tasks, Raffel et al. (2020) unified supervised and unsupervised objectives under a sequence-to-sequence framework, Clark et al. (2020) proposed ELECTRA with replaced token detection for sample efficiency, and Brown et al. (2020) presented large-scale autoregressive models demonstrating few-shot capabilities across benchmarks. Lewis et al. (2020) introduced retrieval-augmented generation combining parametric encoders with nonparametric memory, He et al. (2021) examined efficiency and scaling behavior for contrastive learning, Xue et al. (2021) extended multilingual pretraining to sequences with sparse attention schemes, and Tay et al. (2022) surveyed transformer advances. Most of the existing research papers (Garí Soler and Apidianaki, 2020; Zagoury et al., 2021; Kassner et al., 2020) compare only one or two model families. In contrast to existing work, we perform a large-scale probing of 28 models across 8 different model families. We apply the existing probing benchmarks, namely, oLMpics (Talmor et al., 2019) and psycholinguistic datasets (Ettinger, 2020), to models that differ in the pre-training objective, datasets, size, architecture, and directionality.

document: Encoder–decoder models are widely used for open-domain dialogue response generation. Despite their fluency, they often produce short, generic replies that fail to engage users. Early work mitigated blandness with objective-level diversity rewards and mutual information reranking (Li et al., 2016a; Li et al., 2016b). Decoding strategies such as nucleus sampling and constrained beam search further diversified outputs without retraining (Holtzman et al., 2020; Post and Vilar, 2018). Content-augmentation methods inject retrieved facts or persona traits to ground responses in external information (Zhang et al., 2018; Dinan et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019). Knowledge-aware models also leverage structured resources like ConceptNet and domain ontologies to enrich context representations (Liu et al., 2018; Moon et al., 2019). Another line of research frames dialogue as a one-to-many mapping and introduces latent variables to capture discourse-level variation (Serban et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018). Variational encoder–decoder architectures sample intent or topic codes, enabling multiple plausible continuations from the same context. Recent pretraining objectives align latent spaces with large conversational corpora to stabilize training and increase controllability (Bao et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2020). Retrieval-augmented generation integrates nonparametric memories to balance specificity and faithfulness in long-form answers (Lewis et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020). Smith et al. (2020) introduced dual-encoder persona grounding for multi-turn conversation with human-in-the-loop evaluation. Roller et al. (2021) scaled transformer dialogue agents with blended skills to large parameter counts and open-domain evaluations. Gao et al. (2021) examined prompt-based tuning for few-shot text generation across tasks and metrics. Adiwardana et al. (2020) reported gains with Sensibleness and Specificity. Despite these advances, controllable diversity remains challenging when conditioning on long, knowledge-rich contexts. We address this by combining latent-variable pretraining with retrieval-augmented fine-tuning over conversational knowledge sources. Our approach ties the latent code to evidence spans, enabling content-aware sampling while preserving factual grounding. We further regularize the posterior with contrastive objectives to separate style and content dimensions. In automatic evaluation, the method improves distinct-n and factuality while keeping perplexity comparable to strong baselines. Human studies confirm better specificity and engagement without increased contradiction rates. An ablation shows that removing retrieval supervision collapses the latent space toward generic replies. We release code, pretrained checkpoints, and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducibility and future benchmarking.

document: We study document understanding for long financial and contractual documents, integrating aspect detection, outline generation, and question generation to support non-linear navigation and concise consumption. Keyword detection highlights salient terms (Liu et al., 2009). Topic models structure themes (Blei et al., 2003). Candidate ranking automatically estimates importance in long reports (Wu et al., 2005; Gollapalli and Caragea, 2014). Building on these components, we extend unsupervised aspect detection with TF-IDF weighting for long 10-K filings, and train a BART-based question generator with reinforcement-learning rewards using ELI5 supervision and evaluate cross-domain performance on legal and economics queries.

However, in order to apply the parsing algorithm of Dozat and Manning (2018), both of these approaches have to rely on a lossy conversion to bi-lexical dependencies with ad-hoc internal head choices for the nodes of the abstract sentiment graph. This lossy behaviour is caused by nested text spans in the sentiment graphs, as illustrated by Figure 1, which are ambiguous in their bi-lexical dependency encoding (see Section A in the Appendix). More generally, decoding structured graph information from text has sparked a lot of interest in recent years, especially for parsing meaning representation graphs (Oepen et al., 2020). Large-scale pretraining has reshaped NLP benchmarks, with widely adopted models and objectives reported across studies (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020). Data augmentation with back-translation is prevalent in practice to enrich training distributions and stabilize optimization on varied corpora (Sennrich et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020; Wei and Zou, 2019). There has been tremendous progress in developing complex transition-based and graph-based parsers (Hershcovich et al., 2017; McDonald and Pereira, 2006; Dozat and Manning, 2018), and in this paper, we adopt PERIN (Samuel and Straka, 2020), a state-of-the-art graph-based parser capable of modeling a superset of graph features needed for our task.

document: We now introduce the task of entity set expansion (ESE), existing paradigms and evaluation methods. ESE seeks to grow a concept-specific set given a handful of seed entities by retrieving candidates that share the intended semantic category while avoiding topical drift. Typical systems combine corpus-derived features with seed-guided scoring to rank candidates, and they are deployed in applications from knowledge base completion to lexicon induction for domain-specific NLP. Turney (2001) exploited pattern-based expansion using lexico-syntactic templates over the web to harvest semantically similar terms. Pantel et al. (2009) proposed Espresso-style bootstrapping, Rong et al. (2016) trained embedding models over large corpora for query-by-example expansion, and Shen et al. (2017) introduced SetExpan for interactive expansion on enterprise text, using minimal seeds in noisy settings. Methods fall into pattern-based bootstrapping, distributional similarity, and neural set encoders that model element–set compatibility under constraints such as coverage and consistency. Supervision ranges from unsupervised scoring to weakly supervised ranking with pairwise losses, and recent work emphasizes transparency, drift control, and interactive feedback. Evaluation has used category-labeled corpora like SemEval taxonomy tracks and web lists, with metrics such as mean average precision and precision at k reported across heterogeneous entity types and graded relevance judgments from expert annotators. Elghafari et al. (2016) compiled product categories from forums, Vulic and Mrksic (2018) induced cross-lingual lexicons for expansion, Gupta et al. document: Many recent works on paraphrase generation aim for high-quality paraphrases while balancing diversity and fidelity. Broadly, methods can be categorized into supervised and unsupervised approaches. In supervised settings, diverse decoding strategies encourage variation by penalizing overlap in beams or enforcing dissimilarity among candidates (Vijayakumar et al., 2018). Other studies increase output variety by perturbing latent representations or training multiple generators that specialize in distinct modes (Gupta et al., 2018; Park et al., 2019; Qian et al., 2019). Despite these advances, such mechanisms rarely provide interpretable controls over lexical, syntactic, and semantic dimensions simultaneously. Controlled paraphrase generation introduces explicit levers for targeted variation, yielding models that can elicit lexical or syntactic diversity on demand (Zeng et al., 2019; Thompson and Post, 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). Bao et al. (2019) propose guiding outputs with exemplars drawn from a corpus. Iyyer et al. (2018) use constituency trees to shape paraphrase structure, and Li and Choi (2020) describe controls over syntactic templates. Zeng et al. (2019) specify keywords to manage lexical emphasis during decoding for controlled lexical edits only. We introduce a simple model that jointly controls lexical cues, target syntactic structures, and semantic similarity to steer generation. Exemplar-guided methods condition on a retrieved sentence that supplies a surface plan for rephrasing (Bao et al., 2019). Structure-driven methods specify constituency templates or parse trees to orchestrate reordering and substitution (Iyyer et al., 2018; Li and Choi, 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). In unsupervised regimes, sampling constraints and exploration heuristics yield diverse yet faithful variants without paired supervision (Niu et al., 2020). Optimization-based approaches search sentence space under quality objectives encompassing semantic preservation, fluency, and novelty (Liu et al., 2020). Reinforcement learning with entailment- and diversity-aware rewards further improves trade-offs, and we adopt similar metrics to evaluate supervised controlled generation (Garg et al., 2021; Siddique et al., 2020).

TI-MEDIAL provides dialogue question answering focused on temporal commonsense, and temporal ordering has also been framed as textual entailment (Qin et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Thukral et al., 2021). Tracking state changes in procedural text is presented as related to temporal ordering, and training language models on temporal knowledge is described as a direction worth pursuing for improved temporal generalization in practice (Dalvi et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020).

Acharya et al. (2021) surveyed workers in the United States and India about rituals such as birth, marriage, and funerals, eliciting typical times and durations for key activities. In ConceptNet, the English entry for breakfast highlights pancakes as a common food, and the Chinese entry mentions noodles (Speer et al., 2017). Hovy and Yang (2021) note that NLP rarely accounts for culture in benchmarks.
document: We study temporal and cultural commonsense in natural language understanding. Prior work on temporal reasoning spans extracting and normalizing time expressions as well as inferring event attributes like order, duration, and typical times (Strötgen and Gertz, 2010; Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008; Ning et al., 2018; Vashishtha et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019). Despite progress, many temporal relations remain implicit in text, which complicates modeling and evaluation (Davis and Marcus, 2015). To assess capabilities, researchers have created benchmarks across reading comprehension, dialogue, and entailment settings. The bAbI suite includes a task that asks models to reason about the order of time expressions in synthetic stories (Weston et al., 2015). TI-MEDIAL provides dialogue question answering focused on temporal commonsense, and temporal ordering has also been framed as textual entailment (Qin et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021; Thukral et al., 2021). Tracking state changes in procedural text is presented as related to temporal ordering, and training language models on temporal knowledge is described as a direction worth pursuing for improved temporal generalization in practice (Dalvi et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020). These resources collectively motivate models that can bridge implicit temporal cues with explicit predictions under limited supervision. Cultural commonsense adds a dimension of variation across communities and practices that shapes expectations about when and how events occur. Recent work has begun to chart these differences with targeted data collection and analysis in multilingual and cross-regional settings (Hovy and Yang, 2021). Acharya et al. (2021) surveyed workers in the United States and India about rituals such as birth, marriage, and funerals, eliciting typical times and durations for key activities. In ConceptNet, the English entry for breakfast highlights pancakes as a common food, and the Chinese entry mentions noodles (Speer et al., 2017). Hovy and Yang (2021) note that NLP rarely accounts for culture in benchmarks. Although language and culture are not in one-to-one correspondence, knowledge bases and text corpora can encode culturally specific defaults that models can leverage for temporal interpretation (Speer et al., 2017). Beyond temporal and cultural aspects, our study connects to language grounding and corpus-derived world knowledge, including physical affordances and quantitative attributes (Roy and Reiter, 2005; Carlson et al., 2010; Tandon et al., 2014; Forbes and Choi, 2017; Elazar et al., 2019).

spans: Neural QG with explicit answer cues improved alignment on reading comprehension datasets (Yuan et al., 2017; Narayan et al., 2020). Data selection prioritized question-worthy sentences using salience ranking and reinforcement learning (Li et al., 2019; Scialom et al., 2019), and visual question generation highlighted strong grounding across image–text pairs (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Shukla et al., 2019).
document: Question generation has progressed rapidly with the advent of large datasets and strong neural architectures. Early studies framed QG as sequence transduction from passages to questions using attention-based encoders and decoders. Subsequent work leveraged pretrained transformers to better exploit contextual semantics and long-range dependencies. Hybrid approaches further integrated syntactic cues and answer selection to improve faithfulness and answerability. In supervised settings, models often rely on answer spans annotated in reading comprehension corpora to constrain decoding. Unsupervised and low-resource regimes have explored back-translation and masked span prediction to reduce annotation cost. Content selection remains a core challenge for QG systems targeting multi-sentence inputs and knowledge-intensive passages. Researchers have proposed salience modeling to identify question-worthy facts and entities before generation. Discourse-aware models inject rhetorical structure or coreference features to avoid redundant or ambiguous prompts. Recent advances in controllable generation allow users to steer interrogatives, difficulty, and style with latent variables or prompts. Evaluation practices encompass automatic metrics, human judgments of fluency and relevance, and downstream utility in QA pipelines. End-to-end training often couples QG with question answering to enforce mutual constraints and calibrate uncertainty. In educational technology, QG supports practice item creation, feedback, and adaptive assessment across subjects and proficiency levels. In dialogue, question generation aids proactive assistants that seek clarifications and elicit preferences during task completion. Multimodal QG conditions on images, tables, or charts to craft information-seeking questions grounded outside plain text. Domain-specific QG has emerged for biomedical literature, legal documents, and software engineering artifacts with tailored vocabularies. Cross-lingual transfer leverages multilingual encoders to bootstrap QG in languages with limited labeled data. Robustness studies probe how models handle paraphrases, noisy inputs, and adversarial perturbations in realistic deployments. Our work revisits controllable QG with an emphasis on transparent content selection and verifiable grounding in source passages. We analyze architectural choices that balance coverage and specificity while preventing hallucinated premises and unanswerable queries. We also curate a diagnostic suite that isolates discourse coherence, factual alignment, and calibration under domain shift. For background, we summarize representative strands of research across neural architectures, selection strategies, and control signals. Neural QG with explicit answer cues improved alignment on reading comprehension datasets (Yuan et al., 2017; Narayan et al., 2020). Data selection prioritized question-worthy sentences using salience ranking and reinforcement learning (Li et al., 2019; Scialom et al., 2019), and visual question generation highlighted strong grounding across image–text pairs (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Shukla et al., 2019). We benchmark candidate designs under uniform preprocessing and training budgets to ensure fair comparison and replicability. Finally, we discuss limitations and ethical implications related to dataset bias, over-questioning, and user autonomy.
