spans: Col-BERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) reduces token embedding dimensions with a linear layer, and PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020) reports substantial storage savings using an auto-encoder, float16, and quantization. Dense Passage Retrieval (Karpukhin et al., 2020) and ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021) target dual-encoder retrieval, and the Transformer-Kernel (Hofstätter et al., 2020b) adopts a compact interaction scheme.
document:
Late-interaction models. The idea of running several transformer layers for the document and the query independently, and then combining them in the last transformer layers, was developed concurrently by multiple teams: PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020), EARL (Gao et al., 2020a), DC-BERT (Nie et al., 2020), DiPair , and the Deformer (Cao et al., 2020). These works show that only a few layers where the query and document interact are sufficient to achieve results close to the performance of a full BERT ranker at a fraction of the runtime cost. For each document, the contextual token vectors are stored in a cache and retrieved during the document ranking phase. This impacts both storage cost as well as latency cost of fetching these vectors during the ranking phase. MORES (Gao et al., 2020b), extends lateinteraction models, where in the last interaction layers only the query attends to the document (and not vice-versa). As document are typically much longer, this results in additional performance improvements with similar storage requirements. Col-BERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) is another variant that runs all transformer layers independently for the query and the document, and the interaction between the final vectors is done through a sumof-max operator. A similar work, the Transformer-Kernel (TK) (Hofstätter et al., 2020b), has an interaction block based on a low-complexity kernel operation. Both ColBERT and TK result in models with lower runtime latency at the expense of a drop in ranking quality. However, the storage requirements for both approaches are still significant. Col-BERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) reduces token embedding dimensions with a linear layer, and PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020) reports substantial storage savings using an auto-encoder, float16, and quantization. Dense Passage Retrieval (Karpukhin et al., 2020) and ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021) target dual-encoder retrieval, and the Transformer-Kernel (Hofstätter et al., 2020b) adopts a compact interaction scheme. However, even moderate compression ratios caused a large drop in ranking quality. In the PreTTR model (MacAvaney et al., 2020), it was proposed to address the storage cost by using a standard auto-encoder architecture and the float16 format instead of float32. Again, the ranking quality drops even with moderate compression ratios (they measured up to 12x).

Several other works (Guu et al., 2020;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Xiong et al., 2021;Qu et al., 2020;Lu et al., 2020) proposed representing the queries and documents as vectors (as opposed to a vector per token), and using dot product as the interaction block. While this ranker architecture approach is simple (and can also be used for the retrieval step via an approximate nearest neighbor search such as FAISS (Johnson et al., 2017) or ScaNN (Guo et al., 2020)), the overall ranking quality is generally lower compared to methods that employ a query-document cross-attention interaction.

Compressed embeddings. Our work reduces storage requirements by reducing the number of bits per floating-point value. Quantization gained attention and success in reducing the size of neural network parameters (Gupta et al., 2015;Essam et al., 2017;Wang et al., 2018;Wu et al., 2018) and distributed learning communication costs (Suresh et al., 2017;Alistarh et al., 2017;Konečnỳ and Richtárik, 2018;Vargaftik et al., 2021b). Specifically, compressing word embeddings has been studied as an independent goal. May et al. (2019) studied the effect of quantized word embeddings on downstream applications and proposed a metric for quantifying this effect with simple linear models that operate on the word embeddings directly. As our work is concerned with compressing contextual embeddings, these methods do not apply since the set of possible embeddings values is not bounded by the vocabulary size. Nevertheless, as in (May et al., 2019), we also observe that simple quantization schemes are quite effective. Our work uses recent advances in this area to further reduce storage requirements for document representation, which, to the best of our knowledge, were not previously attempted in this context.

document: Patient-Specific Literature Retrieval. Since 2014, the Text REtrieval Conference (TREC) has organized a series of challenges to advance clinical literature retrieval, with the Clinical Decision Support tracks evaluating systems for retrieving biomedical articles relevant to generic questions about patient records (Simpson et al., 2014; Roberts et al., 2015; Roberts et al., 2016). Ueda et al. (2021) study contextualized representations for structured biomedical retrieval without clinical notes. Voorhees et al. (2021) describe the evaluation protocols used for medical search tasks and resources. Boag et al. (2018) analyze clinical notes for outcome signals using neural encoders, and Jin et al. (2018) explore representation learning for noisy EHR narratives, while van Aken et al. (2021) evaluate large pretrained language models on intensive care outcomes. Johnson et al. (2016) release MIMIC publicly. TREC CDS 2014 and 2015 used short case reports as idealized medical records, while TREC 2016 shifted to real-world records from the MIMIC database. We explore large pretrained language models for retrieval based on EHR text and assess their utility for outcome-aware patient-specific literature augmentation. Finally, we describe a two-stage pipeline that retrieves abstracts from PubMed and fuses them with admission notes to predict outcomes, providing a practical bridge from retrieval to clinical decision support.

document: Our work depends heavily on recent advances in efficient Transformers (Tay et al., 2020) that process long sequences (Rae et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021). Sparse attention, relative position encoding (Shaw et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2021) and other tricks (Dai et al., 2019; Weston et al., 2015; Shen et al., 2020; Stock et al., 2021; Katharopoulos et al., 2020) are commonly adopted by efficient Transformers. Existing long-sequence downstream tasks include long-form QA (Fan et al., 2019; Pang et al., 2021), document-level summarization (Kryściński et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021), and machine translation (Liu and Zhang, 2020). More recently, Shaham et al. (2022) introduce a new benchmark covering multiple domains and tasks, while Tay et al. (2021) propose multimodal long sequence tasks. These developments reveal a common theme of balancing expressivity and memory footprint at scale. Local-window attention restricts quadratic complexity while preserving inductive biases for nearby interactions (Child et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020). Learned sparsity patterns and hashing-based routing extend this idea to capture non-local signals with subquadratic cost (Kitaev et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021). Hierarchical encoders and segment recurrence further extend the effective context length without fully materializing token-to-token scores (Dai et al., 2019; Rae et al., 2020). Li et al. (2020) introduce a memory-compressed transformer for clinical narratives with chunked pooling layers and report improved entity tracking. Wu and Chen (2021) study dilated attention patterns for source code modeling using masked language objectives across multi-file repositories. Xu et al. (2022) analyze cache refresh in streaming speech recognition and observe stable perplexity with sparse globals. A complementary direction integrates retrieval to populate attention with evidence retrieved on the fly, enabling linear-time processing with richer context (Borgeaud et al., 2022; Lewis et al., 2020). Evaluation protocols for long-context models emphasize both exactness on localized spans and fidelity over extended narratives, motivating datasets that stress recall of dispersed cues (Pang et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021). In addition, positional representations remain a critical factor affecting generalization to longer sequences at inference time (Press et al., 2022; Su et al., 2021). Rotary and relative schemes mitigate extrapolation failures by decoupling content and position while maintaining compositional phase properties (Su et al., 2021; Shaw et al., 2018). Our study builds on these insights to design an encoder with mixed-granularity windows, global tokens, and compressed memory tailored to document-level reasoning. We prioritize simple primitives that interoperate with commodity accelerators, avoiding specialized kernels that hinder deployment. By situating our contributions within the above landscape, we aim to provide a practical recipe for long-context modeling that advances accuracy without sacrificing throughput.

spans: A few works explore retrieval-augmented inference for vision-language models, including Flamingo (Alayrac et al., 2022) and BLIP (Li et al., 2022). LiT scales contrastive pretraining for locked-image tuning with text-only updates (Zhai et al., 2022). ALIGN studies noisy web data for dual-encoder training at scale (Jia et al., 2021). VLKD examines knowledge distillation across modalities for label efficiency (Tang et al., 2021) in contexts.

Recent research on instruction-following has emphasized cross-task generalization (Sanh et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). T0 and FLAN study scaling instruction tuning across NLP datasets with templates (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Visual instruction tuning adapts LLaVA and MiniGPT-4 to image-grounded dialogue using synthetic pairs (Liu et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023). Prompt ensembling investigates template mixtures for zero-shot transfer in classification and retrieval (Gao et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022).
document:
Vision-language few-shot learning. Recently, several few-shot learners on vision-language tasks were proposed including GPT (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020), Frozen (Tsimpoukelli et al., 2021), PICa, and SimVLM. Frozen (Tsimpoukelli et al., 2021) is a large language model based on GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and is transformed into a multimodal few-shot learner by extending the soft prompting to incorporate a set of images and text. Their approach shows the few-shot capability on visual question answering and image classification tasks. Similarly, PICa uses GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) to solve VQA tasks in a few-shot manner by providing a few in-context VQA examples. It converts images into textual descriptions so that GPT-3 can understand the images. SimVLM is trained with prefix language modeling on weakly-supervised datasets. It demonstrates its effectiveness on a zero-shot captioning task. A few works explore retrieval-augmented inference for vision-language models, including Flamingo (Alayrac et al., 2022) and BLIP (Li et al., 2022). LiT scales contrastive pretraining for locked-image tuning with text-only updates (Zhai et al., 2022). ALIGN studies noisy web data for dual-encoder training at scale (Jia et al., 2021). VLKD examines knowledge distillation across modalities for label efficiency (Tang et al., 2021) in contexts. While these models achieve improvement on few-shot tasks, they are impractical to use in real-world applications due to their model sizes.

Language model prompting. Providing prompts or task descriptions plays a vital role in improving pre-trained language models in many tasks (Radford et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b; Brown et al., 2020). Among them, GPT models (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020) achieved great success in prompting or task demonstrations in NLP tasks. In light of this direction, prompt-based approaches improve small pre-trained models in few-shot text classification tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b). CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) also explores prompt templates for image classification which affect zero-shot performance. Recent research on instruction-following has emphasized cross-task generalization (Sanh et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). T0 and FLAN study scaling instruction tuning across NLP datasets with templates (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Visual instruction tuning adapts LLaVA and MiniGPT-4 to image-grounded dialogue using synthetic pairs (Liu et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023). Prompt ensembling investigates template mixtures for zero-shot transfer in classification and retrieval (Gao et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022). We follow these core ideas so we aim to improve zero-shot and few-shot performance using prompts in vision-language tasks. We pretrain FEWVLM with masked language modeling (MaskedLM) and prefix language modeling (PrefixLM). Our experiments evaluate transfer to VQA, retrieval, and captioning under limited supervision. We also analyze calibration and prompt sensitivity to understand failure modes and inform design choices.

document: Early approaches to OTD relied primarily upon dictionaries like Hatebase to look up offensive words and phrases. The creation of OTD datasets enabled the development of ML-based approaches utilizing simple features such as bag-of-words representations. With the rise of social media platforms, many resources have been developed for identifying toxic comments in web text (Waseem and Hovy, 2016), including deep learning methods (Pitsilis et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b; Casula et al., 2020; Yasaswini et al., 2021; Djandji et al., 2020). Notably, most of these systems learn contextualized sentence representations, either end-to-end or on top of pre-trained language models, before making a final classification decision. Additional advances leverage transfer learning with large-scale pretraining to capture nuanced pragmatic cues in moderation contexts (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018; Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). Schmidt and Wiegand (2017) provided a comprehensive survey of abusive language detection across platforms and taxonomies. Davidson et al. (2017) introduced a dataset to distinguish hate speech from offensive language, and Mozafari et al. (2019) fine-tuned deep models with class reweighting to handle label imbalance, and Founta et al. (2018) proposed large-scale annotation protocols and guidelines. Work on data augmentation explores paraphrasing, counterfactual rewrites, and toxic span masking to enhance robustness against lexical variation (Qian et al., 2019; Wang and Culotta, 2020; Xia et al., 2020). Recent research also emphasizes fairness and bias mitigation through debiasing objectives, counterfactual evaluations, and calibrated thresholds (Park et al., 2018; Sap et al., 2019), and our study synthesizes these directions by combining pretraining-adapted encoders with targeted augmentation and transparent error analysis to improve OTD performance across domains.

document: The literature on inducing approximately fair models from biased data is rapidly growing. Recent surveys map fundamental definitions, sources of bias, and mitigation strategies across application domains (Mehrabi et al., 2021). We rely on this body of work to ground our definitions of fairness and to choose representative algorithms for empirical comparison. Following a capability-centered perspective, we operationalize fairness as performance parity across groups and as equalized risk for protected populations (Hashimoto et al., 2018; Donini et al., 2018). Our evaluation focuses on algorithms that adjust training objectives to control worst-group error and group-conditional risk. Group Distributionally Robust Optimization targets the worst-performing subgroup by reweighting empirical risk with per-group importance weights estimated during training (Sagawa et al., 2020). Invariant Risk Minimization aims to learn predictors that are stable across environments with differing causal mechanisms (Arjovsky et al., 2020). Prior studies on hate speech classification introduced label noise characterizations and toxicity lexicons alongside demographic annotations (Koh et al., 2021; Davidson et al., 2017; Waseem and Hovy, 2016). These formulations highlight the tension between distributional robustness and subgroup utility in practical deployments. Beyond training-time reweighting, post-processing approaches adjust decision thresholds to equalize error rates across groups without modifying the classifier. Hardt et al. (2016) formalized equalized odds and demonstrated optimal thresholding procedures on calibrated scores. Calibrated equal opportunity further constrains benefits for disadvantaged groups by aligning true positive rates under predictive parity assumptions (Pleiss et al., 2017). Other lines of work focus on representation learning that obfuscates protected attributes while retaining task signal. Adversarial removal of sensitive information through gradient reversal reduces attribute leakage in embeddings (Zhang et al., 2018). Contrastive objectives have also been used to cluster semantically similar instances while regularizing group-level separation (Ravfogel et al., 2020). Causal perspectives attempt to separate spurious correlations from mechanisms that should generalize across environments. Counterfactual fairness requires predictions to be invariant under interventions on sensitive attributes in a structural model (Kusner et al., 2017). Data documentation practices describe dataset collection processes and biases through model cards and datasheets (Mitchell et al., 2019; Gebru et al., 2021). Large-scale pretraining on corpora added bias and toxicity benchmarks, and calibration under shift studied with reliability measures (Bender et al., 2021; Gehman et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2017). Our study complements these strands by examining fairness under shifts induced by domain mismatch and evolving demographic composition. We consider binary and multiclass classification tasks with naturally imbalanced groups and operational constraints on recall. Following prior work on subgroup risk, we report average accuracy, worst-group accuracy, and conditional expected risk by protected attribute. We additionally assess calibration within groups and between groups to capture disparities in probabilistic predictions. To mitigate capacity-driven overfitting, we adopt early stopping on a validation set stratified by sensitive attributes while keeping model selection blind to test groups. Hyperparameters for all compared methods are tuned using a shared grid to ensure comparable budget and search space. We evaluate across text classification, tabular health records, and vision benchmarks with established group labels. Datasets include Civil Comments, Adult, CelebA, and COMPAS, each providing metadata suitable for fairness analysis. We release code, configuration files, and preprocessed splits to facilitate reproduction and to enable downstream benchmarking. By consolidating design choices and reporting standardized metrics, the paper provides an actionable baseline for future algorithmic advances in fair learning.

Supplementary Training: Existing works (Phang et al., 2018;Liu et al., 2019) have observed that starting from the fine-tuned MNLI model results in a better performance than directly from the vanilla pre-trained models for RTE, STS, and MRPC tasks.

A series of work (SentenceBERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), BERT-flow (Li et al., 2020), Sim-CSE (Gao et al., 2021)) explored intermediate training to improve STS tasks. All of them applied pre-fine tuning on NLI datasets. More recently, EFL (Wang et al., 2021) proposed a task transformation paradigm, improving single sentence tasks with less labels using rich sentence-pair datasets.

Adapter Tuning: Adapter tuning has emerged as a novel parameter-efficient transfer learning paradigm (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2020b), in which adapter layers -small bottleneck layers -are inserted and trained between frozen pre-trained transformer layers. On the GLUE benchmark, adapters attain within 0.4% of the performance of full fine-tuning by only training 3.6% parameters per task. Compactor (Mahabadi et al., 2021) substitutes the down-projector and upprojector matrices by a sum of Kronecker products, reducing the parameters by a large margin while maintaining the overall performance. AutoPrompt learns discrete trigger tokens by gradient-guided search over the vocabulary for classification and relation extraction (Shin et al., 2020). Pattern-exploiting training reformulates tasks with cloze-style prompts and uses label words to supervise masked language models (Schick and Schütze, 2021). ADAPET augments PET with auxiliary calibration objectives to improve few-shot performance across several text classification benchmarks (Tam et al., 2021). Contextual calibration methods adjust verbalizer probabilities to mitigate bias in prompt-based predictions without modifying model parameters (Zhao et al., 2021). Prompting: Hand-crafted prompts were shown to be helpful to adapt generation in GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020). Existing works including LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) explored the prompt searching in a few-shot setting.

Recently, several researchers have proposed continuous prompts training to overcome the challenges in discrete prompt searching. Prefix tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) and P-tuningv2 (Liu et al., 2021a) prepend a sequence of trainable embeddings at each transformer layer and optimizes them. Two contemporaneous works -prompt tuning (Lester et al., 2021) and P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021b), interleave the training parameters in the input embedding layer instead of each transformer layer. All these methods focus on task-specific prompt optimization. Our proposed method, IDPG, is the first prompt generator that is not only taskspecific but also instance-specific. While prompt tuning reduces trainable parameters, it often struggles to generalize across domains without careful initialization. To address this, we frame prompt generation as a conditional sequence modeling problem conditioned on task definitions and instance metadata. IDPG learns to emit continuous prefix vectors and discrete verbalizer candidates that are composed on the fly during inference. The generator is trained with a mixture of supervised contrastive loss on development data and reinforcement learning signals from validation metrics. We evaluate on GLUE, SuperGLUE, and a suite of transfer settings with label-limited regimes. Across tasks, IDPG consistently outperforms fixed prompts and matches full fine-tuning with less than 1% of model parameters updated. Ablations indicate that instance-aware conditioning contributes the majority of gains on heterogeneous sentence-pair tasks. We release code, prompts, and trained checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

document: Sequence-to-sequence and pre-trained language models have become the foundation for neural dialogue generation, enabling stronger contextualization and fluency across multi-turn interactions. To reduce hallucination and support factuality, recent approaches ground responses in external sources by combining unstructured documents with structured knowledge graphs through retrieval, selection, and fusion modules. Young et al. (2018) integrated commonsense relations into open-domain dialogue and reported gains on automated and human metrics. Zhou et al. (2018a) leveraged ConceptNet paths for knowledge-aware response selection, Wu et al. (2020a) proposed ConKADI to select felicitous facts from documents, Ye et al. (2020) pretrained models with coreference signals for entity tracking, and Reddy et al. (2019) applied end-to-end memory networks to task-oriented dialogue in real-world dialogue systems. Building on this line, we propose CNTF, a multi-source knowledge-grounded model that unifies document evidence and commonsense or factual triples via multi-hop attention, achieving consistent improvements on Wizard of Wikipedia and CMU_DoG and providing interpretable pathways for how retrieved knowledge influences utterance understanding and response generation.

Peters and Westerståhl (2006) presented an encyclopedic treatment of generalized quantifiers and their variants. document: Generalized quantifiers (GQs) extend first-order logic by denoting relations between sets and underlie many aspects of natural language meaning (Mostowski, 1957; Barwise and Cooper, 1981). Capturing their semantics in NLP is crucial for reasoning over scope, monotonicity, and quantificational dependency in multilingual settings, yet remains difficult for current language models (Keenan and Paperno, 2012; Matthewson, 2004; Steinert-Threlkeld, 2019). Montague (1973) laid out an intensional framework for natural language semantics. Keenan and Westerståhl (1997) surveyed monotonicity, conservativity, and other quantifier properties. Peters and Westerståhl (2006) presented an encyclopedic treatment of generalized quantifiers and their variants. Szymanik (2016) investigated the computational complexity of quantifier verification tasks using finite models. Higginbotham and May (1981) discussed quantifier scope interactions in transformational syntax and their empirical consequences. In this work, we assemble a cross-linguistic categorization of quantifier types grounded in established taxonomies and evaluate pretrained models on targeted NLU benchmarks to diagnose representational gaps and error patterns (Keenan and Westerståhl, 1997; Peters and Westerståhl, 2006; Szymanik and Thorne, 2015).

document: Model uncertainty estimation and posterior calibration is a topic of continued interest not only in the fields of machine learning and statistics, but also in meteorology (Bröcker, 2009), fairness (Liu et al., 2019), healthcare (Jiang et al., 2012), reinforcement learning (Malik et al., 2019), natural language processing (Card and Smith, 2018), speech recognition (Yu et al., 2011) and economics (Gneiting et al., 2007). In probabilistic models, the principal goal of estimation of the posterior p(y|x) given a sample x ∈ X and a label y ∈ [K], is to assign low confidence to samples that were not explained well by the training data. One common way to calibrate multiclass posteriors after training the classifier f : X → R is to treat the problem as K one-vsall binary problems. In this case, model uncertainty is quantified by normalizing the estimation of p(y = k|f (x) k ) where f (x) k is the output score of the classifier for sample x and class k. Various binary calibration methods can be used to estimate the marginal posterior over a calibration dataset, ranging from parametric approaches (e.g. Platt scaling, temperature scaling, vector scaling (Platt et al., 1999;Guo et al., 2017)), to non-parametric methods (e.g. quantile or bayesian binning (Zadrozny and Elkan, 2001;Naeini et al., 2015), and isotonic regression (Zadrozny and Elkan, 2002). Another way to reduce the problem to binary calibration is by estimating model accuracy conditioned on its confidence, p(y = ŷ| max k∈[K] f (x) k ). Multiclass calibration aims to estimate the distribution of class labels conditioned on the estimated probability vector, p(y|f (x)). In this case the sample complexity is exponential in the number of classes and therefore with large number of classes, the main challenge is to constrain the hypothesis space with regularization. Some of the proposed methods for this purpose are matrix scaling and Dirichlet scaling which both use linear models for estimation of p(y = k|f (x)) (Guo et al., 2017;Kull et al., 2019), and MLP and order preserving functions (Rahimi et al., 2020a,b). Another approach is to account for model uncertainty via bayesian models. In Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) the predictive uncertainty will naturally be high in regions where training data is scarce (MacKay, 1992). However, the marginalization of the weights in BNN is intractable. Graves (2011) presented a variational objective for training stochastic neural networks, Lakshminarayanan et al. (2017) trained deep ensembles to obtain calibrated predictive distributions on image benchmarks, and Kendall and Gal (2017) separated aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty by introducing loss attenuation for dense prediction tasks. Hernández-Lobato and Adams (2015) used expectation propagation with probabilistic backpropagation to accelerate approximate inference in large networks for classification and regression across vision and speech benchmarks worldwide. In (Joo et al., 2020) the authors model the distribution on posterior probability using a Dirichlet prior distribution and variational inference. MCDropout is a variational approximation of Gaussian processes that avoids explicit modeling of the posterior distribution (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). Both of these methods require modification of training of the network. In NLP, posterior calibration for tasks with structured outputs is particularly challenging. This is because the number of classes is exponentially large and estimation of every posterior density or marginal posterior density is not possible. Desai and Durrett (2020) examined confidence estimates for question answering and machine translation using posterior tempering and label smoothing adjustments. Vaicenavicius et al. (2019) analyzed calibration metrics theoretically for multiclass predictors, and Guo et al. (2017) reported miscalibration in modern neural networks with temperature scaling on image tasks and provided open-source code for widespread evaluation. Estimation of the sequence calibration error and its correction is intractable, and to cope with this problem, approximate calibration methods using a set of interesting events and feature based calibration are proposed in (Kuleshov and Liang, 2015;Jagannatha and Yu, 2020) and an alternative calibration error estimator was proposed using sequence precision scoring function BLEU in (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019).

(2018) cast proposition-level SRL as span scoring with a deep architecture on PropBank. Dozat and Manning (2017) achieved strong results in dependency parsing with biaffine attention. Peters et al. document: We begin by a background on two span selection tasks considered in our work: coreference resolution and SRL. We describe some previous works on these tasks and contrast them to our work. Coreference resolution seeks to cluster mentions that refer to the same entity across a discourse. Semantic role labeling identifies predicate-argument structures that capture who did what to whom, when, and where. Both tasks can be framed as span selection over token sequences with task-specific constraints. Early systems for coreference relied on mention-pair classifiers with heuristic clustering algorithms. Feature-rich models incorporated syntactic cues, gender and number agreement, and discourse salience signals. Neural approaches replaced manual features with contextual encoders, attention, and span representations. Lee et al. (2017) introduced an end-to-end neural coreference system with span pruning and coarse-to-fine inference. He et al. (2018) cast proposition-level SRL as span scoring with a deep architecture on PropBank. Dozat and Manning (2017) achieved strong results in dependency parsing with biaffine attention. Peters et al. (2018) reported gains from deep contextualized embeddings across tasks, Joshi et al. (2020) optimized masked spans with SpanBERT to enhance coreference and SRL. While these advances improved accuracy, they also increased computational cost, motivating pruning and learned inference. For SRL, span-based formulations compete with token classification models by directly enumerating candidate argument spans. Recent work explores structured losses that reflect task-specific constraints such as non-overlap and role uniqueness. Pretraining has been especially beneficial when fine-tuned with span-sensitive objectives tailored to each task. In coreference, approximate inference with beam search and coarse antecedent scoring reduces quadratic costs while maintaining quality. In SRL, leveraging syntactic signals through latent trees or soft constraints can improve role consistency without full parses. Datasets also shape modeling choices by defining inventories of roles and the granularity of mentions. OntoNotes remains the standard benchmark for coreference, with diverse domains and gold speaker annotations. PropBank and CoNLL-2005/2012 provide span-level SRL supervision that enables thorough evaluation across predicates and domains. Our work builds on span pruning and joint scoring to address both mention detection and argument extraction within a unified framework. We revisit initialization and calibration for span scores to make training stable across datasets and model sizes. We also quantify generalization under domain shift by holding out genres for coreference and argument head types for SRL. Finally, we analyze error patterns to identify where span selection fails due to long-range dependencies or dense mention clusters. This perspective clarifies shared challenges across tasks and motivates methods that trade off recall and precision through adaptive pruning.

(2021) proposed cloze-style prompts that recast classification as masked token prediction using pretrained language models. Liu et al. Personalized Language Modeling. King and Cook (2020) examined methods for creating personalized LMs. They consider interpolating, finetuning, and priming LMs as methods of personalization, though unlike our work, they do not attempt to improve these models with similar user data. They also analyzed model adaptation for models trained on users with similar demographic factors, inspired by Lynn et al. (2017), who showed that these demographic factors could help model a variety of natural language processing tasks, and found that personalized models perform better than those adapted from similar demographics. Shao et al. (2020) have also explored interpolating models for personalization and have focused on handling OOV tokens. Wu et al. (2020) proposed a framework to learn user embeddings from Reddit posts, with embeddings built on the sentence embeddings generated by a BERT model. By using the learned user embeddings to predict gender, detect depression and classify MBTI personality, they concluded that their embeddings incorporate intrinsic attributes of users. In our work, user embeddings are learned in a different approach, and we focus on how to use similarity calculated from user embeddings to build better LMs.

Learning from Limited Data. Antonello et al. (2020) explored training a model to predict what data will be most informative for fine-tuning. The similarity metrics that we derive are used to select data for fine-tuning in one of our methods of leveraging similar user data, however we consider indivisible sets of data grouped by author.

The cold start problem is a well-known problem in recommendation systems. A great amount of previous work addressed how to recommend items to new users, about whom the system has little or no history, often with a focus on matrix factorization methods (Zhou et al., 2011). Work from Huang et al. (2016) approached language modeling as a cold-start problem, in that they had no writing from a user, though they had a social network, from which they interpolated LMs from users linked in their social graph.

Language Models. We use a recently developed LM that has received widespread attention (Merity et al., 2018b). The LSTM-based model combines a number of regularization and optimization techniques explored in recent literature, including averaged SGD, embedding dropout, and recurrent dropout. Subsequent work has developed variations of the model with improved perplexity, but these take at least twice as much time to train (Gong et al., 2018), making them less practical for the user-specific experiments we consider.

Another direction of research has shown impressive results using extremely large models (Radford et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2019). Using these as a basis for experiments could be an interesting direction, but fine-tuning models in low data settings is known to be difficult and highly variable (Dodge et al., 2020). Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated large-scale in-context learning with GPT-3 for diverse NLP tasks without gradient updates. Gao et al. (2021) proposed cloze-style prompts that recast classification as masked token prediction using pretrained language models. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt-based methods and parameter-efficient tuning such as prompt tuning and prefix tuning across benchmarks and tasks. Additionally, when fine-tuning models for new users, little data is available, and contextualized embedding models often require a large amount of data to train effectively, though this type of comparison would be an interesting future direction to explore. Variations of the LSTM have consistently achieved state-of-the-art performance without massive compute resources and thus we chose this architecture for our experiments (Merity et al., 2018a;Melis et al., 2019;Merity, 2019;Li et al., 2020).

Authorship Attribution. One of the tasks we consider as a means of computing similarity is authorship attribution, i.e., identifying the author of a document. Early work on this task used lexical features like word frequencies and word n-grams (Koppel et al., 2009;Stamatatos, 2009). As in (Ge et al., 2016), we employ neural networks to model similarity between users and predict authorship.

document: Our approach is closely related to works on cross-lingual NER, knowledge distillation, and siamese networks. Cross-lingual NER extracts entities in a target language while relying on annotations only in a source language. Prior models fall into shared feature space, translation, and knowledge distillation categories. Shared feature space methods train language-agnostic encoders using source and target data (Tsai et al., 2016). mBERT has proven effective for alignment (Devlin et al., 2019), and some studies add transfer components on top of mBERT for zero-shot transfer (Keung et al., 2019). Performance remains limited due to the scarcity of target-language annotations. Translation-based methods construct pseudo-labeled target data by projecting labels via word-level translation (Wu et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2021). Li et al. (2019) introduce lexicon induction for cross-lingual alignment, and Artetxe et al. (2018) present self-learning mappings for unsupervised machine translation. Conneau et al. (2020) report cross-lingual understanding with XLM-R across tasks. Our model dispenses with translation by learning entity similarity directly in target data. Knowledge distillation frameworks employ a teacher trained on the source language and a student for the target (Wu et al., 2020c). The student imitates soft predictions on unlabeled target sentences to absorb additional task and domain signals. In our design, the student learns both recognition cues from the teacher and an auxiliary signal on entity similarity inspired by multi-task learning. Siamese networks were introduced for signature verification as a metric-learning problem and later used for image and text similarity (Bromley et al., 1994; Koch et al., 2015; Neculoiu et al., 2016). We adapt this paradigm by reconstructing supervision into paired instances and show that learning entity similarity within this framework yields consistent gains.

document: Efficient Pre-training for NLP. Recent studies show that the performance of PLMs can be improved by increasing model size, data size, and training steps (Raffel et al., 2019; Kaplan et al., 2020). This observation has fueled a wave of ever-larger PLMs targeting broad coverage and generalization. Larger models, however, impose substantial computational and environmental costs (Patterson et al., 2021). To mitigate the training burden, researchers accelerate pre-training with mixed-precision arithmetic (Shoeybi et al., 2019) and well-engineered distributed pipelines (Shoeybi et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019). Complementary advances include large-batch optimization with careful learning-rate scaling (You et al., 2020) and architectural innovations that reduce token-level redundancy (Lan et al., 2020). Data-centric approaches further trim compute by curriculum design, deduplication, and dynamic masking strategies (Clark et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2021). Another line of methods proposes to pre-train larger PLMs progressively (Gong et al., 2019). These methods first train a small PLM and then expand depth or width via parameter recycling to initialize a bigger network. While parameter recycling can serve knowledge inheritance, it often imposes tight coupling between student and teacher architectures, limiting practical flexibility. In scenarios requiring heterogeneous backbones or multi-source integration, we instead favor knowledge distillation to achieve inheritance without structural constraints. Furthermore, parameter recycling is ill-suited for aggregating knowledge from multiple teachers or for domain adaptation across divergent corpora. For completeness, we provide extended comparisons between inheritance and recycling in Appendix F. Knowledge Distillation for PLMs. Knowledge Distillation (KD) compresses a high-capacity teacher into an efficient student by matching predictive signals and internal features (Hinton et al., 2015). Within NLP, KD has been explored at pre-training time (Sanh et al., 2019), during task-specific fine-tuning (Sun et al., 2019; Krishna et al., 2020), and in end-to-end pipelines spanning both phases (Jiao et al., 2020). Beyond final logits, many approaches distill hidden representations, attention patterns, and layer-wise transformations to enhance transferability (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). Park et al. (2021) examine layer collapse in students trained with token-level matching and report instability under high temperatures. Chen et al. (2020) introduce data filtering heuristics for multilingual pre-training and curate mappings for low-resource scripts. Li et al. (2021) study sparsity-inducing regularizers for transformer model compression and analyze trade-offs between magnitude pruning and structured masks. Conventional KD often assumes the teacher upper-bounds the student, yet this assumption weakens when the student has greater capacity or better pre-training data. In the knowledge inheritance setting, the student may eventually surpass the teacher, which motivates bidirectional or reversed distillation protocols. Evidence outside NLP shows that weaker teachers can still provide useful inductive biases for specialized tasks (Yuan et al., 2020). Recent studies explore self-distillation, co-training between peers, and stage-wise teachers derived from intermediate checkpoints to stabilize training (Zhang et al., 2019; Furlanello et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2020). We extend these ideas to pre-training by treating an initial student as a provisional teacher and refreshing targets as capacity scales. Our framework accommodates heterogeneous architectures, enabling cross-family inheritance between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder–decoder PLMs. We further support multi-teacher aggregation through soft target ensembling and representation alignment in shared subspaces. Extensive experiments demonstrate accelerated convergence and improved downstream accuracy under fixed compute budgets across language understanding and generation benchmarks. We release code and pre-trained checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility and future research.

While research in Natural Language Processing (NLP) initially prioritized narrow technical interventions to address the social impact of language technologies, we are recently attesting a shift toward a more comprehensive understanding of bias (Shah et al., 2020;Blodgett et al., 2020). Along this line, focus has been given to bias analysis in models' innards and ouputs (Vig et al., 2020;Costajussà et al., 2020b), and to ascertain the validity of bias measurament practices (Blodgett et al., 2021;Antoniak and Mimno, 2021;Goldfarb-Tarrant et al., 2021). Complementary evidence suggests thatrather than striving for generalizations -gender bias detection ought to incorporate contextual and linguistic specificity (González et al., 2020;Ciora et al., 2021;Matthews et al., 2021;Malik et al., 2021;Kurpicz-Briki and Leoni, 2021), which however receives little attention due to a heavy focus on English NLP. Purported agnostic approaches and evaluations (Bender, 2009) can prevent from drawing reliable conclusions and mitigating recommendations, as attested by monolingual studies on grammatical gender languages (Zhou et al., 2019;Gonen et al., 2019) and in automatic translation scenarios (Vanmassenhove et al., 2018;Moryossef et al., 2019). Unlike English, grammatical gender languages exhibit an elaborate morphological and syntactic system, where gender is overtly marked on numerous POS (e.g., verbs, determiners, nouns), and related words have to agree on the same gender features. Still, current corpora and evaluation practices do not fully foreground systems' behaviour on such grammatical constraints.

WinoMT (Stanovsky et al., 2019) represents the standard corpus to evaluate gender bias in MT within an English-to-grammatical gender language scenario. It has been progressively enriched with new features Kocmi et al., 2020), and adapted for ST (Costa-jussà et al., 2020a). While this resource can be useful to diagnose gender stereotyping at scale, it excludes languages' peculiarities since it is built on the concatenation of two corpora designed for English monolingual tasks -WinoGender (Rudinger et al., 2018) and WinoBias (Zhao et al., 2018) -which consist of synthetic sentences with the same structure and a pre-selected occupational lexicon. 3 To increase variability, Troles and Schmid (2021) extend WinoBias by accompanying occupations with highly gender-stereotypical verbs and adjectives. Their evaluation though, still only considers the translated professions as to verify if the co-occuring words might skew the models' assumptions. However, gender-marking involves also several other, so far less accounted POS categories, but whether they are just as problematic is not clear yet.

Existing bilingual (Alhafni et al., 2021), and multilingual natural benchmarks, instead, are manually curated as to identify a variety of gender phenomena specifically modeled on the accounted languages. Sun et al. (2019) introduced domain-adaptive pretraining for translation quality. Prates et al. (2019) examined bias through occupation prompts, and Escudé Font and Costa-jussà (2019) studied debiasing with constraints on Spanish and Catalan. However, since this variability is not mapped into fine-grained linguistic information, evaluations on such corpora do not single out which instances may be more responsible for gender bias. Finally, by considering each word in isolation, they neglect the underlying features of gender agreement, which determine the grammatical acceptability of the translation. To the best of our knowledge, only two works have currently interplayed issues of syntactic agreement and gender bias. Renduchintala and Williams (2021) designed a set of English sentences involving a syntactic construction that requires to translate an occupational term according to its unequivocal "gender trigger" (e.g. that nurse is a funny man). While they find that MT struggles even in such a simple setting, they only inspect the translation of a single disambiguated word (nurse) rather than a whole group of words in agreement. Closer to our intent, prior analyses of different ST systems note that models seem to wrongly pick divergent gender inflections for unrelated words in the same sentence (e.g. fr: En tant que chercheuse F , professeur M ) but not for dependency-related ones (e.g. it: [la classica studentessa asiatica] F ), and although limited in scope, their observation is worth being explored systematically. We thus conduct the very first study that intersects POS, agreement, and gender bias.

(2020) report that negation remains a persistent source of error for commercial sentiment systems, and Ettinger (2020) finds that BERT often fails to complete cloze sentences when negation is present. Wallace et al. (2019) demonstrate that simple adversarial cues can sharply degrade performance, Geiger et al. (2020) and Yanaka et al. document: We study eight English corpora spanning six natural language understanding tasks, as summarized here and illustrated in Table X. For question answering we use CommonsenseQA (Talmor et al., 2019) and COPA (Roemmele et al., 2011), which require selecting plausible answers based on everyday reasoning. For textual similarity and paraphrasing we use QQP and STS-B (Cer et al., 2017), where the goal is to judge paraphrase identity or a graded similarity score. For the remaining tasks we rely on QNLI (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), WiC (Pilehvar and Camacho-Collados, 2019), WSC (Levesque et al., 2012), and SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013), using GLUE or SuperGLUE formats except CommonsenseQA. SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015) and MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) display annotation artifacts that bias inference decisions, as documented by Gururangan et al. (2018). Ribeiro et al. (2020) report that negation remains a persistent source of error for commercial sentiment systems, and Ettinger (2020) finds that BERT often fails to complete cloze sentences when negation is present. Wallace et al. (2019) demonstrate that simple adversarial cues can sharply degrade performance, Geiger et al. (2020) and Yanaka et al. (2019) examine monotonicity with negation in NLI, and Hossain et al. (2020) analyze negation across RTE, SNLI, and MNLI, and paraphrases. Building on these observations, we systematically quantify negation across the eight datasets and train a RoBERTa-based cue detector using ConanDoyle-neg (Morante and Daelemans, 2012), reporting state-of-the-art F1 and releasing code and analysis protocols.

spans: DistilBERT compresses BERT while retaining strong language understanding performance (Sanh et al., 2019). TinyBERT performs layer-wise and attention-based distillation with task-specific fine-tuning to boost accuracy on GLUE (Jiao et al., 2020). MobileBERT focuses on on-device efficiency through an inverted-bottleneck design and a carefully aligned teacher (Sun et al., 2020). BERT-of-Theseus replaces teacher components with student modules during training to gradually reshape the network (Xu et al., 2020). MiniLM transfers self-attention value-key relation knowledge using a compact head configuration for efficiency (Wang et al., 2020). Results are typically reported on SQuAD and other benchmarks without sequence generation components (Rajpurkar et al., 2016).
document: Large pre-trained Seq2Seq Transformer models have advanced text generation tasks including abstractive summarization (Song et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). These models are typically pre-trained with unsupervised text-to-text objectives that encourage robust contextual reasoning over long documents. For example, T5 predicts corrupted spans, BART uses denoising auto-encoding with text infilling and sentence permutation, and PEGASUS masks summary-worthy sentences to align pre-training with summarization (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Despite strong quality, inference latency and memory footprint remain practical barriers, which motivates model compression via knowledge distillation. In distillation, students can learn from soft targets, intermediate states, attention patterns, or gradient-based signals produced by a larger teacher (Ba and Caruana, 2014; Hinton et al., 2015; Romero et al., 2014; Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Czarnecki et al., 2017). DistilBERT compresses BERT while retaining strong language understanding performance (Sanh et al., 2019). TinyBERT performs layer-wise and attention-based distillation with task-specific fine-tuning to boost accuracy on GLUE (Jiao et al., 2020). MobileBERT focuses on on-device efficiency through an inverted-bottleneck design and a carefully aligned teacher (Sun et al., 2020). BERT-of-Theseus replaces teacher components with student modules during training to gradually reshape the network (Xu et al., 2020). MiniLM transfers self-attention value-key relation knowledge using a compact head configuration for efficiency (Wang et al., 2020). Results are typically reported on SQuAD and other benchmarks without sequence generation components (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). For sequence-to-sequence generation, sequence-level knowledge distillation via pseudo labeling has proved effective, and our approach builds on this idea to accelerate summarization models while preserving quality (Kim and Rush, 2016; Gu et al., 2017; Denkowski and Neubig, 2017; Shleifer and Rush, 2020).

ContraWSD (Gonzales et al., 2017) targets lexical ambiguity in translation, MuCoW (Raganato et al., 2019) automatically constructs adversarial pairs using BabelNet, Emelin et al. (2020) curate bias-sensitive sets for English–German, Winata et al. (2021) examine prompting, and Brown et al. (2020) introduce language models.

Word Sense Disambiguation has been first formulated as a computational task by Weaver (1949) in the context of Machine Translation. The two fields then followed parallel paths, with more or less successful attempts over the years to try joining them back together (Carpuat and Wu, 2005;Vickrey et al., 2005;Carpuat and Wu, 2007). Indeed, while Carpuat and Wu (2005) reported negative results when trying to integrate the prediction of a supervised WSD approach into a Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) model, the same authors, two years later, successfully improved the performance of a phrase-based SMT approach by leveraging a new phrase-based WSD model (Carpuat and Wu, 2007). More recently, Pu et al. (2018) and Nguyen et al. (2018) proposed systems that successfully leverage sense information in NMT models, although they introduce a heavy requirement, i.e., that of disambiguating the ambiguous words in the sentence prior to generating a translation, which makes them unfeasible in many realworld settings. Furthermore, contextualized word embeddings have been employed to produce additional back-translated parallel training data via mining sense-specific target sentences, to improve handling of infrequent senses (Hangya et al., 2021).

Nevertheless, the proper treatment of lexical ambiguity is still an open problem, with neural models struggling to translate least frequent senses and often relying on spurious correlations among words (Emelin et al., 2020;Raganato et al., 2019;Gonzales et al., 2017). The disambiguation bias topic received renewed attention, and several benchmarks appeared to directly measure how neural architectures capture word semantics. ContraWSD (Gonzales et al., 2017) targets lexical ambiguity in translation, MuCoW (Raganato et al., 2019) automatically constructs adversarial pairs using BabelNet, Emelin et al. (2020) curate bias-sensitive sets for English–German, Winata et al. (2021) examine prompting, and Brown et al. (2020) introduce language models. In this first attempt to evaluate WSD capabilities of NMT models, the authors built an adversarial test set where source sentences containing an ambiguous word are associated with a correct translation and several incorrect alternatives. These latter are built by replacing the reference translation for the ambiguous word with the translation of one of its other possible meanings. The task measures whether a model ranks the correct translation higher, i.e., it assigns a higher probability, than the adversarial ones. This work provides evaluation data for two language pairs only, i.e., German→English and German→French, and it is now outdated as modern NMT models can easily attain high performances (Emelin et al., 2019). Thus, MuCoW (Raganato et al., 2019) took a step further and leveraged BabelNet (Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012) -a large multilingual knowledge base -and sense embeddings (Camacho-Collados et al., 2016;Mancini et al., 2017) to automatically create adversarial translations for five language pairs while also increasing the difficulty of the task itself; unfortunately, the fully automatic nature of these challenge sets makes them noisy and prone to contain irrelevant challenge samples. Recently, Emelin et al. (2020) proposed two challenge sets for the English→German pair, one measuring the model sensitivity to most frequent senses while the other estimating, through adversarial injections, the susceptibility to changing a correct sense to a wrong one. Contrarily to previous works, these challenge sets are based on correlations among words in the training set and rely on manually-refined sense clusters, making them a great test bed for measuring disambiguation bias.

Despite the effort in putting forward challenging sets of data to test WSD capabilities of NMT models, to the best of our knowledge, only a few approaches (Gonzales et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018) have been proposed to mitigate this issue, and none of them are effective with modern Transformerbased architectures. Furthermore, while parallel corpora have been exploited to produce sense annotations in the past (Bonansinga and Bond, 2016;Delli Bovi et al., 2017), they have been built by utilizing outdated disambiguation approaches that have been recently surpassed by more advanced neural architectures. Indeed, the Word Sense Disambiguation field has received much attention in the most recent years, with several supervised approaches (Conia and Navigli, 2021;Blevins and Zettlemoyer, 2020) and sense embedding models (Loureiro and Jorge, 2019;Scarlini et al., 2020a,b;Wang et al., 2020) performing close to the upper bound limit of the inter-annotator agreement, which finally makes them feasible for inclusion in other downstream tasks, e.g., Machine Translation. Thus, differently from previous works in the literature, we focus on closing the gap between these two fields, i.e., Neural Machine Translation and Word Sense Disambiguation, by putting the recent advances in WSD at the service of NMT models. We propose a novel approach to create high-quality sense-annotated parallel corpora, which leverages the most recent advances in WSD, and use this semantic information to regularize a NMT model, making it less biased and capable of producing higher-quality translations.

document: The automatic processing of referring expressions has been studied for a long time (Winograd, 1972). Early computational accounts treated meaning as logical form and divided expressions into intrinsic and relational types corresponding to unary and binary predicates, respectively (Dale and Haddock, 1991). Subsequent corpus studies showed that even in simple scenes speakers frequently rely on relational content to uniquely identify objects (Viethen and Dale, 2006). Referring expressions are linguistically rich, and overspecification can facilitate identification when redundant attributes are easy to recognize (Paraboni et al., 2017). Vagueness and gradability make appropriateness context dependent, so what counts as “big” in one scene may be “small” in another (Quirk et al., 1980). Syntactic ambiguity in human-produced descriptions has also been examined in the REG literature (Chantree et al., 2005; Khan et al., 2008). Psycholinguistic work emphasized reference as a collaborative process grounded in shared knowledge (Clark and Wilkes-Gibbs, 1986; Clark, 1996). Hawkins (1978) proposed that speakers introduce a referent, collaborate to locate it within a shared set, and ultimately refer to the totality of objects satisfying the description. On this view, reference underlies visually grounded dialogue and related interactive tasks (Dale and Reiter, 1995). More recently, dataset probing has revealed that REC models can exploit biases and remain competitive even when expressions are degraded or removed (Cirik et al., 2018). Karpathy and Fei-Fei (2015) aligned image regions with sentences using multimodal embeddings and reported retrieval gains on datasets using region-sentence correspondence learned from weak supervision. Plummer et al. (2015) introduced Flickr30k Entities linking noun phrases to boxes, and Rohrbach et al. (2016) studied weakly supervised grounding with multiple instance learning. Krishna et al. (2017) released Visual Genome with dense region descriptions, and Anderson et al. (2018) presented bottom-up attention features for many popular vision-language benchmarks. Johnson et al. (2017) developed CLEVR for compositional reasoning, and Yu et al. (2016) built ReferItGame to collect natural language object localization data at scale. We adopt a transformer-based regression approach to REC that directly predicts bounding boxes from an image and a query, building on recent designs in Deng et al. (2021) and Du et al. (2021).

(2020) introduced dense passage retrieval with in-batch negatives for open-domain question answering. Xiong et al. (2021) developed ANCE with asynchronous negative refresh to improve representation quality. Guu et al. (2020) presented REALM to couple retrieval with language model pre-training. Khattab and Zaharia proposed ColBERT for efficient late-interaction matching at scale.
document: Dense retriever pre-training has emerged as a key strategy for improving large-scale open-domain retrieval under weak supervision. Early efforts leveraged implicit user feedback, with Borisov et al. (2016) and Dehghani et al. (2017) distilling click-log and BM25-induced signals into ranking models for web search. Task-driven pre-training followed, most notably the inverse cloze task (ICT), which samples sentences as pseudo-queries and matches them to their source passages to shape query-document alignment. Lee et al. (2019) explored retrieval-augmented pre-training that links latent knowledge to downstream tasks. Karpukhin et al. (2020) introduced dense passage retrieval with in-batch negatives for open-domain question answering. Xiong et al. (2021) developed ANCE with asynchronous negative refresh to improve representation quality. Guu et al. (2020) presented REALM to couple retrieval with language model pre-training. Khattab and Zaharia proposed ColBERT for efficient late-interaction matching at scale. Complementary Wikipedia-centric objectives include wiki link prediction (WLP) and body-first selection (BFS), which exploit hyperlink structure to assemble related text pairs for self-supervision. While effective, WLP often forms pairs with only document-level topical overlap, limiting fine-grained passage grounding for query intents. We next detail the OpenQA retrieval background, the Hyperlink-induced Pre-training objective suite, and the end-to-end training framework used in our experiments.

spans: MacAvaney et al. (2019) investigate contextualized reranking for ad-hoc retrieval using BERT-based architectures on web corpora with click log supervision. Nguyen and Eisenstein (2017) study topical coherence in news discourse with latent variable models and discourse-aware inference. Zhang et al. (2021) examine graph-enhanced retrieval pipelines for biomedical abstracts with term expansion and passage-level signals.
document: Fine-grained document representations: Another similar line of work is modeling fine-grained document-document similarity at the level of words or latent topics. Examples include early work by El-Arini and Guestrin (2011) presenting paper recommendation methods with unigram-level similarity between papers using authorship and citation links or using latent document topics (Gong et al., 2018; Yurochkin et al., 2019; Dieng et al., 2020). These approaches quantify affinity through token or topic correspondences rather than sentence-level structure. While effective for broad matching, they can obscure discourse roles and rhetorical intentions that appear at the sentence granularity. Our approach represents documents via sentences, a common and intuitive structure for reasoning about scientific document facets (Chan et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020). We operationalize facets such as background, method, and result using sentence encoders trained to capture intra-document context. Ginzburg et al. (2021) present a self-supervised model for contextual sentence representations in long documents similar to our ICT baseline (Lee et al., 2019). Unlike contrastive objectives over windows, we also incorporate cross-document negatives aligned by section type to sharpen facet specificity. Ad-hoc Search: A range of recent work in information retrieval presents multi-vector models intended to capture different aspects of candidate documents with score aggregation relying on summations, max, or attention functions (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Luan et al., 2021; Humeau et al., 2020), which focus on short-text queries seen in search or question answering. Mitra et al. (2017) explore an approach to model term-level fine-grained similarities with neural networks, and Liu et al. (2018; 2021) model fine-grained matches for QA at the phrase level. Importantly, these methods rely on supervision from knowledge bases or QA datasets, limiting applicability to specific span definitions and areas with these resources, often not present in the scientific domain (Hope et al., 2021a). In scientific retrieval, queries are frequently multi-sentence and require alignment across rhetorical zones rather than isolated terms. MacAvaney et al. (2019) investigate contextualized reranking for ad-hoc retrieval using BERT-based architectures on web corpora with click log supervision. Nguyen and Eisenstein (2017) study topical coherence in news discourse with latent variable models and discourse-aware inference. Zhang et al. (2021) examine graph-enhanced retrieval pipelines for biomedical abstracts with term expansion and passage-level signals. Multi-vector dense retrieval further motivates our sentence-centric representation, since decomposed similarity can be aggregated without prespecified term spans. Our setting also connects to citation recommendation, where candidate rationales are sentence-scoped instead of document-wide language models. Existing citation recommenders leverage local context windows and bibliographic coupling signals to rank targets with interpretable rationales. We build on such signals by explicitly modeling inter-sentence roles and by calibrating scores across sections to avoid popularity bias. Appendix J details auxiliary tasks and ablations supporting these design choices.

document: To-do Management: Intelligent systems can assist users with task management in many ways (Gil et al., 2012). To-do tasks can be inferred automatically from emails (Mukherjee et al., 2020). Systems can detect types of to-do items and suggest relevant applications or resources to users (Landes and Di Eugenio, 2018;Gil et al., 2012;Shah and White, 2021). Once to-do tasks have been created, a system can help users manage the completion progress, e.g., by sending reminders (Graus et al., 2016). Complex tasks can be decomposed automatically into more manageable sub-steps (Nouri et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). In all these use cases, a common step is to represent the input language as computational vector representations, but none of the existing studies has produced general-purpose representations of to-do tasks. Short-text Representations: Multiple NLP areas involve very short texts with some unique characteristics. Several methods have been developed for tweets (e.g., (Nguyen et al., 2020)). Tweets pose the added challenge of containing many non-standard colloquial expressions and contain non-language text like URLs. Kim et al. (2016) analyze emoji usage in microblogs to improve sentiment classification and report domain-specific gains on Twitter benchmarks using multi-task training and lexicon features. Rao and Spasojevic (2016) present a neural hashtag recommendation method that leverages content features and user metadata across large-scale tweet corpora and retweet graph signals. Godin et al. (2013) investigate short-text entity linking with distributed representations and gazetteers, and Zarrella and Marsh (2016) study rumor detection from tweet streams using character-based encoders for classification. Still, Wang et al. (2020) present a similar finding to ours: massively pre-trained encoders do not always perform well. Search queries are also short, with an average of three terms (Taghavi et al., 2012). Unlike to-dos, information such as click logs (Zhang et al., 2019) can be used as an indicator of user intent. Another key difference is that search queries are written with the goal of having a machine interpret them. Multi-task Learning: Multi-task learning improves the performance of pre-trained language models in various NLP tasks (Liu et al., 2019a;Shuster et al., 2020;Aghajanyan et al., 2021). The common perception in the research community is that auxiliary training tasks are effective when they are similar to the target domain/task (Shui et al., 2019). However, there are few relevant tasks and datasets for the to-do domain. Our study is the first work to propose a time-and cost-efficient way to harvest weak-supervision for MTL in that domain.

document: In this section, we outline the landscape of privacy in machine learning and its implications for modern language technologies. Classical definitions distinguish between cryptographic privacy notions and statistical disclosure control, shaping how risks are formalized and mitigated. Differential privacy (DP) has emerged as a standard for bounding worst-case information leakage from training data while enabling useful computation. Seminal work by Dwork et al. (2006) introduced DP and mechanisms such as the Laplace mechanism to calibrate noise to global sensitivity. Subsequent advances adapted DP to iterative optimization, culminating in differentially private stochastic gradient descent that scales to deep learning (Abadi et al., 2016). Tighter privacy accounting with Rényi differential privacy improved utility for large-batch training and multiple compositions (Mironov, 2017; Balle et al., 2020). Privacy amplification by subsampling and shuffling further reduced effective privacy loss in decentralized settings (Balle et al., 2018; Erlingsson et al., 2019). Parallel to DP, secure multi-party computation and homomorphic encryption enable learning on encrypted data and secure aggregation of gradients (Bonawitz et al., 2017; Gentry, 2009). Federated learning integrates these primitives to avoid raw data centralization while coordinating model updates at the edge (McMahan et al., 2017). Recent surveys document trade-offs between utility, privacy budgets, and system constraints in realistic deployments of private learning (Truex et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). Language models raise unique risks due to memorization of rare or sensitive sequences that may be extractable by adversaries. Empirical studies show that parametric memorization can leak verbatim training fragments and personally identifiable information under targeted prompts (Carlini et al., 2019; Carlini et al., 2021). Membership inference and attribute inference have been demonstrated at scale for text classifiers and generative models, underscoring the need for formal protections (Shokri et al., 2017; Song and Shmatikov, 2019). DP-SGD mitigates memorization in language modeling but often imposes a steep utility cost without careful tuning of clipping norms, noise multipliers, and batch sizes (Abadi et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2021). Privacy-adaptive training pipelines exploit per-sample gradient clipping, public pretraining, and private fine-tuning to narrow the performance gap on downstream tasks (Li et al., 2021; De et al., 2022). Brown et al. (2020) introduced large-scale prompting for language models and reported emergent capabilities across many tasks. Opacus (2021) provides a DP training library for PyTorch, Gehman et al. (2020) curated toxic prompts to probe unsafe generations, and Komeili et al. (2022) described data filtering pipelines for conversational agents. Kang et al. (2022) studied calibrated decoding. Beyond training, auditing techniques estimate exposure or canary memorization without retraining, giving practitioners diagnostics for risk-aware deployment (Carlini et al., 2019; Feldman and Zhang, 2020). Policy and governance considerations emphasize dataset consent, data minimization, and provenance tracking as complementary safeguards that operate alongside technical guarantees (Barocas et al., 2019). Our study synthesizes these strands by focusing on private fine-tuning of generative language models under realistic compute budgets and public-corpus pretraining. We analyze the privacy–utility frontier using RDP accounting, study memorization via exposure metrics, and evaluate instruction-following quality on standard benchmarks. The findings motivate practical recipes for safer adaptation of large language models in resource-constrained environments.

document: Prompt-based Language Models. Prompt-based learning leverages pretrained language models by prepending task-specific instructions to inputs, enabling efficient adaptation to generation tasks. Early work relied on hand-crafted prompts (Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zou et al., 2021), while subsequent research introduced continuous prompt optimization to improve flexibility and trainability (Liu et al., 2021c; Li and Liang, 2021). Among these, prefix-tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) appends learnable vectors to the encoder–decoder, and we build on soft-prompts for text generation, examining prompt transfer between tasks. Brown et al. (2020) introduced few-shot prompting with very large autoregressive models. Raffel et al. (2020) unified diverse tasks under a text-to-text formulation, Gao et al. (2020) searched discrete triggers using gradient signals, Shin et al. (2020) mined prompts from contexts for downstream classification, Li and Liang (2021) optimized continuous prefixes for generation, and Liu et al. (2021c) formulated learnable soft prompts that effectively operate in embedding space. Transferability of Natural Language Processing remains a central concern, with evidence that cross-task adaptation improves performance in data-scarce and reasoning-intensive settings (Wiese et al., 2017; Jeong et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019). We focus on challenging generation problems by using soft prompts to extract implicit task-related knowledge and by conditioning transfer on specific inputs, extending recent NLU-oriented prompt transfer studies (Vu et al., 2021; Su et al., 2021) to a generative regime.

document: Semantic dependency parsing aims to produce a graph of predicate–argument relations for a sentence, and its arc decisions are highly interdependent compared with plain dependency parsing where tree constraints partially mitigate conflicts. To address such interdependence, prior work explores higher-order graph-based scoring and sequential decision processes that encode partial outputs, while the biaffine architecture of Dozat and Manning (2018) yields fast O(n^2) inference with strong accuracy. On English SDP, Wang et al. (2019) obtain scores with second-order factors without pretrained language models. Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez (2020) describe a transition-based system, Kurita and Søgaard (2019) iterate head selections with reinforcement learning, He and Choi (2020) add transformers to the biaffine model of Dozat and Manning (2018), and Oepen et al. (2015) provide the SemEval Task 18 benchmark used for ID and OOD evaluation. We adopt the biaffine model and show that adding simple auxiliary tasks in a multi-task setting introduces limited interdependence among arc decisions and improves results on SemEval 2015 Task 18 English data, with additional gains on highly cyclic French deep syntactic graphs that require unconstrained prediction.

document: While much of this paper discusses the state of the NLP literature, a few related works warrant emphasis as starting points for further reading. Bender and Koller (2020) and Raji et al. (2021) discuss the role of hype in driving bad outcomes from the development of language technology. Jin et al. (2021) and Rogers (2021) offer broader discussion of how to ensure that the net impact of near-future NLP deployments on the world is positive. Brown et al. (2020) introduced GPT-3 and demonstrated few-shot capabilities across tasks. Gao et al. (2021) explored retrieval-augmented prompting for open-domain QA. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt-based learning methods and templates for pretrained models. Wei et al. (2022) reported that chain-of-thought prompts improve reasoning performance on math and logic benchmarks. Schick and Schütze (2021) presented PET and iPET to reduce labeled data requirements in few-shot classification. Prompt tuning was studied. Morris et al. (2020) and Hauser et al. (2021) highlight overly strong negative claims in papers analyzing models' robustness to synonym substitution. Pruthi et al. (2019) and Alzantot et al. (2018) evaluate adversarial word substitutions and illustrate pitfalls of gradient-based heuristics for text attacks. Beyond robustness, dataset design has received attention, with Gebru et al. (2018) and Bender et al. (2021) advocating transparent documentation practices for data provenance and model reporting. Long-term governance concerns intersect with technical evaluations, as Amodei et al. (2016) articulate concrete safety problems alongside measurement desiderata for deployed systems. Looking to the longer term, Bommasani et al. (2021, §4.9) provides an introduction to the AI risk and AI alignment literature from a perspective that emphasizes NLP and language. Welty et al. (2019) and Linzen (2020, 2021), among many others, discuss the challenges involved in designing evaluations that yield trustworthy and accurate depictions of the capabilities of ML models. Recent benchmark analyses caution against overinterpreting aggregate scores, with Dehghani et al. (2021) and Geiger et al. (2021) underscoring the need for fine-grained competency models and data ablations. Kaplan et al. (2020) proposed scaling laws that relate performance to model size, dataset size, and compute budget. Hoffmann et al. (2022) introduced Chinchilla and emphasized data-efficient scaling with optimal token allocation. Goyal et al. (2022) reported improvements in instruction following using supervised fine-tuning on curated prompt-response pairs. Sanh et al. (2022) released the multitask FLAN-T5 suite with broad task coverage. Open datasets were central to several of these scaling studies. Together, these strands motivate our focus on evaluation practices that emphasize clear assumptions, reproducible protocols, and careful links between capabilities claims and real-world decision contexts.
