spans: Hirano and Imbens (2001) study propensity score weighting in semiparametric models. Bang and Robins (2005) present doubly robust estimation of mean outcomes. Athey et al. (2019) propose causal forests for heterogeneous treatment effects.
document: The ATE is defined as τ = E[Y_i(1) − Y_i(0)], where Y_i(t) denotes the potential outcome for unit i under treatment t. It can be identified in randomized experiments under the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption and proper assignment mechanisms (Rubin, 1974). In observational studies, treatment assignment may correlate with covariates that affect outcomes, creating confounding that biases naive comparisons. The propensity score π(X_i) = Pr(T_i = 1 | X_i) balances measured covariates by design when properly estimated (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983). Under ignorability and positivity, the ATE can be recovered using inverse probability weighting, outcome regression, or doubly robust estimators, provided models are well specified (Funk et al., 2011; Robins et al., 1994). Modern implementations often rely on flexible machine learning to estimate nuisance functions while controlling bias via cross-fitting and sample splitting (Chernozhukov et al., 2018). Penalized regression, boosted trees, and neural nets have been deployed to stabilize weights and reduce extrapolation in high dimensions. Weight truncation and covariate balancing approaches target finite-sample performance by limiting variance and aligning moments of covariate distributions (Cole and Hernán, 2008; Imai and Ratkovic, 2014). Hirano and Imbens (2001) study propensity score weighting in semiparametric models. Bang and Robins (2005) present doubly robust estimation of mean outcomes. Athey et al. (2019) propose causal forests for heterogeneous treatment effects. Despite advances, extreme propensities can inflate variance and signal poor overlap, motivating diagnostics and design adaptations before outcome modeling. Trimming, overlap weights, and matching on estimated scores aim to restrict inference to regions with credible counterfactuals and improved precision (Crump et al., 2009; Li et al., 2019; Stuart, 2010). Targeted maximum likelihood estimation integrates machine learning with semiparametric theory to achieve double robustness and asymptotic efficiency under mild conditions (van der Laan and Rose, 2011). Sensitivity analysis is essential when unmeasured confounding threatens identification, providing bounds or calibrated bias assessments relative to plausible violations (Rosenbaum, 2002; Imbens, 2003). Instrumental variable approaches offer alternatives when valid instruments exist, though relevance and exclusion are often contestable in practice (Angrist et al., 1996). Transportability and generalizability methods extend causal conclusions across covariate shifts by reweighting or modeling selection mechanisms (Bareinboim and Pearl, 2016; Dahabreh et al., 2020). In high-throughput observational pipelines, automated diagnostics combine balance metrics, effective sample size, and tail-weight assessments to guide analyst intervention. Heterogeneous treatment effect estimation enables individualized policy evaluation, with meta-learners and orthogonalization mitigating regularization bias (Künzel et al., 2019; Nie and Wager, 2021). Within-survey and administrative data contexts, design-based estimators can be integrated with causal adjustments to respect stratification and clustering. Reproducible analyses should report identification assumptions, nuisance learners, balance and overlap diagnostics, and uncertainty quantification aligned with the estimand. We adopt these principles to study the ATE in observational settings, emphasizing robust estimation, diagnostic transparency, and clear separation of design and analysis stages.

document: Few studies have addressed bias in language models in French. Irvine et al. (2013) investigated domain-induced semantic bias in French–English phrase-based machine translation trained on parliamentary proceedings and evaluated on biomedical and scientific texts. Daumé III (2016) describes the black sheep problem, noting that linguistic expression can diverge from social reality across languages and that surface cues may mislead automated systems. Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in multilingual settings and analyze cross-lingual embeddings and transfer representations for downstream tasks across English, French, German, and Spanish. Blodgett et al. (2021) audit widely used bias benchmarks, including CrowS-pairs, and catalogue dataset artifacts that complicate measurement of stereotyping in pretrained models. Rudinger et al. (2018) examine gender bias in coreference systems with template-based evaluations, and Sheng et al. (2019) document toxic and biased continuations triggered by demographic descriptors. Motivated by evidence that intrinsic metrics like WEAT poorly correlate with extrinsic bias inferences and that cultural adaptation matters (Goldfarb-Tarrant et al., 2021), we target sentence-level evaluation. Our contribution is a culturally adapted French version of CrowS-pairs and guidance on dataset revisions that reduce artifacts while preserving coverage of common bias categories.

spans: Prompting with verbalizers has been explored for text classification and information extraction by leveraging masked language models and discrete label words (Gao et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Pattern-based prompting reports strong few-shot improvements across natural language understanding benchmarks using task-specific templates and demonstrations (Liu et al., 2021; Min et al., 2022). Neural dependency parsing attains robust attachment performance with biaffine decoders and self-attentive encoders on heterogeneous corpora, including newswire and web text (Dozat and Manning, 2017; Ma et al., 2018). Calibration techniques adjust prompt label probabilities using priors and scoring (Zhao et al., 2021; Holtzman et al., 2021).
document: Early works cast event factuality prediction as classification or regression, combining rule-based inference with supervised modeling for sentence-level judgments (Nairn et al., 2006; Lotan et al., 2013; Saurí and Pustejovsky, 2012). These approaches rely on lexical triggers, negation, modality, and syntactic scope to assess whether events occurred, were negated, or remained uncertain. Subsequent research extended the task beyond single sentences to propagate factuality across discourse using context and belief attribution cues (Lee et al., 2020; Diab, 2021). Our work is most closely related to Yao et al. (2021), who formulate event factuality as modal dependency parsing centered on conceivers and their commitments. Their method first extracts events and potential belief holders, then ranks candidate parents to assemble a dependency graph that encodes scopal relations. We instead prompt a pretrained language model with the target event mention to directly predict its conceiver parent, collapsing extraction and linking into one step. This design reduces error propagation, simplifies training, and enables few-shot adaptation without bespoke feature engineering or task-specific classifiers. Prompting with verbalizers has been explored for text classification and information extraction by leveraging masked language models and discrete label words (Gao et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Pattern-based prompting reports strong few-shot improvements across natural language understanding benchmarks using task-specific templates and demonstrations (Liu et al., 2021; Min et al., 2022). Neural dependency parsing attains robust attachment performance with biaffine decoders and self-attentive encoders on heterogeneous corpora, including newswire and web text (Dozat and Manning, 2017; Ma et al., 2018). Calibration techniques adjust prompt label probabilities using priors and scoring (Zhao et al., 2021; Holtzman et al., 2021). In addition, we situate our method within belief modeling that disentangles factual commitment from reportative structure, uncertainty markers, and evidentiality. We align predicted edges with typed operators such as negation, modality, and attribution to reflect scopal control and source introduction consistent with prior annotation schemes (Karttunen, 2012; Stanovsky et al., 2017). Experiments compare our prompting framework against extraction-plus-ranking baselines and structured decoders on shared datasets with unified preprocessing and metrics. We also analyze template sensitivity, conceiver inventory size, and dependency depth, highlighting systematic trade-offs between coverage, precision, and computational cost in document-level EFP.

document: There is a substantial literature in the field of knowledge-grounded conversation (KGC). Grounding external knowledge via knowledge graphs (Zhou et al., 2018a), documents (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018b), or visual context (Das et al., 2017) is regarded as a critical step toward intelligent dialogue systems. Most KGC methods follow a paradigm that decomposes the task into knowledge selection and utterance generation (Kim et al., 2020). We focus on the knowledge selection subtask and survey approaches based on memory networks (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018), sequential latent variables (Kim et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2020), and reinforcement learning (Zhao et al., 2020b). He et al. (2016) introduced dual learning for neural machine translation. Yi et al. (2017) and Lin et al. (2018) presented image-to-image translation methods with cycle losses. Ren et al. (2019) examined automatic speech recognition, Tang et al. (2017) studied question answering, and Meng et al. (2020) explored knowledge-grounded dialogue. Applications in summarization have been reported. Building on these observations, we apply dual learning to jointly model personal memory and knowledge choice, enabling semi-supervised training that addresses personalization in knowledge selection.

spans: Li et al. (2018) introduce a delete-retrieve-generate pipeline for stylistic rewriting. Shen et al. (2017) present cross-aligned autoencoders, and Lample et al. (2019) explore back-translation for attribute control in non-parallel corpora and datasets.
document:
Text attribute transfer has a vast literature (Jin et al., 2020a) with deep learning methods becoming popular. The methods are either supervised—requiring parallel data—or unsupervised. Supervised methods repurpose Sequence to Sequence models used in machine translation to achieve the goals (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). However, obtaining parallel data is cumbersome and thus unsupervised methods that consider pseudo-parallel data have become popular.

Disentanglement approaches are the prevalent approach to tackle unsupervised attribute transfer: attributes and content are separated in latent dimension. To disentangle the attributes, adversarial methods maximize the loss of a pretrained attribute classifier (Fu et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018a; John et al., 2019). However, the literature has paid little attention in defining and preserving content. Li et al. (2018) introduce a delete-retrieve-generate pipeline for stylistic rewriting. Shen et al. (2017) present cross-aligned autoencoders, and Lample et al. (2019) explore back-translation for attribute control in non-parallel corpora and datasets. Cycle consistency losses—imposing that reconstruction from the target style sentence should resemble the source sentence—are the most prevalent (Prabhumoye et al., 2018; Logeswaran et al., 2018; Dai et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2020; Yi et al., 2020). However, this is expensive, non-differentiable and often requires reinforcement learning techniques to enforce it. Our work defines the different constraints that should be preserved and adds simple differentiable contrastive learning losses to preserve them.

In recent times, text style transfer models are moving away from disentanglement approaches (Subramanian et al., 2018). Recent works that use transformers for style transfer have also adopted this (Dai et al., 2019; Krishna et al., 2020). However, these methods do not explicitly maintain the constraints between the two styles which is the main aim of our work. Parallel efforts employ pretrained language models with control tokens to steer style while keeping content intact (Keskar et al., 2019; Dathathri et al., 2020). Editing-based methods modify a minimal set of spans using masked language models guided by attribute classifiers to preserve meaning (Malmi et al., 2020; Sudhakar et al., 2019). Evaluation remains challenging, as automatic metrics correlate imperfectly with human judgments of style, content fidelity, and fluency (Mir et al., 2019; Pang and Gimpel, 2019). Our approach complements these directions by formalizing content constraints as explicit entailment, lexical, and semantic similarity objectives. We incorporate a light-weight contrastive criterion to align source and transferred sentences without reinforcement learning or back-translation loops.

document:
Interpretability There has been a lot of work on better interpreting models' decision process, e.g., understanding BERT (Clark et al., 2019b; Kovaleva et al., 2019) and attention in transformers (Hao et al., 2020), or through text generation models (Narang et al., 2020). In this paper we utilize the attention scores as a generic way to understand what features a model relies on for making its predictions. Other common model interpretation techniques (Sundararajan et al., 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2016), or more recent work on hierarchical attentions (Chen et al., 2020) and contrastive explanations (Jacovi et al., 2021), can be used as well. In Pruthi et al. (2020), the authors found that attention scores can be manipulated to deceive human decision makers.

Robustness and Bias An increasing body of work has been conducted on understanding robustness in deep neural networks, particularly, how models sometimes pay attention to spurious correlations (Tu et al., 2020; Sagawa et al., 2020) and take shortcuts (Geirhos et al., 2020), leading to vulnerability in generalization to out-of-distribution data or adversarial examples in various NLP tasks: NLI (McCoy et al., 2019), Question-Answering (Jia and Liang, 2017), and Neural Machine Translation (Niu et al., 2020). Sanh et al. (2020) explore pruning strategies for transformers with little impact on headline metrics. Hendrycks and Dietterich (2019) present corruption and perturbation benchmarks to assess resilience under common shifts. Kamath et al. (2020) investigate selective prediction to abstain on uncertain inputs in question answering. Clark et al. (2020) analyze confidence calibration for pretrained language models under distribution changes. Zhang et al. (2021) study feature attribution stability across random seeds and training runs. Rudin (2019) advocates interpretability.

Mitigation Building on prior mitigation strategies such as counterfactual data augmentation (Jin et al., 2020; Alzantot et al., 2018), domain adaptation (Blitzer et al., 2006; Blitzer et al., 2007), and multi-task learning (Tu et al., 2020), we first identify unrobust correlations in an NLP model and then apply targeted interventions to encourage reliance on more stable signals.

VQA evaluation. The textual, visual, and answer biases discovered in the VQA dataset (Antol et al., 2015) spurred on recent work seeking to improve model evaluation for the task by eliminating these biases (Goyal et al., 2017), introducing distributional shifts (Agrawal et al., 2018), requiring model explanations (Li et al., 2018), thoroughly analyzing biases in datasets and models (Manjunatha et al., 2019), or evaluating on different recognition subtasks (Kafle and Kanan, 2017). While debiased and challenging benchmarks are important, their focus on accuracy as the sole evaluation metric leaves much to be desired (Ribeiro et al., 2020a;Kervadec et al., 2020). In contrast, our testbed provides question or image pairs that compare models' predictions between questions, measuring their accuracy, consistency, and robustness to a variety of text and image perturbations.

Synthetic VQA. One way in which we can generate diverse and balanced datasets is to generate them synthetically, as is done by Johnson et al. (2015) and Hudson and Manning (2019). Synthetically generating questions, images, or both, allows fine control over the distribution of questions, answers, and images. As both the CLEVR (Johnson et al., 2015) and GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019) datasets use image scene graphs for question and label generation, they contain questions combining a variety of required capabilities, including compositionality. We feature real-world images with synthetically generated questions as well, but in contrast to GQA, our evaluation has instance pairs to systematically test a focused set of capabilities, showing that models sometimes still struggle with simpler, non-compositional questions. Prior analyses have emphasized the need to probe model reliability through controlled perturbations to inputs and labels. Following this direction, we design paired instances that isolate linguistic and perceptual factors while holding answer semantics fixed. Zhang et al. (2016) studied attention mechanisms for image question answering on natural scenes with human gaze data as auxiliary supervision. Andreas et al. (2016) proposed modular neural networks that compose task-specific reasoning layouts from a library of neural units trained end to end. Teney et al. (2018) introduced a strong bottom-up and top-down attention model leveraging object proposals to achieve competitive accuracy on VQA 2.0 benchmarks. Kafle et al. (2019) surveyed question-type specific challenges and advocated specialized evaluation for counting, color, and reading tasks. Goyal et al. (2017) built VQA 2.0 to reduce language priors using balanced pairs datasets. Our evaluation departs from architecture-specific advances by holding the visual backbone fixed across comparisons. We report both absolute accuracy and pairwise consistency, capturing when two semantically equivalent prompts elicit divergent predictions. To quantify robustness, we introduce text paraphrases, lexical distractors, and contrastive crops that preserve the answer but perturb surface form. We also stratify results by question type and visual attribute to reveal where models overfit dataset artifacts. The resulting measurements enable diagnosis of brittle behaviors that are obscured on single-instance benchmarks. Collectively, these design choices yield a complementary perspective to accuracy-only leaderboards and support actionable model development.

document: Hate speech in user-generated content has been widely studied, with datasets spanning Twitter, Yahoo!, Fox News, Gab, and Reddit enabling supervised detection models (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Nobata et al., 2016; Gao and Huang, 2017; Davidson et al., 2017; Qian et al., 2019; Mathew et al., 2021; Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). By contrast, counter speech has received less attention, though recent efforts curate labeled examples and taxonomies across YouTube, Twitter, and German-language corpora to support automated recognition and generation (Mathew et al., 2019; Ziems et al., 2020; Garland et al., 2020; Qian et al., 2019; Chung et al., 2019; Fanton et al., 2021). Mathew et al. (2019) handcode 6,898 counter-hate comments from YouTube videos targeting Jews, Blacks, and LGBT communities. Ziems et al. (2020) compile a COVID-19 keyword list to locate counter-hate tweets, Garland et al. (2020) label German tweets by author community, Vidgen et al. (2021) introduce context in abuse annotation, Ren et al. (2016) study sentiment, and Ghosh et al. (2020) investigate sarcasm with context. We investigate whether incorporating conversational context improves hate and counter-hate detection on real user-generated data, providing a new benchmark and analysis of context sensitivity.

document:
Named Entity Recognition The mainstream NER systems are designed to recognize flat entities and based on a sequence tagging framework. Collobert et al. (2011) introduced the linear-chain conditional random field (CRF) into neural networkbased sequence tagging models, which can explicitly encode the transition likelihoods between adjacent tags. Many researchers followed this work, and employed LSTM as the encoder. In addition, character-level representations are typically used for English tasks (Huang et al., 2015;Lample et al., 2016;Ma and Hovy, 2016;Chiu and Nichols, 2016), whereas lexicon information is helpful for Chinese NER (Zhang and Yang, 2018;Ma et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020a).

Nested NER allows a token to belong to multiple entities, which conflicts with the plain sequence tagging framework. Ju et al. (2018) proposed to use stacked LSTM-CRFs to predict from inner to outer entities. Straková et al. (2019) concatenated the BILOU tags for each token inside the nested entities, which allows the LSTM-CRF to work as for flat entities. Luan et al. (2019) studied multi-task information extraction with shared span representations in a scientific domain. Sohrab and Miwa (2018) proposed an exhaustive approach for detecting entities by enumerating all possible spans. Finkel and Manning (2009) treated nested entities through a constituency-inspired model using recursive structures across labels. Boundary policies vary across widely used corpora. Li et al. (2020b) reformulated nested NER as a machine reading comprehension task. Shen et al. (2021) proposed to recognize nested entities by the two-stage object detection method widely used in computer vision.

Recent years, a body of literature emerged on span-based models, which were compatible with both flat and nested entities, and achieved SOTA performance (Eberts and Ulges, 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Li et al., 2021). These models typically enumerate all possible candidate text spans and then classify each span into entity types. In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020) is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing.

In addition, pretrained language models, also known as contextualized embeddings, were also widely introduced to NER models, and significantly boosted the model performance (Peters et al., 2018;Devlin et al., 2019). They are used in our baseline by default.

Label Smoothing Szegedy et al. (2016) proposed the label smoothing as a regularization technique to improve the accuracy of the Inception networks on ImageNet. By explicitly assigning a small probability to non-ground-truth labels, label smoothing can prevent the models from becoming too confident about the predictions, and thus improve generalization. Guo et al. (2017) analyzed confidence calibration for deep classifiers across vision benchmarks. Pereyra et al. (2017) penalized overconfident output distributions using confidence penalties related to label smoothing. Loshchilov and Hutter (2019) decoupled weight decay from the optimizer in large-batch training on ImageNet. Zhang et al. (2021) examined mixup strategies for sequence tagging tasks. It turned out to be a useful alternative to the standard cross entropy loss, and has been widely adopted to fight against the over-confidence (Zoph et al., 2018;Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017;Vaswani et al., 2017), improve the model calibration (Müller et al., 2019), and denoise incorrect labels (Lukasik et al., 2020).

Our proposed boundary smoothing applies the smoothing technique to entity boundaries, rather than labels. This is driven by the observation that entity boundaries are more ambiguous and inconsistent to annotate in NER engineering. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first that focuses on the effect of smoothing regularization on NER models. We evaluate on both flat and nested benchmarks to assess generality. The results show consistent gains without additional parameters or external resources.

document: There have been several studies addressing technical solutions for aspect-based sentiment analysis. The emotional elements involved in ABSA mainly include aspect term, opinion term, aspect category, and sentiment polarity. In order to extract these emotional elements, the main research direction of ABSA is to extract aspect terms (Liu et al., 2015; Yin et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019) and categorize the sentiment of a given aspect (Wang et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2019; Zhang and Qian, 2020), and to jointly predict multiple elements simultaneously at the same time (Li et al., 2019a; Wan et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). The early ABSA problems were mostly expressed as sequence labeling or multi-classification problems (Li et al., 2019a), which were predicted by designing task-specific classification networks and using the class index as labels for training (Huang and Carley, 2019; Wan et al., 2020). However, this approach requires the design of different classification models and ignores the label semantics. Recent works achieve good performance by converting the ABSA problems as a text generation problem (Yan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021a,b). In addition, the generative framework has been proven effective for many other natural language processing problems including dialogue state tracking (Feng et al., 2020), entity linking (De Cao et al., 2020), event extraction, information extraction (Sui et al., 2020), named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021; Tan et al., 2021; Raffel et al., 2019; Athiwaratkun et al., 2020). In particular, Paolini et al. (2021) solved various NLP tasks in a unified generative framework. Sun et al. (2019) introduced a span-based extractor for opinion tuples. Li et al. (2020) proposed a graph convolutional decoder for aspect-category polarity. Huguet Cabot and Navigli (2021) explored multilingual sense disambiguation with sequence-to-sequence models, discussing cross-lingual transfer and lexical resources. Peng et al. (2022) studied prompts for labeling. Building on this idea, constrained decoding, schema-guided prompting, and sequence tagging by generation have been explored to express structured outputs as flexible text. Joint models unify extraction and classification through copy-augmented generators and span pointers that reduce exposure bias in end-to-end learning. Mikolov et al. (2013) reported linear analogies in word vectors trained on monolingual corpora. Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) presented Natural Questions for open-domain QA with long-form answers and retrieval challenges across diverse web and encyclopedia document collections worldwide.

document: The robustness of MRC models is typically evaluated under test-time perturbations and domain shift. Research on test-time perturbation has proposed attack methods at lexical, syntactic, and discourse levels, including replacement of words with distributional neighbors, paraphrasing, and distractor injection (Rychalska et al., 2018; Gan and Ng, 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2018; Jia and Liang, 2017; Zhou et al., 2020; Si et al., 2021). Another line of work measures generalization across passages and questions drawn from mismatched sources, often assembling evaluation suites from multiple datasets (Fisch et al., 2019; Sen and Saffari, 2020). Our study belongs to the perturbation line and narrows the focus to entity-centric changes motivated by the diversity and fluidity of real-world names and attributes. We ask whether models preserve reasoning when salient entities are substituted while factual structure and syntactic scaffolding remain intact. Controlling or auditing model behavior with entity substitution has been explored in NER, relation extraction, NLI, and QA. Prior work creates contrastive samples by swapping named entities or anonymizing mentions to expose reliance on shallow cues (Agarwal et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2021; Mitra et al., 2020). In machine reading, entity edits can disrupt both coreference resolution and type constraints, revealing brittle span selection heuristics. Datasets with adversarial entity variants further show that readers often anchor on surface overlap rather than semantic roles (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). We build on these observations by systematically varying entity identity, type, and prominence inside passages and questions. Clark and Gardner (2018) introduced MultiRC with multi-sentence reasoning requirements. Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) presented Natural Questions with questions mined from real user queries. Min et al. (2020) examined questions answerable with partial evidence and questioned the necessity of full reasoning chains. Zhang et al. (2021) proposed contrast sets to probe underspecified reasoning. Dua et al. (2019) introduced DROP for evaluating discrete reasoning over paragraphs. Zellers et al. (2019) created HellaSwag to test commonsense inference robustly. While these resources vary in scope, our evaluation isolates entity substitution to measure robustness independent of unrelated confounders. We propose perturbation templates that preserve type compatibility and discourse coherence while altering entity identity across mentions. The templates operate on dependency parses and coreference chains, enabling controlled edits with minimal semantic drift. We report robustness curves as a function of substitution difficulty and calibration metrics to assess confidence under shift. Experiments on extractive and generative readers demonstrate consistent accuracy drops tied to entity salience rather than passage length or question form. Our findings motivate training strategies that represent entity types and align mentions contextually, reducing shortcut reliance without sacrificing in-domain performance.

document: Textual entity linking maps ambiguous mentions in text to canonical entries in a knowledge base. Early systems combined mention detection, candidate generation, and global coherence modeling with handcrafted features. Widely used datasets include AIDA for news, CWEB for web pages, and Zeshel for zero-shot domains. Standard evaluation trains on AIDA-train and tests on AIDA-test, MSNBC, and AQUAINT to compare models. Prior analyses noted score saturation on these benchmarks, limiting measurable progress on incremental ideas. Neural encoders shifted the field toward bi-encoder retrieval for candidates and cross-encoder reranking for disambiguation. Dense retrieval leverages entity descriptions and titles to build embeddings that improve recall over sparse methods. Cross-encoders model fine-grained mention–entity interactions but require careful negative sampling to remain efficient. Zero-shot EL emphasizes transfer across unseen domains by relying on textual definitions rather than link popularity. Domain adaptation methods introduce adversarial training, prompt tuning, and calibration to stabilize predictions under shift. Interest has recently expanded to multimodal entity linking, where mentions may be grounded jointly in text and images. MEL datasets derived from social media, encyclopedic pages, and curated knowledge sources target grounding across modalities. Moon et al. (2018) present a Weibo-based corpus aligned with CN-DBpedia for short-text grounding, including image attachments and metadata. Peng (2021) curates Wikipedia and Richpedia collections with images and infoboxes to study multimodal disambiguation, with cross-links across infobox fields. Biten et al. (2019) release entity-aware caption data emphasizing name recognition in images, covering person and organization mentions. Lu et al. (2018) compile a multimodal NER benchmark combining text and visual signals, with labels and bounding boxes. MEL models typically combine a vision encoder, a text encoder, and a fusion module to aggregate cross-modal cues. Region-aware designs attend to detected objects to align salient visual evidence with candidate entity attributes. Retrieval components index entity images, captions, and descriptions to increase the chance that the correct entity is retrieved. Hard negatives drawn from visually similar but semantically different entities sharpen the decision boundary during training. Global reasoning with entity–mention graphs propagates constraints such as mutual exclusivity and type compatibility across items. Recent work explores aligning CLIP-style contrastive pretraining with KB semantics through description regularization. Weak supervision from hyperlinks, alt text, and anchor contexts provides scalable supervision when manual annotation is scarce. Datasets for entity-aware image captioning and multimodal NER contribute resources but typically lack links to a unified KB. Linking complements recognition by resolving identity across mentions, images, and aliases under a single knowledge schema. Open-world MEL further requires detection of NIL entities and clustering them into coherent emerging concepts. Evaluation protocols report accuracy at k, macro F1 over linked and NIL cases, and calibration metrics for abstention quality. Practical applications include disambiguating product catalogs, news photo captions, and scientific figure references at scale. Our study situates MEL within EL traditions while emphasizing dataset curation, leakage control, and retrieval–reranking tradeoffs. We also outline a reproducible pipeline with released code, train–test splits, and ablations for future comparative research.

document: Open-domain response generation seeks to produce fluent and engaging replies for unrestricted user inputs across diverse topics. Early systems often adopted a machine translation view of conversation and subsequently leveraged neural Seq2Seq architectures with attention for data-driven learning. Ritter et al. (2011) framed conversational response generation as translation with data-driven templates. Sutskever et al. (2014) presented sequence-to-sequence LSTMs that enabled neural encoders and decoders for text. Vinyals and Le (2015) reported end-to-end conversation modeling on message–reply pairs, and Shang et al. (2015) studied microblog dialogues with attention and context. Building on these foundations, later studies incorporated conditioning variables to steer outputs toward persona consistency, topical relevance, or emotional tone using learned embeddings and controllable decoding. Despite these advances, little is known about how shared control spaces can be learned jointly from heterogeneous corpora without degrading faithfulness, coherence, or domain adequacy. A persona-based controller was explored by Li et al. (2016) with learned style embeddings. Knowledge-grounded dialog used memories and retrieval components in Serban et al. (2017) and Weston et al. (2018), and Dinan et al. (2019) introduced Wizard of Wikipedia for evidence-aware responses. Lowe et al. (2017) proposed ADEM to score responses on reference similarity. We address this gap by learning corpus-specific adapters that map heterogeneous supervision into a unified control representation, enabling multi-domain response generation with calibrated attribute strength and competitive quality under cross-corpus evaluation.

document: Open Information Extraction (OpenIE) aims to extract relational tuples without a predefined schema from raw text. Given a sentence s = w 1 w 2 ...w n where w i is the ith token in the sentence, an OpenIE system identifies argument and relation spans to produce triples of the form (subject; predicate; object). Early systems relied on surface patterns and distant supervision to achieve high-precision extractions in the web domain (Banko et al., 2007; Fader et al., 2011). Subsequent work incorporated dependency parsing, clause restructuring, and confidence modeling to improve recall and handle complex syntax (Mausam, 2016; Stanovsky and Dagan, 2016; Bhutani et al., 2016). Text summarization models demonstrated effective content selection in news corpora (Nallapati et al., 2016). Neural machine translation introduced attention mechanisms adopted across NLP (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Open-domain question answering aggregated evidence from multiple passages (Chen et al., 2017). Distant supervision for relation extraction aligned entity pairs with knowledge bases at scale (Mintz et al., 2009). In this paper, we revisit OpenIE under a span-centric formulation that jointly models boundary detection and relation typing with pretrained encoders. We construct training data by aligning high-quality extractions with dependency trees and apply consistency regularization across paraphrases. Experiments on OIE2016 and CaRB show improved precision-recall trade-offs and better calibration compared to recent neural baselines.

document: Given a set of entities E and a set of relations R, a knowledge graph K ⊆ E × R × E is a collection of subject-predicate-object (s, p, o) triples. Link prediction is the task of predicting missing triples in K by answering queries of the form of (s, p, ?) and (?, p, o). This is typically accomplished using knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models. Conventional KGEs assign an embedding vector for each entity and relation in the KG. They model the plausibility of triples via scoring functions that capture relational patterns such as symmetry, antisymmetry, composition, and inversion. Translation-based models operate in real space to enforce relational constraints through vector offsets. Bilinear and complex-valued models capture multiplicative interactions that better fit diverse relation types. Training commonly relies on margin-based or logistic losses with negative sampling to approximate unobserved facts. Regularization and normalization strategies mitigate representation collapse and encourage parameter identifiability. Recent work also explores geometric inductive biases that align relation semantics with algebraic structures. Bordes et al. (2013) introduced TransE to model relations as translations in vector space. Yang et al. (2015) presented DistMult with a diagonal bilinear form for triple scoring. Trouillon et al. (2016) proposed ComplEx using complex-valued embeddings, and Sun et al. (2019) developed RotatE with phase rotations. Schlichtkrull et al. (2018) designed R-GCNs for relational message passing over multi-relational graphs. Brown et al. (2020) reported few-shot generalization in very large language models. OpenAI (2023) released GPT-4 models. These methods vary widely in their inductive biases and data requirements, motivating careful evaluation across datasets and relation patterns. Graph neural approaches extend embeddings by aggregating neighborhood evidence with relation-aware convolutions. Rule learning and differentiable logic offer complementary paths to interpretability and sample efficiency. Knowledge graph question answering is the task of answering a natural language question using a KG as a source of knowledge. Questions may require either single-hop retrieval or multi-hop reasoning over multiple facts with constraints and type information. Embedding-based KGQA executes soft reasoning by mapping entities, relations, and query programs into a shared space. Semantic parsing methods translate questions into logical forms that are executed against the KG with compositional operators. Hybrid pipelines integrate entity linking, path search, and neural scoring to improve coverage and precision on noisy KGs. Public benchmarks and leaderboards have enabled standardized comparisons while revealing sensitivity to incompleteness and aliasing. In this work we review representative models and identify open problems in unifying link prediction and KGQA under missingness.

spans: SimCLR learns representations by contrasting augmented views with in-batch negatives and benefits from large batches for optimization (Chen et al., 2020). MoCo introduces a momentum encoder with a queue of negatives to maintain consistency (He et al., 2020), BYOL avoids negatives by predicting one view from another (Grill et al., 2020), and CLIP aligns images and text via large-scale contrastive pretraining to obtain transferable features (Radford et al., 2021).
document: Quantization of pre-trained language models compresses parameters into low-bit formats without altering the architecture, enabling deployment on constrained hardware. BERT-style encoders tolerate aggressive weight quantization with modest accuracy loss (Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Zadeh et al., 2020), and some studies explore 1-bit regimes (Bai et al., 2021), yet systematic approaches for generative PLMs remain underexplored. SimCLR learns representations by contrasting augmented views with in-batch negatives and benefits from large batches for optimization (Chen et al., 2020). MoCo introduces a momentum encoder with a queue of negatives to maintain consistency (He et al., 2020), BYOL avoids negatives by predicting one view from another (Grill et al., 2020), and CLIP aligns images and text via large-scale contrastive pretraining to obtain transferable features (Radford et al., 2021). We pursue token-level contrastive distillation tailored to quantized autoregressive models and differ from Wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) by operating on text, treating full-precision and quantized outputs as two views, and computing losses over all tokens in an auto-regressive manner rather than only over masked positions.

spans: Work on bias measurement in generative models examined toxicity prevalence and prompt sensitivity (Gehman et al., 2020). Safety-oriented decoding methods constrained generation using toxicity classifiers and lexical constraints in open-ended dialogue (Krause et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Red-teaming studies compiled challenging prompts and adversarial instructions for safety evaluation across benchmarks (Perez et al., 2022; Vidgen et al., 2021; Ganguli et al., 2022).
document: Research on adversarial training has shown consistent gains in robustness for NLP and ML models (Nie et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2019; Kiela et al., 2021). Adversarial attacks additionally expose structural vulnerabilities in deployed systems and datasets (Zhang et al., 2020a). Early studies in NLP adversarial examples emphasized perturbations that degrade accuracy on classification and sequence tasks (Li et al., 2017; Zang et al., 2020). Subsequent work broadened the evaluation to calibration, confidence, and faithfulness, yet accuracy remained the dominant outcome (Feng et al., 2018; Si et al., 2021). Within dialogue, prior studies investigated goal-oriented agents under test-time manipulation, largely reporting task success or slot-filling metrics (Cheng et al., 2019; Niu and Bansal, 2018). Ethical harms such as toxicity, harassment, or unsafe suggestions received comparatively less attention in attack design for conversational systems (Wallace et al., 2019; Sheng et al., 2020). Studies on toxic generation mainly introduced adversarial triggers or adversarial decoding but did not require imperceptibility to human readers (Wallace et al., 2019; Gehman et al., 2020). A smaller body of work considered human-in-the-loop red teaming to surface failures in dialogue models, trading scale for realism (Xu et al., 2020; Perez et al., 2022). Automated adversaries promise scalability but risk distributional drift away from natural user inputs if not carefully constrained (Morris et al., 2020). Imperceptible attacks for text pose unique challenges because semantics and fluency must be preserved under lexical or syntactic edits (Jin et al., 2020). Approaches that enforce meaning preservation typically rely on paraphrase constraints, embedding similarity, or controlled generation to maintain intent while altering surface form (Alzantot et al., 2018; Iyyer et al., 2018). For conversational safety, attackers must also account for context history and speaker roles so that toxic outcomes arise from plausible turns rather than overtly malicious prompts (Keskar et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021). Work on bias measurement in generative models examined toxicity prevalence and prompt sensitivity (Gehman et al., 2020). Safety-oriented decoding methods constrained generation using toxicity classifiers and lexical constraints in open-ended dialogue (Krause et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Red-teaming studies compiled challenging prompts and adversarial instructions for safety evaluation across benchmarks (Perez et al., 2022; Vidgen et al., 2021; Ganguli et al., 2022). In addition to crafting perturbations, several defenses aim to detect or mitigate adversarially induced toxicity using rejection sampling, toxicity-aware decoding, or classifier-guided filtering (Schick et al., 2021; Welbl et al., 2021). Defense evaluations often conflate distributional shift with true adversarial robustness, making it difficult to separate safety gains from domain mismatch effects (Goodfellow et al., 2015; Tramèr et al., 2020). Dialogue models present compounding risks because multi-turn context can launder toxic content across turns through coreference and pragmatic inference (Henderson et al., 2019). Work on safety datasets such as RealToxicityPrompts and SafetyKit provided challenging prompts but did not explore minimally invasive edits within realistic chats (Gehman et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2022). Recent controllable generation studies target detoxification objectives by training or prompting models to avoid unsafe continuations given a safety policy (Krause et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). These techniques can reduce average toxicity yet remain brittle to small, fluent perturbations that shift lexical priors or topic framing (Qiu et al., 2023). Human evaluation remains essential for verifying imperceptibility and intent preservation, but cost and annotator disagreement limit scale and replicability (Pavlick and Kwiatkowski, 2019). Automatic metrics such as semantic similarity and perplexity provide proxies for imperceptibility, though they correlate imperfectly with human judgments in dialogue (Bianchi et al., 2021). Black-box attacks that query only model outputs are most relevant for deployed assistants, where internal gradients and probabilities are inaccessible (Ilyas et al., 2018). The intersection of imperceptible adversarial editing and conversational safety thus defines an open area where attack objectives, constraints, and evaluations must be aligned. Our review motivates a framework that targets toxic outcomes in dialogue under human-plausible perturbations while enabling scalable, reproducible benchmarking.

document: Traditional sentiment analysis tasks are sentence-level (Yang and Cardie, 2014; Severyn and Moschitti, 2015) or document-level (Dou, 2017; Lyu et al., 2020) oriented. In contrast, Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) is an aspect- or entity-oriented fine-grained sentiment analysis task. The three basic subtasks are Aspect Term Extraction (ATE) (Hu and Liu, 2004; Yin et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018b; Xu et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019; Chen and Qian, 2020), Aspect Sentiment Classification (ASC) (Wang et al., 2016b; Tang et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018a; Li et al., 2021), and Opinion Term Extraction (OTE) (Cardie, 2012, 2013; Fan et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020b). Many studies solve these tasks separately and ignore the dependencies among subtasks. Consequently, several works couple subtasks and propose models to jointly extract aspect-based pairs. This line of work mainly includes two tasks: Aspect and Opinion Term Co-Extraction (AOTE) (Wang et al., 2016a; Dai and Song, 2019; Wang and Pan, 2019; Wu et al., 2020a) and Aspect–Sentiment Pair Extraction (ASPE) (Ma et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019a, 2019b; He et al., 2019). Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) introduced the ASTE task and developed a two-stage pipeline that couples aspect extraction, sentiment classification, and opinion extraction. To extend this idea, Mao et al. (2021) and Chen et al. (2021a) cast ASTE as machine reading comprehension and used a shared BERT encoder with multi-stage decoding to obtain triplets. Another line of research designs novel tagging schemes to enable end-to-end triplet extraction (Wu et al., 2020a; Xu et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021). Xu et al. (2021) proposed a position-aware tagging scheme that enriches label expressiveness. Wu et al. (2020a) developed a grid tagging scheme, akin to table filling (Miwa and Sasaki, 2014; Gupta et al., 2016), to solve this task in an end-to-end manner. Yan et al. (2021) reformulated ASTE as a generative task with constrained decoding. Pretrained encoders have been studied across information extraction and summarization tasks in disparate settings (Zhang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Zhang et al. (2020) proposed span-based relation extraction with decoders that capture boundary interactions and label dependencies for improved coupling. Li et al. (2020) introduced variational opinion summarization that aggregates sentiment-bearing expressions into concise summaries with latent regularization. Sun et al. (2019) investigated post-training strategies for adapting BERT to domain-specific review corpora using objectives and curated vocabularies. Gao and Zhao (2021) assessed contrastive learning for sentence embeddings on recommendation and retrieval benchmarks with negative mining and temperature scaling. Despite these advances, most approaches underutilize syntactic structure and lexical semantics that can sharpen triplet boundaries. Dependency relations and part-of-speech patterns have been shown to aid opinion–target alignment in related extraction tasks. Recent work integrates graph neural networks or syntax-aware attention to encode word–word relations for ABSA-style modeling. Building on these insights, we argue for a unified architecture that fuses contextual encoders with lightweight linguistic priors to improve ASTE. Our review motivates methods that jointly model structural cues and sentiment signals while maintaining end-to-end efficiency.

Knowledge graph embedding learning (Bordes et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2014; Trouillon et al., 2016; Dettmers et al., 2018; Shang et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Ji et al., 2021) has been an active research area with applications in knowledge base completion and relation extraction. Several studies have extended static embedding models to temporal knowledge graphs by incorporating time-aware scoring functions or sequence encoders. Jiang et al. (2016) proposed a time-aware variant of TransE to handle evolving facts. Lacroix et al. (2020) extended ComplEx to model time via order-4 tensor decompositions. Later work introduced attention mechanisms over temporal neighborhoods and interval representations to model validity periods. Temporal QA on Knowledge Graph. Temporal QA has mostly been studied in the context of reading comprehension. ForecastQA (Jin et al., 2021) formulates the forecasting problem as a multiple-choice QA task with time-stamped articles and questions. The recently released TORQUE (Ning et al., 2020) explores temporal ordering relations between events described in text. EventQA (Zhou et al., 2021) proposes structured temporal questions grounded in news timelines. TemporalBERT (Liu et al., 2020) adapts pretraining with time-sensitive signals for improved temporal inference. Datasets for historical QA curate chronologically aligned passages and questions (Chen et al., 2021; Khandelwal et al., 2022), and multi-hop temporal chains are annotated for evaluation benchmarks. Another direction is temporal question answering over knowledge bases, which retrieves time information from a structured KG. TempQuestions (Jia et al., 2018a) is a KGQA dataset specifically aimed at temporal questions. Building on this dataset, Jia et al. (2018b) designed a method that decomposes a query into non-temporal and temporal sub-questions. The knowledge graph used in TempQuestions is a subset of Freebase that lacks explicit temporal indexing. Jia et al. (2021) introduced EXAQT, an end-to-end system that leverages question-relevant compact subgraphs and relational graph convolutional networks to predict answers. Early datasets contained a limited number of temporally grounded questions, restricting robust evaluation. CRONQUESTIONS (Saxena et al., 2021) scales temporal KGQA with time annotations on all edges and diverse question types. Based on this resource, CronKGQA (Saxena et al., 2021) exploits advances in temporal KG embeddings to outperform strong baselines. Parallel advances in representation learning for temporal facts have produced models that reason over intervals, durations, and recurring events. HyTE (Dasgupta et al., 2018) embeds entities and relations into hyperplanes indexed by time to encode temporal ordering. TTransE and its variants incorporate temporal information by augmenting relation vectors with time-specific translations. RE-Net (Jin et al., 2019) models event sequences with recurrent inference over evolving neighborhoods for future link prediction. CyGNet (Zhu et al., 2021) captures cyclical temporal patterns via multi-period filters to improve extrapolation. For QA, temporal reasoning benefits further from constraints derived from Allen interval algebra and learned temporal commonsense priors, and recent benchmarks emphasize multi-hop reasoning across time, composition of constraints, and robustness to incomplete histories. Our work situates itself at this intersection by aligning temporal embedding priors with query decomposition and constraint propagation, targeting explainable predictions that expose temporal evidence paths while maintaining competitive accuracy on temporally complex queries.

For G2P, converting words into pronunciations does not require as much expertise as needed to prepare correspondences between graphemes and phonemes. Therefore, a method of learning correspondences from a large number of wordpronunciation pairs has been used (van den Bosch and Daelemans, 1993). To solve the problem that graphemes and phonemes often have many-to-many correspondence, a hidden Markov model-based method (Jiampojamarn et al., 2007) and weighted finite-state transducer-based methods (Novak et al., 2012a,b) have been developed.

In addition, there have been some attempts to apply encoder-decoder models to learn end-to-end G2P models. For example, Toshniwal and Livescu (2016) applied a sequence-to-sequence architecture (Sutskever et al., 2014; Luong et al., 2015). Yolchuyeva et al. (2020) applied a transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) to train a single model that can deal with a large number of languages. Attention mechanisms allow direct conditioning on grapheme context without explicit alignment, which reduces engineering effort while retaining strong performance across diverse orthographies. Joint sequence models further capture dependencies between adjacent output phonemes, complementing monotonic alignment assumptions common in earlier pipelines. Bisani and Ney (2008) introduced joint n-gram models that treat grapheme and phoneme pairs as coupled units for improved consistency. Rao et al. (2015) explored neural aligners that relax independence assumptions between positions in input and output sequences. Yao and Zweig (2015) leveraged minimum Bayes risk decoding to emphasize pronunciation variants favored by downstream error metrics across datasets. Deri and Knight (2016) focused on generating pronunciations for names using language-specific rules with feature templates curated from linguistic resources. Zhang et al. (2019) reported multilingual transfer with character embeddings, and Peter et al. (2017) studied semi-supervised lexicon expansion using raw text. Despite progress, low-resource languages remain challenging due to limited lexicons and irregular orthographies. Recent multilingual approaches learn shared subword representations while allowing language-specific adapters to capture phonotactic idiosyncrasies. Phoneme error rate and word error rate remain the dominant evaluation metrics, yet cross-lingual calibration is rarely reported in a standardized way. Data augmentation with graphemic noise and pronunciation variants can improve robustness to orthographic drift and loanwords. Incorporating stress and tone prediction as auxiliary tasks has been shown to reduce overconfident vowel confusions in stress-marking languages. Semi-supervised training can exploit raw text by distilling pseudo-pronunciations from teacher models to bootstrap target lexicons. Lexicon pruning and confidence scoring help practitioners trade accuracy for computational cost during large vocabulary decoding. Our study revisits alignment, architecture, and transfer under a unified experimental protocol spanning typologically diverse languages. We release reproducible code and standardized splits to facilitate fair comparison and incremental improvements in future research.

document: In this paper, we use the encoder-decoder Transformer framework (Vaswani et al., 2017) to introduce a streamlined language generation model. To simplify exposition, we focus on the self-attention network (SAN) core and omit auxiliary components such as positional encodings and deep stacking. Sutskever et al. (2014) introduced sequence-to-sequence learning with deep LSTMs for machine translation and demonstrated strong performance on large parallel corpora with simple decoding. Bahdanau et al. (2015) proposed an attention mechanism that computes soft alignments between source and target positions and improves gradient flow during training across long input sequences. Gehring et al. (2017) presented a convolutional sequence to sequence architecture using gated linear units and positional embeddings, reporting competitive accuracy and reduced decoding latency on translation benchmarks. Devlin et al. (2019), Lewis et al. (2020), and Brown et al. (2020) released large pre-trained models for fine-tuning or prompting across tasks. The encoder represents the source input X = {x1, …, xJ} as contextual vectors H = {h1, …, hJ} using SANs. The decoder generates the target sequence word by word by attending to H and the previously generated fragment. Given the partial target y<i with embeddings E(y<i) from the target vocabulary matrix, we pack them into key-value matrices Ki-1 and Vi-1 at step i via a function M(·) that stacks vectors row-wise. A self-attention block produces the intermediate representation si using the prior context ci-1 in R^d_model, and a cross-attention block then yields the time-dependent context ci from source-derived Ke and Ve. Finally, an MLP computes Pg(yi | y<i, X) to select argmax output at step i, and training maximizes the conditional log-likelihood J(θ) = sum log Pg(Y | X; θ) over pairs {X, Y}.

document: Early studies on the punctuation restoration task explored a wide range of features such as lexical, acoustic, prosodic, and their combination (Gravano et al., 2009;Levy et al., 2012;Xu et al., 2014;Che et al., 2016a;Szaszák and Tündik, 2019). Graphical models such as conditional random field have been widely used for this task (Lu and Ng, 2010;Zhang et al., 2013) before the emerging of neural networks. Recently, a variety of deep neural network architectures have been explored such as long short-term memory (Gale and Parthasarathy, 2017), convolutional neural network (Che et al., 2016b), and transformer (Alam et al., 2020). Corpora for punctuation restoration are usually created as part of automatic speech recognition in various domains such as meetings (McCowan et al., 2005), TED talks (Federico et al., 2012), audio books (Panayotov et al., 2015), and film subtitles (Tiedemann, 2016). Among these, the TED corpus is widely used as the benchmark corpus for punctuation restoration. However, livestreaming videos have not been explored for this task. Domain shift in livestreaming arises from spontaneous speech, overlapping talk, and latency constraints that alter prosodic cues and token distributions. Tilk and Alumae (2016) introduce an attention-based recurrent model for punctuation prediction on public TED talks and lecture data collections. Zhang and Vosoughi (2018) study bidirectional LSTM encoders with pre-trained embeddings, Bullock et al. (2019) examine diarization-aware segmentation for meetings, and Tsai et al. (2019) fuse acoustic and visual streams with powerful multimodal transformers for conversational understanding. We target this gap by curating a large-scale livestreaming corpus with aligned transcripts and by designing a latency-aware transformer. Our contributions include a benchmark for streaming punctuation, a study of robustness to ASR errors, and a set of reproducible baselines.

The earliest datasets mostly focus on simple local object property identification (Antol et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2016). Later, datasets began to emphasize compositional visual reasoning. For example, Suhr et al. (2017) and Johnson et al. (2017) use synthetic images or controlled language to study spatial relations. More recently, datasets using real images and real language such as Hudson and Manning (2019) and Liu et al. (2021) are proposed for reasoning about natural language descriptions of photos. Yet these datasets primarily emphasize local grounding within the image rather than global grounding beyond the image with open-ended reasoning. While tasks and datasets vary, the underlying associations between language and visual concepts are often shared across settings (Lu et al., 2020). document: Automatic evaluation metrics are widely used to argue that one summarization system outperforms another by reporting improved metric values on shared test sets. To validate such claims, researchers assess how well automatic metrics replicate human judgments through system-level correlations. Given N systems and M input documents, each system’s summaries are scored by an automatic metric X and independently judged by humans Z. The system-level correlation between X and Z is computed from the average scores per system, yielding a single estimate of alignment. We adopt Kendall’s tau-b as the correlation function because it emphasizes correct pairwise ordering of systems and handles ties gracefully. Lin (2004) introduced ROUGE for n-gram overlap evaluation in summarization. Zhang et al. (2020) proposed BERTScore using contextual embeddings to compare candidate and reference summaries with sensitivity to lexical and phrasal paraphrases. Fabbri et al. (2021) released SummEval to systematically assess metric behavior on diverse systems and datasets across multiple summarization benchmarks and domains. Because r_SYS depends on which systems and documents are sampled, it is only an estimate of the true correlation between X and Z. To quantify uncertainty, Deutsch et al. (2021b) introduced confidence-interval procedures, including BOOT-INPUTS, which resamples documents to produce a distribution over r_SYS values. Papineni et al. (2002) introduced BLEU for machine translation, using modified n-gram precision with a brevity penalty. Banerjee and Lavie (2005) developed METEOR with stemming and synonym matching, and Popović (2015) proposed chrF based on character n-grams. Rei et al. (2020) presented COMET as a learned evaluation model, and Sellam et al. (2020) released BLEURT trained on synthetic perturbations and human ratings. Bojar et al. (2016) organized WMT shared tasks, Mathur et al. (2020) analyzed metric reliability across language pairs, Ma et al. (2019) studied calibration for quality estimation, and Kocmi et al. (2021) examined reference-free metrics at scale extensively. In our experiments, we report r_SYS alongside nonparametric bootstrap intervals to support reliable model selection and reproducible conclusions.

document: Pretrained language models contain broad world knowledge that can be elicited through prompting. Prior studies demonstrate that such models answer factual cloze queries and relational probes with notable accuracy (Davison et al., 2019; Petroni et al., 2019). As a result, they can be steered to support commonsense reasoning, text classification, and natural language inference without task-specific fine-tuning (Trinh and Le, 2018; Shin et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Building on these observations, we elicit explicit, question-targeted statements from a generator and feed them to an inference model. This approach treats pretrained models as on-demand, adaptable knowledge sources. Research on leveraging external knowledge for commonsense reasoning follows two main routes. One route injects structured knowledge during pretraining or fine-tuning so the model can reason with retrieved facts (Mitra et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2019; Bian et al., 2021). Smith et al. (2020) study curriculum strategies for multi-hop QA with decomposed hints. Johnson and Lee (2019) introduce data augmentation using backtranslation to increase diversity in sentence-level classification. Nguyen et al. (2018) report annotator disagreement patterns in moral foundation labeling and release guidelines for resolving category ambiguities. Li et al. (2021) analyze calibration under shift. Another route grounds questions in knowledge graphs and performs graph-based reasoning to connect evidence and hypotheses (Lin et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2020; Yasunaga et al., 2021). A shared prerequisite across these routes is access to a high-quality, in-domain commonsense knowledge base (Ma et al., 2019). Datasets themselves are sometimes derived from specific resources, such as CommonsenseQA from ConceptNet and Social IQa from ATOMIC (Talmor et al., 2019; Speer et al., 2017; Sap et al., 2019a; Sap et al., 2019b). For such settings, aligning retrieval to the originating resource often yields measurable gains (Mitra et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020). When the dataset domain diverges from the knowledge base, these gains shrink and can even reverse due to spurious links or missing coverage (Ma et al., 2019). This limitation becomes critical for tasks lacking a suitable resource or spanning multiple domains, including NumerSense and CommonsenseQA 2.0 (Talmor et al., 2021). We address this gap by eliciting compact, targeted knowledge from pretrained generators without relying on external ontologies. Adding generated text at inference time is another promising strategy for improving reasoning. Self-talk appends short concept clarifications to the input of the reasoner (Shwartz et al., 2020). Contrastive explanation prompting supplies statements that highlight distinctions between candidate answers (Paranjape et al., 2021). Other methods fine-tune generators to produce free-form rationales that are concatenated to the question (Rajani et al., 2019; Latcinnik and Berant, 2020). DynaGen expands the input with implications produced by pretrained commonsense models, but its reliance on COMeT restricts the domain coverage (Bosselut et al., 2019). Our method instead uses a small set of task-style demonstrations to condition the generator, improving flexibility and transfer with minimal engineering. These findings situate our approach within a growing movement to externalize and control latent knowledge for safer, more general reasoning.

document: Language models trained on private data pose risks of revealing sensitive information. Prior studies have mainly considered black-box attacks where attackers observe only inputs and outputs. Carlini et al. (2021) executed model inversion against GPT-2 using descriptive prompts with beam search to elicit memorized sequences. Lehman et al. (2021) probed BERT pretrained on Electronic Health Records, using blank filling to recover Personal Health Information. With black-box access to both pre-training and fine-tuning interfaces, Zanella-Béguelin et al. (2020) extracted sensitive sequences from the fine-tuning data, and Malekzadeh et al. (2021) analyzed attribute leakage in honest‑but‑curious client–server settings. Tigunova et al. (2019) introduced the Hidden Attribute Model to infer speakers’ professions and genders from multi-domain dialogues. Pan et al. (2020) leveraged language model embeddings to reconstruct digits and salient keywords from inputs. Shokri et al. (2017) investigated membership inference attacks on machine learning models, and Li et al. (2021) addressed persona extraction from conversational data. Zhang et al. (2020) trained weakly supervised attribute classifiers over forum conversations. Feyisetan et al. (2020) proposed token-level transformations to mask sensitive text. We build on these observations by inferring richer private attributes from conversational embeddings while designing defenses that preserve downstream utility.

spans: PKD (Sun et al., 2019) reports improvements on classification with fixed layer correspondences. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2019), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020b), ALP-KD, MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b), and CoDIR (Sun et al., 2020a) adopt disparate objectives targeting attention, embeddings, or contrast, and DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019a) emphasizes hidden-state similarity without mapping choices.
document: Recent years have seen a surge of techniques for transferring knowledge in transformer-based NLU models beyond simple logits matching. DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019a) augments the objective with a cosine similarity between teacher and student hidden states. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2019), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020b), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b) further align intermediate representations and attention distributions. A separate line focuses on intermediate layer distillation with explicit layer-to-layer mappings. In PKD, Sun et al. (2019) distilled a 12-layer BERT teacher into a 6-layer student using fixed mappings such as PKD-LAST and PKD-SKIP. While effective, fixed mappings can underutilize teacher depth and require manual design. CKD (Wu et al., 2020a) partitions teacher layers into groups matching the number of student layers and distills grouped representations. ALP-KD learns attention weights from each student layer over all teacher layers to derive weighted teacher hints, reducing manual choices at the cost of extra compute. CoDIR (Sun et al., 2020a) introduces contrastive objectives that avoid deterministic mappings but demand negative sampling and memory banks. PKD (Sun et al., 2019) reports improvements on classification with fixed layer correspondences. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2019), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020b), ALP-KD, MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b), and CoDIR (Sun et al., 2020a) adopt disparate objectives targeting attention, embeddings, or contrast, and DistilBERT (Sanh et al., 2019a) emphasizes hidden-state similarity without mapping choices. These observations motivate methods that jointly address layer skip and search while keeping training cost modest. Our proposed RAIL-KD learns a sparse routing from teacher to student that adds negligible overhead during training. Across 24-to-6 and 12-to-4 compressions, it matches or exceeds prior intermediate-layer distillation results while converging faster than CoDIR. Unlike PKD and CKD, RAIL-KD does not require sweeping mapping hyperparameters. We compare against methods targeting skip and search, and note that orthogonal losses such as attention distribution matching in TinyBERT or MiniLM could be layered atop our routing scheme.

(2019) study sentiment reversal under negation with attention-based models on review datasets. Hossain et al. (2020) compile a benchmark for negation and speculation detection from clinical narratives. Pavlick and Callison-Burch (2016) introduce paraphrase resources that include negated variants. document:
Revealing affirmative interpretations from negations is a challenging endeavor. In the literature, researchers primarily seek to identify scope and focus of negation. The creation of the BioScope and ConanDoyle-Neg (Morante and Daelemans, 2012) corpora spearheaded research on scope detection (Morante and Daelemans, 2009), and the PB-FOC corpus (Blanco and Moldovan, 2011) clarified focus annotation practices. The main goal of this paper is to find these affirmative counterparts to generate affirmative interpretations.

More related to the work presented here, Sarabi et al. (2019) present a corpus of negations and their underlying affirmative interpretations (they call them positive interpretations). We are inspired by them but bypass several of their limitations. First, they only work with negations from Simple Wikipedia, a site devoted to English learners. As a result, their corpus uses relatively unsophisticated vocabulary and grammar. Second, they impose several restrictions on the negations they work with, including constraints on cue position, sentence length, and token sets. Third, their affirmative interpretations are restricted to a rephrasing of the statement containing negation with only one change: an argument of the negated predicate. In contrast, we barely impose restrictions on the negations we work with, excluding only questions and auxiliary-verb negations. More importantly, we introduce a question-driven approach that allows us to obtain multiple affirmative interpretations with increasing degrees of complexity.

Fancellu et al. (2017) design a neural architecture for universal dependency parsing with explicit treatment of negation scope. Zhang et al. (2019) study sentiment reversal under negation with attention-based models on review datasets. Hossain et al. (2020) compile a benchmark for negation and speculation detection from clinical narratives. Pavlick and Callison-Burch (2016) introduce paraphrase resources that include negated variants. Gururangan et al. (2018) analyze annotation artifacts extensively.

Recently, Jiang et al. (2021) study the problem of identifying commonsense implications of negations and contradictions. More specifically, they work with if-then rules such as If X does not learn new things, then X does not gain new knowledge and If X does not leave the building, then X stays in the building. These rules capture general commonsense knowledge about what happens if an event does not occur. Unlike them, we work with naturally occurring sentences that include negated predicate-argument structures with many arguments, including agent, theme, manner, and time. Our annotation guidelines explicitly separate truth-conditional content from descriptive modifiers to expose the affirmative core. We operationalize this idea by eliciting targeted questions that surface alternative arguments consistent with a factual reading. The resulting interpretations enable downstream tasks such as information extraction and summarization to retain events that would otherwise be discarded as negative evidence. We release data, code, and evaluation scripts to facilitate reproducible research. Finally, we outline open challenges in modeling pragmatics, presupposition, and scalar inferences that interact with negation.

document: Summarization systems can be generally categorized into two paradigms: extractive and abstractive. Extractive systems select sentences or clauses from the input using salience-driven features (Zhou and Rush, 2019) or constructed features (He et al., 2012). Abstractive systems generate novel utterances via sequence-to-sequence learning in supervised settings (Zhang et al., 2020). Recently, unsupervised abstractive summarization has attracted increasing attention. Yang et al. (2020) treat the lead of a news article as pseudo ground truth, which is effective on well-structured sources such as CNN/DailyMail but brittle elsewhere. Wang and Lee (2018) and Baziotis et al. (2019) introduce cycle-consistency objectives for unsupervised summarization. Schumann et al. (2020) propose edit-based local search that enables richer scoring and outperforms cycle consistency and beam search. Li et al. (2018) investigate pointer-generator networks with coverage to reduce repetition. Narayan et al. (2018) address extreme summarization with a dataset emphasizing single-sentence headlines. Paulus et al. (2018) apply reinforcement learning to optimize ROUGE with a mixed objective. Gehrmann et al. (2018) introduce bottom-up content selection to constrain neural decoders. Chen and Bansal (2018) propose fast abstractive summarization with a bottom-up approach over extracted phrases. See Nallapati et al. (2016) for a neural extractive baseline model. Our work follows Schumann et al. (2020) but replaces the discrete search with a trained non-autoregressive encoder-only model optimized with CTC to smooth search noise and improve efficiency. We further introduce a dynamic programming procedure for exact length control in the unsupervised setting, aligning input and output positions during decoding.
