document: Probing pretrained language models has become a central method for characterizing the internal structure of contextual encoders. The unprecedented capability of modern PLMs to provide rich contextualized representations has motivated a surge of diagnostic studies. Researchers have examined what knowledge these models capture, probing for syntax, semantics, and world knowledge (Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Chi et al., 2020; Vulić et al., 2020). This line of inquiry has consolidated into the so‑called BERTology literature that surveys mechanisms and findings across tasks (Rogers et al., 2020). A typical probing setup defines a targeted prediction objective and trains a lightweight classifier on frozen model states to test for linear separability of information. The rationale is that a simple probe reduces confounds and attributes success to representational content rather than probe capacity. Nonetheless, some relations appear to require nonlinear probes, which calls for careful controls to avoid learning the task from scratch (White et al., 2021; Hewitt and Liang, 2019). Across languages and datasets, probing has revealed rediscovery of pipeline structures, syntactic distances, lexical relations, and morphology (Tenney et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Vulić et al., 2020; Chi et al., 2020). Semantic role labeling offers a fertile testbed for probing because it links predicates to argument spans and requires structural integration. Early diagnostic work applied edge probing tasks to measure which BERT layers best capture predicate–argument relations, with strongest signals in middle layers (Tenney et al., 2019). Follow-up analyses have studied the localization of role information and the interaction of syntactic heads with argument boundaries in transformer attention maps. In parallel, state-of-the-art SRL systems now rely on PLMs as their primary input representation across span-based and dependency formulations (Conia and Navigli, 2020; Blloshmi et al., 2021). Performance on gold-standard corpora such as CoNLL‑2009 and CoNLL‑2012 has continued to improve with multilingual and cross-lingual extensions (Hajič et al., 2009; Pradhan et al., 2012). Peters et al. (2018) introduced deep contextual embeddings for sequence modeling and showed gains on tagging and parsing. Clark et al. (2019) examined attention patterns and proposed probing heads to interpret transformer behaviors. Kovaleva et al. (2019) analyzed BERT heads and layer redundancy in fine-tuning across tasks. Ethayarajh (2019) quantified contextuality and similarity of token embeddings using self-similarity metrics across layers. Liu et al. (2019) surveyed transfer learning practices for NLP. Despite these insights, the relationship between latent structure and SRL generalization remains partly open, especially under domain shift. We focus on layer-wise representations and design probes that target frame-evoking predicates and long-distance arguments in challenging settings. Our experiments compare linear and shallow nonlinear probes while controlling for memorization with selective parameterization and control tasks. The results contextualize probing readouts with end-task SRL performance and point to design choices that yield faithful, task-relevant diagnostics.

spans: Prompt-based retrieval has been explored for open-domain QA (Guu et al., 2020;Lewis et al., 2020). Domain-adaptive pretraining improves answer selection by refining contextualized representations and stabilizing training dynamics (Gururangan et al., 2020;Glass et al., 2020). See Zhang (2021).
document: Discourse structure. Our work is closely related to functional structures defined through content types explored in other domains; prior work has affirmed the usefulness of these structures in downstream NLP tasks. In news, Choubey et al. (2020) adopted Van Dijk (2013)'s content schema cataloging events (e.g., main event, anecdotal), which they showed to improve the performance of event coreference resolution. In scientific writing, content types (e.g., background, methodology) are shown to be useful for summarization (Teufel and Moens, 2002;Cohan et al., 2018), information extraction (Mizuta et al., 2006;Liakata et al., 2012), and information retrieval (Kircz, 1991;Liddy, 1991). The discourse structure of argumentative texts (e.g., support, rebuttal) (Peldszus and Stede, 2013;Becker et al., 2016;Stab and Gurevych, 2017) has also been applied to argumentation mining. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has studied the discourse structure of long-form answers. Question Answering. Recent work (Cao and Wang, 2021) has investigated the ontology of questions, which includes comparison questions, verification questions, judgement questions, etc. We construct the ontology of functional roles of answer sentences. One of the roles in our ontology is summary, yielding an extractive summarization dataset. This shares motivation with a line of work studying query-focused summarization (Xu and Lapata, 2020). Prompt-based retrieval has been explored for open-domain QA (Guu et al., 2020;Lewis et al., 2020). Domain-adaptive pretraining improves answer selection by refining contextualized representations and stabilizing training dynamics (Gururangan et al., 2020;Glass et al., 2020). See Zhang (2021). We focus on long-form answers where sentences fulfill heterogeneous roles such as definition, evidence, and caveat. Our taxonomy is informed by preliminary qualitative analysis of community QA forums and Wikipedia references. We release annotation guidelines and baselines to support reproducible study. Lastly, our work builds upon two datasets containing long form answers (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019;Fan et al., 2019) and extends the analysis of long form answers from earlier studies (Krishna et al., 2021).

document: Table-based fact verification aims to assess claims against semi-structured evidence such as relational tables by combining schema-aware retrieval with numerical and logical reasoning. Zhong et al. (2020) propose executing latent programs over relational tables to mediate compositional operations for fact verification. Shi et al. (2020) design table-aware transformers with cell, row, and column encodings for claim checking, and Yang and Zhu (2021) study numeric consistency constraints on aggregation-sensitive claims across heterogeneous schemas. Chen et al. (2020) curate TabFact, emphasizing natural language statements paired with tables without symbolic annotations or metadata. Building on these advances, recent systems integrate program induction with neural retrievers and verifiers to better capture arithmetic operations and row selection in realistic tables. Jacobs et al. (1991) formalize mixture-of-experts with a gating network that allocates inputs to expert models, Jordan and Jacobs (1994) analyze competitive learning dynamics for expert specialization in expectation-maximization settings, and Shazeer et al. (2017) scale sparse gating to thousands of experts with conditional computation for language modeling. Fedus et al. (2021) demonstrate Switch Transformers with single-active experts per token to increase capacity without proportional latency, Lepikhin et al. (2020) report cross-device routing strategies for large sparse models, and Riquelme et al. (2021) study expert capacity allocation in routing algorithms for balanced utilization across tasks and modalities and data domains. Motivated by the complementary strengths of specialist modules and structured reasoning, we present a self-adaptive mixture-of-experts verifier that routes claims to numeric, symbolic, and retrieval experts using performance-aware gating trained on development feedback.

spans: Neural Variational Document Model introduces a latent Gaussian layer for topic discovery with amortized inference (Miao et al., 2016). ProdLDA replaces the mixture with a product of experts to sharpen topics under a logistic-normal prior (Srivastava and Sutton, 2017). Contextualized Topic Models use sentence-transformer embeddings to anchor word distributions across documents in practice (Bianchi et al., 2021).
document:
Seed-Guided Topic Discovery. Seed-guided topic models aim to leverage user-provided seeds to discover underlying topics according to users' interests. Early studies take LDA (Blei et al., 2003) as the backbone and incorporate seeds into model learning. For example, Andrzejewski et al. (2009) consider must-link and cannot-link constraints among seeds as priors. SeededLDA (Jagarlamudi et al., 2012) encourages topics to contain more seeds and encourages documents to select topics related to the seeds they contain. Anchored CorEx (Gallagher et al., 2017) extracts maximally informative topics by jointly compressing the corpus and preserving seed relevant informa-tion. Recent studies start to utilize embedding techniques to learn better word semantics. For example, CatE (Meng et al., 2020) explicitly encourages distinction among retrieved topics via categoryname guided embedding learning. However, all these models require the provided seeds to be invocabulary, mainly because they focus on the input corpus only and are not equipped with general knowledge of PLMs.

Embedding-Based Topic Discovery. A number of studies extend LDA to involve word embedding. The common strategy is to adapt distributions in LDA to generate real-valued data (e.g., Gaussian LDA (Das et al., 2015), LFTM (Nguyen et al., 2015), Spherical HDP (Batmanghelich et al., 2016), andCGTM (Xun et al., 2017b)). Some other studies think out of the LDA backbone. For example, TWE (Liu et al., 2015) uses topic structures to jointly learn topic embeddings and improve word embeddings. CLM (Xun et al., 2017a) collaboratively improves topic modeling and word embedding by coordinating global and local contexts. Neural Variational Document Model introduces a latent Gaussian layer for topic discovery with amortized inference (Miao et al., 2016). ProdLDA replaces the mixture with a product of experts to sharpen topics under a logistic-normal prior (Srivastava and Sutton, 2017). Contextualized Topic Models use sentence-transformer embeddings to anchor word distributions across documents in practice (Bianchi et al., 2021). ETM (Dieng et al., 2020) models word-topic correlations via word embeddings to improve the expressiveness of topic models. More recently, Sia et al. (2020) show that directly clustering word embeddings (e.g., word2vec or BERT) also generates good topics; Thompson and Mimno (2020) further find that BERT and GPT-2 discover high quality topics, but RoBERTa does not. These models are unsupervised and hard to be applied to seed-guided settings. In contrast, our SEETOPIC framework joint leverages PLMs, word embeddings, and seed information.

document: Research into automatic cloze test generation is substantial (Mostow et al., 2017; Kurdi et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021), yet open cloze tests for language learning remain underexplored compared to closed formats. Rule-driven and data-driven systems have targeted specific gap types, emulating exam styles or focusing on phrasal verbs and parts of speech, and neural sequence models have learned where to insert a single gap (Malafeev, 2014; Chinkina et al., 2017; Soonklang et al., 2017; Marrese-Taylor et al., 2018). Pino et al. (2008) generate open cloze items from learner-dictionary sentences using criteria for context and complexity. Grotjahn et al. (2002) vary gap position and length in C-tests, and Settles et al. (2020) estimate difficulty aligned to CEFR. Building on these foundations, our work predicts a set of paragraph-level gaps that satisfy design constraints and proficiency targeting, and we evaluate models automatically during development and with human raters to assess test quality (Felice and Buttery, 2019; McCarthy et al., 2021).

document: Data augmentation in NLP has a long history, including word substitution methods such as synonym, KNN, TF-IDF, and entity-aware replacements (Zhang et al., 2015; Wang and Yang, 2015; Vijayaraghavan et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2020a; Kobayashi, 2018; Raiman and Miller, 2017; Miao et al., 2020; Yue and Zhou, 2020). Simple heuristics like EDA and back-translation remain popular yet show diminishing returns with large pretrained encoders (Wei and Zou, 2019; Fadaee et al., 2017; Sennrich et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2018; Longpre et al., 2020). Zhang et al. (2018a) perturb hidden representations, and Guo et al. (2020) apply Mixup-like feature interpolation, while Chen et al. (2020b) examine virtual adversarial training. Yoo et al. (2019) and Ng et al. (2020) generate synthetic sentences; Hou et al. (2018) study paraphrase generation; Anaby-Tavor et al. (2020) and Kumar et al. (2020) utilize pretrained models. Beyond augmentation, self-training augments labeled sets with model predictions and encompasses pseudo-labeling and knowledge distillation, with strong augmentation and teacher capacity often improving results (III, 1965; Yarowsky, 1995; Riloff, 1996; Hinton et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2013; Zoph et al., 2020). Our approach, FlipDA, resembles the filtering phase of self-training while avoiding reliance on unlabeled corpora by leveraging label flipping to curate informative negatives. Manual label flipping has been used to probe spurious correlations and dataset artifacts, yet our goal is few-shot generalization and we evaluate on standard i.i.d. test sets (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). FlipDA also relates to contrastive learning by inducing label-aware views and automatically generating hard negatives, yielding gains in low-resource regimes (He et al., 2020b; Yoo et al., 2021).

document: Hallucination in neural language generation has recently received increasing attention from the NLP community, including machine translation (Raunak et al., 2021; Wang and Sennrich, 2020). By hallucination we refer to outputs that are fluent yet ungrounded in source evidence, a failure mode that undermines reliability in summarization, dialogue, data-to-text, and MT (Ji et al., 2023). Existing research clusters around detecting hallucinations and mitigating them during training or decoding, often with task-specific adaptations (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Maynez et al., 2020). Detection methods span entailment-style verification, QA-based consistency checks, and model-based critics that score factuality or faithfulness (Komeili et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Nie et al. (2019) introduced ANLI to stress-test NLI robustness. Brown et al. (2020) presented GPT-3 for few-shot learning across tasks. Lewandowski et al. (2022) examined calibration of confidence estimates in probabilistic classifiers. Mitigation approaches include retrieval-augmented generation that constrains content to retrieved passages and constrained decoding that prunes unsupported continuations (Lewis et al., 2020; Miao et al., 2019). Training-time interventions such as reinforcement learning from human feedback and negative sampling reduce exposure bias and penalize unsupported claims (Ouyang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023). In machine translation, source-conditioned contrastive objectives and adequacy-aware beam search lower the rate of hallucinated content without sacrificing BLEU (Lee et al., 2018; Raunak et al., 2021). We build on these insights by proposing a unified evaluation protocol and lightweight verifier that scales across tasks while preserving task-specific annotation efficiency.

(2020) proposed ALBERT using factorized embeddings and parameter sharing to cut parameters. Wang et al. (2021a) explored dynamic token pruning with learnable gates for long sequences. Xiao et al. (2022) studied early exit classifiers for Transformer inference. Chen et al. (2023) evaluated routing policies across tasks and model scales under constraints.
document:
There has been much prior literature on improving the efficiency of Transformers. The most common technologies include: Knowledge distillation refers to training a smaller student model using outputs from various intermediate representations of larger pre-trained teacher models. In the BERT model, there are multiple representations that the student can learn from, such as the logits in the final layer, the outputs of the encoder units, and the attention maps. The distillation on output logits is most commonly used to train smaller BERT models (Sanh et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). The output tensors of encoder units contain meaningful semantic and contextual relationships between input tokens. Some work creates a smaller model by learning from the outputs of teacher's encoder (Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a). Attention map refers to the softmax distribution output of the self-attention layers and indicates the contextual dependence between the input tokens. A common practice of distillation on attention maps is to directly minimize the difference between the self-attention outputs of the teacher and the student (Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2020). This line of work is orthogonal to our approach and our proposed FCA can be applied to the distilled models to further accelerate their inference speed.

Pruning refers to identifying and removing less important weights or computation units. Pruning methods for BERT broadly fall into two categories. Unstructured pruning methods prune individual weights by comparing their absolute values or gradients with a pre-defined threshold (Mao et al., 2020; Gordon et al., 2020). The weights lower than the threshold are set to zero. Unlike unstructured pruning, structured pruning aims to prune structured blocks of weights or even complete architectural components in the BERT model. Voita et al. (2019) pruned attention heads using a method based on stochastic gates and a differentiable relaxation of the L0 penalty. Fan et al. (2019) randomly dropped Transformer layers to sample small sub-networks from the larger model during training which are selected as the inference models. Clark et al. (2019) introduced ELECTRA with replaced token detection to speed pretraining. Lan et al. (2020) proposed ALBERT using factorized embeddings and parameter sharing to cut parameters. Wang et al. (2021a) explored dynamic token pruning with learnable gates for long sequences. Xiao et al. (2022) studied early exit classifiers for Transformer inference. Chen et al. (2023) evaluated routing policies across tasks and model scales under constraints. Goyal et al. (2020) progressively reduced sequence length by pruning word-vectors based on the attention values. This work is partly similar to the fine-grained computing units in our proposed FCA. However they ignored the coarse-grained units that may cause information loss.

In addition, there are some engineering techniques to speed up the inference speed, such as Mixed Precision (Micikevicius et al., 2018) and Quantization (Zafrir et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2021). Using half-precision or mixed-precision representations of floating points is popular in deep learning to accelerate training and inference speed. Quantization refers to reducing the number of unique values required to represent the model weights, which in turn allows to represent them using fewer bits. Kernel fusion and operator reordering reduce memory traffic and improve utilization on modern accelerators (Dao et al., 2022). Graph compilers that specialize attention patterns further cut overhead by eliminating redundant computation across layers (Kjolstad et al., 2017). Our FCA is complementary to these systems techniques and focuses on balancing coarse- and fine-grained compute paths without altering model accuracy.

(2021) proposed a hierarchical neural model to process segmented input blocks. Compared with SUMM N, these models only split the input once, implying limited flexibility when handling longer input. Xu et al. (2020) study discourse-aware summarization with hierarchical encoders. Narayan et al. (2018) introduce extractive objectives for sentence selection in news, and Chen and Bansal (2018) explore fast abstractive models trained with reinforcement learning. Results on CNN/DailyMail. The GovReport dataset was recently introduced, containing documents with more than 9000 words, thus greatly challenging the capabilities of current models such as PEGASUS, TLM (Subramanian et al., 2019), and BIG-BIRD (Zaheer et al., 2020). To handle this dataset, Huang et al. (2021) proposed head-wise positional strides to reduce the cost of the encoder-decoder attention. Models such as Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) and Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020) adjust attention mechanisms in Transformers to consume longer inputs. However, these models sparsify the attention structure of the pretrained model to fit the longer source text, whereas SUMM N is able to maintain the full structure of various pretrained models. Long Dialogue Summarization has also been explored with models tailored to lengthy multi-speaker transcripts. HMNet (Zhu et al., 2020) and HAT-BART (Rohde et al., 2021) leverage a two-level Transformer-based model to obtain word-level and sentence-level representations. DialLM (Zhong et al., 2021a) and Longformer-BART-arg (Fabbri et al., 2021) use fine-tuning or data augmentation to incorporate external knowledge and maintain the accuracy of lengthy input. Different from these models, SUMM N is a framework that does not modify the structure of the backbone attention model.

document: Parameter-efficient tuning of PLMs has emerged as a practical alternative to full fine-tuning in multi-task deployment scenarios. As it is increasingly infeasible to train and store full copies of large PLMs for various downstream tasks, how to efficiently tune the PLMs with few trainable parameters becomes critical. Existing PELT methods can be largely divided into two categories based on whether new trainable parameters are introduced. Specifically, one may either train a subset of the model parameters such as the prediction head or bias terms, or attach small trainable modules while freezing the backbone. BitFit tunes only bias parameters and reports competitive accuracy under strict memory budgets (Zaken et al., 2022). Adapter layers insert bottlenecks in transformer blocks (Houlsby et al., 2019), Prefix-Tuning optimizes attention prefixes (Li and Liang, 2021), Hinton et al. (2015) present distillation, Gururangan et al. (2020) explore domain-adaptive pretraining. Low-rank adaptation reduces the update space by injecting trainable rank-decomposed matrices into attention and MLP projections, achieving strong trade-offs between quality and memory (Hu et al., 2022). Quantization-aware tuning further lowers activation and optimizer footprints, enabling 4-bit training of adapters without sacrificing accuracy (Dettmers et al., 2023). Our work synthesizes these lines by benchmarking bias-only, adapter, prefix, and low-rank strategies under uniform budgets and datasets, providing actionable guidance for practitioners deploying many task-specific variants.

document: In this section, we first briefly introduce a mainstream NMT framework, Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), with a focus on how to generate prediction probabilities. We then present an analysis of the confidence miscalibration observed in NMT, which motivates our ideas discussed afterward. The Transformer architecture uses self-attention and cross-attention to condition next-token predictions on source and target context. Given decoder states, a linear projection followed by softmax yields a categorical distribution over the vocabulary at each time step. Label smoothing with a small epsilon is often applied during training and reduces overfitting but may blur confidence signals. At inference, beam search and length penalties interact with probability calibration, affecting the ranking of candidate translations. Guo et al. (2017) introduced temperature scaling for classifiers to adjust softmax confidences. Müller et al. (2019) examined how label smoothing changes the geometry of the predictive distribution. Ott et al. (2018) demonstrated large-batch training for NMT with optimization and regularization. Zhang et al. (2020) investigated uncertainty estimation for abstractive summarization using metrics. Kuleshov and Liang (2015) explored calibrated probabilities for structured outputs with isotonic regression. Despite these developments, token probabilities in NMT remain misaligned with human-judged adequacy and fluency. Recent work highlights that calibration should be assessed at both token and sequence levels to support risk-aware decoding. We define calibration using reliability diagrams and expected calibration error tailored to length-normalized log-probabilities. Desai and Durrett (2020) evaluated calibration of pre-trained transformers on text classification benchmarks. Fomicheva et al. (2020) analyzed uncertainty indicators for machine translation quality estimation. Wang et al. (2021) introduced confidence-based reranking for beam search in ASR. Malinin and Gales (2018) proposed prior networks to capture distributional uncertainty. Kamath et al. (2020) studied selective prediction methods. Building on these insights, we explore loss functions that couple token likelihoods with sentence-level Bayes risk under translation metrics. We further study post-hoc recalibration via temperature fitting conditioned on length and coverage, integrated into beam search scoring. Our analysis connects calibration quality with downstream selection decisions, demonstrating gains in error detection and minimum risk decoding.

spans: Neural calibration for sequence models has been examined in classification and QA settings (Guo et al., 2017; Desai and Durrett, 2020). Length-controlled summarization shows that decoding constraints modulate factuality and coverage (Kikuchi et al., 2016; Fan et al., 2018). Exposure bias has been linked to search errors and compounding mistakes in sequence prediction (Ranzato et al., 2016; Wiseman and Rush, 2016). Evaluation under distribution shift highlights brittleness across tasks and datasets, including calibration drift and unstable decoding preferences (Ovadia et al., 2019; Miller et al., 2021). Alternative objectives alter beam behavior in unexpected regimes and temperatures (Eikema and Aziz, 2020).
document: The techniques used in this work reflect an outgrowth of several lines of literature, including analyses of text generation behavior (Xu and Durrett, 2021; Zhong et al., 2021), investigations into beam search (Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019; Meister et al., 2020a), and studies of diversity in generation. We focus on decoding-time choices that shape model outputs, connecting algorithmic search with properties of the learned distribution. In terms of search strategies, best-first beam search (Meister et al., 2020b) integrates best-first exploration with bounded-width decoding. Additional variants of search have been explored, including globally normalized scoring and breadth-controlled decoding (Meister et al., 2021a; Meister et al., 2021b). Beam search has been critically examined in recent work (Huang et al., 2017; Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019), but the focus has largely been on specific challenges in machine translation. Neural calibration for sequence models has been examined in classification and QA settings (Guo et al., 2017; Desai and Durrett, 2020). Length-controlled summarization shows that decoding constraints modulate factuality and coverage (Kikuchi et al., 2016; Fan et al., 2018). Exposure bias has been linked to search errors and compounding mistakes in sequence prediction (Ranzato et al., 2016; Wiseman and Rush, 2016). Evaluation under distribution shift highlights brittleness across tasks and datasets, including calibration drift and unstable decoding preferences (Ovadia et al., 2019; Miller et al., 2021). Alternative objectives alter beam behavior in unexpected regimes and temperatures (Eikema and Aziz, 2020). As for diverse generation, prior work explores reducing degeneration and promoting variety in dialogue and storytelling (Holtzman et al., 2020; Li et al., 2016; Fan et al., 2019), and we adapt these insights to search-time controls.

document: This paper situates cognitively inspired NLP within the broader landscape of representation learning that transfers knowledge across tasks and modalities. We focus on work that maps linguistic representations to neural and behavioral signals to probe what models encode and to harvest supervision for downstream NLP. Mitchell et al. (2008) predicted fMRI activation for concrete nouns using hand-crafted semantic features and linear models. Huth et al. (2016) derived voxel-wise semantic maps from naturalistic listening with a regression linking word embeddings to cortical responses. Pereira et al. (2018) released a shared fMRI benchmark for sentence and story comprehension with standardized evaluation protocols. Schrimpf et al. Toneva and Wehbe (2019) selected training examples using brain alignment scores to improve generalization in vision-and-language tasks. Jain and Huth (2018) linked phoneme features to cortical tuning profiles. Building on these insights, we propose a unified framework that jointly optimizes for task performance and neural predictivity, enabling principled transfer between human data and language objectives.

document:
Zero-Shot Relation Extraction Zero-shot relation extraction was previously framed as a slotfilling task and solved by reading comprehension methods (Levy et al., 2017). However, their approach requires manual template design for each relation label, which cannot scale well to new relation types. On the other hand, RelationPrompt generates synthetic relation samples for any relation without relying on label-specific templates. Another approach to zero-shot relation extraction is the formulation into an entailment task (Obamuyide and Vlachos, 2018), which is not constrained to a fixed relation label space and instead determines if arbitrary pairs of sentences and relation labels are compatible, allowing for zero-shot inference. However, it is designed for sentence-level classification and cannot be easily applied to ZeroRTE.

Data Augmentation A popular method for improving model performance in supervised low-resource tasks is data augmentation. Simple heuristics such as token manipulation (Kobayashi, 2018) were initially developed, and new methods in language modeling improved the quality of augmented samples (Xie et al., 2020; Wei and Zou, 2019). Although there are data augmentation methods that can be applied to structured tasks such as named entity recognition (Ding et al., 2020) and relation extraction (Papanikolaou and Pierleoni, 2020; Lee et al., 2021), they require existing training samples and cannot be easily adapted to zero-shot tasks.

Language Model Prompts Prompting-based methods have shown promise as a new paradigm for inference in natural language processing. Given language models that have large-scale and general language pre-training, well-designed prompts are able to elicit the desired answer sequences for different tasks in few-shot or even zero-shot settings. Another advantage is the potential to adapt very large language models (Reynolds and McDonell, 2021) to new tasks without relatively expensive fine-tuning. Prompt calibration and verbalizer learning in cloze-style classification were studied with models and limited spaces (Gao et al., 2021; Logan IV et al., 2021). Prefix- and prompt-tuning introduced lightweight controllers for steering frozen transformers and improved transfer across tasks (Li and Liang, 2021). Instruction corpora aided generalization (Sanh et al., 2022). However, such methods have not yet proven effective for more complex tasks such as triplet extraction.

Knowledge Retrieval RelationPrompt also leverages the knowledge stored in language models (Roberts et al., 2020) to compose relation samples that are grounded in realistic contexts. To ensure that the generated samples are factually accurate, the language model requires strong knowledge retrieval capabilities (Petroni et al., 2019). In practice, we pair retrieved evidence with subject–object mentions to reduce hallucinations during sample construction. This design aligns the synthetic data distribution with evaluation formats while avoiding costly annotation.

6 Conclusions and Future Work. We presented a prompting-based framework for zero-shot relation extraction that integrates retrieval, generation, and lightweight schema conditioning. Future work will explore automatic prompt discovery and controllable negative sampling to further improve zero-shot generalization.

document: We study how models learn concepts from auxiliary natural language information that accompanies supervision. Classical approaches incorporate side-information as constraints or priors during training (Mann and McCallum, 2010; Ganchev et al., 2010). Recent work leverages language in low-resource settings for tasks such as text classification and question answering, while we examine generalization of language-supervised classifiers to novel tasks rather than only seen ones (Srivastava et al., 2017; Hancock et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2020). A growing body of research investigates how explanations influence model behavior and evaluation (Wiegreffe and Marasović, 2021). Rajani et al. (2019) introduce annotation pipelines where explanations accompany answers for commonsense reasoning, and Camburu et al. (2018) curate natural language justifications for recognizing textual entailment. DeYoung et al. (2020) assemble resources for evaluating rationales, Pruthi et al. (2020) study the fragility of explanation-based training under adversarial perturbations, and Jain and Wallace (2019) question the faithfulness of attention as explanation. Liu et al. (2022) explore programmatic supervision with textual rules. In contrast to instance-level rationales, we target definition-style explanations that describe concepts and support broad generalization across many examples, aligning with concept definitions used by Andreas et al. (2018) while extending evaluation to a large, domain-agnostic suite of tabular classification tasks. Large pretrained language models such as BERT, RoBERTa, and T5 demonstrate strong few-shot and zero-shot capabilities (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Lester et al., 2021). Pattern-based reformulations improve few-shot learning for smaller models and prompt-based multi-task training has been used to assess generalization to unseen tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Tam et al., 2021; Mishra et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2021; Sanh et al., 2021; Min et al., 2021; Aghajanyan et al., 2021). We differ by operationalizing explanations as decision rules over structured inputs and by designing models that ingest such rules to generalize to novel tasks beyond those observed during training.

document:
Edit Intention Identification. Identification of edit intentions is an integral part of the iterative text revision task. Prior works have studied the categorization of different types of edit actions to help understand why editors do what they do and how effective their actions are (Yang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017; Ito et al., 2019). These studies outline taxonomies and tagging schemes for revisions across tasks such as grammar correction and encyclopedic editing (Yang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017; Ito et al., 2019). Bhat et al. (2020) investigate talk-page rationales in Wikipedia, Anthonio et al. (2020) curate sentence-level edits; Faltings et al. (2021) introduce a corpus of automated assistance cases. As a result, their ability to generalize to other domains remains an open question.

Single-Pass Text Revision Models. Some prior works (Botha et al., 2018; Ito et al., 2019; Faltings et al., 2021) simplify the text revision task to a single-pass "original-to-final" sentence-to-sentence generation task. However, it is very challenging to conduct multiple perfect edits at once. Lee and Goldwasser (2019) propose iterative content planning for opinion revision using structured predictors, Nguyen et al. (2017) analyze peer feedback corpora for revision purposes, and Park and Cardie (2012) report stylistic transformation patterns in persuasive writing. Du et al. (2022) tune sequence-to-sequence models for rewriting, and Botha et al. (2018) frame generation via constrained decoding. In this work, we explore the performance of text revision models in multiple iterations and multiple granularities.

Iterative Text Revision Datasets. While some prior works have constructed iterative text revision datasets, they are limited to singular writing domains, such as Wikipedia-style articles (Anthonio et al., 2020), academic essays (Zhang et al., 2017) or news articles (Spangher and May, 2021). In this work, we develop a unified taxonomy to analyze the characteristics of iterative text revision behaviors across different domains and collect large scale text revisions of human writings from multiple domains. The differences between ITERATER and the prior datasets are summarized in Table .

document: Referring Expression Comprehension (REC) localizes image regions for natural language expressions by aligning visual features with linguistic cues. A prevalent strategy learns a joint embedding space and varies in how the cross-modal similarity is computed and exploited for proposal ranking or selection. Hu et al. (2016b) and Rohrbach et al. (2016) optimized joint embeddings to score region proposals with accompanying expressions, and Plummer et al. (2018) enriched the space with phrase-region correspondences. Deng et al. (2018) introduced proposal-level attention to identify the most likely referent from candidate boxes in common benchmark settings. Lu et al. (2019) adopted a transformer to project multimodal inputs into a shared semantic space, and Chen et al. (2020) leveraged self-attention for fine-grained alignment of words and proposals. Building on these insights, we model fine-grained REC by integrating pixel-level cues that capture boundaries and textures often missed by proposal-only pipelines. Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) extends REC by predicting a dense mask for the described object, addressing the mismatch between rectangular boxes and true object shapes. Following the seminal RIS formulation of Hu et al. (2016a), subsequent work exploits multi-scale visual context and expression-aware attention to refine masks, and our approach complements this line by explicitly modeling inter- and intra-modal interactions across pixels, regions, and words.

document: While elaborated feedback datasets on language learning (Caines et al., 2020; Pilan et al., 2020; Stasaski et al., 2020) appeared recently, they focus on linguistic mistakes, such as grammatical errors, instead of content. Our extensive literature review did not reveal datasets that included content-focused elaborated feedback on short answer responses. However, SAF's feedback can be viewed as a textual explanation of the assigned score. Therefore, comparable NLP datasets with textual explanations and publicly available ASAG datasets without explanations are discussed in the following sections. Automatic short answer grading has a long tradition of scoring content adequacy using supervised models trained on rubric-aligned features (Mohler et al., 2011; Sultan et al., 2016; Riordan et al., 2017). Earlier systems emphasized lexical overlap and semantic similarity with reference answers, often using word alignments, n-grams, and paraphrase resources (Dzikovska et al., 2013; Nguyen and Litman, 2016). More recent approaches adopt pretrained language models to capture concept-level matches and partial correctness in a data-driven fashion (Uto et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2021). Despite these advances, explanations remain scarce, with most ASAG datasets providing only numeric labels on ordinal or categorical scales (Shermis and Burstein, 2013; Zhang et al., 2020). Work on rationale extraction introduces span-level justifications, but typically for sentiment or natural language inference rather than science short answers (DeYoung et al., 2020; Jain et al., 2020). Studies on faithful explanation generation argue for evidence-grounded textual rationales tied to input spans and rubrics, yet standardized resources are limited for content-oriented tutoring feedback (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020; Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019). In educational NLP, formative feedback has been modeled as hints, next-step suggestions, or error diagnoses, which differs from content-targeted elaboration following disciplinary rubrics (Xie et al., 2019; Nye, 2015; Wambsganss et al., 2021). Shute (2008) cataloged principles for formative feedback effectiveness in computer-based learning environments, outlining guidance on specificity, timing, and learner control. Hattie and Timperley (2007) examined feedback levels and impacts on achievement across educational settings, detailing task, process, and self-regulatory components. Nicol and Macfarlane-Dick (2006) described formative assessment practices emphasizing self-regulation in learners, with design features that support autonomy and calibration. Kulhavy and Stock (1989) studied explanatory feedback timing and its relation to error correction, reporting retention and transfer under conditions. Although these traditions inform feedback theory, generating rubric-aligned content feedback for short answers requires modeling domain concepts and misconceptions. We situate our work alongside textual explanation datasets that provide natural language justifications for model predictions in other tasks. Datasets such as e-SNLI supply human-written explanations attached to entailment labels, enabling supervised training of natural language rationales (Camburu et al., 2018). In QA, FaithfulQA and CoT collections pair answers with chains of reasoning or evidence snippets, but they typically target open-domain knowledge rather than curriculum-aligned science responses (Narang et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022; Lakhotia et al., 2021). For student writing, rubric-annotated corpora exist for holistic essay scoring and error correction, yet they rarely articulate content-specific guidance tied to canonical answers (Attali and Burstein, 2006; Bryant et al., 2019). Public ASAG benchmarks such as SemEval 2013 Task 7 and the Kaggle ASAP-SAS dataset include question-specific scoring rubrics and labeled responses with gold scores but lack sentence-level or concept-level explanations (Dzikovska et al., 2013; Shermis, 2013). Recent work explores prompting large language models to justify grading decisions, but evaluations expose risks of hallucinated rationales and misalignment with rubrics (Marasović et al., 2022; Chiang and Lee, 2023). Our study addresses this gap by assembling a dataset where each response is paired with content-grounded, reference-oriented elaborations authored under explicit scoring criteria. We formalize elaborated feedback as a conditional generation problem anchored to the prompt, the student response, and a rubric-derived target concept set. In parallel, we relate to explainable ASAG models that learn to highlight key phrases as evidence while predicting scores, creating opportunities for joint training with textual feedback. We compare to baseline approaches that produce templated comments from error taxonomies, which have shown utility in grammar feedback but underperform for open-ended science answers. For evaluation, we adopt metrics that reflect both textual quality and faithfulness to gold evidence, combining semantic similarity with content selection measures, and we also report human judgments by subject-matter experts on helpfulness, specificity, and alignment with rubrics to triangulate automatic scores. Our contributions include a curated corpus, a modeling framework that couples concept extraction and feedback generation, protocols for robust evaluation under limited supervision, and public releases of data and code to facilitate reproducibility and further research. This line of work aims to bridge the gap between predictive scoring and instructionally meaningful guidance in automated assessment.

document: Zero-shot cross-lingual transfer learning. Recently, thanks to the rapid development of multilingual pre-trained language models such as multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), a number of approaches have been proposed for zero-shot cross-lingual transfer on tasks including semantic parsing (Sherborne and Lapata, 2021), natural language generation (Shen et al., 2018), and natural language understanding (Lauscher et al., 2020). In this work, we claim our method is zero-shot because no non-Chinese CSRL annotations are seen during the CSRL training stage. For decoding, we directly use the cross-lingual CSRL model trained on Chinese CSRL data to analyze conversations in other languages. To our best knowledge, our work is the first step toward cross-lingual CSRL. Prior research indicates that multilingual models learn partially shared representations that can enable transfer even under typological divergence (Pires et al., 2019). Stronger alignment is often obtained by task-specific finetuning that encourages language-invariant features (Keung et al., 2020). Data augmentation with parallel corpora or dictionaries can further improve transfer by exposing models to cross-lingual correspondences (Conneau et al., 2018). Ruder et al. (2019) surveyed transfer methods and taxonomies across multilingual NLP. Keung et al. (2020) adopted adversarial objectives to encourage language-invariant features in fine-tuning. Lewis et al. (2020) presented multilingual question answering datasets for cross-lingual evaluation. Xue et al. (2021) introduced mT5 as a unified sequence-to-sequence model trained on a multilingual corpus. Hu et al. (2020) released XTREME as a benchmark covering diverse tasks and languages globally. Conversational semantic role labeling. While ellipsis and anaphora frequently occur in dialogues, Xu et al. (2021) observed that most of the dropped or referred components can be found in dialogue histories. Following this observation, they proposed conversational semantic role labeling (CSRL), which requires the model to find predicate–argument structures over the entire conversation instead of a single sentence. In this way, when analyzing a predicate in the latest utterance, a CSRL model needs to consider both the current turn and previous turns to search potential arguments, and thus can recover omitted components. Xu et al. (2020; 2021) also confirmed the usefulness of CSRL for Chinese dialogue by injecting CSRL features into downstream tasks. Despite these advances, current state-of-the-art CSRL models remain limited by shallow discourse modeling and insufficient access to global conversational semantics. Another open question concerns whether CSRL benefits conversational tasks in non-Chinese languages, given the lack of cross-lingual CSRL models and resources. In this work, we primarily address the latter question and propose a simple yet effective model to perform cross-lingual CSRL. Our approach relies on multilingual encoders and task-specific prompting to stabilize inference across languages without task-side supervision. We also investigate inference-time retrieval of analogous conversational frames from Chinese to provide weak guidance in target languages. We distinguish our work from the concurrent study of Wu et al. (2021b), which focuses on improving monolingual CSRL by modeling predicate-aware representations. While such predicate-centric designs can benefit monolingual CSRL, they may hurt cross-lingual performance because the relative positions of predicates vary across languages.

To mitigate noise from global features, attention-based models refine visual grounding and question focus (Malinowski et al., 2018). Region-centric pipelines replace CNN grid features with object proposals as in BUTD using Faster R-CNN and hybridize regions with grids in Pythia (Anderson et al., 2018; Ren et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2018). Advances on the language side improve question modeling, while fusion research explores compact bilinear and tensor methods such as MFB and MUTAN (Lu et al., 2016; Nam et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2017; Ben-Younes et al., 2017). We describe related studies on Chinese BERT models and probing methodologies.

The original BERT authors released a Chinese model trained primarily on Chinese Wikipedia (Devlin et al., 2018). Building on that checkpoint, Cui et al. (2019) trained RoBERTa-wwm-ext with whole word masking on extended corpora. Cui et al. (2020) further introduced Chinese ELECTRA and MacBERT, both removing explicit [MASK] tokens from pretraining. ELECTRA framed pretraining as token-level replacement detection to discriminate original versus replaced tokens. MacBERT replaced [MASK] with near-synonyms and adopted WWM and n-gram masking to reduce pretrain–finetune mismatch. ERNIE for Chinese applied entity-level masking so that all tokens of a named entity were masked jointly (Sun et al., 2019). Subsequent approaches incorporated linguistic features beyond characters. AMBERT and Lattice-BERT integrated lexicon-derived word spans into the encoder to exploit multi-granularity segmentation (Lai et al., 2021). Chinese-BERT leveraged pinyin and glyph features to augment subcharacter and phonetic information (Sun et al., 2021). These architectural and objective variants yield complementary strengths on Chinese understanding and generation benchmarks. Nevertheless, a systematic picture of what knowledge they internalize remains incomplete.

Probing techniques address this gap by testing frozen representations for specific linguistic or factual capabilities. Petroni et al. (2019) proposed LAMA-style cloze probes to assess factual recall with masked templates. Davison et al. (2019) evaluated relational commonsense probing with hand-crafted templates and filtered knowledge assertions. Talmor et al. (2020) presented multi-step semantic parsing for compositional generalization in question answering. Roberts et al. (2020) introduced retrieval-augmented generation, Jiang et al. (2020) analyzed prompt sensitivity in factual cloze tests, Bouraoui et al. (2020) modeled relation induction with lexical patterns, Elazar et al. (2021) surveyed probing limitations, and Poerner et al. (2020) explored entity-aware representations. Beyond templates, Talmor et al. (2020) examined symbolic reasoning requirements, highlighting limits of surface plausibility heuristics. Jiang et al. (2020) showed that prompt paraphrasing substantially modulates probe accuracy, complicating conclusions about stored facts. Ravichander et al. (2020) cautioned that probes may reflect dataset artifacts rather than model knowledge when control conditions are weak. For Chinese, Xu et al. (2021) constructed pinyin-aware probes to disentangle phonetic versus semantic cues in character embeddings. Li et al. (2021) designed syntax-oriented probes to test whether dependency relations are linearly recoverable from contextual states. Our work extends these lines by creating bilingual probes that align Chinese templates with validated English counterparts. We compare ELECTRA-style and MLM-style pretraining under matched corpora to attribute probe differences to objectives rather than data. We further analyze the role of lexicon integration by contrasting Lattice-BERT with character-only baselines on identical probes.

document: Data Augmentation. Data augmentation (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Cheng et al., 2016; Hoang et al., 2018; Edunov et al., 2018; Kobayashi, 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019; Wei and Zou, 2020) has been widely used in neural machine translation to address generalization from limited supervision. Among existing methods, back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016a) leverages reverse models to synthesize source text from monolingual targets. Edunov et al. (2018) scaled this approach with noisy decoding to produce diverse synthetic pairs for large-scale training. Wang et al. (2018) introduced lexical substitution that replaces random tokens to create alternative surface forms. Kobayashi (2018) proposed contextual augmentation guided by a language model to preserve fluency during replacement. Cheng et al. (2020) interpolated embeddings to select semantically plausible candidates under shared context. Beyond token-level noise, Gao et al. (2019) advocated paraphrase-based augmentation to expand meaning-preserving variants. Wei and Zou (2020) summarized simple operations such as deletion and swap that remain effective across tasks. Our approach belongs to this family, focusing on augmenting each instance with an adjacency semantic region that groups expressions with shared intent. We formalize these regions in a metric space so models witness multiple formulations anchored to the same semantics. Semantic agreement modeling has been explored broadly in universal sentence representations. Zhang et al. (2016) introduced variational NMT to encode latent semantics for parallel data. Schwenk and Douze (2017) built multilingual embeddings to align sentences across languages and domains. Yang et al. (2019) examined shared vector spaces for paraphrases and equivalent statements in practice. Chi et al. (2021) employed contrastive objectives to draw cross-lingual pairs together in embedding space. In our formulation, the learned space enables consistent training signals that reward predictions stable under paraphrastic variation. Experiments show that constructing neighborhoods around seed sentences yields gains over standard augmentation on low-resource translation benchmarks.

(2019) study insertion-based decoding with locally normalized policies across multiple tasks. Lee et al. document: Non-autoregressive edit-based models frame sequence generation as iterative refinement. Following prior work, the process is modeled as a Markov Decision Process with state space Y, action set A, transition function E, reward R, and an initial sequence y0. A state y is a token sequence where each element is drawn from a fixed vocabulary V and L denotes length. The reward typically inversely tracks a distance D between the current output and a reference sequence y*. At each decoding step, a policy π selects an action a to refine y, yielding the next state E(y, a). Models differ in the nature of edit actions and their roll-in policies. Common operations include insertion, deletion, reposition, and substitution. We adopt the EDITOR architecture for constrained decoding scenarios that require modifying non-empty initial sequences due to its demonstrated competitiveness (Xu and Carpuat, 2021). EDITOR is a Transformer that applies two edits per iteration: reposition and insertion. The reposition module πrps predicts new positions for all tokens, implicitly enabling deletions. The insertion module comprises placeholder prediction πplh and token prediction πins to fill generated placeholders. Gu et al. (2019) present a non-autoregressive refinement strategy using edit operations and show improved efficiency on machine translation benchmarks. Stern et al. (2019) study insertion-based decoding with locally normalized policies across multiple tasks. Lee et al. (2018) propose iterative refinement with denoising objectives for translation, and Ghazvininejad et al. (2019) explore mask-predict generation with progressively fewer masks. Kasai et al. (2020) examine latency in such systems. Decoding repeats until two consecutive iterations return identical outputs or a maximum step budget is reached (Gu et al., 2019; Ghazvininejad et al., 2019). Training uses imitation learning with a roll-in policy to generate states and a roll-out cost-to-go estimated against oracle demonstrations. For machine translation, we summarize typical roll-in and roll-out designs in Table 1. In EDITOR, roll-ins for the reposition module and for the insertion module are stochastic mixtures of the other module’s outputs and a noised version of the gold sequence. The oracle objective is defined by the Levenshtein edit distance between the generated sequence and the reference (Gu et al., 2019). Noised sequences are produced with random word dropping and limited-distance word shuffling to increase robustness during training (Gu et al., 2019; Lample et al., 2018). This strategy mitigates exposure bias by aligning training-time trajectories with those encountered at inference time (Ross and Bagnell, 2010).

document: Multimodal entity and relation extraction have emerged as key extensions of classic information extraction, driven by the rise of image-rich social media. While early NER and RE methods remained text-only, multimodal approaches aim to ground mentions and relations in visual context to resolve ambiguity and elide noise. Foundational surveys document the evolution from RNN–CNN baselines to Transformer backbones that fuse textual and regional visual features (Liu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021a; Chen et al., 2021b). In multimodal NER, Moon et al. (2018) and Arshad et al. (2019) model sentence-level text with RNNs and encode whole images with CNNs, followed by cross-modal attention to align cues. Later work replaces global images with detected regions, using object features from Faster R-CNN and visual transformers to capture entity-level correspondences (Zheng et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2020). For relation extraction, Zareian et al. (2020) incorporate scene graphs to bind textual predicates with visual relations, improving robustness to incomplete captions. Adversarial and noise-aware designs further mitigate misleading or irrelevant images by calibrating cross-modal attention and gating fusion (Wu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). Distant supervision and weak labels have been explored to reduce annotation cost, pairing posts with retrieved images or using alt-text alignment as a proxy signal (Zhou et al., 2021). Kiela et al. (2019) study embedding spaces for image–text retrieval, Lu et al. (2019) present ViLBERT for two-stream fusion, and Tan and Bansal (2019) design LXMERT with grounding. Li et al. (2020) introduce UNITER with word–region alignment, Li et al. (2019) explore VisualBERT via masked modeling, and Sun et al. (2020) analyze pretraining dynamics on downstream classification. Beyond architecture, recent datasets provide entity- and relation-level boxes, masks, and rationales to enable fine-grained evaluation (Zhang et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2022). Pretraining strategies exploit large image–text corpora with contrastive objectives and masked modeling to initialize multimodal encoders before task-specific finetuning (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021). Cross-modal alignment losses are commonly combined with token-level NER or span-level RE losses to balance local cues and global coherence. A critical open problem is error sensitivity, where spurious visual features dominate rare entity cues, yielding brittle predictions in the wild. Robust methods explicitly predict image–text relevance and downweight incongruent images using small auxiliary classifiers attached to multimodal BERT or ViLBERT encoders (Lei et al., 2022). Our work situates in this trajectory by proposing a lightweight consistency module that enforces agreement between textual spans and visual regions without requiring extra annotations.

Media Framing Bias Detection and Prediction. Media bias has been studied extensively in social science, economics, and political science, with many methods proposed to analyze the political preference and framing bias of news outlets (Groseclose and Milyo, 2005; Miller and Riechert, 2001; Park et al., 2011; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010; Haselmayer and Jenny, 2017). Framing bias is the selective reporting of an event to sway readers' opinions through choices such as emphasis, omission, and wording (Entman, 1993; Entman, 2007; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2006). In NLP, computational approaches to media bias often model linguistic cues that signal partisanship in political text (Recasens et al., 2013; Yano et al., 2010; Hamborg et al., 2019b). For instance, Gentzkow and Shapiro count the frequency of slanted words within articles. These methods mainly attend to the stylistic dimension of framing, that is, how an outlet covers a topic. In contrast, fewer studies target the informational dimension, namely what to cover and which aspects to foreground, and they typically remain at detection rather than prediction (Park et al., 2011; Fan et al., 2019). Budak et al. (2014) quantify ideological slant by modeling audience exposure to topics. Potthast et al. (2018) construct a dataset for hyperpartisan news and present baseline classifiers. Baly et al. (2018) infer source-level bias and credibility using multi-modal signals. Card et al. (2015) release the Media Frames Corpus to study issue framing. Rashkin et al. (2017) analyze stylistic markers of online propaganda and deception. Building on these observations, researchers have probed event selection, source quoting, and salience as signals of informational bias in headlines and leads. Recent work also considers framing prediction as anticipating which angle an outlet will adopt given early evidence about an unfolding story. We focus on headlines because they concentrate framing choices and strongly shape reader expectations. Our approach views headline neutralization as conditional summarization that rewrites partisan cues while preserving core propositions. We operationalize neutrality via lexicon-based indicators, stance distance to reference outlets, and human preference judgments. We further model cross-outlet variation by conditioning on source metadata to avoid collapsing distinct editorial styles.

Media Bias Mitigation. News aggregation that displays articles from different outlets on a topic is a widely used strategy to mitigate bias, yet it still has limitations (Hamborg et al., 2019a). Other systems provide added context, including multi-perspective classification (Park et al., 2009), multinational viewpoints (Hamborg et al., 2017), and detailed media profiles (Zhang et al., 2019b). Our goal differs in delivering a single neutral headline that reduces cognitive load while retaining the salient facts.

document: We also compare our method with previous SOTA work, where all referred systems exploit the pre-trained BERT model. As shown, compared to Chen et al. (2020), our method performs better when using very limited training data. Besides, our method can match their performance of semi-supervised setting that uses additional 10K unlabeled training data. Moreover, our final model, without utilizing much external knowledge (e.g., document-level or manually designed features), can achieve very competitive performance on the full training set in comparison to most of the previous systems. Furthermore, it should be noted that achieving the absolute SOTA results of our model on the full training data is not the primary motivation of our work. Our goal is to analyze data efficiency and robustness under distribution shift while maintaining competitive accuracy. To this end, we benchmark against representative baselines and ablations under identical preprocessing and evaluation protocols. Devlin et al. (2019) present BERT with masked language modeling and next sentence prediction for general language understanding. Liu et al. (2019) extend pretraining with whole word masking and larger batches under RoBERTa. Clark et al. (2020) analyze self-attention patterns and layer behavior on GLUE and MNLI diagnostics. Lan et al. (2020) reduce parameters with ALBERT and increase training steps substantially. Despite sharing BERT-style encoders, these baselines differ in data usage and regularization, which motivates our controlled comparisons. We adopt a unified tokenization, learning rate schedule, and early-stopping criterion to eliminate confounds across runs. Evaluation follows standard splits and metrics, including accuracy, macro-F1, and calibration error where applicable. We further report computational cost in GPU hours to contextualize performance gains for practitioners. Xu and Liu (2020) propose adversarial data augmentation to improve robustness on text classifiers. Gururangan et al. (2020) examine domain-adaptive pretraining on in-domain corpora for transfer learning. He et al. (2021) study weight decay and layer-wise learning rates for fine-tuning stability. Pruksachatkun et al. (2020) analyze intermediate-task training effects across probing tasks and downstream generalization. Zhang et al. (2021) report benefits from warmup and small batch sizes on sentence encoders. These works illustrate diverse modeling choices, yet our experiments focus on isolating the contribution of curriculum-aware sampling and lightweight consistency training. The resulting analysis clarifies when reduced supervision is sufficient and when additional unlabeled data or capacity yields measurable returns.

spans: Reformer introduces locality-sensitive hashing to approximate attention and enables sub-quadratic complexity in long-context processing (Kitaev et al., 2020). Pointer-generator networks for abstractive summarization incorporate a copying mechanism to better handle out-of-vocabulary words, rare tokens, and factual consistency (See et al., 2017). ELECTRA replaces masked language modeling with a discriminator trained to detect token substitutions, resulting in sample-efficient pre-training and strong downstream results (Clark et al., 2020).
document: Language model pre-training followed by task-specific fine-tuning has proven to be a powerful tool for numerous NLP tasks (Liu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b; Radford et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019a; Lewis et al., 2020; Joshi et al., 2020). BERT introduced the Masked Language Modeling (MLM) objective, where a model predicts masked tokens given a sequence of text input. Fine-tuning a pre-trained BERT model has led to improved performance on various NLP tasks. Yet MLM predictions are not made auto-regressively, which limits the capability of the BERT family for generation tasks. Raffel et al. (2019a) introduced the span corruption task in T5 as the pre-training objective, where a model predicts the masked token span using an autoregressive model. This objective can handle generation tasks because pre-training is done in a generative way. BART (Lewis et al., 2020) is similar to T5 but uses a slightly different pre-training objective, in which spans are masked from the input but the complete output is predicted. Despite these advances, none of these works thoroughly investigate pre-training for very long sequence inputs. They often use a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture as the backbone, the complexity of which is quadratic in input length, making them impractical to model very long sequences. Recent research has focused on improving the memory and computation efficiency of Transformer models for handling long input (Tay et al., 2020b; Tay et al., 2021). Reformer introduces locality-sensitive hashing to approximate attention and enables sub-quadratic complexity in long-context processing (Kitaev et al., 2020). Pointer-generator networks for abstractive summarization incorporate a copying mechanism to better handle out-of-vocabulary words, rare tokens, and factual consistency (See et al., 2017). ELECTRA replaces masked language modeling with a discriminator trained to detect token substitutions, resulting in sample-efficient pre-training and strong downstream results (Clark et al., 2020). One family of approaches uses non-full attention patterns to restrict the attention field, reducing attention complexity from O(n^2) to O(n log n) or O(n), including Sinkhorn (Tay et al., 2020a), Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020), ETC (Ainslie et al., 2020), and BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020a). Another family leverages low-rank or kernel-based approximations of the attention matrix, such as Linformer (Wang et al., 2020), Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021), Random Feature Attention (Peng et al., 2021), and LUNA (Ma et al., 2021).

Two major approaches are used for evaluating chatbots or dialogue systems. Some evaluate a single turn at a time by comparing the response with a ground truth utterance from a static script of real human dialogue or get a score by combining the response with the context. Others evaluate the interaction between human and bot or between bot and bot by some scoring metrics. We will present some typical systems from each of these approaches below and discuss their pros and cons. Most existing evaluation systems are based on static scripts. Traditional metrics such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002b), ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) are widely used for evaluating text generation systems. More recently, automatic evaluation methods based on static scripts are gradually moving toward using pre-trained language models. Zhang et al. (2020) present BERTScore for text generation evaluation, and Ma et al. (2017) develop discriminative evaluators trained on response quality annotations. Lowe et al. (2017) introduce ADEM for dialogue evaluation with learned scoring. Hashimoto et al. (2019) analyze evaluation risk and sample complexity across generation tasks. Mehri et al. (2022) examine turn-level user satisfaction signals. Pang et al. (2020) uses GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) and Mehri and Eskenazi (2020) uses RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) to automatically evaluate the generation in an unsupervised way with a higher correlation to human evaluation than traditional ones. However, these static evaluation metrics which need fixed contexts are not flexible enough to assess chatbot's ability, in need of adapting to dynamic and changing contexts. We argue that interactive systems like ChatMatch are more promising as they test chatbots in a real conversation mode.

Interactive evaluation systems attract increasing attention lately. Ghandeharioun et al. (2019) and Deriu and Cieliebak (2019) use dialogues between a bot and itself, which is called self-talk, to evaluate the bot in a more automatic manner. But it often leads to a lot of repeated chat context. Deriu et al. (2020) designed Spot The Bot, a framework that enables a group of bots to chat with each other and then asks humans to annotate if the bots talk more like a human or more like a bot. Prior to our work, there was still no interactive and automatic evaluation framework that works without any participation of human annotators. Our work fills this gap and moreover, it's very flexible since more complex metrics or algorithms can be plugged in as scoring functions. Our results also show that there is a strong correlation between automatic evaluation results and human judgments. We release our evaluation platform and datasets to support community benchmarks.

Prior Beliefs in Argumentation Studying persuasiveness in argumentation is an active field of research. Besides planning argument content, 2 Reviewers can find all in the supplementary material.

structure, and style , researchers also considered audience-based features to model persuasiveness. Among these, both Durmus and Cardie (2018) and El Baff et al. (2020) study how user factors such as religion and political background affect persuasiveness, whereas Al Khatib et al. ( 2020) demonstrated thats userbased features reflecting beliefs, characteristics, and personality can increase the predictability of argument persuasiveness.

The moral foundation theory offers another conceptual model of understanding human judgments in daily life (Haidt and Joseph, 2004). According to the theory, humans subconsciously adhere to five basic moral foundations when judging controversial issues: fairness (importance of justice, rights, and equality), care (being kind, and avoiding harm), loyalty (self-sacrifice, solidarity, belongingness), authority (respect to traditions and hierarchy), and purity (sacredness of religion and human). Based on the theory, the disagreement between liberals and conservatives can be explained by the moral gap between the two parties, for example. While liberals rely mainly on care and fairness (so called individualizing morals) in their assessment of controversial issues, conservatives consider all moral foundations more evenly, somewhat skewed towards the binding morals though, that is, loyalty, authority, and purity (Graham et al., 2009).

Several studies provided evidence of the robustness of the moral foundation theory in understanding people's behaviors and decisions (Feinberg and Willer, 2015;Fulgoni et al., 2016;Johnson and Goldwasser, 2018). For computational purposes, Kobbe et al. (2020) annotated moral foundations in arguments and analyzed the correlation between morals and argument quality. We user their corpus in our experiments to evaluate our moral classifier.

Argument Generation Argument generation approaches have been proposed for a spectrum of different tasks. Hua et al. (2019) and Alshomary et al. (2021b) worked on counter-argument generation, tackling the task by rebutting an argument's conclusion or by undermining one of its weak premises respectively. Others opposed a given claim (Bilu et al., 2015;Hidey and McKeown, 2019), generated argumentative claims on a given topic controlled for certain aspects (Schiller et al., 2020), or reconstructed implicit conclusions (Alshomary et al., 2020;Syed et al., 2021). Brown et al. (2020) introduced large language models that follow natural language prompts across tasks, exhibiting emergent few-shot abilities. Gao et al. (2021) showed that cloze-style prompts and learned verbalizers calibrate label semantics for text classification with limited supervision. Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt-based learning and cataloged tuning strategies for encoders and decoders, noting open questions about argumentative generation in practical deployments today. However, none of these approaches considered generating augmenta- tive texts that target a specific audience.

Recently, Alshomary et al. (2021a) introduced the task of belief-based claim generation, aiming to generate claims on a given topic that match a given target audience. As a model of beliefs, they considered people's stances on big issues. While they demonstrated the feasibility of encoding beliefs into claims, they did not study the effectiveness of these claims on an audience. Another driver in the field of argument generation is Project Debater by Slonim et al. (2021). Project Debater is an endto-end system for argument mining, retrieval, and generation. For generation, it relies on a hybrid approach consisting of retrieval, mining, clustering, and rephrasing components. In their manual evaluation, Slonim et al. (2021) observe a competent quality of generated arguments compared to those crafted by humans. To generate morally framed arguments, we extend their system. Relying on Project Debater helps us alleviate confounding effects of argument quality and, so, to focus on testing whether moral utilization affects the audience. We present empirical results across multiple topics and demographics to test whether morally targeted claims shift stated support. The remainder of this paper details the modeling components, experimental design, and validation protocols.

Meta-Learning.
Meta-learning aims to improve the learning algorithm itself based on previously learned experience (Thrun and Pratt, 1998; Hospedales et al., 2021).
In general, methods are often grouped into model-based, metric-based, and optimization-based families.
Model-based approaches depend on specialized architectures that support fast adaptation (Santoro et al., 2016; Obamuyide et al., 2019).
Metric-based approaches embed samples and classify by distance in a learned space (Vinyals et al., 2016; Snell et al., 2017; Geng et al., 2019).
Optimization-based approaches learn initializations that adapt with a few gradient steps (Finn et al., 2017; Mi et al., 2019).
In low-resource NLP, such techniques have been applied to dialog generation, machine translation, and slot tagging with encouraging results (Madotto et al., 2019; Gu et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020; Sharaf et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021).
Memorization overfitting in meta-learning remains a central challenge.
Yin et al. (2020) introduce an information bottleneck that restricts task-specific information but can hurt performance under strong compression.
Rajendran et al. (2020) modify supervision by injecting noise into support and query labels during episodic training.
Liu et al. (2019) study task interpolation for few-shot image classification and report improvements on miniImageNet.
Chen et al. (2020) describe prototype refinement that updates class centroids with episodic memory in metric-based frameworks for cross-domain adaptation in standard meta-learning benchmarks.
We instead propose to harness information stored in external memories of support sets to guide adaptation without altering labels.
External memory has proven effective for few-shot learning across modalities (Santoro et al., 2016; Geng et al., 2019; Munkhdalai et al., 2019).
Methods either refine representations stored in memory or update parameters using memory-augmented controllers (Ramalho and Garnelo, 2018; Munkhdalai and Yu, 2017; Cai et al., 2018).
In NLP, several systems persist encoded context in differentiable memories to enable rapid retrieval during adaptation (Holla et al., 2020).
Geng et al. (2019) develop a memory induction module with dynamic routing for few-shot text classification, and Munkhdalai et al. (2019) learn a neural memory for task adaptation.
Wang et al. (2021) demonstrate that reusing learned features from prior tasks benefits few-shot slot tagging.
