spans: Automatic metrics such as ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) were introduced with comparisons to concurrent metrics on non-dialogue corpora, typically reporting correlations with reference-oriented criteria. Bhandari et al. (2020) applied pyramid (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004) on several news summarization datasets to score system outputs, Fabbri et al. (2021b) used CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) to gather human ratings for relevance, consistency, fluency, and coherence, and later re-evaluated dozens of metric variants across multiple categories.

None of these target dialogue summarization corpora or introduce fresh human-judgment collections; Gabriel et al. (2021) analyzes factual consistency on SAMSum (Gliwa et al., 2019) while reporting results for a small set of metrics. Tang et al. (2021) study system outputs on SAMSum and enumerate error types, Chen and Yang (2020) sample model generations on the same dataset and report qualitative patterns, and ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and BERTScore are presented across papers without a unified assessment protocol or shared pool of candidate models; DialogSum (Chen et al., 2021) appears in some evaluations alongside MediaSum (Zhu et al., 2021).
document: We review meta-evaluation of automatic metrics and human assessments for summarization, with an emphasis on dialogue summarization. Automatic metrics such as ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) were introduced with comparisons to concurrent metrics on non-dialogue corpora, typically reporting correlations with reference-oriented criteria. Bhandari et al. (2020) applied pyramid (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004) on several news summarization datasets to score system outputs, Fabbri et al. (2021b) used CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015) to gather human ratings for relevance, consistency, fluency, and coherence, and later re-evaluated dozens of metric variants across multiple categories. None of these target dialogue summarization corpora or introduce fresh human-judgment collections; Gabriel et al. (2021) analyzes factual consistency on SAMSum (Gliwa et al., 2019) while reporting results for a small set of metrics. Tang et al. (2021) study system outputs on SAMSum and enumerate error types, Chen and Yang (2020) sample model generations on the same dataset and report qualitative patterns, and ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and BERTScore are presented across papers without a unified assessment protocol or shared pool of candidate models; DialogSum (Chen et al., 2021) appears in some evaluations alongside MediaSum (Zhu et al., 2021).

Despite being an important research topic with real-life applications, domain adaptation for lowresource/few-shot concept-to-text NLG has not been extensively researched. Wen et al. (2016) leveraged the scarcity of target in-domain data by augmenting it with synthetic data, Tran and Nguyen (2018) used variational autoencoders in conjunction with text similarity and domain critics to better guide the fine-tuning process, while Mi et al. (2019) tackled the problem by defining domain adaptation as an optimisation meta-learning task. Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) and Kale and Rastogi (2020) have proposed the use of pretrained language models to tackle few-shot and zero-shot learning in concept-to-text NLG, achieving significant gains over strong non-pretrained baselines. Specifically, Peng et al. (2020) proposed SC-GPT, a semantically conditioned GPT-2 model, wherein, prior to few-shot learning, the GPT-2 model is further fine-tuned on a number of task-oriented dialogue datasets in order to mitigate the problem of representation bias. On the other hand, in Kale and Rastogi (2020), a set of human-authored templates are used to generate high-quality sentences corresponding to each unit of information in an MR. Radford et al. (2019) introduced GPT-2 for controllable text generation in broad domains. Ziegler et al. (2019) studied preference-guided fine-tuning with feedback to adjust model outputs, and Keskar et al. (2019) analyzed control by conditioning tokens in transformers. In this paper's evaluation, we opt to compare our approach against the naive T5 baseline introduced by Kale and Rastogi (2020), as it is shown to vastly outperform SC-GPT by effectively replacing the underlying GPT-2 model with T5, and SC-GPT had outperformed all previous non-pretrained baselines. We do not compare against T2G2, as access to human authored templates or other such manually annotated resources, which are by nature very domain-specific and costly to create, are not necessarily guaranteed in low-resource settings. We note that T2G2 is equivalent to the naive T5 when templates are not employed.

In our proposed system, the hierarchy emerges from modelling the lexicalisation and aggregation sub-tasks on separate layers. Previous attempts in exploring hierarchical structures for text generation tasks instead focused on modelling different aspects of the input or output. In concept-to-text NLG for task-oriented dialogues, Su et al. ( 2018) proposed a multi-layered decoding process where each layer was responsible for generating words associated with specific part-of-speech tags. Chen et al. (2019) and Tseng et al. (2019) took advantage of the intrinsically hierarchical structure of dialogue acts to create better input representations and ease domain adaptation. Lewis et al. (2020) released BART, a denoising autoencoder that improved generation quality across summarization and translation benchmarks. Raffel et al. (2020) unified transfer learning with T5 and reported strong results on heterogeneous tasks within a text-to-text framework across supervised and unsupervised settings. See et al. (2017) proposed a pointer-generator for summarization with coverage to reduce repetition, Zellers et al. (2019) introduced GROVER for news generation and evaluated detection, and He et al. (2020) modeled knowledge-grounded dialogue with retrieval-augmented responses. However, in these tasks, the output is not necessarily restricted to be an exact realisation of the input, and can be initially loosely prompted or drafted, and subsequently expanded.

(2017) used kernel density estimates of hidden features, and Ma et al. (2018) argued Local Intrinsic Dimensionality better captures adversarial subspaces, while Carrara et al. (2019b) trained LSTMs on layer-wise trajectories. Building on class-conditional structure, Lee et al. (2018) introduced a Mahalanobis distance detector defined in logit space, and Li and Li (2016) analyzed convolutional-layer statistics with PCA and a cascade classifier. Brown et al. (2020) introduced pretraining that influences downstream robustness, Zhang and Wang (2019) studied feature space separability under class-conditional models, Nguyen et al. (2015) examined fooling images and semantics, and Carlini and Wagner (2017) proposed strong attack objectives used to benchmark detectors. Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017) discussed confidence calibration for OOD inputs, Lee et al. (2017) analyzed softmax density estimates, and Song et al. (2018) explored noise models. Cohen et al. (2019) reported that influence functions with nearest-neighbor distances compete with such embedding-space detectors, and Bayesian uncertainty signals have been widely approximated for detection (Smith and Gal, 2018; Gal, 2016; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016; Leibig et al., 2017). In NLP, early detectors targeted character noise via grammar and spelling cues (Sakaguchi et al., 2017; Mays et al., 1991; Islam and Inkpen, 2009). These attacks are often trivial to flag, so attention shifted to semantically preserving word-level perturbations that maintain fluency and meaning. Perplexity-based criteria have been effective for such cases (Raina et al., 2020; Han et al., 2020; Minervini and Riedel, 2018). Token-level discrimination has also been explored by learning to classify perturbed embeddings and then editing them toward the original decision boundary. Most recently, Mozes et al. (2020) demonstrated strong results with Frequency-Guided Word Substitution, where targeted replacements of rare words reveal adversarial manipulations.

document: Document-level neural machine translation extends sentence-level modeling by leveraging cross-sentence context for improved coherence and coreference. Research has explored architectures, objectives, and decoding strategies that inject document information without incurring prohibitive computational cost. Early encoder–decoder extensions added hierarchical context encoders that summarize preceding sentences into compact vectors for the decoder. Wang et al. (2017) introduced a context attention over prior sentences, and Miculicich et al. (2018) proposed a hierarchical transformer blending local and global cues. Tan et al. (2019) presented relevance-driven context selection to dynamically weight history at each generation step. Memory mechanisms retain discourse signals across sentences to stabilize lexical choice and style. Tu et al. (2018) designed a cache-based decoder to retrieve topical and lexical entries, while Maruf and Haffari (2018) employed key–value memories for long-range retrieval. Beyond architecture, regularization and objectives encourage context use without excessive parameter growth. Jean and Cho (2019) formulated a pairwise ranking loss that prefers translations attending to salient context tokens. Noisy-channel reranking reintroduces adequacy modeling by combining direct models with language models over larger contexts. Yu et al. (2020) instantiated a document-aware reranker that integrates bilingual likelihoods and monolingual fluency across candidate sets. Concatenation-based approaches keep multiple sentences in a single pass to preserve dependencies needed for pronoun resolution and ellipsis. Tiedemann and Scherrer (2017) concatenated adjacent sentences during training and testing, and Bawden et al. (2018) evaluated contrastive pairs targeting discourse phenomena. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated that scaling language models enables strong few-shot performance across tasks using in-context learning. Gao et al. (2021) explored prompt-based fine-tuning methods for classification with limited data, and Liu et al. (2021) surveyed prompt design strategies. Gururangan et al. (2020) showed that continued pretraining on domain-specific corpora can yield notable improvements on downstream tasks. Beltagy et al. (2020) introduced sparse attention for contexts. Our work integrates hierarchical encoding with a lightweight cache and a discriminative objective to improve document-wide consistency. We train with multi-resolution batches mixing sentence, paragraph, and document instances to calibrate attention scope without truncation. Experiments on Europarl, News Commentary, and OpenSubtitles show gains on pronoun test suites, lexical cohesion, and human-rated coherence.

spans: ORQA first introduced Inverse Cloze Task pretraining for passage retrieval (Lee et al., 2019). DPR adopted a contrastive objective to learn dense representations and reported strong benchmarks (Karpukhin et al., 2020). ANCE explored negative sampling via asynchronous updates to improve top-k recall (Xiong et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation from cross-encoders to bi-encoders was investigated for enhanced effectiveness on open-domain QA datasets (Izacard and Grave, 2021; Yang et al., 2021). Some studies reported further gains on multilingual retrieval with similar setups.

Contrastive learning was widely studied in vision with instance discrimination and momentum encoders, including MoCo and SimCLR, and it inspired formulations for dense retrieval using in-batch negatives and temperature scaling (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). Phrase-level indexing and late interaction were explored in ODQA, including DensePhrase, ColBERT, and FiD, reported across setups without clarifying retrieval versus reader effects (Lee et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2020).
document: Open-Domain Passage Retrieval has been a hot research topic in recent years. It aims to extract evidence passages for a specific question from large corpora like Wikipedia while meeting strict accuracy and latency requirements. Traditional methods such as TF-IDF (Ramos et al., 2003) and BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) rely on lexical matching between queries and passages. These approaches are fast but miss semantic matches and often underperform on retrieval accuracy. Cross-encoder neural rankers built on pretrained language models improved accuracy markedly (Vig and Ramea, 2019; Wolf et al., 2019). Their computational cost per query limits scalability for web-scale search. Bi-encoder architectures precompute corpus embeddings to deliver low-latency dense retrieval at scale. ORQA first introduced Inverse Cloze Task pretraining for passage retrieval (Lee et al., 2019). DPR adopted a contrastive objective to learn dense representations and reported strong benchmarks (Karpukhin et al., 2020). ANCE explored negative sampling via asynchronous updates to improve top-k recall (Xiong et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation from cross-encoders to bi-encoders was investigated for enhanced effectiveness on open-domain QA datasets (Izacard and Grave, 2021; Yang et al., 2021). Some studies reported further gains on multilingual retrieval with similar setups. Contrastive learning was widely studied in vision with instance discrimination and momentum encoders, including MoCo and SimCLR, and it inspired formulations for dense retrieval using in-batch negatives and temperature scaling (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). Phrase-level indexing and late interaction were explored in ODQA, including DensePhrase, ColBERT, and FiD, reported across setups without clarifying retrieval versus reader effects (Lee et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2020). Our method builds on bi-encoder contrastive training and directly optimizes evidence granularity to mitigate conflicts between positives and hard negatives while retaining low latency.

(2019) analyze multilingual BERT and show emergent alignment without explicit supervision on a range of token-level tasks. Conneau et al. (2020) introduce XLM-R, trained on CommonCrawl, achieving strong results on named entity recognition and entailment across many typologically diverse languages in practice.
document: One important aspect of our study is the evaluation of cross-lingual consistency in multilingual NER and EL tasks. Prior work emphasizes that consistency should be measured across language-invariant properties rather than surface overlap. Bianchi et al. (2021) formalize criteria for evaluating invariance across translations and paraphrases. They also report that commercial translation pipelines introduce domain-dependent shifts that compromise stable entity decisions. This motivates protocols that decouple task evaluation from artifacts introduced by language transformation models. Effective measurement of dataset quality is another aspect of growing significance for robust multilingual modeling. Training large language models requires huge amounts of data, and both pretrained and fine-tuned systems inherit dataset biases. Swayamdipta et al. (2020) demonstrate that data-quality-aware design decisions can improve overall model performance. They propose a taxonomy of data regions characterized by out-of-distribution features, class-probability fluctuations, and annotation-level discrepancies. Subsequent studies adapt these ideas to multilingual corpora to guide sampling and error analysis. Usually, multilingual datasets are collected from diverse sources that vary in register, genre, and sociolinguistic profile. It is important to assess whether the resulting samples are representative of native speaker usage and local norms. MasakhaNER (Adelani et al., 2021) exemplifies a community-curated dataset whose provenance aligns with local usage and annotator knowledge. Rahimi et al. (2019) study cross-lingual transfer for sequence labeling using language-agnostic representations and report uneven gains across morphologically rich languages. Pires et al. (2019) analyze multilingual BERT and show emergent alignment without explicit supervision on a range of token-level tasks. Conneau et al. (2020) introduce XLM-R, trained on CommonCrawl, achieving strong results on named entity recognition and entailment across many typologically diverse languages in practice. These system-level advances do not on their own guarantee equitable performance in low-resource settings. Joshi et al. (2020) survey language technologies and reveal persistent disparities in coverage and quality across the world’s languages. Their quantitative analysis ties capability gaps to data availability, community involvement, and infrastructural constraints. In response, recent work emphasizes participatory dataset creation and transparent reporting for multilingual benchmarks. For entity-centric tasks, shared annotation schemas and culturally aware guidelines are essential to maintain cross-lingual comparability. Evaluation protocols should include calibration, robustness to perturbations, and fairness metrics stratified by language, domain, and script. Our study builds on these insights by proposing diagnostic tests that isolate invariance violations from dataset artifacts. We also introduce sampling strategies that prioritize community-sourced text while maintaining control over genre and topical balance. Together, these components yield a principled framework for assessing cross-lingual consistency in multilingual NER and EL.

Podolskiy et al. (2021) evaluate uncertainty scores for out-of-distribution detection in transformers. Zeng et al. (2021) study score-based criteria on intent classification benchmarks. Hu and Khan (2021) explore confidence estimation for noisy labels in text. Hein et al. document: Reliable uncertainty estimates can be obtained by constructing ensembles of decorrelated neural networks, known as deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Despite their effectiveness, ensembles entail substantial compute and memory overhead for training, inference, and storage, which limits practical deployment. Bayesian neural networks offer uncertainty as a built-in capability and formalize predictive distributions over weights (Blundell et al., 2015). These models often require specialized training procedures and can mirror the computational burdens seen with ensembles. Dropout has been shown to correspond to approximate variational inference in deep Gaussian processes (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). This perspective motivates Monte Carlo dropout, which samples subnetworks at test time without adding parameters to the learned posterior. MC dropout imposes little training or memory overhead but may require many forward passes to achieve stable estimates. Deterministic approximations to Bayesian inference aim to capture predictive uncertainty while preserving the speed of standard neural networks. Lee et al. (2018) analyze neural networks in the Gaussian process limit and study predictive covariances. Van Amersfoort et al. (2020) present deterministic uncertainty quantification using feature space density models. Mukhoti et al. (2021) examine training losses that shape predictive distributions for classification. Malinin and Gales (2018) introduce Dirichlet Prior Networks for explicit distributional shift sensitivity. Sensoy et al. (2018) propose evidential deep learning using subjective logic for classification uncertainty modeling. These lines of work target scalable uncertainty while differing in assumptions, calibration behavior, and robustness under distribution shift. In computer vision, benchmark studies report competitive performance of deterministic scores and deep ensembles across classification and detection tasks (Ovadia et al., 2019; Ashukha et al., 2020). By contrast, evidence in NLP remains fragmented due to task heterogeneity and dataset biases. Prior research on OOD detection for text often relies on maximum softmax probability and energy scores as baselines (Hendrycks and Gimpel, 2017; Liu et al., 2020). Follow-up work introduces temperature scaling, input perturbations, and pretraining-aware features to improve separability of in-domain and out-of-domain samples (Liang et al., 2018; Hendrycks et al., 2020). Calibration has also received attention, highlighting the gap between nominal confidence and empirical accuracy in modern language models (Guo et al., 2017; Desai and Durrett, 2020). Beyond OOD detection, misclassification detection for text requires sensitivity to both epistemic and aleatoric components of predictive uncertainty (Kendall and Gal, 2017). Recent methods regularize training to shape decision boundaries and confidence surfaces for ambiguous examples (Mukhoti et al., 2021; Shelmanov et al., 2021). Mixup-based augmentation has been adopted to synthesize harder examples and encourage smoother confidence landscapes (Thulasidasan et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Podolskiy et al. (2021) evaluate uncertainty scores for out-of-distribution detection in transformers. Zeng et al. (2021) study score-based criteria on intent classification benchmarks. Hu and Khan (2021) explore confidence estimation for noisy labels in text. Hein et al. (2019) analyze confidence under adversarial perturbations using certified bounds with stronger theoretical guarantees. Despite promising results, the comparative picture across NLP tasks remains incomplete due to varying architectures and evaluation metrics. We conduct a systematic study of uncertainty estimation for text classification and named entity recognition covering both misclassification and OOD detection. We evaluate ensembles, MC dropout, deterministic density surrogates, calibration post-hoc methods, and loss-based regularizers under common data splits and controlled shifts. We also introduce two simple modifications that improve calibration and detection performance while keeping training and inference budgets comparable to standard baselines.

document: Anaphora encompasses coreference, where expressions denote the same entity, and bridging, where expressions are linked by semantic or encyclopedic relations (Asher and Lascarides, 1998; Clark and Manning, 2015; Hou et al., 2018). Research has concentrated on declarative genres with large, multi-domain corpora such as OntoNotes and CoNLL 2012, with complementary resources like WikiCoref extending coverage to encyclopedic text (Pradhan et al., 2012; Weischedel et al., 2013; Ghaddar and Langlais, 2016b). These corpora adopt mention clustering schemas that record identity and apposition while abstracting away directionality of links, which shapes downstream model design and evaluation. In specialized domains, BioNLP-ST 2011 targets gene-related references in abstracts, whereas CRAFT annotates full biomedical articles using OntoNotes-style conventions (Kim et al., 2011; Cohen et al., 2017). Beyond biomedicine, SCIERC labels coreference across scientific abstracts to support entity-centric information extraction (Luan et al., 2018). Definitions of bridging vary across corpora, distinguishing referential links that presuppose discourse entities from lexical-semantic associations such as part–whole and subtype relations (Zeldes, 2017; Hou et al., 2018). ARRAU, ISNotes, and BASHI provide complementary coverage of bridging in news and WSJ texts, while SciCorp brings the same scheme to scientific prose (Poesio et al., 2008; Hou et al., 2018; Rösiger, 2016; Rösiger, 2018a). Procedural discourse remains comparatively under-resourced despite application interest in instructions, recipes, and laboratory protocols (Mysore et al., 2019; Friedrich et al., 2020). Existing efforts include ChEMU-ref for chemistry narratives and RISeC for English recipes with zero anaphora candidates, but general bridging is rarely marked (Modi et al., 2016; Fang et al., 2021a; Jiang et al., 2020). Pradhan et al. (2012) detail multilingual coreference in OntoNotes with identity and appositive links. Kiddon et al. (2015) model recipes as action graphs to support entity tracking across procedural steps. Hou (2020) frames bridging as question answering trained on external QA corpora. Nishimura et al. Marin et al. (2019) present a multimodal recipe dataset pairing images with stepwise text for retrieval tasks. Rösiger (2018a) describes referential bridging in WSJ articles without altering the label space. Methodologically, neural span-ranking displaced mention-ranking by learning contextualized spans and antecedent scoring with coarse-to-fine pruning (Lee et al., 2017; Kantor and Globerson, 2019). Transfer learning further improves coreference by pretraining on diverse corpora and adapting to target domains with limited supervision (Brack et al., 2021; Xia and Van Durme, 2021). Bridging has seen rule-based heuristics and neural formulations, including question answering setups and joint training with coreference (Hou et al., 2014; Hou, 2020; Yu and Poesio, 2020). Despite progress, unified treatment of coreference and bridging in procedural text remains scarce, motivating datasets and models tailored to instructional discourse.

document: With the rise of social media and hostile forums online (Phadke and Mitra, 2020; Szendro, 2021), offensive language detection has become more important than ever before. Several recent works characterize taxonomies, datasets, and modeling strategies for abusive, hateful, and toxic speech (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018; Shushkevich and Cardiff, 2019; Mishra et al., 2019; Parekh and Patel, 2017). Yet research that focuses specifically on stereotype detection in English remains limited. This scarcity arises because stereotypes are often subtler than overt insults and require annotators to recognize culturally contingent knowledge, complicating reliable labeling. We address this by decomposing stereotypical cues into linguistic dimensions and crowdsourcing only judgments that depend on textual inference rather than encyclopedic world knowledge. Targeted studies isolate stereotypes, including diagnostic test suites that probe model sensitivity (Nadeem et al., 2020; Nangia et al., 2020), as well as modeling approaches that leverage external knowledge and semi-supervised signals (Badjatiya et al., 2019; Fraser et al., 2021). Large-scale hate speech datasets catalog abusive lexicons and user dynamics (Davidson et al., 2017; Founta et al., 2018; Waseem and Hovy, 2016). Metrics for social bias in NLP evaluate stereotype harms and counterfactual fairness (Sap et al., 2019; Blodgett et al., 2020). Cross-lingual studies report domain shifts (Zhao, 2021). Existing computational treatments delineate examples spanning explicit stereotypes, implicit associations, anti-stereotypes, and neutral descriptions, yet much prior work either repurposes datasets built for hate speech and toxicity or adapts diagnostic sets for classification. Diagnostic resources, being largely crowd-constructed, provide limited coverage of naturally occurring discourse and stylistic variety (Blodgett et al., 2021b). To complement these resources, we curate in-the-wild data from Reddit by mining subforums that regularly feature subtle, stereotype-laden narratives in everyday language. Given the low-resource character of stereotype detection, semi-supervised annotation paired with reinforcement learning for data selection is a promising strategy (Ye et al., 2020; Konyushkova et al., 2020; Laskin et al., 2020). Ye et al. (2020) study a single task with unlabeled corpora to produce automated annotations, while our framework uses neighboring tasks with distinct labels in a multi-task setup and trains an RL agent to select auxiliary examples that most benefit the target objective. We empirically compare baselines trained solely on diagnostic suites with models trained on our Reddit corpus and semi-supervised mixtures, and we release guidelines that operationalize linguistic signals of stereotyping for replicability and extension.

Demonstration-based prompting is exemplified by LM-BFF, which constructs exemplars from masked templates on similar inputs to stabilize training and reduce sensitivity to prompt choices (Gao et al., 2021). document: Controllable text generation seeks to modify style or attributes while preserving core content in fluent outputs. Early approaches rely on text-based autoencoders to induce sentence representation spaces that permit attribute manipulations (Shen et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2020; Mai Montero et al., 2021). We argue that steering vectors can be extracted from large pretrained language models, enabling attribute control without training new models. Prior work also adapts hidden states with steerable layers, adapters, or logit adjustments using auxiliary models (Gulcehre et al., 2015; Dathathri et al., 2019; Houlsby et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021; Krause et al., 2021). Hu et al. (2017) propose a variational framework that controls attributes with discriminators attached to a decoder. Yang and Klein (2017) explore element-wise transformations for style transfer and compare reconstruction objectives across domains. Prabhumoye et al. (2018) study back-translation for attribute modification and discuss disentangling content from form in latent variables. Radford et al. (2019) introduce large-scale generative pretraining that yields controllable behaviors when prompted with carefully chosen prefixes. Brown et al. (2020) describe few-shot prompting and observe emergent control signals in attention patterns, while Keskar et al. (2019) relate control to conditional generation using plug-in attributes. Gehrmann et al. (2019) examine human-in-the-loop editing interfaces that guide surface realization under content constraints. Our method operates directly in the latent spaces of frozen generators by computing and applying steering vectors. The procedure manipulates only intermediate representations associated with target attributes and never fine-tunes model parameters. Subramani et al. (2019) ask whether LSTM language models encode sentence vectors sufficient for reconstruction, which motivates our investigation of extractable control directions. We additionally evaluate whether vector arithmetic supports unsupervised style transfer while keeping semantics intact. Li and Liang (2021) present prefix-tuning to steer frozen transformers using continuous prompts. Lester et al. (2021) study prompt tuning across scales and report control effects for tasks with discrete attributes. Schick and Schütze (2021) examine pattern-based prompting with verbalizers and compare classifier-guided decoding. Raffel et al. (2020) discuss sequence-to-sequence pretraining objectives that support flexible conditioning for generation. We benchmark on sentiment, toxicity, and formality control with standardized preprocessing and strong baselines. We assess preservation with semantic similarity, reference-free adequacy judgments, and faithfulness probes based on masked spans. Our contributions include a simple extraction pipeline, a diagnostic for attribute locality, and an analysis of robustness to prompt variation. The remainder of the paper details the method, experimental setup, results, and limitations.

document: The standard practice of using BERT is fine-tuning, i.e. the entirety of the model parameters is adjusted on the training corpus of the downstream task, so that the model is adapted to that specific task (Devlin et al., 2019). There is also an alternative feature-based approach, used by ELMo (Peters et al., 2018). In the latter approach, the pre-trained model is regarded as a feature extractor with frozen parameters. During the learning of a downstream task, one feeds a fixed or learnable combination of the model's intermediate representations as input to the task-specific module, and only the parameters of the latter will be updated. It has been shown that the fine-tuning approach is generally superior to the feature-based approach for BERT in terms of task performance (Devlin et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2019). A natural middle ground between these two approaches is partial fine-tuning, i.e. only fine-tuning some topmost layers of BERT while keeping the remaining bottom layers frozen. This approach has been studied in (Houlsby et al., 2019;Merchant et al., 2020), where the authors observed that finetuning only the top layers can almost achieve the performance of full fine-tuning on several GLUE tasks. The approach of partial fine-tuning essentially regards the bottom layers of BERT as a feature extractor. Freezing weights from bottom layers is a sensible idea as previous studies show that the mid layer representations produced by BERT are most transferrable, whereas the top layers representations are more task-oriented (Wang et al., 2019;Tenney et al., 2019b,a;Merchant et al., 2020). Building on this perspective, recent work has examined how to allocate trainable capacity while retaining as much of the pretrained model as possible. Adapter layers inject small bottleneck modules into each transformer block, enabling task transfer with a fraction of parameters (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation transfers supervision from a large teacher to a compact student via softened targets (Hinton et al., 2015) in practice. Back-translation augments parallel data by translating monolingual corpora to improve sequence learning (Sennrich et al., 2016) today. EDA applies simple lexical operations to expand scarce datasets (Wei and Zou, 2019). Large-scale prompting with GPT-3 demonstrates emergent few-shot capabilities without weight updates (Brown et al., 2020) for many tasks. Prompt- and prefix-based methods instead train small continuous vectors to condition frozen backbones, reducing compute while maintaining accuracy on many classification and generation tasks (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021). Low-rank adaptation further limits trainable parameters by constraining updates to factorizations of the weight matrices, yielding strong trade-offs between speed and quality (Hu et al., 2022). Bias-only tuning and layerwise scaling provide even lighter updates, though they can lag on complex sequence-to-sequence tasks (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022;Mahabadi et al., 2021). Recent analyses suggest that parameter-efficient schemes benefit from distributing small capacity across many layers rather than concentrating it at the top (He et al., 2022;Pfeiffer et al., 2021). In domain adaptation, such methods enable rapid personalization while preserving a robust, shared backbone across tasks (Ansell et al., 2021). Our study situates partial fine-tuning within this spectrum by quantifying which subsets of layers capture reusable knowledge versus task-specific behavior. We provide controlled comparisons that match compute budgets and training data to isolate the conditions under which partial fine-tuning is competitive.

document:
Rational Speech Act (RSA)

This paper examines pragmatic inference in referential communication through the Rational Speech Act framework. Our work is inspired by Andreas and Klein (2016) and by Lazaridou and Baroni (2020), who used comparable referential games to train rational speakers that produce natural image captions. The Rational Speech Act (RSA) model (Frank and Goodman, 2012) is a probabilistic account of speakers and listeners who recursively reason about each other's intentions. In visually grounded reference, given an image m, a literal speaker S0 proposes a caption c with probability PS0(c|m). A pragmatic listener L1 infers the most likely image given the caption by inverting the literal model. A pragmatic speaker S1 then chooses a caption that maximizes expected collaborative utility under L1. Standard treatments often assume speakers and listeners share identical capabilities and knowledge. This assumption rarely holds in real applications where users vary in expertise, attention, and access to context. We formalize listener disparity d as variation in priors, perceptual noise, and lexical knowledge within RSA. The resulting model calibrates description specificity to anticipated listener uncertainty while preserving fluency. We instantiate these ideas in a referential game with controlled visual ambiguity and domain gaps. The training objective combines likelihood for S0 with utility-weighted imitation for S1 using differentiable approximations. We report results on datasets that stress fine-grained attributes and out-of-vocabulary synonyms. Baselines include literal captioners, non-recursive pragmatic decoders, and risk-neutral utilities. Our evaluation measures target identification accuracy, description length, and human preference. We additionally analyze error types by disparity source to isolate perceptual versus lexical effects. Prior research has developed numerous pragmatic systems at the interface of language and vision. Work on pragmatic vision–language has examined color reference games (Monroe et al., 2017). Pragmatically informative captioning was studied with attribute-based rational speakers (Cohn-Gordon et al., 2018). Visual question answering introduced biases and evaluation metrics such as CIDEr (Goyal et al., 2017; Vedantam et al., 2015). Neural pragmatic agents were tested in grounded dialogue (Hawkins et al., 2020). While these threads are valuable, few directly address asymmetric abilities between interlocutors. Our disparity-aware RSA augments the literal channel with listener-conditioned costs over attributes and referents. The approach yields interpretable trade-offs between brevity, redundancy, and discriminativity under bounded listeners. Empirical gains persist across models and training regimes, indicating robustness to architecture and data variation. We release code, experimental protocols, and trained checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparison.

document: In NLP, transformer-based models have achieved strong performance, and many works analyze the interpretability of attention mechanisms. For example, Serrano and Smith (2019) and Jain and Wallace (2019) studied single-layer attention and found that weights correlate weakly with importance, while Wiegreffe and Pinter (2019) emphasize assessing the model as a whole. Chefer et al. (2021) propose relevance propagation tailored to transformers in vision. Baan et al. (2019) examine faithfulness in abstractive summarization, and Belinkov and Glass (2019) survey adversarial fragility in neural NLP models. In this section, we primarily review gradient-based feature attribution methods. Simonyan et al. (2013) introduced saliency maps by computing input-output gradients, which adapt to NLP by differentiating with respect to word embeddings. Gradient*Input (Shrikumar et al., 2017) sharpens saliency by scaling gradients with inputs, while Layerwise Relevance Propagation (Bach et al., 2015) is closely related under a scaling view. SmoothGrad (Smilkov et al., 2017) averages gradients over noisy inputs to reduce visual noise in attribution. Guided Backpropagation (Springenberg et al., 2014) and Grad-CAM variants (Selvaraju et al., 2017) were designed for vision architectures, limiting their direct applicability to transformer-based language models. Sundararajan et al. (2017) define Integrated Gradients along a path from a baseline, Adebayo et al. (2018) critique saliency sensitivity tests, and Kindermans et al. (2019) analyze input invariance in gradient-based attribution properties. Lundberg and Lee (2017) formalize SHAP as expectations, Shrikumar et al. document: Zero Shot Transfer. Multilingual models like mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020) have shown surprising effectiveness in zero-shot transfer, where fine-tuning the MMLM on a task in some source language often leads to impressive performance on the same task in other languages as well without explicitly training on them. Pires et al. (2019) first observed this phenomenon for NER (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002;Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003;Levow, 2006) and POS tagging (Nivre et al., 2018) tasks. Concurrently, Wu and Dredze (2019) also showed this surprisingly cross lingual transfer ability of mBERT additionally on tasks like Document Classification (Schwenk and Li, 2018), Natural Language Inference (Conneau et al., 2018) and Dependency Parsing (Nivre et al., 2018). Factors Affecting Zero Shot Transfer. Pires et al. (2019) showed that vocabulary memorization played little role in zero-shot generalization as language pairs with little word piece overlap also exhibited impressive crosslingual performance. K et al. arrived at a similar conclusion by training BERT on an artificially generated language to zero out the word overlap with the target languages, and observed only minor drops in the performance compared to training the model on English. On the contrary Wu and Dredze (2019), observed strong correlations between the sub-word overlap and the zero-shot performance in four out of five tasks. Wu and Dredze (2020) showed that mBERT performed much worse for zero-shot transfer to low resource languages (i.e., less pre-training data) than high resource ones on POS Tagging, NER and Dependency Parsing tasks. Lauscher et al. (2020) also had a similar observation on tasks like XNLI and XQuAD (Artetxe et al., 2020), though they found that the zero-shot performance on NER, POS tagging and Dependency Parsing tasks might not strictly depend on the pre-training size and could be better explained by different linguistic relatedness features like syntactic and phonological similarities between the language pair. Similar dependence on the typological relatedness such as word order had also been observed by Pires et al. (2019). Adapter-based tuning achieved strong multilingual transfer with compact parameters (Pfeiffer et al., 2020), and language-specific adapters can be composed to control sharing. Back-translation and retrieval-augmented data generation improved cross-lingual question answering and retrieval performance (Asai et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020a). Unsupervised lexicon mapping and alignment reduce representation gaps across languages (Artetxe et al., 2018). Performance Prediction. Prior work has explored predicting the performance of machine learning models from unlabelled data by either measuring (dis)agreements between multiple classifiers (Platanios et al., 2014(Platanios et al., , 2017 or by utilizing underlying information about data distribution (Domhan et al., 2015). In the context of NLP Birch et al. (2008) explored predicting the performance of a Machine Translation system by utilizing different explanatory variables for the language pairs. Lin et al. (2019) proposed a learning to rank approach to choose transfer languages for cross lingual learning using several linguistic and dataset specific features. Specifically predicting the zero-shot performance of MMLMs was first explored in Lauscher et al. (2020), where they used a linear regression model to estimate the cross-lingual transfer performance based on pre-training data size and linguistic relatedness features. Srinivasan et al. (2021) tackled this problem by utilizing XGBoost Regressor for the prediction along with a larger set of features. Recently, Dolicki and Spanakis (2021) explored individual syntactic features for zero-shot performance prediction instead of working with aggregate similarity values, and showed about 2 to 4 times gain in performance. We extend these work by considering a multi-task learning approach, where performance prediction in a task utilizes not only the data available for that task, but also the patterns observed for other tasks.

document: The rapid growth of legal text processing is evidenced by numerous papers at top NLP and AI venues (Luo et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2018; Chalkidis et al., 2019a; Valvoda et al., 2021). Overviews and surveys consolidate progress in the area (Chalkidis and Kampas, 2018; Zhong et al., 2020b). Dedicated workshops on NLP for legal text are now held regularly (Aletras et al., 2019; Di Fatta et al., 2020; Aletras et al., 2020). A central task is legal judgment prediction, where models forecast case outcomes. Research has coalesced into three main lines. One line predicts violations of the European Convention on Human Rights using ECtHR cases (Aletras et al., 2016; Chalkidis et al., 2019a; Medvedeva et al., 2020; Medvedeva et al., 2021). A second line addresses Chinese criminal cases by predicting applicable statutes, charges, and penalty terms (Luo et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019). A third line studies outcome prediction for the U.S. Supreme Court (Ruger et al., 2004; Katz et al., 2017; Kaufman et al., 2019). Similar tasks have been explored in France, the Philippines, Turkey, Thailand, the United Kingdom, Germany, and Switzerland (Sulea et al., 2017; Virtucio et al., 2018; Mumcuoglu et al., 2021; Kowsrihawat et al., 2018; Strickson and De La Iglesia, 2020; Urchs et al., 2021; Niklaus et al., 2021). Work on explainability examines how courts articulate reasons for decisions and how models can surface them (Ye et al., 2018; Chalkidis et al., 2021c; Branting et al., 2021). Another widely studied area is legal topic classification. Early work identified domain-specific challenges using U.S. docket entries (Nallapati and Manning, 2008). Subsequent efforts mapped EU legislation to EuroVoc concepts with attention to few- and zero-shot settings (Mencia and Fürnkranz, 2007; Chalkidis et al., 2020a). Studies on Brazilian Supreme Court cases further expanded multilingual topic classification (Luz de Araujo et al., 2020). Contract analytics has driven information extraction methods that capture parties, payments, dates, and governing law (Hendrycks et al., 2021; Chalkidis et al., 2019c). Related extraction systems target statutes and judicial opinions (Cardellino et al., 2017; Angelidis et al., 2018; Leitner et al., 2019). Legal question answering trains systems to retrieve and synthesize answers from statutes and cases (Kim et al., 2015; Ravichander et al., 2019; Kien et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2020a, 2020c). Kim et al. (2016) detail the COLIEE tasks and associated datasets. Kano et al. (2017) present results across entailment and retrieval tracks with varied team strategies. Kano et al. (2018) release additional resources for statute-based QA and expand baseline systems. Rabelo et al. (2022) curate new legal QA benchmarks emphasizing multilingual statute retrieval tasks. Concurrently, domain-pretrained Transformers for legal text achieve strong results (Chalkidis et al., 2020b; Zheng et al., 2021), motivating our unified English benchmark that standardizes tasks, documentation, and evaluation to assess modern NLP methods in legal NLU.

document: In recent years there has been active interest in the application of NLP techniques to the legal domain (Zhong et al., 2020a). A number of tasks and models have been proposed, inter alia, Legal Judgment Prediction (Chalkidis et al., 2019), Legal Summarization (Bhattacharya et al., 2019; Tran et al., 2019), Prior Case Retrieval (Jackson et al., 2003; Shao et al., 2020), Legal Question Answering, Catchphrase Extraction (Galgani et al., 2012). Legal Judgement Prediction (LJP) involves predicting the final decision from the facts and arguments of the case. Majority of corpora for Legal-NLP tasks have been in English; recently, there have been efforts to address other languages as well, for example, Xiao et al. (2018), have created a large-scale Chinese criminal judgment prediction dataset with over 2.68 million legal documents. Work on Legal-NLP in languages other than English is still in its incipient stages. Our paper contributes toward these efforts by releasing a corpus in Hindi. Majority of the work in the legal domain has focused on the higher court (Malik et al., 2021; Strickson and De La Iglesia, 2020; Zhong et al., 2020b); however, the lower courts handle the maximum number of cases. We try to address this gap by releasing a large corpus of district court level legal documents. The COLIEE organizers (2017; 2019) have published legal data in Japanese, along with their English translation. The competition had two sub-tasks, a legal information retrieval task and an entailment identification task between law articles and queries. Multiple datasets in Chinese have been released for different tasks, namely Reading Comprehension (Duan et al., 2019), Similar Case Matching (Xiao et al., 2019), Question Answering (Zhong et al., 2020b). Chalkidis et al. (2019) examined multi-label classification of legal topics in European court cases. Aletras et al. (2016) predicted judgments at the European Court of Human Rights using textual indicators. Sulea et al. (2017) built corpora for the French Supreme Court and categorized judicial decisions. Katz et al. (2017) analyzed US Supreme Court rulings with machine learning to forecast vote outcomes. Results were reported. Duan et al. (2019) proposed the Chinese Judicial Reading Comprehension (CJRC) dataset with about 10K documents and almost 50K questions with answers. Zhong et al. (2020b) presented JEC-QA, a legal question answering dataset collected from the National Judicial Examination of China with about 26K multiple-choice questions. They augment the dataset with a database containing the legal knowledge required to answer the questions and also assign meta information to each of the questions for in-depth analysis. Xiao et al. (2019) proposed CAIL2019-SCM, a dataset containing 8,964 triplets of the case document, with the objective to identify which two cases are more similar in the triplets. Similar case matching has a crucial application as it helps to identify comparable historical cases. A historical case with similar facts often serves as a legal precedent and influences the judgement. Such historical information can be used to make the legal judgement prediction models more robust. Kleinberg et al. (2017) proposed bail decision prediction as a good proxy to gauge if machine learning can improve human decision making. A large number of bail documents along with the binary decision (granted or denied) makes it an ideal task for automation. In this paper, we also propose the bail prediction task using the HLDC corpus. The HLDC corpus comprises annotated bail orders from multiple states with standardized labels for decision and cited statutes. We release preprocessing scripts and baseline classifiers to facilitate reproducibility and downstream benchmarking for Hindi legal texts. Beyond bail prediction, we outline future extensions to sentencing recommendation and statute retrieval to broaden coverage at the lower court level.

document: Our work is related to dataset construction for persuasive dialogue systems that elicit concrete actions, extending earlier corpora where skilled persuaders targeted narrow goals such as purchasing or donating (Hiraoka et al., 2014; Yoshino et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019), and we scale the setting while broadening scenarios beyond single-task persuasion. Wu et al. (2019) built recommendation dialogues from knowledge-graph paths, and Zhou et al. (2020) and Xu et al. (2020) used similar path-guided collection with annotators following topic nodes across multiple collected datasets. Tang et al. (2019) generated transitions conditioned on a pivot word, Sevegnani et al. (2021) curated one-turn transitions between mismatched topics, and Sun et al. (2021) appended chit-chat to task-oriented dialogues. Unlike these resources, our corpus targets persuasive goal completion while enabling natural movement from small talk to concrete actions, and we evaluate models that exploit pre-trained encoders to plan and realize turns beyond entity recommendation. This design reduces reliance on expert-crafted strategies and costly annotation, facilitates extension to new domains through modular substitution of components, and allows semi-supervised use of the generated data for cold-start cases.

spans: SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021) benchmarks SSL encoders on ten speech tasks using representations and heads, with an emphasis on shallow classification. LeBenchmark (Evain et al., 2021) evaluates French SSL and reports results for wav2vec 2.0 variants (Baevski et al., 2020), and the Zero Resource Speech Benchmark 2021 targets unsupervised spoken language modeling (Nguyen et al., 2020).

The HEAR 2021 Challenge aims to produce general-purpose audio representations across diverse non-speech and speech tasks, including event detection, command recognition, and pitch chroma classification, encouraging unified embeddings for downstream reuse. Our study concentrates on spoken language understanding and generation with broader evaluation and SSL encoders, prior work reports zero-shot probing at phonetic and semantic levels (Nguyen et al., 2020), and LeBenchmark provides evidence with limited diversity (Evain et al., 2021).

document:
Readability Assessment has been an active area in educational research for almost a century. Early research created readability formulae using simple proxies such as word and sentence length and word lists, and hundreds of such formulae were proposed with DuBay (2007) providing a comprehensive history (Lively and Pressey, 1923; Flesch, 1948; Stenner, 1996). With advances in NLP and machine learning, research shifted toward data-driven Automatic Readability Assessment. Studies explored statistical language models and rich lexical, syntactic, and discourse features for machine-learning ARA (Si and Callan, 2001; Heilman et al., 2007; Petersen and Ostendorf, 2009; Pitler and Nenkova, 2008; Todirascu et al., 2013; Xia et al., 2016; Ambati et al., 2016). Features motivated by psycholinguistics and cognitive science were also investigated (Feng et al., 2009; Howcroft and Demberg, 2017).

In recent years, ARA has increasingly focused on textual embeddings and deep architectures. Word embeddings combined with domain knowledge or language modeling have been applied to ARA (Cha et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2018). Azpiazu and Pera (2019) introduced a multi-attentive RNN for assessing textual difficulty, and Mohammadi and Khasteh (2019) explored deep reinforcement learning for readability prediction. Deutsch et al. (2020) found no gains from adding linguistic features to transformers, Lee et al. (2021) combined syntax with BERT, and Imperial (2021) examined pretraining choices. Reports differ on the utility of linguistic features alongside deep models.

Cross-linguistic work expanded ARA beyond English using language-specific feature sets for German, French, Italian, and Bangla, and some studies developed language-agnostic features evaluated on multiple languages (Hancke et al., 2012; François and Fairon, 2012; Dell'Orletta et al., 2011; Sinha et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2013; Azpiazu and Pera, 2019; Madrazo Azpiazu and Pera, 2020a,b; Martinc et al., 2021; Weiss et al., 2021). Madrazo Azpiazu and Pera (2020b) trained on related languages to improve low-resource ARA, and Weiss et al. (2021) applied an English-trained model to German using a broad set of handcrafted features. Martinc et al. (2021) reported multilingual experiments, Shen et al. (2013) proposed language-agnostic features, and cross-lingual embeddings for ARA remain largely untested to date. ARA is most often cast as classification, though alternatives have been considered. Ranking formulations using SVM-rank and handcrafted features have been explored with mixed results (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008; Tanaka-Ishii et al., 2010; Ma et al., 2012; Mesgar and Strube, 2015; Ambati et al., 2016; Howcroft and Demberg, 2017; Xia et al., 2016). Motivated by this background, we revisit ranking for ARA with a new neural pairwise model.

(2019) argue that lower layers capture local patterns and higher layers code semantics. Tenney et al. (2019) introduce a task-based diagnostic suite to map linguistic phenomena to transformer layers. Reif et al. (2019) study how sense clusters emerge in representation space using influence functions with qualitative analyses of nearest neighbors.
document: There has been a growing body of research studying the inner workings of BERT and trying to quantify what it learns in various scenarios, dubbed "BERTology" (Rogers et al., 2020). Of particular interest to this paper is work that analyzes BERT's output embeddings. Recent studies have found that embeddings created from the final layer of BERT tend to cluster according to word senses (Wiedemann et al., 2019), though this varies somewhat based on the position of a word in a sentence (Mickus et al., 2020). The final BERT layers also produce more contextualized word embeddings than the earlier layers (Ethayarajh, 2019), a finding we revisit using paraphrases in Section 3.3. Probing classifiers have been widely used to interpret what linguistic features are linearly recoverable from hidden states (Hewitt and Liang, 2019). Structural probes indicate that syntactic information is encoded in geometry that approximates dependency trees (Hewitt and Manning, 2019). Layer-wise analyses suggest a progressive shift from lexical to syntactic to semantic information across the stack (Tenney et al., 2019). Attention patterns have been examined to reveal whether heads specialize for coreference, syntax, and positional biases (Clark et al., 2019). Calibrating these observations across tasks remains challenging due to variations in datasets and evaluation protocols (Belinkov and Glass, 2019). Another question is how stable these properties are across random seeds and pretraining corpora (Sellam et al., 2022). Contextual geometry has been studied through anisotropy and layer normalization effects, which influence cosine-based similarity measures (Ethayarajh, 2019; Gao et al., 2019). Sentence-level embedding aggregation from BERT has been shown to be sensitive to pooling choices and whitening (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Su et al., 2021). Work on sense representations highlights that subword composition and local syntax can separate contextualized usages even without supervision (Wiedemann et al., 2019; Amrami and Goldberg, 2019). Clark et al. (2019) analyze attention head specialization in BERT across layers and tasks. Jawahar et al. (2019) argue that lower layers capture local patterns and higher layers code semantics. Tenney et al. (2019) introduce a task-based diagnostic suite to map linguistic phenomena to transformer layers. Reif et al. (2019) study how sense clusters emerge in representation space using influence functions with qualitative analyses of nearest neighbors. Beyond individual layers, mixing representations can improve downstream performance through learned pooling and attention over layers (Peters et al., 2018). Contrastive objectives have been used to structure embedding spaces so that paraphrases attract and confounders repel (Gao et al., 2021). Recent work indicates that prompt conditioning can steer representations toward task-relevant subspaces without fine-tuning all parameters (Lester et al., 2021). These findings suggest that controllable geometry is a viable lever for adaptation. In multilingual settings, cross-lingual alignment benefits from both shared subword vocabularies and parallel objectives on sentence pairs (Conneau et al., 2020). Perturbation studies further show that token dropout and span masking affect how robustly senses are separated in context (Kobayashi, 2018; Wettig et al., 2022). While probing informs what is encoded, counterfactual data augmentation clarifies when phenomena are causally used by models (Kaushik et al., 2020). For evaluation, nearest-neighbor retrieval and clustering metrics provide complementary views of representation quality (Van der Maaten and Hinton, 2008; Rosenberg and Hirschberg, 2007). We focus on distinguishing genuine contextualization from lexical memorization by contrasting paraphrase pairs against hard negatives. Our approach integrates layer reweighting with local isotropy calibration to obtain more faithful semantic neighborhoods. The method requires no task-specific supervision and scales linearly with corpus size. Empirically, we observe consistent gains across semantic textual similarity and lexical substitution benchmarks.

Feng et al. (2021) study constrained decoding to enforce ontology consistency in generation. Peng et al. (2020) introduce SOLOIST, a pretrained sequence-to-sequence model for task-oriented dialogue that integrates belief states. Hosseini-Asl et al. (2020) propose SimpleTOD, an autoregressive LM that serializes belief spans and actions in a unified text format for end-to-end modeling.
document: Dialogue state tracking (DST) estimates user goals and slot-value constraints over the course of a task-oriented dialogue. Prior approaches framed DST as question answering (Ruan et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Schema-guided and cross-domain models commonly leverage slot names and natural-language descriptions on MultiWOZ and related corpora (Wu et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2021a). Pretrained generative LLMs such as T5 and GPT-3 enable casting DST as a sequence-to-sequence problem that outputs belief states (Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020). Prompting and in-context learning have attracted interest for controllable state updates and data-efficient generalization (Jiang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021; Madotto et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2021). Smith et al. (2021) present a span-based tracker for unseen slots trained with synthetic paraphrases. Zhao et al. (2021a) analyze schema regularization using slot descriptions on MultiWOZ. Feng et al. (2021) study constrained decoding to enforce ontology consistency in generation. Peng et al. (2020) introduce SOLOIST, a pretrained sequence-to-sequence model for task-oriented dialogue that integrates belief states. Hosseini-Asl et al. (2020) propose SimpleTOD, an autoregressive LM that serializes belief spans and actions in a unified text format for end-to-end modeling. Despite progress, zero-shot and cross-ontology transfer remain difficult, particularly when slot definitions change or are underspecified. Recent DST papers focus on settings with minimal train-test discrepancy and rely on curated ontologies that limit robustness (Zhao et al., 2021a; Feng et al., 2021). We address these gaps by unifying schema descriptions, dialogue context, and latent exemplars in a lightweight prompting interface. Our approach conditions generation on slot-specific instructions while decoupling value realization from schema identifiers to better handle unseen domains. We evaluate on MultiWOZ 2.1 and cross-domain splits, reporting joint goal accuracy, slot F1, and calibration metrics to assess deployment readiness.

Compositional generalization is examined with synthetic datasets and structured decoders that target systematicity and controlled recombination of primitives (Johnson et al., 2017; Hudson and Manning, 2019). Large-scale pretraining aligns images and text using web captions and contrastive loss to obtain transferable representations across tasks and domains (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). Curriculum and pruning strategies choose training examples that emphasize linguistic structures and reduce shortcut learning in protocols (Zhang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2022).
document: Symbol grounding systems traditionally rely on batch learning under static datasets, whereas interactive teaching and learning requires incremental updates so that guidance can shape in-the-moment inferences. Many grounders further assume pre-defined visual features and a fixed vocabulary at initialization, an assumption common in early systems (Kennington et al., 2015; Kennington and Schlangen, 2017; Wang et al., 2017). We relax these assumptions and target linguistically complex referring expressions whose logical structure includes quantification and negation. In multimodal representation learning, models either align regions with words through bounding boxes or instance masks (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Kamath et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021) or exploit web-scale image–caption corpora (Radford et al., 2021). These approaches are optimized offline on large corpora, while our aim is few-shot incremental extraction from sequential, weakly labeled interactions that may introduce neologisms. Grounded language acquisition is often framed as grammar induction from image–caption pairs or as reward-driven semantic parsing for visual reasoning and planning (Shi et al., 2019; Zhao and Titov, 2020; Williams, 1992; Azaria et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016, 2017; Karamcheti et al., 2020). Neural module networks decompose questions into functional programs executed over convolutional features to yield interpretable intermediate results (Andreas et al., 2016). Compositional generalization is examined with synthetic datasets and structured decoders that target systematicity and controlled recombination of primitives (Johnson et al., 2017; Hudson and Manning, 2019). Large-scale pretraining aligns images and text using web captions and contrastive loss to obtain transferable representations across tasks and domains (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). Curriculum and pruning strategies choose training examples that emphasize linguistic structures and reduce shortcut learning in protocols (Zhang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2022). Finally, in visual reference resolution, we do not assume a unique referent at test time, enabling plans that flexibly choose among all objects satisfying an RE to optimize downstream control (Kazemzadeh et al., 2014; Whitney et al., 2016).

Unified meaning representations that merge intent, slot, and belief updates target streamlined multitask objectives (Chen et al., 2020). document: A Brief History of NLU Datasets. As a core module of task-oriented dialogue systems, natural language understanding has been studied since the early 1990s, when the Airline Travel Information System corpus enabled intent and slot modeling from spoken flight queries (Hemphill et al., 1990). In the subsequent two decades, only a handful of resources appeared, limiting comparative evaluation and cross-domain generalization. The release of dialogue state tracking datasets in 2013 initiated a sustained focus on modeling user goals over multi-turn histories rather than single-turn classification (Williams et al., 2013; Henderson et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2016). DST reformulated NLU for ToD as a sequential belief tracking problem grounded in an explicit ontology and evolving slot-value assignments. Concurrent with the neural renaissance, larger multi-domain corpora were introduced to fuel data-hungry models and joint training of intent, slot, and state tracking modules (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2018; Rastogi et al., 2019; Peskov et al., 2019). Despite scale, these datasets inherit constraints from domain-specific schemas, which complicate reuse and create brittleness when ontologies shift across applications. Annotation complexity and crowd-sourcing variability further introduce inconsistent labels, slot boundaries, and implicit goals that degrade downstream robustness and reproducibility (Eric et al., 2019; Zang et al., 2020). To mitigate schema lock-in, several efforts pursue ontology-agnostic or schema-guided formulations that expose natural language descriptions of slots and values to models at training and test time. Schema-Guided Dialogue emphasizes scalable annotations via machine-readable service schemas and has become a standard benchmark for cross-domain generalization (Rastogi et al., 2020). Parallel advances explore weak supervision, transfer learning, and prompt-based adaptation to diminish annotation cost while preserving coverage of rare dialogue phenomena. Synthetic data generation through user simulators, paraphrase models, and controllable text planning augments scarce interactions and stress-tests tracking under distribution shift (Shah et al., 2018; Campagna et al., 2020; Kulhánek et al., 2021). Pretrained language models have been applied to dialogue state tracking with domain adaptation layers (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Unified meaning representations that merge intent, slot, and belief updates target streamlined multitask objectives (Chen et al., 2020). Cross-lingual ToD benchmarks assess zero-shot transfer with multilingual encoders and translated ontologies (Schuster et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). Reported results include goal accuracy, slot F1, and request tracking under revised schemas (Henderson et al., 2014). Alongside modeling, evaluation practices have broadened to include calibration, robustness to ontology drift, and dialogue-level success measures beyond slot accuracy. Recent work highlights the need for transparent error taxonomies and per-turn rationales that expose where models fail in mixed-initiative settings. Multilingual and low-resource scenarios motivate active learning and cross-lingual projection to prioritize utterances with high expected information gain. Open-domain data sources such as web manuals and API documentation are increasingly mined to bootstrap service schemas and natural language descriptions. Ethical and privacy concerns have spurred redaction protocols, consent-aware logging, and synthetic proxies to limit exposure of sensitive attributes. Together, these developments chart a path toward reusable, schema-light NLU benchmarks that better reflect realistic task variability and deployment constraints.

document: In this section, we discuss related studies with respect to the contrastive learning framework and sentence embedding. Contrastive methods have become a dominant paradigm for learning universal sentence representations by pulling semantically aligned texts together and pushing unrelated texts apart. Early work on distributional sentence vectors relied on bag-of-words and averaging word embeddings, which limited expressiveness. Neural encoders trained with supervised natural language inference provided stronger baselines but required costly labels. Unsupervised contrastive pretraining reduced dependency on task-specific annotations while retaining downstream transfer. A central insight in these approaches is the construction of effective positive and negative pairs. Methods such as SimCLR-style augmentation adopt dropout masks and token deletion to create views of the same sentence. In natural language, many studies instead harvest positives from adjacent utterances or back-translated variants. Hard negatives sourced from in-batch examples or mined retrieval sets have been shown to improve discrimination. Temperature scaling and loss normalization also affect the geometry of the embedding space. Smith et al. (2020) introduced a temperature-scaled objective for sentence pairs. Lee and Park (2019) proposed a pooling strategy for multilingual embeddings. Johnson et al. (2018) examined negative sampling distributions in graph contexts. Brown et al. (2021) studied prompt-based models for few-shot tasks without clear linkage to embedding alignment and training dynamics across domains robustly. Beyond objective design, encoder architecture choices influence isotropy and clustering. Transformer-based bi-encoders such as Sentence-BERT employ siamese or triplet structures to enable efficient similarity search. Large-scale distillation from cross-encoders further enhances ranking quality without costly inference. Domain-adaptive pretraining tailors the embedding space to specialized corpora like biomedical abstracts and legal opinions. Multilingual transfer has been explored through shared subword vocabularies and language-adaptive layers. Another line of work augments contrastive learning with explicit semantic signals. Auxiliary tasks include entailment classification, paraphrase identification, and contradiction detection. Curriculum strategies order negatives by estimated hardness to stabilize training. Mining positives from knowledge bases and conversation threads reduces reliance on data augmentation. Evaluation protocols increasingly focus on zero-shot retrieval, clustering, and semantic textual similarity across diverse distributions. Our study synthesizes these insights by analyzing pair construction, loss temperature, and retrieval-oriented fine-tuning under compute-constrained settings.

Embedding tying links the input and softmax weights and reportedly reduces overfitting in translation (Press and Wolf, 2017; Inan et al., 2017). Multilingual joint vocabularies support zero-shot generalization and parameter sharing across encoders (Johnson et al., 2017; Ha et al., 2016; Conneau et al., 2020).
document: Label smoothing is a regularization technique that replaces hard one-hot targets with a convex combination of the true label and a uniform prior over K classes, controlled by a parameter α. Formally, given K classes and a one-hot vector ŷ, the smoothed target ŷLS assigns 1−α to the correct label and α/K to each class, which calibrates predictive probabilities and mitigates overconfidence. The method was popularized in image classification and later adapted to sequence modeling (Szegedy et al., 2016; Pereyra et al., 2017; Müller et al., 2019). In neural machine translation, label smoothing improves BLEU, stabilizes training, and interacts with sequence-level objectives and calibration metrics (Vaswani et al., 2017; Edunov et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2020). Orthogonal to these supervision targets, vocabulary sharing concerns tokenization and parameter tying across source and target languages to improve efficiency and cross-lingual transfer. Subword segmentation has been examined in widely used byte pair encoding and unigram language models (Sennrich et al., 2016; Kudo, 2018). Embedding tying links the input and softmax weights and reportedly reduces overfitting in translation (Press and Wolf, 2017; Inan et al., 2017). Multilingual joint vocabularies support zero-shot generalization and parameter sharing across encoders (Johnson et al., 2017; Ha et al., 2016; Conneau et al., 2020). Building on these strands, we investigate how the choice of shared subword inventories and embedding tying modulates the optimization dynamics of label-smoothed transformers, and we provide guidelines for configuring α and vocabulary design in practical translation systems.

document: Our study addresses linking procedural steps across wikiHow to construct a large-scale procedural knowledge graph. This problem requires identifying semantically related steps and aligning them across articles and domains for improved coverage and reuse. Lagos et al. (2017) formulate a closely related task, linking steps to other wikiHow articles with a heuristic ranking pipeline. We adopt the same high-level objective but redesign the retrieval and reranking stages to scale across the full site. Specifically, we introduce a search-then-rerank approach that boosts recall while maintaining precision through pairwise calibration. We also provide broader human evaluation and downstream assessments to quantify the utility of the links for reasoning and generation. Our resulting resource covers substantially more categories than prior work that focused on the Computer domain. Procedural knowledge is commonly modeled as scripts and event schemata that encode stereotypical sequences of actions and states (Abelson and Schank, 1977; Rumelhart, 1975). Prior work has extracted procedural structures from instructional texts using rule-based and statistical methods (Paris et al., 2002; Delpech and Saint-Dizier, 2008; Zhang et al., 2012). Crowdsourced datasets such as OMICS, SCRIPTS, and ProPara emphasize coverage but often remain small or noisy after manual cleaning (Singh et al., 2002; Regneri et al., 2010; Li et al., 2012; Wanzare et al., 2016; Rashkin et al., 2018). Researchers have also studied reasoning over procedural chains for precondition and effect inference (Takechi et al., 2003; Rajagopal et al., 2020). Applications include intent modeling, planning, and text generation grounded in action sequences (Pareti, 8; Sap et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2020c; Zhang et al., 2020d). wikiHow has served as a rich source for large-scale procedural extraction and workflow mining due to its breadth of topics and step-level structure (Jung et al., 2010; Chu et al., 2017; Park and Motahari Nezhad, 2018). Liu et al. (2021) introduce a taxonomy of prompting techniques for pretrained language models and categorize parameter and prompt tuning regimes. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrate few-shot capabilities of large models across heterogeneous benchmarks using natural language instructions. Raffel et al. (2020) frame supervised and unsupervised NLP under a unified text-to-text paradigm with scaling studies. In contrast to these model-centric studies, our focus is on linking real-world procedural steps to enable cross-article consolidation. We operationalize linking as passage retrieval followed by neural reranking with features tailored to step semantics and argument structure. The retriever indexes step texts and normalized anchors, while the reranker integrates contextualized encodings and lexical overlap. We evaluate link quality with expert judgments on coverage, specificity, and redundancy across diverse categories. Downstream, we show gains for script induction, intent prediction, and instruction following by providing denser cross-article evidence. We release the code, models, and a reproducible benchmark to facilitate fair comparison on large-scale procedural linking.
