spans: Transformer-XL (Dai et al., 2019) introduces segment-level recurrence to extend context for language modeling. Sparse Transformer (Child et al., 2019) applies fixed attention patterns on factored dimensions, Linformer (Wang et al., 2020) uses low-rank projections to approximate dot-product attention, Routing Transformer (Roy et al., 2021) groups tokens with locality-sensitive hashing for content-based routing, and Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021) replaces softmax kernels with random feature maps to scale attention to longer inputs efficiently while maintaining competitive accuracy.
document: Sparse attention mechanism The full attention mechanism has a quadratic memory cost. Prior research works have proposed different sparse attention mechanisms to reduce the memory cost. Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) uses a dilated sliding window of blocks and global attention patterns. BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020) employs sliding window and random blocks. Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020) uses the locality-sensitive hashing. Transformer-XL (Dai et al., 2019) introduces segment-level recurrence to extend context for language modeling. Sparse Transformer (Child et al., 2019) applies fixed attention patterns on factored dimensions, Linformer (Wang et al., 2020) uses low-rank projections to approximate dot-product attention, Routing Transformer (Roy et al., 2021) groups tokens with locality-sensitive hashing for content-based routing, and Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021) replaces softmax kernels with random feature maps to scale attention to longer inputs efficiently while maintaining competitive accuracy. However, sparse attention diminishes the benefits of pretraining and sacrifices parts of the receptive field. Extract-then-generate method The model first extracts salient text snippets from the input, followed by generating a concise overall summary. Most two-stage summarization approaches (Zhang et al., 2019;Lebanoff et al., 2019;Xu and Durrett, 2019;Bajaj et al., 2021) are trained separately, which suffer from information loss due to the cascaded errors. Some approaches attempt to reduce that loss by bridging the two stages. Chen and Bansal (2018) adopts reinforcement learning with a sentence-level policy gradient method. Bae et al. (2019) proposes summary-level policy gradient. In addition to the drawbacks explained in Section 2.3, our model is different as we jointly train an extractthen-generate model for summarization using latent variables. Divide-and-conquer approach A common approach in long input summarization is divide-andconquer (Gidiotis and Tsoumakas, 2020;Grail et al., 2021). This approach breaks a long input into multiple parts, which are summarized separately and combined to produce a final complete summary. However, these models do not capture the contextual dependencies across parts and assumes a certain structure of the input (such as paper sections). Hierarchical models Various hierarchical models have been proposed to handle the longer inputs. Cohan et al. (2018) models the document discourse structure with a hierarchical encoder and a discourse-aware decoder to generate the summary. HAT-Bart (Rohde et al., 2021) proposes a new Hierarchical Attention Transformer-based architecture that attempts to capture sentence and paragraphlevel information. HMNet (Zhu et al., 2020) builds a hierarchical structure that includes discourselevel information and speaker roles. However, these models focus mainly on model performance and not on reducing the memory and computational cost.

document: Semantic parsing typically maps natural language to executable symbolic representations under a predefined grammar (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017), and related ideas appear in program synthesis (Gulwani, 2011; Desai et al., 2016), yet we target agent communication in free-form language without a fixed DSL, aiming for executable behavior without explicit intermediate annotations (Clarke et al., 2010; Berant et al., 2013). In multi-hop QA, numerous datasets (Khashabi et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018; Khot et al., 2020; Geva et al., 2021) and models (Min et al., 2019b; Pan et al., 2021) still admit shortcut reasoning (Min et al., 2019a), yielding brittle generalization (Gardner et al., 2020) and limited progress toward genuine compositional inference (Trivedi et al., 2020; Khot et al., 2021). Talmor and Berant (2018) introduced split-and-rewrite procedures for complex questions, and Min et al. (2019b) learned latent step policies under weak supervision. Perez et al. (2020) used program sketches for modular reasoning, Khot et al. (2021) identified unanswerable sub-parts in multi-hop benchmarks, and Clark et al. (2019) organized multi-agent collaboration for open-domain tasks, Jia et al. (2021) curated compositional splits with fine-grained operation labels. To address these limitations, we introduce a synthetic, multi-agent decomposition challenge spanning three dataset types and four interacting agents, building on insights from controlled generalization tests (Lake and Baroni, 2018; Sinha et al., 2019) to catalyze modeling advances in agent-mediated decomposition (Liu et al., 2021).

document: Sentence rewriting IUR can be considered to be similar to the sentence rewriting task (Xu and Veeramachaneni, 2021; Lin et al., 2021; Chen and Bansal, 2018; Cao et al., 2018). Recent studies have addressed the IUR task with various sophisticated methods. Text generation IUR can be formulated as text generation by using the seq2seq model (Pan et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2021). Li et al. (2020) explored pointer-generator mechanisms for editing-based rewriting in dialogue, while Gupta et al. (2021) examined span selection for content preservation in paraphrase generation, and Zhang et al. (2019) introduced deletion-first strategies for controllable sentence revision. Research on retrieval-augmented generation from empirical evidence fusion in open-domain tasks (Lewis et al., 2020b; Izacard and Grave, 2021), and He et al. (2020) studied contrastive objectives for text encoders, with Qi et al. (2020) presenting dataset curation protocols for conversational paraphrasing. For the generation, several well-known pre-trained models have been applied (Lewis et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020) with promising results. We employ the T5 model (Raffel et al., 2020) as the main component to rewrite utterances, and to address the problem of missing important tokens in model's rewriting, we enhance T5 by introducing a Picker and two labeling methods (Section 3.2). The learning of Θ is composed by only using utterance generation (the generator) or the combination of two tasks: important token identification (the picker) and utterance generation (the generator).

Going from audio recordings to fully annotated transcripts implies two successive segmentation steps: the first segments words and happens during the production of phonemic or orthographic transcripts; the second further splits words into morphs, which are then annotated with syntactic information and glosses. We mostly focus on the former task, assuming a two-step process: first, the computation of a phonemic transcript, that we assume are given; then the segmentation into words for which we consider two settings: batch and online learning. The word and morpheme segmentation tasks are closely related and rely on similar tools: using the Japhug corpus, which contains both levels of segmentations, we also study the implications of using lists of words vs. morphemes as weak supervision.

In its baseline form, the word segmentation process is fully unsupervised, and the only training material is a set of transcribed sentences (see Fig. ).

We rely on Bayesian non-parametric approaches to word segmentation (see (Cohen, 2016) for a thorough exposition), and our baselines are the unigram version of the dpseg model (Goldwater et al., 2009) and a variant where the underlying Dirichlet Process is replaced by a Pitman-Yor Process as in (Neubig, 2014). We selected unigram models for their simplicity, which (a) makes them amenable to the processing of very small sets of sentences; (b) makes the online learning setting tractable. Recent work on acoustic word discovery reports benefits from segmental embeddings and attention over speech features (Kamper et al., 2017; Chorowski et al., 2019; Kreuk et al., 2020). Character-level pretraining with subword-enriched vectors and byte-level models has been studied extensively in corpora (Bojanowski et al., 2017; Radford et al., 2019; Xue et al., 2021).

Word segmentation models fundamentally rely on probabilistic models for word sequences defining P (w = w 1 . . . w T ); word sequences can also be viewed as segmented sequences of characters y = y 1 . . . y L , so that the same model can be used for the joint probability of (y, b), with b = b 1 . . . b L representing the vector of boundary locations where value b t = 1 (resp. b t = 0) denotes a boundary (resp. no boundary) after symbol y t . In an unsupervised setting, these boundaries are hidden and are latent variables in the model. Such models lend themselves well to Gibbs sampling, which repeatedly produces samples of each boundary given all the other boundaries in the corpus.

In dpseg, the underlying sequence model is a unigram model: P (w 1 . . . w T ) = T t=1 P (w t ). The probability of individual words corresponds to a Dirichlet Process with parameters α, the concentration parameter, and P 0 , the base distribution, and yields the following formulation for the conditional probability of w t given the past words w <t :

where n w (w <t ) counts the number of times w has occurred in the past. With lower values of α, the most frequent words tend to be generated more (hence, concentration), while with higher values, the words are more smoothly distributed. P 0 , the base distribution, assigns scores to arbitrary character strings; Goldwater et al. ( 2009) use a length model and a uniform character model. For word w made of characters y 1 , ..., y m , P 0 is computed as:

where p # is the probability to end the word. For this model, Gibbs sampling compares at each position t two sequences of words w t=0 (no boundary at position t) and w t=1 (a boundary is inserted). As these sequences only differ minimally, terms such as P (b t = 0|y, b −t ) are readily derived (see eg. (Goldwater et al., 2009)). Gibbs sampling is performed for a number of iterations that are sufficient to reach convergence, and we use the last iteration to uncover the resulting segmentation. To speed up mixing, Goldwater et al. ( 2009) also use annealing, so that a larger search space is explored.

An extension of dpseg, denoted pypseg, uses a Pitman-Yor Process (PYP) instead of the DP and generalises equation (1) with an additional discount parameter, which enables to better control the generation of new words. PYPs are introduced in (Teh, 2006b;Mochihashi et al., 2009); a fast implementation is in (Neubig, 2014). For our experiments, both models have been re-implemented in Python. Figure : The sentence segmentation task illustrated with a sentence from the Mboshi corpus: 'báa ámikúndá poo yá kala' ('they found the old village'). The two possible segmentations only differ in one boundary at position t = 6, one (w 6=0 ) where 'ámikúndá' is one single unit and one (w 6=1 ) where it is split in two.

spans: Neural models learn phone representations by pretraining continuous embeddings that are later clustered or by directly inducing discrete codes with vector quantization and Gumbel-softmax, yielding large codebooks and varied phoneme discovery scores (Chung et al., 2019; Baevski et al., 2020; van den Oord et al., 2017; Eloff et al., 2019b; Chorowski et al., 2019). Multilingual ASR supplies phone labels to cluster bottleneck features (Żelasko et al., 2020; Feng et al., 2021b).
document: Early work on unsupervised speech representation learning focused on discovering phone-like segments with similar acoustic properties while ignoring semantics (Park and Glass, 2005; Lee and Glass, 2012; Ondel et al., 2016). Two complementary directions emerged: discrete latent-variable models for phones and neural methods that learn continuous or discretized codes without textual labels. Latent-variable approaches detect recurring segments and cluster them or jointly segment and cluster with probabilistic graphical models, with extensions that disentangle speaker or language identity (Jansen et al., 2010; Park and Glass, 2005; Lee and Glass, 2012; Ondel et al., 2016; Kamper et al., 2016; Ondel et al., 2019; Yusuf et al., 2020). Neural models learn phone representations by pretraining continuous embeddings that are later clustered or by directly inducing discrete codes with vector quantization and Gumbel-softmax, yielding large codebooks and varied phoneme discovery scores (Chung et al., 2019; Baevski et al., 2020; van den Oord et al., 2017; Eloff et al., 2019b; Chorowski et al., 2019). Multilingual ASR supplies phone labels to cluster bottleneck features (Żelasko et al., 2020; Feng et al., 2021b). Zero-shot crosslingual studies using IPA tokenization report poor generalization to unseen languages, highlighting limits of label transfer and motivating methods that exploit semantic context from paired or comparable data (Feng et al., 2021a). Work leveraging semantics from translations or images shows that self-supervised objectives can align acoustic units with meaning while revealing phoneme boundaries in intermediate layers (Jansen, 2013; Harwath and Glass, 2019; Harwath et al., 2020; Godard et al., 2018; Boito et al., 2019). Building on these insights, we target principled phoneme discovery with probabilistic formulations that relate priors and posteriors on the simplex while maintaining robust, language- and speaker-invariant representations.

document: This section provides background and motivation for task and domain adaptability applied to concern detection in the 2017 French Election domain, including concern values induced from Moral Foundations Theory (MFT). Concern detection seeks to infer which moral or civic issues are salient for an author or audience based on textual cues. Framing these concerns through MFT enables a principled mapping from language to value-laden dimensions such as care, fairness, loyalty, authority, and sanctity. Political events like the 2017 French presidential race exhibit rapid shifts in topics, styles, and rhetorical norms that challenge static models. Robust systems must adapt across tasks such as stance, topic, and concern tagging while transferring across media genres and time. Transfer learning with pre-trained language models has become the standard paradigm for such adaptation in computational social science. Prior studies have combined lexicon-driven signals with contextual encoders to improve moral and political inference in noisy social media. Recent work on political text classification explores targets and resources across languages and platforms. Mohammad et al. (2016) developed stance datasets around controversial topics and analyzed feature-rich classifiers. Baly et al. (2018) studied propaganda detection with multi-modal cues in news media. Graham et al. (2009) introduced the Moral Foundations Dictionary to quantify moral concerns in text. Hoover et al. (2020) provided expanded lexicons and validation for moral categories on social media. Liu et al. (2019) presented RoBERTa with robust pretraining that improves downstream classification. Devlin et al. (2019) released BERT and demonstrated strong performance on sentence-level tasks across varied benchmarks. While these advances inform representation and resources, their interdependencies for joint task and domain adaptation in a single electoral setting remain under-explored. We target the 2017 French Election because it provides a compact timeline with rich issue diversity across candidates and outlets. We operationalize concerns by aligning crowd annotations with MFT categories and by calibrating class priors using event-specific lexicon frequencies. Our modeling pipeline compares lightweight adapters, continued pretraining on in-domain corpora, and multi-task heads that share a common encoder. We examine both inductive transfer from stance and topic labels and transductive signals from unlabeled timelines surrounding key debates and announcements. Evaluation spans Twitter posts, candidate speeches, and online news to test generalization under topic, medium, and temporal shifts. Ben-David et al. (2010) established generalization bounds for domain adaptation based on divergence measures between distributions. Blitzer et al. (2007) proposed structural correspondence learning to bridge spaces across domains using pivot features. Pan and Yang (2010) surveyed transfer learning taxonomies and emphasized negative transfer risks. Gururangan et al. (2020) showed that continued pretraining on task or domain corpora yields consistent gains for NLP. Howard and Ruder (2018) introduced ULMFiT with discriminative fine-tuning and slanted triangular learning rates for effective adaptation. These strands motivate our focus on balancing inductive biases from value lexicons with distributional adaptation in a unified framework. Our contributions include a principled labeling scheme for concerns grounded in MFT, a reusable adaptation protocol across sources, and an open benchmark for the 2017 French Election. We discuss privacy, bias, and annotation reliability to contextualize model performance for sensitive political inference. The resulting insights support adaptive concern detection that travels across tasks and domains while preserving interpretability for applied research.

document: Discrete Prompts The release of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) has led to interest in prompting, a new way to leverage pretrained language models (PLM). Brown et al. (2020) proposes an intuitive in-context learning paradigm by concatenating a few input and output examples and feeding them to the language model and let the model autoregressively generate answers for new examples. Recent works (Petroni et al., 2019;Davison et al., 2019;Jiang et al., 2020) design prompts to probe the factual and commonsense knowledge encoded within a PLM. Recent works (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b;Gao et al., 2021) demonstrate that even smaller PLMs have similar few-shot learning capacity. Le Scao and Rush (2021) analyzes the effect of prompting and concludes that a single prompt may be worth 100 training examples in fine-tuning. Brown et al. (2020) describe GPT-3 as a general-purpose learner via in-context examples. Schick and Schütze (2021a,b) propose PET to leverage cloze-style prompts with label words. Liu et al. (2021) introduce prefix-tuning to steer generation with trainable continuous vectors. Wei et al. (2022) investigate instruction tuning across diverse tasks to elicit capabilities from pretrained models. Min et al. (2022) analyze prompt sensitivity and show substantial variance across prompt formulations. Instead of manually designing prompts (i.e., prompt engineering), some recent studies also explore automatic prompt generation. PETAL (Schick et al., 2020) augments Pattern Exploiting Training (PET, Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b) with automatically identified label words; Gao et al. (2021) uses re-ranking to find the best label words by fine-tuning a RoBERTa model on the candidates searched by RoBERTa, and using an external generation model for data augmentation of prompt templates; AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020) uses a gradient-based search to determine both prompts and label words. However, these methods require parameter updates with gradient descent, which is infeasible without access to the model weights (e.g., GPT-3). PET and its variants also require a large unlabeled set and need to be fine-tuned multiple times. AutoPrompt uses discretization techniques to approximately map a continuous vector back to tokens in the vocabulary (i.e., "vocablization"). These searched prompts and labels are often uninterpretable by humans. Different from these prior studies, our proposed AMuLaP is a simple and interpretable method for few-shot prompting that can work well with and without access to model weights. Concurrently to our work, others propose a method that exploits an external knowledge base to find label mapping.

spans: Recent studies introduced contextualized scoring for figurative language (Bulgarov and Angheluta, 2017). Neural phrase embeddings have been explored for multiword expressions in translation (Salehi et al., 2015). Work on discourse relation identification leveraged attention mechanisms for clause pairing (Ji and Eisenstein, 2015), and character-level models were evaluated for segmentation in morphologically rich languages (Botha et al., 2014). Large-scale annotation efforts focused on quality control across annotators (crowdsourcing) (Snow et al., 2008).
document: Many idiom detection models build on insights about unconventional meaning in metaphor. A number of approaches use distributional models, such as Kintsch (2000), Utsumi (2011), Sa-Pereira (2016), and Shutova et al. (2012), the latter of which was one of the first to implement a fully unsupervised approach for encoding relationships between words, their contexts, and their dependencies. A related line of work aims to automatically determine whether potentially idiomatic expressions are being used idiomatically or literally, based on contextual information (Katz and Giesbrecht, 2006; Fazly et al., 2009; Li, 2009, 2014). Our measure of conventionality is inspired by the insights of these models, and as described in Section 3.2, our measure uses differences in embeddings across contexts. Meanwhile, approaches to collocation detection have taken probabilistic and information-theoretic perspectives that seek to identify collocations using word combination probabilities. PMI is a frequently used quantity for measuring co-occurrence probabilities (Fano, 1961; Church and Hanks, 1990). Other implementations include selectional association (Resnik, 1996), symmetric conditional probability (Ferreira and Pereira Lopes, 1999), and log-likelihood (Dunning, 1993; Daille, 1996). Recent studies introduced contextualized scoring for figurative language (Bulgarov and Angheluta, 2017). Neural phrase embeddings have been explored for multiword expressions in translation (Salehi et al., 2015). Work on discourse relation identification leveraged attention mechanisms for clause pairing (Ji and Eisenstein, 2015), and character-level models were evaluated for segmentation in morphologically rich languages (Botha et al., 2014). Large-scale annotation efforts focused on quality control across annotators (crowdsourcing) (Snow et al., 2008). Beyond collocations, supervised classifiers using syntactic features have targeted idiom token classification in running text (Salton et al., 2016; Birke and Sarkar, 2006). Recent transformer-based models leverage contextualized representations to capture noncompositionality signals that vary across instances (Kundura et al., 2020; Zang et al., 2021). Embedding-space neighborhood changes across domains offer another operationalization of conventionality, which we exploit through contrastive comparisons. In cognitive science, idioms have been discussed in terms of entrenchment and schematicity, capturing how conventional units are stored and generalized (Langacker, 1987; Goldberg, 2006). Our first dimension formalizes entrenchment as stability of context-conditioned meaning. Our second dimension formalizes schematicity as variability in syntactic and lexical dispersion around the expression. We then relate these dimensions to measurable distributional cues derived from large corpora. While much of the literature in NLP recognizes that idioms share a cluster of properties, including semantic idiosyncrasy, syntactic inflexibility, and institutionalization (e.g., Sag et al., 2002; Fazly and Stevenson, 2006; Fazly et al., 2009), our approach is novel in attempting to characterize idioms along two orthogonal dimensions that correspond to specific proposals from the cognitive science literature. We evaluate our measures against established idiom and multiword expression datasets to examine token-level and type-level behavior. The results show that our conventionality scores capture complementary signals to PMI and selectional association, improving classification when combined. Our measures may offer a new avenue for tackling the problem of automatic idiom detection.

spans: Masica (1993) offers a broad survey of Indo-Aryan typology and classification based on phonological and morphological criteria. Caldwell (1856) provides comparative evidence for Dravidian relationships drawing on lexical correspondences and verb morphology. Peterson (2017) reports contact-induced changes in central India with detailed case studies of differential case marking and clause structure. Liljegren et al. (2021) describe Northwestern zone isoglosses across Indo-Aryan and Nuristani varieties with new dialectometric analyses.
document: The state of NLP in South Asia remains uneven across languages and regions. Most initiatives emphasize officially recognized and standardized languages with sizable speaker bases. Notable resources include Indic-NLP Suite (Kakwani et al., 2020), the EMILLE corpus (McEnery et al., 2000), and iNLTK (Arora, 2020), alongside workshops such as DravidianLangTech (Chakravarthi et al., 2021) and WILDRE (Jha et al., 2020). As shown in Table 1, only a handful of languages benefit from sustained NLP research, and even fewer have commercial tools like machine translation or OCR. Computational and historical linguistics intersect around core tasks including corpus construction, POS tagging, parsing, morphological analysis, and lexicon building. For high-resource languages, progress has been rapid, with Hindi featuring large dependency treebanks, transformer models (Jain et al., 2020; Khanuja et al., 2021), and robust English↔Hindi machine translation (Saini and Sahula, 2018). Yet many languages that Joshi et al. (2020) termed the Left-Behinds still lack digitized resources despite scattered descriptive work. Linguists have compiled preliminary treebanks and descriptive datasets for varieties such as Palula (Liljegren and Haider, 2015) and Toda (Emeneau, 1984), awaiting conversion to formats like Universal Dependencies. Masica (1993) offers a broad survey of Indo-Aryan typology and classification based on phonological and morphological criteria. Caldwell (1856) provides comparative evidence for Dravidian relationships drawing on lexical correspondences and verb morphology. Peterson (2017) reports contact-induced changes in central India with detailed case studies of differential case marking and clause structure. Liljegren et al. (2021) describe Northwestern zone isoglosses across Indo-Aryan and Nuristani varieties with new dialectometric analyses. Despite advances in select areas, there remains no comprehensive, centralized repository that spans typology, lexicon, and sociolinguistic variables for the subcontinent. The only effort approaching this scope was the early twentieth-century Linguistic Survey of India (Grierson, 1903–1928), which is invaluable yet uneven in coverage and methodology. Recent community initiatives have started new Universal Dependencies treebanks for Kangri, Mandeali, Bhojpuri, and Magahi (Ojha and Zeman, 2020), but consistency and depth vary greatly. Our work argues for coordinated, open, and ethically grounded data creation pipelines that bridge descriptive linguistics and NLP to serve under-resourced South Asian languages.

spans: Work on temporal reasoning in videos includes multi-step attention and sequence alignment models and long-range context tracking methods (Jin et al., 2020; Lei et al., 2018). Robust graph-based approaches link entities across frames to support cross-image queries (Narasimhan et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2020). Language grounding with referring expressions has been extended to multi-image setups (Kazemzadeh et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2016; Luo et al., 2020).
document: There is a long tradition of grounding language understanding on single images, in the form of visual question answering (Goyal et al., 2017; Hudson and Manning, 2019), visual dialogue (de Vries et al., 2017; Das et al., 2017), or visual entailment (Xie et al., 2019). Recently, more and more focus has been directed to settings where the visual context consists of multiple images, either conventional static pictures (Vedantam et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019; Suhr et al., 2019; Forbes et al., 2019; Hendricks and Nematzadeh, 2021; Yan et al., 2021; Hosseinzadeh and Wang, 2021; Bogin et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021), or video frames (Jhamtani and Berg-Kirkpatrick, 2018a; Bansal et al., 2020). While many of these benchmarks involve just two images, COVR (Bogin et al., 2021) and ISVQA (Bansal et al., 2020) provide more images, similar to our sets of 10 images. Work on temporal reasoning in videos includes multi-step attention and sequence alignment models and long-range context tracking methods (Jin et al., 2020; Lei et al., 2018). Robust graph-based approaches link entities across frames to support cross-image queries (Narasimhan et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2020). Language grounding with referring expressions has been extended to multi-image setups (Kazemzadeh et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2016; Luo et al., 2020). ISVQA and Spot-the-diff (Jhamtani and Berg-Kirkpatrick, 2018a) are most similar to our dataset, IMAGECODE. ISVQA is based on several video frames that are synthetic and cover a restricted domain, with short questions for Visual Question Answering. Spot-the-diff provides two frames from surveillance video cameras and descriptions of all their differences. IMAGECODE is unique as a) we cover a wider range of domains; b) we construct image sets that are maximally similar while being distinguishable through natural language (Section 3) and c) we limit descriptions to relevant differences. This results in (a) diverse, (b) complex and (c) pragmatically informative descriptions. IMAGECODE elicits pragmatic reasoning (Andreas and Klein, 2016; Cohn-Gordon et al., 2018) as a listener has to consider the context and resolve ambiguities resulting from nuanced differences to solve the task. We collect 50k image sets paired with 120k human-written descriptions, balancing attribute, object, and relational differences. Annotators are instructed to mention only contrastive content that disambiguates the target set from near-neighbors. To evaluate, we adapt VQA-style accuracy and retrieval-based grounding metrics, and we release strong baselines using contrastive captioners and multi-instance reasoning encoders. Our analyses show gains from context-aware training objectives that reward discriminative references across sets of ten images.

spans: Thorne et al. (2021) introduced WIKINLDB with open-domain queries requiring reasoning over textual facts. Queries are instantiated from a knowledge graph and facts produced by a language model. Data augmentation has been extensively explored in RC, QA, and dialogue (Khashabi et al., 2020; Alberti et al., 2019; Puri et al., 2020; Bartolo et al., 2021).
document: Template-based data generation has been used for data augmentation to inject numerical skills (Geva et al., 2020), improve consistency (Asai and Hajishirzi, 2020), and enhance zero-shot accuracy (Zhao et al., 2019). Templates have also supported dataset construction (Talmor and Berant, 2018; Thorne et al., 2021) and analyses of model generalization (Rozen et al., 2019). We extend this line by automatically instantiating templates with structured tables, yielding scalable generation with rich lexical diversity and straightforward portability to new skills and domains. Thorne et al. (2021) introduced WIKINLDB with open-domain queries requiring reasoning over textual facts. Queries are instantiated from a knowledge graph and facts produced by a language model. Data augmentation has been extensively explored in RC, QA, and dialogue (Khashabi et al., 2020; Alberti et al., 2019; Puri et al., 2020; Bartolo et al., 2021). We focus on tables as a scalable source for generating training examples that endow pretrained language models with new reasoning skills, in contrast to knowledge graphs with limited coverage. Pre-training over tables has emphasized table and knowledge-base reasoning (Yin et al., 2020; Herzig et al., 2020; Müller et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021; Neeraja et al., 2021b), while we leverage table-driven pre-training to improve reasoning over text. We also adopt error-driven sampling from active learning and related settings (Sharma et al., 2018; Graves et al., 2017; Glover and Hokamp, 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Pilault et al., 2021; Oren et al., 2019; Sagawa et al., 2020), computing heterogeneous batches as in Gottumukkala et al. (2020) to improve efficiency and performance.

spans: Hinson et al. (2020) introduced a Seq2Edit approach for CGEC with competitive results on shared tasks. Grundkiewicz and Junczys-Dowmunt (2018) demonstrated the effectiveness of sequence editing for English GEC using parallel data and edit tag inventories. Omelianchuk et al. (2020) presented GECToR, a transformer tagger that expands the operation set and benefits from large-scale pre-training. Kaneko et al. (2020) explored corruption and masking strategies for grammatical error correction with pretrained encoders and reported gains on corpora.
document: There is a lot of work on EGEC data construction. As the two earliest EGEC datasets, FCE (Yannakoudakis et al., 2011) and NU-CLE (Dahlmeier et al., 2013) adopt the error-coded annotation paradigm. JFLEG (Napoles et al., 2017) collects sentences from TOEFL exams and adopts the direct rewriting paradigm. W&I (Bryant et al., 2019) also chooses direct rewriting and, for each original sentence, additionally provides the writer’s proficiency level. All four datasets comprise essays from non‑native English speakers and provide multiple references. Recently, researchers have annotated small-scale EGEC data for native English writers, including AESW (Daudaravicius et al., 2016), LOCNESS (Bryant et al., 2019), GMEG (Napoles et al., 2019), and CWEB (Flachs et al., 2020). In the future, we plan to extend this work to texts written by native Chinese speakers. Compared with EGEC, progress in CGEC data construction largely lags behind. Hinson et al. (2020) introduced a Seq2Edit approach for CGEC with competitive results on shared tasks. Grundkiewicz and Junczys-Dowmunt (2018) demonstrated the effectiveness of sequence editing for English GEC using parallel data and edit tag inventories. Omelianchuk et al. (2020) presented GECToR, a transformer tagger that expands the operation set and benefits from large-scale pre-training. Kaneko et al. (2020) explored corruption and masking strategies for grammatical error correction with pretrained encoders and reported gains on corpora. NLPCC18 (Zhao et al., 2018) and CGED (Rao et al., 2018, 2020) are the only two evaluation datasets for CGEC research. In addition, there are resources for training, such as Lang8 (Zhao et al., 2018) and HSK (Xun, 2018). In the NLPCC-2018 shared task (Zhao et al., 2018), many systems adopted Seq2Seq models based on RNNs or CNNs, whereas recent work mainly utilizes Transformer architectures (Zhao and Wang, 2020; Tang et al., 2021).

spans: Due to limited CS resources, several MT studies introduced synthetic mixing by translating a subset of words within monolingual sentences (Song et al., 2019; Nakayama et al., 2019). Xu and Yvon (2021) examined constraints on switching points and evaluated multilingual NMT systems on mixed inputs using controlled perturbations and reported variability.
document: Code-switching in NLP has seen a rise of interest in recent years, including a dedicated workshop starting in 2014 (Diab et al., 2014) and still ongoing (Solorio et al., 2021). CS in machine translation also has a long history (Le Féal, 1990; Climent et al., 2003; Sinha and Thakur, 2005; Johnson et al., 2017; Elmadany et al., 2021; Xu and Yvon, 2021), but has seen a rise of interest with the advent of large multilingual models such as mBART (Liu et al., 2020) or mT5 (Xue et al., 2020; Gautam et al., 2021; Jawahar et al., 2021). Due to limited CS resources, several MT studies introduced synthetic mixing by translating a subset of words within monolingual sentences (Song et al., 2019; Nakayama et al., 2019). Xu and Yvon (2021) examined constraints on switching points and evaluated multilingual NMT systems on mixed inputs using controlled perturbations and reported variability. We differ from those works by using naturally occurring CS data (Section 3) which models the real-world CS distribution rather than arbitrary language mixing. For spoken input, as present in ASR and ST, synthetically creating realistic CS data is more challenging than it is for MT. However, dedicated ASR corpora that contain natural CS exist, including the Bangor Miami (Deuchar et al., 2014), SEAME (Zeng et al., 2018), and the recent large-scale ASRU 2019 task. These corpora generally do not contain translations of the ASR annotations, since they were designed for the ASR task only. However, there exist two exceptions, which we leverage to derive our ST CS data set, described in Section 3. There also exists a wide range of prior modeling work on CS in ASR models, for a variety of strategies (Lyu et al., 2006; Ahmed and Tan, 2012; Seki et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Du et al., 2021). However, the recently introduced large multilingual models for speech, such as Wav2Vec (Schneider et al., 2019), Wav2Vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021), are still underexplored with regards to their CS performance. Handling mixed languages also requires understanding what languages are being spoken. Systems that support mixed language input therefore require some form of automatic LID -either as an explicit component on the utterance (Mabokela et al., 2014; Xu and Yvon, 2021) or word-level (Lyu and Lyu, 2008a; Nakayama et al., 2019), or implicitly learned by the underlying model(s) via a multi-task learning setup (Lyu and Lyu, 2008b; Watanabe et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2020). In our work, we leverage both, exploring utterance-level LID components as well as implicit learning of utterance and word level LID. In both MT and ASR, prior publications have also included the study of intra-word mixing of languages (Yılmaz et al., 2018; Mager et al., 2019), a phenomenon we do not explore in our work.

document: Text Question Answering has advanced rapidly, with substantial efforts dedicated to modeling and evaluation in textual settings (Huang et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Gong et al., 2020). Within machine reading comprehension, progress is driven by large benchmark datasets and powerful pre-trained encoders that enable robust context reasoning (Choi et al., 2018; Elgohary et al., 2018; Reddy et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2018; Lan et al., 2020). Tseng et al. (2016) introduced TOEFL-style listening comprehension with multiple-choice answers to evaluate spoken understanding under exam conditions and dataset splits for evaluation. Serdyuk et al. (2018) proposed end-to-end acoustic-to-word models trained on noisy corpora and reported gains on speech recognition benchmarks. Su and Fung (2020) explored adaptation strategies for mismatched domains in ASR pipelines targeting downstream understanding tasks in realistic settings. Lugosch et al. (2019) investigated self-supervised pretraining for spoken language understanding with frame-level objectives and lightweight decoders in resource-limited scenarios. Palogiannidi et al. (2020) analyzed conversational audio cues and paralinguistic signals using attention-based architectures across varied interaction scenarios. To robustly model multi-turn spoken question answering that integrates speech and text, we adopt knowledge distillation to transfer conversational structure from a teacher to a student under noisy ASR conditions, building on established KD techniques in NLP and speech (Hinton et al., 2015; Kim and Rush, 2016; Hu et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2018b; Hahn and Choi, 2019).

spans: Recent works investigate prompt-based rationalization. Camburu et al. (2018) collected natural language justifications for NLI. Rajani et al. (2019) introduced commonsense explanations for QA using human-authored statements. Hase and Bansal (2020) examined sufficiency and comprehensiveness in explanations empirically.
document:
Current self-explaining models often enforce shortest yet sufficient rationales, with the assumption that short rationales are more intuitive to humans (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019). Paranjape et al. (2020) propose an information bottleneck approach to enable the rationale length control. However, current studies only assessed the methods with auto-metrics and did not evaluate human understanding on different rationale lengths.

On the other hand, a line of studies measure the "human rationales alignment" (Paranjape et al., 2020), which compares how well the model-generated rationales agree with human-grounded annotations (DeYoung et al., 2019). There are also studies involving human-in-the-loop to evaluate the explanations, such as asking humans to choose a better model (Ribeiro et al., 2016). However, there is a lack of human evaluations on validating the effect of rationale length on human understanding. Beyond length control, explanations can be optimized for sufficiency and comprehensiveness, which evaluate whether a rationale alone supports the prediction or whether removing it alters the outcome (DeYoung et al., 2020). These properties are typically computed with model perturbations and automatic scores, risking misalignment with human judgment when rationales are terse. Recent works investigate prompt-based rationalization. Camburu et al. (2018) collected natural language justifications for NLI. Rajani et al. (2019) introduced commonsense explanations for QA using human-authored statements. Hase and Bansal (2020) examined sufficiency and comprehensiveness in explanations empirically.

Despite these advances, the link between rationale length and reader comprehension has not been systematically isolated in controlled studies. Cognitive psychology suggests that overly concise explanations can omit inferential steps that aid understanding, while overly verbose ones impose extraneous cognitive load (Sweller, 1988). In explainable NLP, user studies have primarily focused on task performance or trust calibration rather than comprehension of the evidence itself (Hase and Bansal, 2022). Prior work on highlights and extractive rationales indicates that snippet size interacts with domain expertise and time pressure (Lertvittayakumjorn and Toni, 2021). Yet, most benchmarks provide a single gold span per instance, obscuring whether multiple lengths could be equally helpful to humans. To address this gap, we design a human evaluation that directly varies rationale length while holding content constant across conditions. Our study measures comprehension, calibration, and perceived helpfulness across short, medium, and long rationales for the same model predictions. We also compare automatic sufficiency and comprehensiveness scores with human outcomes to test their predictive value for people. The results reveal non-monotonic effects of length, with medium rationales often achieving the best trade-off between clarity and completeness. These findings provide guidance for length-aware explainer objectives and underscore the need for human-centered evaluation protocols.

(2018) proposed style transfer objectives for rewriting abusive language without paired references. Raji and Buolamwini (2019) audited commercial vision models and reported disparate performance on intersectional subpopulations. Sun et al. (2019) examined bias in contextual word representations.

Bender and Friedman (2018) outlined data statements for natural language processing to document dataset provenance and communicative intent. Zhao et al. (2019) introduced counterfactual data augmentation for gender robustness in coreference benchmarks and reported gains under anti-stereotypical swaps. Prabhakaran et al. document: Measuring bias in NLP requires precise definitions, carefully constructed datasets, and robust evaluation protocols. Prior work has documented stereotype leakage and unequal error rates across demographic groups in language models and classifiers (Caliskan et al., 2017; May et al., 2019; Davidson et al., 2019). Blodgett et al. (2020) argued that studies conflate distinct notions of bias, urging clearer problem formulations and reporting standards. We align with representational harms that reinforce social subordination, consistent with Crawford (2017) and follow-up operationalizations in NLP. To measure such harms, we contrast sentences that refer to different social groups while controlling for topical content, following templates and causal matching strategies (Dev et al., 2021; Dixon et al., 2018). Zhao et al. (2018) analyzed gender bias in coreference systems using structured interventions on training data. Park et al. (2018) proposed style transfer objectives for rewriting abusive language without paired references. Raji and Buolamwini (2019) audited commercial vision models and reported disparate performance on intersectional subpopulations. Sun et al. (2019) examined bias in contextual word representations. Collectively, these strands motivate dataset designs that isolate representational associations from task competence and facilitate clear attribution of observed disparities. We operationalize bias through counterfactual evaluation, pairing minimally different sentences that swap group terms while preserving semantic roles and syntax. This design connects to causal testing for fairness under interventions on protected attributes in text classification (Garg et al., 2019; Pryzant et al., 2021). Beyond measurement, mitigation strategies range from data augmentation and adversarial training to debiasing objectives in embedding spaces (Zhang et al., 2018; Ravfogel et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2021). Our focus is on constructing a reliable probe rather than competing mitigation algorithms, enabling consistent comparisons across model families and training regimes. Bender and Friedman (2018) outlined data statements for natural language processing to document dataset provenance and communicative intent. Zhao et al. (2019) introduced counterfactual data augmentation for gender robustness in coreference benchmarks and reported gains under anti-stereotypical swaps. Prabhakaran et al. (2021) examined power dynamics in annotation and highlighted how rater identity can significantly shape toxicity judgments. Building on these insights, our dataset enumerates salient social group tokens, contextual occupations, and sentiment-bearing modifiers to elicit measurable asymmetries. We accompany each template with lexically diverse surface realizations to avoid spurious artifacts and to broaden linguistic coverage. Evaluation is conducted across masked language models, decoder-only models, and instruction-tuned variants to compare sensitivity to group perturbations. We report effect sizes, uncertainty estimates, and ablations that isolate the role of pretraining data and finetuning objectives. The resulting analysis clarifies where representational harms appear most prominently and establishes a reproducible benchmark for future mitigation work.

spans: Neural models for deception use contextual embeddings to capture pragmatic and stylistic cues (Zhou et al., 2019). Crowdsourced corpora of deceptive reviews reveal domain shift and annotator disagreement (Ott et al., 2011; Pérez-Rosas and Mihalcea, 2015). Social deduction games model strategic signaling and coalition pressure under partial observability (Peskov et al., 2020). Argumentation mining relates claim structure to persuasive outcomes in public online debates (Hidey et al., 2017).
document: The game of Mafia is particularly well-suited for the goal of determining whether the deceptive participants in a conversation can be identified from the contents of their utterances. Prior research in computational deception has identified lexical, syntactic, and pragmatic markers that differentiate truthful and deceptive speech in text-based settings. Among social deduction formats, Mafia provides clear roles, explicit incentives to mislead, and repeated day–night cycles that structure interaction. These properties make it possible to align rhetorical moves with ground-truth roles and to study how group dynamics influence suspicion and trust over time. Work on dialogue-level deception has connected turn-taking, hedging, uncertainty expressions, and stance shifts to lying behavior in interviews and chats (Levitan et al., 2018; Feng and Hirst, 2013; Rubin et al., 2017). Neural models for deception use contextual embeddings to capture pragmatic and stylistic cues (Zhou et al., 2019). Crowdsourced corpora of deceptive reviews reveal domain shift and annotator disagreement (Ott et al., 2011; Pérez-Rosas and Mihalcea, 2015). Social deduction games model strategic signaling and coalition pressure under partial observability (Peskov et al., 2020). Argumentation mining relates claim structure to persuasive outcomes in public online debates (Hidey et al., 2017). While each of these strands is relevant, their joint implications for strategic multi-party games remain underexplored. We address this gap by constructing a corpus of online Mafia games with utterance-level timestamps, speaker roles, votes, and fine-grained annotations of claims, accusations, and defenses. Our modeling framework combines a transformer-based utterance encoder with a graph neural network over speaker interactions to capture both content and relational context. We benchmark against content-only baselines, content-free timing and voting features, and prior dialog-act models adapted to this domain. Evaluation across held-out games demonstrates significant gains in identifying deceptive players early in the day phase and improved calibration of suspicion scores. We discuss ethical considerations around labeling deception, limitations of role-playing datasets, and the generalizability of conversational cues beyond the Mafia genre.

spans: Recent works report advances on prompt-based relation extraction with masked language models (Petroni et al., 2019; Han et al., 2021). Few-shot formulations explore task adaptation using meta-learning and calibration on small support sets (Gao et al., 2021; Bansal et al., 2020; Cong et al., 2023), and knowledge graph completion leverages textual patterns aligned with structured triples (Bordes et al., 2013; Yao et al., 2019) in open-domain contexts globally.
document: Relation extraction aims at identifying semantic relations between entity mentions in text. Early approaches relied on feature-based supervised classifiers using lexical, syntactic, and dependency cues (Qian et al., 2008; Zeng et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2016). The advent of pre-trained language models has shifted the field toward contextualized representations that reduce manual engineering (Devlin et al., 2019; Lan et al., 2020). Wu and He (2019) introduced R-BERT, augmenting entity markers within BERT to improve sentence-level relation classification. Soares et al. (2019) proposed a learned relation representation via matching entity-pooled embeddings, enabling instance-agnostic retrieval and clustering. Despite strong results, these supervised architectures require substantial labeled data that may be scarce in specialized domains. To reduce annotation cost, distant supervision aligns knowledge base triples with text, albeit at the expense of noisy labels (Mintz et al., 2009; Riedel et al., 2010; Zeng et al., 2015). Noise-robust training with multi-instance learning and selective attention has proven effective for denoising distant supervision signals (Lin et al., 2016; Feng et al., 2018). Recent works report advances on prompt-based relation extraction with masked language models (Petroni et al., 2019; Han et al., 2021). Few-shot formulations explore task adaptation using meta-learning and calibration on small support sets (Gao et al., 2021; Bansal et al., 2020; Cong et al., 2023), and knowledge graph completion leverages textual patterns aligned with structured triples (Bordes et al., 2013; Yao et al., 2019) in open-domain contexts globally. Document-level relation extraction extends beyond single sentences to aggregate evidence across mentions and discourse structure (Yao et al., 2019; Christopoulou et al., 2019). Graph neural networks and transformers with entity-aware attention help propagate cross-sentence cues for multi-hop inference (Wang et al., 2020; Nan et al., 2020). Domain adaptation methods address distribution shift by leveraging unlabeled corpora and adversarial objectives to learn domain-invariant features (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015; Peng et al., 2020). Cross-lingual transfer exploits multilingual encoders and parallel data to generalize relations to low-resource languages without labels (Wu and Dredze, 2019; Keung et al., 2020). Recent studies explore generative formulations that cast relation extraction as conditional text generation with constrained decoding for label controllability (Paolini et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021). Calibration and thresholding strategies mitigate label bias in multi-label extraction settings and improve precision-recall tradeoffs (Desai and Durrett, 2020; Jiang et al., 2021). Large language models are further used as weak annotators or teachers, combining self-training with retrieval to harvest high-quality pseudo-labeled instances (Zhang et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2023). Evaluation commonly relies on micro/macro F1, with attention to calibration, robustness to entity boundary errors, and cross-domain generalization. Our work builds on these strands by integrating entity-aware prompting with denoising objectives to handle distant supervision noise while preserving label semantics. We also provide an open benchmark suite and analysis protocols to standardize comparisons across sentence-level and document-level settings.

Our work is in the research line of recommender systems widely studied because of their practical value in industry. For example, previous work explores users' chatting history to recommend conversations (Zeng et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2020b) and hashtags (Li et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2021), browsing history to recommend news (Wu et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2021), and purchase history to recommend products (Guo et al., 2020). In contrast to most recommendation studies focusing on exploiting target users' personal interest modeling from their historical behavior, our work largely relies on the wordings of a short query to figure out what is needed by a target user because they are anonymous for privacy concerns.

Within several branches of recommendation research, our task is conceptually similar to expert recommendation for question answering (Nikzad-Khasmakhi et al., 2019). In this field, many previous studies encode expertise knowledge in diverse streams, such as software engineering (Bhat et al., 2018), social activities (Bok et al., 2021), and scholarly communities (Zhang and Ma, 2012). Tang et al. (2018) deploy a heterogeneous graph to rank candidates for technical question routing. Zhou et al. (2020) analyze side-profile features to alleviate sparsity in skill tagging for enterprise forums. Yang and Sun (2019) investigate attention over asker context when matching experts across community platforms. Kim et al. (2022) model expertise with transformers. Nevertheless, only a small subset of expert recommendation studies leverage deep semantic matching between queries and candidate profiles in cold-start settings. Our approach emphasizes lexical-semantic signals elicited by a patient’s brief query and aligns them with doctor-centric evidence.

Our work is also related to language understanding research over doctor–patient dialogues on online health forums (Zeng et al., 2020a). Prior studies investigate information extraction for clinical entities and relations in consumer health text (Ramponi et al., 2020; Du et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020c). Other lines explore community question answering in medical domains with knowledge-aware models (Pampari et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019). There is also growing interest in medical report generation and summarization grounded in dialog or EHR content (Enarvi et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020a). Compared with these threads, we focus on modeling doctor expertise and how it manifests in past interactions and structured profiles. We treat prior dialogs as indirect evidence of specialization rather than as direct supervision for answer generation. This perspective motivates learning representations that capture topical acuity, procedural familiarity, and patient-facing communication style.

Recent advances in representation learning for retrieval provide tools for mapping short queries to expert attributes. Pretrained language models enable robust semantics under limited context and have been effective in matching problems (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Domain adaptation further refines these models to capture medical terminology and layperson phrasing (Gururangan et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2020). We incorporate profile fields such as specialties, certifications, and affiliated departments to anchor textual signals. We also index conversational cues like symptom focus and follow-up patterns to refine expertise estimation. We evaluate on anonymized forum data with strict privacy safeguards that restrict access to longitudinal identifiers. Candidate doctors are retrieved from a curated panel with verified specialties and minimal overlap in scope of practice. We compare against collaborative filtering, content-based baselines, and neural expert finders adapted from open-domain settings. Our results show consistent gains on precision-oriented metrics when queries are short and information-poor. Ablations confirm that joint encoding of profile text and selected dialog snippets yields the largest improvements. In summary, our study extends expert recommendation to privacy-constrained healthcare scenarios by leveraging query semantics and interpretable expertise signals.

document: Entity linking aims to resolve textual mentions to entries in a knowledge base, enabling downstream reasoning and retrieval. Early approaches exploited link structure and Wikipedia signals to score candidates and model global coherence across mentions. Traditional pipelines decomposed mention detection, candidate generation, and ranking, with features derived from context and graph statistics. Neural methods further improved ranking with cross-encoders that jointly encode mention context and entity descriptions for fine-grained matching. These models achieve strong accuracy but are expensive at inference due to quadratic interactions between mentions and candidates. Dual-encoders address this cost by precomputing entity embeddings and retrieving with approximate nearest neighbor search while trading some accuracy. Clustering-based training has emerged as a means to shape embedding spaces for discovery, linking, and hard negative mining in dual-encoder regimes. We investigate how cluster-aware objectives and prediction-time pruning can close the gap to cross-encoders without sacrificing scalability. Prior work spans symbolic heuristics, collective inference, and end-to-end neural architectures across multiple datasets. Milne and Witten (2008) introduce link relatedness for disambiguation. Cucerzan (2007) leverages evidence from Wikipedia for entity recognition. Gupta et al. (2017) present neural architectures for collective disambiguation. Kolitsas et al. (2018) design end to end models for mention detection and linking. Cao et al. (2021) study cross lingual linking with encoders. Raiman and Raiman (2018) explore candidate generation under structural constraints. Barhom et al. (2019) propose tower models. Related lines of research in cross-document coreference provide signals for merging mentions before linking to a canonical identifier. Works such as Bagga and Baldwin (1998) and Gooi and Allan (2004) pioneered entity clustering that reduces ambiguity by aggregating topical evidence across documents. Recent neural approaches build on this idea with pairwise scoring, transitivity constraints, and discourse cues to create clusters amenable to efficient linking. Our method adapts these insights by computing cluster centroids in representation space and constraining retrieval to centroid-selected neighborhoods. When efficiency is paramount, model distillation and late-interaction architectures like poly-encoders offer practical deployment trade-offs while maintaining competitive quality. We show that combining cluster-aware dual-encoders with lightweight reranking delivers favorable accuracy–efficiency curves against cross-encoders on standard entity linking benchmarks.

document: Entity-based modeling has long been the dominant perspective on discourse coherence in pre-neural approaches. The entity grid formalism represents noun phrase mentions across sentences to model local transitions and has spawned many extensions. Barzilay and Lapata (2008) introduced the original grid representation with salience-based transitions at the sentence level for modeling local coherence. Guinaudeau and Strube (2013) mapped entity grids to connectivity graphs to estimate document flow using graph measures. Tien Nguyen and Joty (2017) converted grids into convolutional architectures with feature maps that operate over role sequences from coreference chains. Elsner and Charniak (2011) examined discourse cues and entity persistence to assess textual coherence in essays and newswire using coreference-driven representations. Li and Hovy (2014), Feng and Hirst (2014), and Li and Jurafsky (2017) proposed models targeting topical continuity and ordering tests. Despite these advances, entity-centric methods often struggle to yield interpretable rationales for predictions when deployed on downstream tasks beyond synthetic shuffling. Lai and Tetreault (2018) reported extreme sparsity in grids used for classification and noted limited gains over baselines in real applications. Moon et al. (2019) optimized a neural coherence scorer on the shuffle test, Pishdad et al. (2020) questioned the validity of that objective, and Mohiuddin et al. (2021) measured weak correlations with downstream tasks overall. We build on these observations by modeling discourse focus with noun phrases and by evaluating jointly on shuffle detection and task-oriented settings to demonstrate interpretability and robustness.

Parallel corpora pretrain sequence-to-sequence models for generation, with XNLG using cross-lingual masking and autoencoding and VECO relying on cross-attention masking across languages to couple encoders and decoders. mT6 improves the mT5 architecture by learning translation span corruption on parallel data, while ∆LM focuses on aligning multilingual encoders to strengthen sequence-to-sequence transfer, and these approaches appear alongside TLM-style objectives and code-switched inputs without clarifying assumptions or benefits across NLU and NLG settings.

document: Here we first review the related work of DA for NLP, then expound the differences between our method and the major existing ones. According to the methodology of data generation, existing methods can be categorized into three types: rule-based, interpolation-based, and model-based, respectively. Rule-based approaches include lexical substitution, part-of-speech aware shuffling, and back-translation templates, exemplified by EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019), contextual synonym replacement (Kobayashi, 2018), and constraint-based lexical swaps (Fadaee et al., 2017). Interpolation-based techniques mix discrete examples or their representations, such as MixUp-style convex combinations for text classification (Guo et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020) and manifold interpolation within pretrained embedding spaces (Zhang et al., 2020). Model-based methods generate new sentences with sequence-to-sequence or decoder-only language models, including back-translation with neural MT (Sennrich et al., 2016), controllable paraphrasing with T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), and prompt-driven sampling from GPT-2 or GPT-3 (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020). Smith et al. (2020) introduced curriculum augmentation with difficulty-aware sampling for sentiment analysis. Zhang and Li (2019) proposed adversarial examples for robustness in question answering. Brown et al. (2020) described large-scale prompting with few-shot abilities in generative models, and Gao et al. (2021) presented LM-bias calibration for zero-shot classification. Nguyen et al. (2018) analyzed annotation variance in moral foundations corpora across diverse demographic groups. These paradigms vary in how strongly they preserve task labels while expanding coverage of rare patterns, and recent studies emphasize the need to quantify semantic faithfulness alongside diversity (Kiyono et al., 2020; Longpre et al., 2020). For rule-based edits, excessive lexical perturbation can flip sentiment or entailment labels unless substitution is constrained by context-aware probabilities or lexical semantics (Alzantot et al., 2018; Morris et al., 2020). In interpolation-based DA, mixing at the token surface often yields ungrammatical hybrids, motivating strategies that interpolate hidden states or align examples by semantic roles before blending (Chen et al., 2020; Berthelot et al., 2019). Model-driven generation benefits from task conditioning and decoding constraints to avoid hallucinations, with work on quality filters, self-consistency reranking, and classifier-guided sampling improving utility for downstream training (Ippolito et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2021; Holtzman et al., 2020). Xu et al. (2020) investigated mixup schedules for low-resource intent detection. Pruksachatkun et al. (2020) studied intermediate-task training effects on generalization across GLUE tasks. Clark et al. (2020) introduced self-training with confidence-based filtering for semi-supervised learning in language understanding. Lin et al. (2021) analyzed calibration shifts under distributional drift in classification. In contrast to prior work, our approach formulates DA as constrained decoding over a learned neighborhood, coupling lexical control with representation-level objectives to balance fidelity and novelty. We integrate a task-calibrated acceptance test that rejects augmentations predicted to alter the gold label while still promoting coverage of minority phenomena. The remainder of the paper details the algorithmic design, reports ablations across the three DA families, and presents cross-dataset results on classification and sequence labeling benchmarks.

(2012) induce POS categories by clustering tokens with substitute-based embeddings, Shen et al. (2018) infer latent constituency from language modeling signals, and Wu et al. document: Unsupervised POS tagging. In addition to HMMs and CRF-AE, other approaches for unsupervised POS tagging are as follows. Clustering is a mainstream unsupervised learning technique and has been investigated for POS induction (Yatbaz et al., 2012; Yuret et al., 2014; Gupta et al., 2020). All three works adopt k-means to partition tokens, differing mainly in how words are represented. Yatbaz et al. (2012) learn context-free substitutes-based embeddings using an n-gram language model. Yuret et al. (2014) extend this idea to yield context-sensitive representations. Gupta et al. (2020) employ a deep clustering objective to transform mBERT features into a lower-dimensional space with joint reconstruction. Their model achieves strong results on multilingual UD benchmarks, though source code is unavailable. Inspired by Brown et al. (1992), Stratos (2019) proposes maximizing mutual information between word-based and context-based predictors to induce tags. Research across tagging and parsing reports diverse strategies without a unifying account. Yatbaz et al. (2012) induce POS categories by clustering tokens with substitute-based embeddings, Shen et al. (2018) infer latent constituency from language modeling signals, and Wu et al. (2020) recover inter-word correlations in PLMs using perturbed masking. Kim et al. (2020) score adjacency in contextual representations to place boundaries, and Cao et al. (2020) apply constituency tests over pretrained encoders to achieve strong unsupervised parses. A small but growing line of work leverages PLMs for unsupervised tagging and parsing. The clustering approach of Gupta et al. (2020) is, to our knowledge, the only use of PLMs for unsupervised POS induction. Wu et al. (2020) estimate inter-word correlations via perturbed masking to derive syntax trees from contextualized encodings. Inspired by Shen et al. (2018), Kim et al. (2020) extract constituency trees by measuring proximity of adjacent token representations. When proximity is low, the midpoint is treated as a likely constituent boundary. Cao et al. (2020) report state-of-the-art unsupervised parsing by operationalizing constituency tests over PLMs. Ammar et al. (2014) introduce CRF autoencoders for unsupervised sequence labeling. Cai et al. (2017) adapt CRF-AE to unsupervised dependency parsing, using the encoder to select trees and the decoder to reconstruct sentences. Subsequent work shows the framework is also effective in semi-supervised regimes. Zhang et al. (2017) improve semi-supervised sequence labeling with a neural CRF-AE and Jia et al. (2020) demonstrate benefits for semantic parsing.

document: Our approach draws insights from multi-label classification (MLC) (Tsoumakas and Katakis, 2007; Zhou, 2006, 2014). One of the earliest approaches for MLC was to decompose the task into multiple binary problems while ignoring correlations between labels (Boutell et al., 2004). More recent work has modeled MLC in a sequence-to-sequence framework where a decoder generates labels sequentially and preserves inter-label dependencies (Yang et al., 2018). Most prior work in MLC focuses on classification settings and is not directly applicable to machine translation. In contrast, our training strategy is tailored for sequence-to-sequence problems. Unlike prior work (Yang et al., 2018), SCONES enables MLC-style training with any underlying NMT architecture by changing only the loss function. By jointly training all label-specific binary classifiers, our strategy can account for label correlations during decoding. Wang et al. (2019) used an MLC-inspired objective to improve machine translation by aligning multiple candidate tokens to each context. Unlike our approach, they attempted to predict all words in the target sentence with a bag-of-words loss function. We formulate next-word prediction at each time step as an MLC problem to handle intrinsic uncertainty while still producing ordered target sequences. Ma et al. (2018) introduced a reinforcement learning objective for sequence generation with a reward based on n-gram overlap. Edunov et al. (2018) explored training with multiple targets using back-translation and noisy channel modeling. Wang and Cho (2019) studied the exposure bias of teacher forcing and proposed scheduled sampling variants. He et al. (2016) reported improvements from deep residual networks for translation encoders in large-scale production workloads globally. The speed-ups from SCONES partly arise from avoiding normalization over the full vocabulary at training time. This idea relates to self-normalizing objectives that encourage the model to assign approximately normalized scores without explicit softmax computations (Mnih and Teh, 2012; Devlin et al., 2014; Goldberger and Melamud, 2018). Unlike self-normalization, SCONES does not approximate a single distribution over the vocabulary but learns multiple independent binary classifiers per step. This design allows the model to represent multi-modality without forcing probability mass to be shared among competing tokens. Our formulation also connects to auxiliary bag-of-words and future-prediction losses that decouple content selection from ordering (Zhao et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2020). In SCONES, the ordering signal is fully delegated to the autoregressive factorization while the binary heads capture content uncertainty. Prior work on label smoothing demonstrates that calibrated margins can improve generalization and uncertainty estimates in NMT (Szegedy et al., 2016; Müller et al., 2019). We similarly encourage calibrated decision boundaries by aggregating binary risks across candidate tokens. Negative sampling and noise-contrastive estimation offer computational shortcuts for large vocabularies but still rely on a normalized objective (Gutmann and Hyvärinen, 2010; Vaswani et al., 2013). Our experiments show that SCONES achieves comparable or better accuracy than these baselines while reducing per-step computation. The method is complementary to vocabulary selection and can be layered with lattice-based decoding to further reduce latency (Shi et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019). Taken together, these results position SCONES as a practical alternative for uncertainty-aware, efficient sequence-to-sequence learning.

document:
Multidomain and multilingual translation have received considerable attention in recent years, and a comprehensive survey lies beyond the scope of this paper. Domain adaptation for neural MT is reviewed by Chu et al. (2017), multidomain MT by Saunders (2021) and Pham et al. (2021), and multilingual MT by Chu and Dabre (2018) and Dabre et al. (2020). We focus on the most relevant strands that inform our design.

Language similarity approaches exploit proximity across languages to guide parameter sharing (Sen et al., 2019; Kong et al., 2021). A common design shares a single encoder while routing language families to distinct decoders through a multi-decoder topology. Our method also encodes task proximity but learns it from latent commonalities in data rather than from predefined families as in Kong et al. (2021).

Work on language-specific sub-networks draws on the lottery-ticket hypothesis to identify salient parameters for a task (Frankle and Carbin, 2019). Lin et al. (2021) port this idea to multilingual NMT by pruning fine-tuned models to extract language-dependent subnetworks. We similarly map tasks to subnetworks by masking layer outputs and learn the masks with variational objectives instead of heuristics.

Within sparse Transformers, Li et al. (2020) vary model depth by task to align capacity with difficulty. Gong et al. (2021a; 2021b) select attention heads, layers, and feed-forward blocks through task-conditional sparsity. Mixture-of-experts achieves related effects, with GShard replacing FFN sublayers by expert modules (Lepikhin et al., 2021; Fedus et al., 2021).

Adapters provide an alternative multi-task mechanism by inserting small trainable modules into frozen layers (Houlsby et al., 2019; Bapna and Firat, 2019; Pham et al., 2020; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Smith et al. (2020) present a deep encoder with cross-layer attention that improves translation accuracy on WMT and increases robustness under domain shift. Johnson and Lee (2019) propose span-level data augmentation for low-resource settings, and Brown et al. (2020) explore prompting in large language models for machine translation. Gao et al. (2021) evaluate retrieval-augmented prompting for factual tasks, Liu et al. (2021) survey pre-train prompt methods across NLP tasks, and Wei et al. (2022) study chain-of-thought prompting for complex reasoning. Yet adapters can suffer from brittle domain boundaries and catastrophic forgetting in multidomain translation (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; McCloskey and Cohen, 1989; Pham et al., 2021). We address multi-task routing with learned, proximity-aware gating that selects between shared and task-specific representations.

document: Probing Knowledge in Pretrained Models Many pieces of previous work aim to measure knowledge stored in pretrained models. Petroni et al. (2019) propose to retrieve knowledge in pretrained models (such as BERT) using cloze queries. Their experiments show that BERT has a strong ability to recall factual knowledge without any finetuning. Logan et al. (2019) examine corpora composition for impact on memorization. Khandelwal et al. (2020) study nearest-neighbor language models and Lewis et al. (2020) introduce retrieval-augmented generation for knowledge-intensive tasks, focusing on external evidence rather than parametric recall. Jiang et al. (2020b) improve the cloze queries with mining-based and paraphrasing-based methods. Roberts et al. (2020) propose the closedbook question answering to measure how much knowledge a pretrained model has stored in its parameters. Elazar et al. (2021) measure and improve the consistency of pretrained models with respect to factual knowledge prediction. Rather than examining only the model outputs, we provide an open-the-black-box analysis for the knowledge neurons in pretrained Transformers. We analyze which neurons are causally implicated in recalling facts and how they interact across layers. Our findings complement prior evaluations by linking behavioral probes to internal mechanisms, offering guidance for controllable editing of stored knowledge.

document: In this section we mainly discuss and compare related work in the two most relevant directions: the assessment benchmarks to the general narrative comprehension skills; and the tasks specifically designed for character-centered predictions over narratives. Smith et al. (2018) introduced plot-level probes for event ordering, Johnson and Lee (2019) collected annotations of character goals across genres, and Patel et al. (2021) modeled multimodal salience in movie-based summaries, each reporting distinct metrics and sampling schemes. Garcia and Wu (2022) released a distant-supervision dataset for pronoun resolution in novels using co-reference heuristics and silver labels derived from rule-based templates and omitted human validation entirely. Table gives a summary of these narrative comprehension tasks, associated with their required skills of comprehension, and we also reviewed studies on character-centered tasks over non-narrative texts like synopses and chit-chat (i.e., not story-related) conversations. Detailed rationales of the required skills for each task are discussed in Appendix A.
