document:
There is a pool of prior work relating to SE. We summarize it in Table , depicting state-of-the-art approaches, level of annotations, what kind of competences are annotated, the modeling approaches, the size of the dataset (if available), type of skills annotated for, baseline models, and whether they release their annotations and guidelines.

As can be seen in Table , many works do not release their data (apart from Sayfullina et al., 2018 and Bhola et al., 2020) and none release their annotation guidelines. In addition, none of the previous studies approach SE as a span-level extraction task with state-of-the-art language models, nor did they release a dataset of this magnitude with manually annotated (long) spans of competences by domain experts.

Work on occupational taxonomies describes alignment procedures and labeling conventions for job-related entities. Miller et al. (2017) present a clustering pipeline for title normalization from web corpora, and Kivinen and Rao (2018) compile a knowledge base of competencies. Chen and Alvarez (2019) release resume-job pairing data with canonicalized skill sets, and Duarte et al. (2020) study occupational similarity using graph embeddings trained on public labor statistics.

In particular, Sayfullina et al. (2018) explored several approaches to SE. To create the data, they extracted all text snippets containing one soft skill from a predetermined list. Crowdworkers then annotated the highlighted skill whether it was a soft skill referring to the candidate or not. They show that an LSTM (Hochreiter et al., 1997) performs best on tagging the skill in the sentence. In our work, we annotated a dataset three times their size (Table ) for both hard and soft skills with domain experts. Tamburri et al. (2020) classify sentences that contain skills in the JP. The authors manually labeled their dataset with domain experts. They annotated whether a sentence contains a skill or not. Once the sentence is identified as containing a skill, the skill cited within is extracted. In contrast, we directly annotate for the span within the sentence. Bhola et al. (2020) cast the task of skill extraction as a multi-label skill classification at the document-level. There is a predefined set of unique skills given the job descriptions and they predict multiple skills that are connected to a given job description using BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). In addition, they experiment with several additional layers for better prediction performance. We instead explore domain-adaptive pre-training for SE.

The work closest to ours is by Chernova (2020), who approaches the task similarly with span-level annotations (including longer spans) for the Finnish language. It is unclear whether they were annotated by domain experts. Also, neither the data nor the annotation guidelines are released. For a comprehensive overview with respect to SE, we refer to Khaouja et al. (2021).

spans: Prior studies reported strong dual-encoder baselines on open-domain QA (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Multi-stage retrievers with improved negatives achieved higher recall (Xiong et al., 2021;Qu et al., 2021). Data augmentation with backtranslation improved classification robustness (Wei and Zou, 2019;Ma, 2019), and representation smoothing was explored for summarization (Jeong et al., 2021). Other tasks benefited.
document: Dense retrieval has become a central paradigm for open-domain question answering and passage search. Dual-encoder architectures learn query and document embeddings and score relevance with inner products or cosine similarity. With large-scale pretraining and contrastive objectives, models such as DPR demonstrate strong recall on Wikipedia-scale corpora (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Despite these advances, performance degrades under domain shift and adversarial lexical variation. Data augmentation aims to improve robustness by exposing models to plausible variants of training examples. In computer vision, interpolation and perturbation methods like Mixup and Cutout have yielded sizable gains (Zhang et al., 2018;DeVries and Taylor, 2017). Adaptations of mixup to NLP operate in token, embedding, or hidden-state space to regularize classifiers (Chen et al., 2020a;Verma et al., 2019;Yin et al., 2021). However, the retrieval objective couples queries and documents, making naive interpolation risk altering relevance structure. Token-level perturbations such as synonym replacement and random deletion remain widely used for classification (Wei and Zou, 2019;Ma, 2019). For retrieval, perturbing subword embeddings or applying Gaussian noise to hidden states has been proposed to encourage invariance (Lee et al., 2021). Related work also examines perturbing document embeddings to diversify supervision signals for summarization and generation (Jeong et al., 2021). Our approach applies dropout to document representations during contrastive training to induce stochastic neighborhoods. This yields multiple noisy views of the same passage without modifying surface forms. We test the method with in-batch negatives and mined hard negatives to probe interactions with sampling strategy. The evaluation spans MSMARCO, Natural Questions, and BEIR datasets to assess both in-domain and zero-shot performance (Nguyen et al., 2016;Kwiatkowski et al., 2019;Thakur et al., 2021). We further analyze sensitivity to dropout rate and placement within the encoder stack. Prior studies reported strong dual-encoder baselines on open-domain QA (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Multi-stage retrievers with improved negatives achieved higher recall (Xiong et al., 2021;Qu et al., 2021). Data augmentation with backtranslation improved classification robustness (Wei and Zou, 2019;Ma, 2019), and representation smoothing was explored for summarization (Jeong et al., 2021). Other tasks benefited. Unlike token or embedding noise, dropout on document vectors modulates only representational sparsity while preserving lexical evidence. By fitting against multiple stochastic realizations, the scorer learns to discount spurious peaks and better align semantic neighborhoods. We show that this regularization complements hard-negative mining by stabilizing gradients when negatives are semantically close. The method adds no inference cost because dropout is disabled at retrieval time and ANN indexing remains unchanged. Altogether, these results position dropout perturbation as a simple, effective augmentation for dense retrieval.

document: Early work on controllable text generation explored attribute conditioning with recurrent models and auxiliary objectives, including Ficler and Goldberg (2017) for stylistic control, Holtzman et al. (2018) with discriminator committees to guide quality, and Hu et al. (2017) combining VAEs and attribute discriminators to steer sentiment and tense. With the advent of large pretrained Transformers such as GPT-2, research expanded to code-based control and plug-and-play guidance, with Keskar et al. (2019) introducing control codes and Dathathri et al. (2020) perturbing hidden activations, while token-level updates motivated faster inference via generative discriminators at decoding time (Krause et al., 2020; Yang and Klein, 2021; Lin and Riedl, 2021). Keskar et al. (2019) train a 1.63B-parameter conditional Transformer with 55 control codes to guide generation. Dinan et al. (2019) study knowledge-grounded conversation with retrieval-augmented dialogue models, and Ghazvininejad et al. (2017) propose constrained decoding for poetry that enforces rhyme and meter, and Li and Liang (2021) introduce prefix-tuning for task control in pretrained language models, and Brown et al. (2020) present GPT-3 exhibiting few-shot capabilities across diverse NLP benchmarks, covering summarization and code generation tasks. Our approach is closest to Yu et al. (2021) and Li and Liang (2021): rather than encoding attribute tokens as in Yu et al., we directly learn a trainable prefix of hidden states for GPT-2 to control target attributes, focusing on attributeful generation rather than task conditioning and employing a distinct prefix-training objective detailed in Section 1.

spans: Prompt-based classification with PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021) reported few-shot accuracy on GLUE, meta-learning with MetaICL (Min et al., 2022) explored in-context exemplars for task coverage, UnifiedQA (Khashabi et al., 2020) combined supervision across question styles, and MAML-style adapters for multitask training (Bansal et al., 2019) targeted adaptation. InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022) refined responses using preference data, PaLM (Chowdhery et al., 2022) scaled language modeling to hundreds of billions of parameters, Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022) aggregated templated tasks at scale, XGLUE (Liang et al., 2020) compiled cross-lingual benchmarks, and T5.1.1 XXL (Raffel et al., 2020) emphasized pretraining stability.
document: Owing to the problems associated with single-task learning in terms of their space and time requirements, several multi-task learning approaches have been proposed over the years. DecaNLP (McCann et al., 2018) built a multi-tasking model by converting the format of each task to a question answering format. Several other works have followed a similar approach by converting tasks to a reading comprehension format (Mishra et al., 2020) and to textual entailment (Wang et al., 2021b). The multitasking model T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) was built with a unified framework that converts all text-based language problems into a text-to-text format; SCIFIVE (Phan et al., 2021) adapted this text-to-text model for biomedical literature. T0 (Sanh et al., 2021) uses prompts along with instances for multitask learning and focuses on achieving zero-shot task generalization. Prompt-based classification with PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021) reported few-shot accuracy on GLUE, meta-learning with MetaICL (Min et al., 2022) explored in-context exemplars for task coverage, UnifiedQA (Khashabi et al., 2020) combined supervision across question styles, and MAML-style adapters for multitask training (Bansal et al., 2019) targeted adaptation. InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022) refined responses using preference data, PaLM (Chowdhery et al., 2022) scaled language modeling to hundreds of billions of parameters, Super-NaturalInstructions (Wang et al., 2022) aggregated templated tasks at scale, XGLUE (Liang et al., 2020) compiled cross-lingual benchmarks, and T5.1.1 XXL (Raffel et al., 2020) emphasized pretraining stability. For instruction learning, the turking test (Efrat and Levy, 2020) was proposed to measure the efficacy of models in following instructions. Natural Instructions (Mishra et al., 2021b) broke down each task into multiple sub-tasks that helped models follow instructions and subsequently generalize to unseen tasks. FLAN (Wei et al., 2021) was built by leveraging instruction tuning on a diverse range of tasks and achieving zero-shot generalization on target unseen tasks. Task reframing (Mishra et al., 2021a) proposed several guidelines to reframe task instructions to improve model responses to follow instructions.

spans: Prabhumoye et al. (2018) exploit back-translation to transfer attributes without parallel supervision in monolingual settings using iterative cycles and a style-invariant latent space. Holtzman et al. (2020) analyze sampling strategies and propose nucleus sampling for improving quality and diversity in open-ended generation with tractable control of tail probabilities. document: Common approaches for flexible attribute-based generation range from retraining or fine-tuning a large underlying base model for generation on domain-specific data (Ziegler et al., 2019), to modifying the architecture of the large pre-trained model (Keskar et al., 2019). Several style transfer approaches hinge on training large generative models with non-parallel (He et al., 2020; Lample et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2020; Reif et al., 2021) data across the domains of interest. Instead of retraining large base models or training new architectures from scratch, recent work has used attribute discriminators to steer the generation (Gu et al., 2017) from a large autoregressive language model. Prabhumoye et al. (2018) exploit back-translation to transfer attributes without parallel supervision in monolingual settings using iterative cycles and a style-invariant latent space. Holtzman et al. (2020) analyze sampling strategies and propose nucleus sampling for improving quality and diversity in open-ended generation with tractable control of tail probabilities. Li and Liang (2021) introduce prefix-tuning to condition large language models via continuous prompts for controllable generation and robustness. Plug-and-Play LM (Dathathri et al., 2020) uses discriminators learned from the LM's top-level hidden layer to modify the LM's states toward increasing probability of the desired attribute via gradient ascent at each step. This restricts the parametrization of the discriminator and also requires access to multiple gradients from the discriminator multiple times per generated sentence, making this approach fairly expensive and restrictive. GeDi (Krause et al., 2020) and Fudge (Yang and Klein, 2021) take similar approaches and guide generation from an LM using specially trained generative and future discriminators, respectively. These approaches in addition to requiring some kind of optimization, also rely on heuristics to manipulate the local softmax distributions of autoregressive models and do not enjoy the benefits of incorporating global features into the generation mechanism in a simple probabilistic manner. In contrast, our energy-based formulation is not only optimization-free, but also fully modular, allowing for heterogeneous black-box experts to be combined with each other.

document: In encoder-based language models (such as BERT), a Transformer encoder layer is composed of several components (Figure ). The core component of the encoder is the self-attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017), which is responsible for the information mixture of a sequence of token representations (x 1 , ..., x n ). Each self-attention head computes a set of attention weights A h = {α h i,j |1 ≤ i, j ≤ n}, where α h i,j is the raw attention weight from the i th token to the j th token in head h ∈ {1, ..., H}. Therefore, the output representation (z i ∈ R d ) for the i th token of a multi-head (with H heads) selfattention module is computed by concatenating the heads' outputs followed by a head-mixing W O projection: Each head's output vector is generated by performing a weighted sum over the transformed value vectors v(x j ) ∈ R dv : Norm-based attention. While one may interpret the attention mechanism using the attention weights A, Kobayashi et al. (2020) argued that doing so would ignore the norm of the transformed vectors multiplied by the weights, elucidating that the weights are insufficient for interpretation. Their solution enhanced the interpretability of attention weights by incorporating the value vectors v(x j ) and the following projection W O . By reformulating Equation 1, we can consider z i as a summation over the attentions heads: Using this reformulation, Kobayashi et al. proposed a norm-based token attribution analysis method, N := (||z i←j ||) ∈ R n×n, to measure each token's contribution in a self-attention module: They showed that incorporating the magnitude of the transformation function (f h (x)) is crucial in assessing the input tokens' contribution to the selfattention output. Clark et al. (2019) examined attention as explanation in fine-tuned models and reported limited faithfulness. Jain and Wallace (2019) questioned whether attention weights correlate with importance by designing counterfactual tests. Michel et al. (2019) analyzed head importance via structured pruning and observed task-specific sparsity. Abnar and Zuidema (2020) proposed attention flow to trace signal paths across layers and assessed alignment with syntactic structure. Pruthi et al. (2020) reported fragility. Residual connections & Layer Normalizations. Kobayashi et al. (2021) added the attention block's Layer Normalization (LN #1) and Residual connection (RES #1) to its prior norm-based analysis to assess the impact of residual connections and layer normalization inside an attention block. N RES := (||z + i←j ||) ∈ R n×n is the analysis method which incorporates the attention block's residual connection. For more information about the reformulation process, see Appendix C in Kobayashi et al. (2021). The input vector x is added to the attribution of each token to itself to incorporate the influence of residual connection #1: They proposed a method for decomposing LN #2 into a summation of normalizations: where m(.) and s(.) are the element-wise mean and standard deviation of the input vector (cf. §A.1). The decomposition can be applied to the contribution vectors: Accordingly, we can compute the magnitude N RESLN := (||z i←j ||) ∈ R n×n, which represents the amount of influence of an encoder layer's input token j on its output token i. Based on this formulation, a context-mixing ratio could be defined as: We focus on encoder layers to isolate the role of value transformations from cross-layer residual pathways. Our analysis targets base-sized models to reduce confounds from scale-related sparsity effects. Experiments by Kobayashi et al. (2021) revealed considerably low r values which indicates the huge impact of the residual connections. In other words, the model tends to preserve token representations more than mixing them with each other.

spans: Diff-pruning introduces task-specific sparse offsets to model weights (Guo et al., 2020). AdapterDrop removes adapters from selected layers during training and inference to cut computation (Rücklé et al., 2020). Compacter compresses adapters with low-rank and parameter sharing for better efficiency per task (Mahabadi et al., 2021), and prompt-based tuning learns continuous prompts as alternatives to parameter updates (Lester et al., 2021) for sequence classification tasks too.
document: For NLP tasks, adapters are introduced for the transformer architecture. A set of adapter parameters is added at each transformer layer, typically in a bottleneck configuration (Houlsby et al., 2019), keeping the output dimension identical so the base model’s structure remains unchanged. Adapters quickly gained popularity in NLP with various applications. For multi-task learning (Caruana, 1997; Zhang and Yang, 2017; Liu et al., 2019b), a projected self-attention layer was proposed by Stickland and Murray (2019), while Bapna et al. (2019) proposed an additional layer norm suitable for machine translation. Besides the applications of adapters, researchers are also dedicated to improving their performance. Based on the architecture introduced by Houlsby et al. (2019), AdapterFusion (Pfeiffer et al., 2020a) leveraged knowledge from multiple tasks with a new two-stage learning algorithm. Despite the recent popularity of these methods, they still train a relatively large number of parameters. Recently, studies have started to focus on improving the parameter efficiency of adapters. Diff-pruning introduces task-specific sparse offsets to model weights (Guo et al., 2020). AdapterDrop removes adapters from selected layers during training and inference to cut computation (Rücklé et al., 2020). Compacter compresses adapters with low-rank and parameter sharing for better efficiency per task (Mahabadi et al., 2021), and prompt-based tuning learns continuous prompts as alternatives to parameter updates (Lester et al., 2021) for sequence classification tasks too. Diff-pruning (Guo et al., 2020) achieves parameter efficiency by adding a sparse, task-specific difference vector to the fixed original parameters, and the vector is pruned during training with a differentiable approximation to the L0-norm penalty. Rücklé et al. (2020) introduced AdapterDrop, later integrated into AdapterHub (Pfeiffer et al., 2020b), by removing adapters from lower transformer layers during training and inference to reduce compute, while Mahabadi et al. (2021) proposed Compacter with low-rank parameterization and shared tensors. Without modifying the PLM architecture, BitFit (Ben Zaken et al., 2021) shows that tuning only bias terms can match full fine-tuning on many tasks. While BitFit applies the same shift to all token representations, our approach allows token-dependent shifts that capture finer-grained context.

spans: Recent works survey prompting methods for eliciting knowledge from large language models (Liu et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2022). Lester et al. (2021) optimized continuous prompts for T5, Lewis et al. (2020) improved QA with retrieval, Min et al. (2022) questioned demonstrations, and Houlsby et al. (2019) advocated adapters.
document: Prompt tuning with GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) demonstrates few-shot performance by prepending textual prompts that steer a pretrained language model toward desired outputs. Motivated by this, subsequent work explored manual prompt design using pattern templates and verbalizers (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b; Mishra et al., 2021). Others proposed automatic search for discrete prompts via mining, gradient-free methods, or scoring heuristics (Jiang et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). Parallel to hard prompts, soft prompts were introduced as tunable continuous embeddings trained with task supervision (Li and Liang, 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021). These studies report that prompt tuning can match or approach full fine-tuning when the underlying model is very large, suggesting an attractive parameter-efficient alternative. Despite accuracy gains, convergence time and instability often make prompt tuning less efficient than fine-tuning under practical budgets. We investigate whether prompt transfer can mitigate these costs by reusing knowledge across settings and examining what governs transferability. Cross-task knowledge transfer has long been used to improve effectiveness and efficiency in NLP systems (Ruder, 2017). In the PLM era, intermediate-task training before target-task fine-tuning has yielded gains across robustness and sample efficiency (Phang et al., 2018; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020; Gururangan et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019a; Vu et al., 2020; Poth et al., 2021). Vu et al. (2020) provided an empirical map of transfer relations among tasks under this paradigm. These explorations primarily address full or partial model fine-tuning rather than prompt tuning directly. Given the potential of prompt-based methods, understanding the mechanics of transfer for prompts remains an open question. As an initial indication, several reports suggest that cross-domain generalization of prompts can be competitive with or stronger than fine-tuning baselines. Recent works survey prompting methods for eliciting knowledge from large language models (Liu et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2022). Lester et al. (2021) optimized continuous prompts for T5, Lewis et al. (2020) improved QA with retrieval, Min et al. (2022) questioned demonstrations, and Houlsby et al. (2019) advocated adapters. Our study complements this line by focusing on efficiency improvements through transfer initialization and by isolating task and model factors that predict success. Concretely, we evaluate prompt reuse across tasks within a model family and across model sizes within and across families. We analyze indicators including task similarity from label semantics, lexical overlap, domain divergence, and calibration measures derived from prompt-conditioned language modeling. We also consider cross-model transfer inspired by architecture-agnostic knowledge transfer methods such as Net2Net transformations (Chen et al., 2016), knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015), and knowledge inheritance for PLMs (Qin et al., 2021). Our transfer protocols include freezing the base model while initializing new soft prompts from a source task or model checkpoint. We compare against training-from-scratch prompts and full fine-tuning under matched compute budgets, with experiments spanning classification, natural language inference, and extractive question answering. The results reveal consistent gains from prompt transfer and highlight when negative transfer arises, offering practical guidance for parameter-efficient adaptation.
